## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define time complexity and space complexity of an algorithm. What is the relationship between them?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Time Complexity:** Time complexity of an algorithm is the amount of **computational time** required to run the algorithm as a function of the input size `n`. It is measured by counting the number of elementary operations (comparisons, assignments, arithmetic operations) executed by the algorithm, independent of the machine or language. It is expressed asymptotically using Big-O, Big-Omega, or Big-Theta notation.

**Space Complexity:** Space complexity of an algorithm is the amount of **memory** required by the algorithm to complete its execution as a function of input size `n`. It includes:
- **Input space:** memory to store the input.
- **Auxiliary space:** extra memory for variables, recursion stack, temporary arrays, etc.

**Relationship:** Time and space are both **computational resources**, but they are typically **traded off** against each other (Time-Space Tradeoff). For example, Merge Sort uses $O(n)$ extra space to achieve $O(n \log n)$ time, while Heap Sort uses $O(1)$ space but takes slightly more constant time. An algorithm that uses less space may need more time, and vice versa.

---

#### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is a recurrence relation? Give two examples of recurrences arising from algorithm analysis.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Recurrence Relation:** A recurrence relation is a mathematical equation that defines a function `T(n)` in terms of its value at **smaller inputs** plus some additional non-recursive work. It is the natural way to express the time complexity of a **recursive algorithm**, because the total work equals the work done at the current level plus the work of recursive sub-calls.

General form:
$$T(n) = a \cdot T(n/b) + f(n)$$

where `a` = number of recursive calls, `n/b` = size of each sub-problem, `f(n)` = cost of non-recursive work.

**Two Examples:**

1. **Binary Search:** $T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1)$ — one recursive call on half the input, plus $O(1)$ comparison.
   - Solution: $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$.

2. **Merge Sort:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(n)$ — two recursive calls on halves, plus linear merging.
   - Solution: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**A. (a)** Explain the asymptotic notations $O$, $\Omega$, and $\Theta$ with neat diagrams. (7 marks)

**A. (b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the recursion tree method. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution for A(a): Asymptotic Notations (7 marks)**

**Definition of Big-O ($O$):** $T(n) = O(f(n))$ means there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$, $T(n) \leq c \cdot f(n)$. It is the **asymptotic upper bound** — meaning the algorithm's running time will never grow faster than $c \cdot f(n)$ beyond $n_0$.

**Definition of Big-Omega ($\Omega$):** $T(n) = \Omega(f(n))$ means there exist positive constants $c$ and $n_0$ such that for all $n \geq n_0$, $T(n) \geq c \cdot f(n)$. It is the **asymptotic lower bound** — the algorithm takes at least $c \cdot f(n)$ time.

**Definition of Big-Theta ($\Theta$):** $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ if and only if $T(n) = O(f(n))$ AND $T(n) = \Omega(f(n))$. It is a **tight bound**, meaning $T(n)$ grows at the same rate as $f(n)$.

**Graphical Interpretation:**

For Big-O, the curve $c \cdot f(n)$ lies **above** $T(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$.

For Big-Omega, the curve $c \cdot f(n)$ lies **below** $T(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$.

For Big-Theta, $T(n)$ is **sandwiched** between $c_1 \cdot f(n)$ and $c_2 \cdot f(n)$ for $n \geq n_0$.

**Example:** $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n + 100$
- $T(n) = O(n^2)$ (drop lower-order term `5n` and constant `100`).
- $T(n) = \Omega(n^2)$ (lower bound dominated by $3n^2$).
- $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$ (tight bound).

**Properties:**
- If $T_1(n) = O(f(n))$ and $T_2(n) = O(g(n))$, then $T_1 + T_2 = O(\max(f(n), g(n)))$.
- If $T(n)$ is a polynomial of degree $d$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^d)$.
- $O(1) < O(\log n) < O(n) < O(n \log n) < O(n^2) < O(2^n) < O(n!)$.

---

#### **Solution for A(b): Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (7 marks)**

**Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$, $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1: Identify parameters.** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2: Build recursion tree.**

- **Level 0 (Root):** One sub-problem of size $n$, cost = $n$. Total level cost = $n$.
- **Level 1:** Two sub-problems each of size $n/2$, cost per sub-problem = $n/2$. Total level cost = $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$.
- **Level 2:** Four sub-problems each of size $n/4$, cost per sub-problem = $n/4$. Total level cost = $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$.
- **Level $i$:** $2^i$ sub-problems each of size $n/2^i$, cost per sub-problem = $n/2^i$. Total level cost = $2^i \cdot (n/2^i) = n$.
- **Last level ($i = \log_2 n$):** $n$ sub-problems of size 1, each cost = $T(1) = 1$. Total leaf cost = $n$.

**Step 3: Total cost across all levels.**

Number of levels (including leaves) = $\log_2 n + 1$.

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} n + n = n \log_2 n + n$$

**Step 4: Asymptotic bound.**

$$T(n) = n \log_2 n + n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

---

#### **Question B: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 Model Paper Alternative]**

**B. (a)** Explain the Master's Theorem for solving recurrences. List its three cases with conditions and solutions. (7 marks)

**B. (b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ using the Master's Theorem and verify by the substitution method. (7 marks)

---

#### **Solution for B(a): Master's Theorem (7 marks)**

The **Master's Theorem** is a direct method to solve recurrences of the form:

$$T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$$

where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is an asymptotically positive function. It compares $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$ (the cost of the leaves if all work were in the leaves).

**Case 1 — Recursion dominates (Leaves are heavier than work at root):**
If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$, then:
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$$

**Case 2 — Balanced work at every level:**
If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$, then:
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$$

**Case 3 — Root work dominates (Cost of root level is heavier than leaves):**
If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$ for some $\epsilon > 0$ AND regularity condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ holds for some $c < 1$, then:
$$T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$$

**Intuition:**
- Case 1: The cost decreases geometrically as we go down the tree → leaf level dominates.
- Case 2: Cost is roughly the same at every level → multiply by number of levels.
- Case 3: Cost increases geometrically as we go down → root level dominates.

**Limitations of Master's Theorem:**
1. Cannot be applied when $b = 1$ (e.g., $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$).
2. Cannot be applied when sub-problems are of **unequal sizes** (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$).
3. Cannot be applied when $f(n)$ is not polynomially smaller or larger than $n^{\log_b a}$.

---

#### **Solution for B(b): $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ (7 marks)**

**By Master's Theorem:**

Parameters: $a = 1$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = 1$.

Critical exponent: $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 1} = n^0 = 1$.

Compare: $f(n) = 1$ vs $n^{\log_b a} = 1$.

$$f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^0 n) = \Theta(1)$$

This matches **Case 2** with $k = 0$.

**By Case 2:**
$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n) = \Theta(1 \cdot \log^1 n) = \Theta(\log n)$$

**Verification by Substitution Method:**

**Guess:** $T(n) = c \log n$ for some $c > 0$.

**Inductive Proof:**

**Base case:** $T(1) = c \log 1 = 0 \leq c \cdot 1 \cdot \log 1 = 0$. Holds for $c = 1$ trivially. Let $T(2) = 1$, then $c \log 2 = c \cdot 1 = c$. For $c = 1$, $T(2) = 1 = \log 2 \approx 1$? Actually $\log_2 2 = 1$. Holds.

**Inductive step:** Assume $T(n/2) \leq c \log(n/2)$ for some $n/2 \geq 2$. Show $T(n) \leq c \log n$.

$$T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 \leq c \log(n/2) + 1 = c(\log n - \log 2) + 1 = c \log n - c + 1$$

For this to be $\leq c \log n$, we need $-c + 1 \leq 0$, i.e., $c \geq 1$.

With $c = 1$:
$$T(n) \leq 1 \cdot \log n = \log n$$

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(\log n)$. Combined with the lower bound from the recursion tree, $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
> 1. **Applying Master's Theorem to non-divide-and-conquer recurrences:** Students often try to apply Master's Theorem to $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ (Tower of Hanoi-style or linear recursions). This is **WRONG** — Master's Theorem requires $b > 1$. For these, use **iteration method**.
> 2. **Forgetting the regularity condition in Case 3:** When $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \epsilon})$, you must also verify the regularity condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$. Skipping this loses 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Confusing Big-O with Big-Theta:** Big-O is an upper bound, Big-Theta is tight. $T(n) = 3n^2 + 5n = O(n^3)$ is true, but the **tight** bound is $\Theta(n^2)$. Examiners expect the tightest bound.
> 4. **Ignoring the base case:** A recurrence without a base case (e.g., $T(1) = 1$) is incomplete. Always specify the base case.
> 5. **Forgetting the log base in Master Theorem:** $n^{\log_b a}$ is the critical quantity. Get $a$ and $b$ correct: e.g., for $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^2$, $a=4, b=2$, so $n^{\log_2 4} = n^2$.
> 6. **Mixing up space and time:** When asked for space complexity, students sometimes give time complexity. Always distinguish — "space complexity of the algorithm is $O(n)$ because of the auxiliary array used in merging".

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Module 1 — Time/Space Complexity & Recurrences:**

- [x] **Time complexity** is the count of basic operations, NOT wall-clock time.
- [x] **Space complexity** = input space + auxiliary space. Always state both.
- [x] **Asymptotic notations:** $O$ (upper), $\Omega$ (lower), $\Theta$ (tight).
- [x] **Order of growth (best to worst):** $1 < \log n < n < n \log n < n^2 < 2^n < n!$.
- [x] A **recurrence equation** expresses $T(n)$ in terms of $T$ of smaller input.
- [x] General divide-and-conquer form: $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, with $a \geq 1, b > 1$.
- [x] **Master's Theorem** has 3 cases — compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$.
- [x] **Case 1 (leaves dominate):** $f(n)$ is polynomially smaller than $n^{\log_b a}$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
- [x] **Case 2 (balanced):** $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$.
- [x] **Case 3 (root dominates):** $f(n)$ is polynomially larger → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$, only if regularity holds.
- [x] **Recursion Tree Method:** Sum the cost at each level; last term in geometric series dominates.
- [x] **Substitution Method:** Guess a bound, then prove by strong mathematical induction.
- [x] **Iteration Method:** Repeatedly substitute until a pattern emerges; solve the resulting sum.
- [x] Master Theorem **does NOT apply** when $b = 1$ or sub-problems are unequal in size.
- [x] Standard recurrences to memorize: Binary Search $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1 = \Theta(\log n)$, Merge Sort $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n = \Theta(n \log n)$.
- [x] Always **drop lower-order terms and constants** when stating Big-O / Big-Theta.
- [x] In KTU 14-mark questions, **show all 3 levels of the recursion tree** clearly, label costs, and sum explicitly.
- [x] Mention **base case** (e.g., $T(1) = 1$) whenever solving a recurrence.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Solving Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solving Recurrence Equations - Iteration Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Recurrence Relation** (or **Recurrence Equation**) is a mathematical equation or inequality that describes a function $T(n)$ in terms of its own value on smaller inputs. It forms the mathematical backbone for analyzing the time complexity of **recursive algorithms** and is the standard formalism taught in Algorithm Analysis under the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PECAT415.

Mathematically, a recurrence has two essential components:

- **Recursive Case:** An expression of the form $T(n) = f(T(k_1), T(k_2), \ldots)$ where $k_i < n$.
- **Base Case (Boundary Condition):** A constant or explicit value, e.g., $T(1) = c$ or $T(0) = \Theta(1)$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Highlight:** In PECAT415 Module 1, students are expected to **solve** given recurrences, not just write them. The **Iteration Method** (also called the **Substitution Method** or **Unfolding Method**) is one of the four canonical techniques listed in the syllabus (the others being Recursion Tree, Master Theorem, and Guess-and-Verify via Induction).

### 1.2 The Iteration Method — Formal Definition

The **Iteration Method** is a direct, constructive technique for solving a recurrence $T(n)$ by repeatedly substituting the recurrence into itself, "unfolding" each recursive call until the base case is reached. The result is expressed as a sum (or closed-form) of the per-level costs.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board-Examiner Definition (verbatim-style for writing in exams):**
> *"The Iteration Method solves a recurrence by repeatedly expanding the recursive term and accumulating the non-recursive cost at each level, eventually expressing T(n) as a summation that can be evaluated using standard algebraic, geometric, or harmonic series formulas."*

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Russian Doll" of Recursion

Imagine a set of **Russian nesting dolls (Matryoshka)**. Each doll contains a smaller doll inside it. To count the total number of layers, you do not have a magic formula — you simply **open the current doll, count it, then open the doll inside it, count it, and continue until you reach the smallest solid doll (the base case)**.

- Each doll you open = **one level of recursion** (one iteration of substitution).
- The size label on each doll = **the input size at that level** ($n$, $n-1$, $n/2$, $n/4$, ...).
- The total count = **the cumulative cost $T(n)$** = sum of all per-level costs.

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition for Recursion:** A recursive algorithm "spends" some work at the current level and then delegates a smaller problem to a copy of itself. Iteration is the process of *rewriting* this delegation in expanded algebraic form.

### 1.4 Why the Iteration Method Matters in Algorithm Design

In real engineering practice, the iteration method is the **foundation upon which the Master Theorem and the Recursion Tree method are justified**. It is also the most reliable technique when the recurrence has a non-standard form that the Master Theorem cannot classify (e.g., $T(n) = T(n-3) + T(n-5) + n$).

| Domain | Practical Use |
|---|---|
| **Compiler Design** | Cost of recursive-descent parsers |
| **Database Systems** | Cost of B-tree / divide-and-conquer joins |
| **Computer Graphics** | Recursive subdivision of polygons |
| **Operating Systems** | Recursive scheduling / fork-join models |
| **Numerical Methods** | Recursive FFT, Strassen, divide-and-conquer solvers |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The standard metrics you will encounter in KTU questions are **asymptotic bounds**: $O$, $\Omega$, and $\Theta$. The iteration method usually yields the **exact sum**, from which the asymptotic class is then extracted.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Operational Steps of the Iteration Method

The method follows a strict, repeatable **5-step algorithm**. Mastering this sequence guarantees full marks on derivation questions in KTU End-Semester Examinations (ESE).

**Step 1 — Identify the Form of the Recurrence**
Recognize the pattern: Is it a *decrease-and-conquer* recurrence $T(n) = T(n-b) + f(n)$ or a *divide-and-conquer* recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$? This dictates how the input size shrinks at each level.

**Step 2 — Expand / Unfold the Recurrence**
Repeatedly substitute the right-hand side into itself. For a recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, expand up to $i$ levels:

$$T(n) = aT\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n) = a^{2}T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{2}}\right) + af\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

After $i$ iterations:

$$T(n) = a^{i}T\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right) + \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} a^{j} f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{j}}\right)$$

**Step 3 — Identify the Termination Condition**
The recursion stops when the sub-problem size reaches the base case (typically $T(1)$ or $T(c)$). Solve $n / b^{i} = c$ to find the number of levels $i = \log_{b}(n/c) = \log_{b} n - \log_{b} c$.

**Step 4 — Substitute the Depth into the Sum**
Replace $i$ in the closed form. The term $a^{i}T(n/b^{i})$ becomes the **base-cost contribution**, typically $\Theta(1)$ times a power of $a$ and $b$.

**Step 5 — Evaluate the Summation and Apply Asymptotic Bound**
Use one of the closed-form sum identities in §2.3 to collapse the sum into a Big-Oh expression.

### 2.2 Why the Method Works (Theoretical Justification)

The iteration method works because the recurrence defines $T(n)$ as a fixed point of a well-defined operator. By unrolling the recurrence $k$ times, we obtain an *upper bound* (or exact value) on $T(n)$ for any finite $k$, and by choosing $k$ to match the depth of recursion, we capture the full cost. This is formally justified by the **induction principle**, which is why the iteration method is often paired with **proof by mathematical induction** in KTU 2-mark questions.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Print-friendly reference table for ESE.** Replace vertical pipes inside math with $\vert$ or $\mid$ when copying to handwritten sheets.

| # | Series Identity | Closed Form | Used When |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 1 = i$ | $i$ | Constant per-level cost |
| 2 | $\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} x^{j} = \dfrac{x^{i}-1}{x-1},\ x \ne 1$ | Geometric (finite) | Geometric growth/decay |
| 3 | $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} x^{j} = \dfrac{1}{1-x},\ \vert x \vert < 1$ | Geometric (infinite) | Decaying geometric series |
| 4 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} j = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^{2})$ | Arithmetic / triangular | Linear cost summed $n$ times |
| 5 | $\sum_{j=0}^{\log_{b} n - 1} b^{j} = \Theta(n)$ | Geometric with ratio $b$ | Divide-and-conquer with $a=b$ |
| 6 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} j^{k} \approx \dfrac{n^{k+1}}{k+1}$ | Power sum | Polynomial per-level cost |
| 7 | $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{j} = \ln n + \gamma = \Theta(\log n)$ | Harmonic | $\log$ per-level cost |

### 2.4 Recurrence Class Reference (For Pattern Recognition)

| Class | General Form | Common Algorithm | Solution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Linear search type | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | Sequential scan | $T(n) = \Theta(n)$ |
| Sum of integers | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | Triangle / prefix sum | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$ |
| Power recursion | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | Binary search | $T(n) = \Theta(\log n)$ |
| Standard D&C | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | Merge sort | $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$ |
| Master Case 1 | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + 1,\ a > b^{0}$ | Karatsuba-like | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$ |
| Master Case 2 | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$ | Merge sort | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \log n)$ |
| Master Case 3 | $T(n) = aT(n/b) + n^{k},\ k > \log_{b} a$ | Strassen (variant) | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{k})$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Utility:** The iteration method is the only technique that works for **non-homogeneous**, **non-standard**, or **irregular** recurrences (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$). It is the universal fallback when the Master Theorem's regularity condition fails.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

This section contains the **exhaustive, line-by-line algebraic derivations** that KTU examiners expect for full marks. Every step is justified; no transitions are skipped.

---

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Linear Recurrence (Decrease-by-Constant)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ with the base case $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1: Unfold the recurrence for one level.**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + n$$

**Step 2: Substitute $T(n-1)$ using the recurrence relation with argument $(n-1)$.**

$$T(n) = \bigl[T(n-2) + (n-1)\bigr] + n$$

**Step 3: Substitute $T(n-2)$ using the recurrence with argument $(n-2)$.**

$$T(n) = \bigl[\bigl(T(n-3) + (n-2)\bigr) + (n-1)\bigr] + n$$

**Step 4: Generalize to $i$ levels of unfolding.**

$$T(n) = T(n-i) + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} (n-k)$$

**Step 5: Identify termination.** The base case is $T(1)$, so set $n - i = 1 \Rightarrow i = n - 1$.

**Step 6: Substitute $i = n-1$.**

$$T(n) = T(1) + \sum_{k=0}^{n-2} (n-k)$$

**Step 7: Simplify the sum using the identity** $\sum_{j=1}^{m} j = \frac{m(m+1)}{2}$**.**

$$T(n) = 1 + \sum_{j=2}^{n} j = 1 + \left[\frac{n(n+1)}{2} - 1\right]$$

**Step 8: Final closed form and asymptotic bound.**

$$T(n) = \frac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^{2})$$

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence (Merge Sort Style)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1: First level unfolding.**

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$

**Step 2: Apply the recurrence to each of the two $T(n/2)$ terms.**

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n$$

**Step 3: Distribute the leading 2.**

$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2 \cdot \frac{n}{2} + n = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n + n$$

**Step 4: Third level unfolding.**

$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2n = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 4 \cdot \frac{n}{4} + 2n$$

**Step 5: Recognize the pattern.** After $i$ levels:

$$T(n) = 2^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + i \cdot n$$

**Step 6: Identify the depth.** Base case $T(1)$ reached when $n / 2^{i} = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 7: Substitute $i = \log_{2} n$.**

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + n \log_{2} n$$

**Step 8: Simplify using $2^{\log_{2} n} = n$ and $T(1) = 1$.**

$$T(n) = n + n \log_{2} n = \Theta(n \log n)$$

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Exponential Cost Recurrence (Master Case 1)

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$ with $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1: First unfolding.**

$$T(n) = 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + n$$

**Step 2: Second unfolding.**

$$T(n) = 3\left[3T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + n = 9T\!\left(\frac{n}{16}\right) + \frac{3n}{4} + n$$

**Step 3: General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = 3^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^{i}}\right) + n \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{j}$$

**Step 4: Use the geometric sum identity.**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{j} = \frac{1 - (3/4)^{i}}{1 - 3/4} = 4\left[1 - \left(\frac{3}{4}\right)^{i}\right]$$

**Step 5: Identify depth.** $n/4^{i} = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_{4} n$.

**Step 6: Substitute and evaluate the base term.**

$$3^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{4^{i}}\right) = 3^{\log_{4} n} \cdot T(1) = n^{\log_{4} 3}$$

**Step 7: Substitute the sum and bound asymptotically.** Since $(3/4)^{i} \to 0$ as $i \to \infty$:

$$n \cdot 4 \left[1 - 0\right] = 4n$$

**Step 8: Final bound.**

$$T(n) = n^{\log_{4} 3} + 4n = \Theta(n^{\log_{4} 3}) \quad \text{since} \ \log_{4} 3 \approx 0.792 < 1$$

> [!NOTE]
> This matches **Master Theorem Case 1** with $a=3$, $b=4$, $f(n)=n$, and $n^{\log_{4} 3} = \Omega(n)$.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Geometric Decay in Sub-problem Size

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ with $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 1: Unfolding sequence.**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 1 = \left[T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 1\right] + 1 = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2$$

**Step 2: Continue.**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3$$

**Step 3: General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + i$$

**Step 4: Depth.** $n/2^{i} = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 5: Final form.**

$$T(n) = T(1) + \log_{2} n = 1 + \log_{2} n = \Theta(\log n)$$

> [!TIP]
> **Pattern Recognition Tip:** When the recurrence has the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ and the recursion dominates (Case 1 of Master Theorem), the iteration method always yields a sum of the form $\sum a^{j} f(n/b^{j})$, which collapses to a polynomial in $n$.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Linear Recurrence with Constant Cost

**Problem:** Solve $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ with $T(0) = 0$.

**Step 1: Unfold once.**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + c$$

**Step 2: Unfold twice.**

$$T(n) = T(n-2) + c + c = T(n-2) + 2c$$

**Step 3: General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = T(n-i) + i \cdot c$$

**Step 4: Termination.** $n - i = 0 \Rightarrow i = n$.

**Step 5: Final form.**

$$T(n) = T(0) + n \cdot c = cn = \Theta(n)$$

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Summary of Examples

| Example | Recurrence | Pattern at Level $i$ | Depth $i$ | Closed Form | Asymptotic Class |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 3.1 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + n$ | $T(n-i) + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1}(n-k)$ | $n-1$ | $\dfrac{n(n+1)}{2}$ | $\Theta(n^{2})$ |
| 3.2 | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | $2^{i}T(n/2^{i}) + i \cdot n$ | $\log_{2} n$ | $n + n\log n$ | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| 3.3 | $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$ | $3^{i}T(n/4^{i}) + 4n[1-(3/4)^{i}]$ | $\log_{4} n$ | $n^{\log_{4} 3} + 4n$ | $\Theta(n^{\log_{4} 3})$ |
| 3.4 | $T(n) = T(n/2) + 1$ | $T(n/2^{i}) + i$ | $\log_{2} n$ | $1 + \log_{2} n$ | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| 3.5 | $T(n) = T(n-1) + c$ | $T(n-i) + i \cdot c$ | $n$ | $cn$ | $\Theta(n)$ |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — The Iteration Method as a Functional Pipeline

This diagram captures the **algorithmic flow** of solving any recurrence via the iteration method. Each block represents an algebraic stage a student must pass through on the way to the final asymptotic answer.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["INPUT Recurrence T of n and Base Case"] --> B["STEP 1 Classify Form: D and C or Decrease and Conquer"]
    B --> C["STEP 2 Unfold Recurrence for i Levels"]
    C --> D["STEP 3 Detect Pattern: Closed Form in i"]
    D --> E["STEP 4 Solve Depth Equation n by b power i equals constant"]
    E --> F{"STEP 5 Evaluate Sum Using Formula Sheet"}
    F --> G["Geometric Sum Identity"] 
    F --> H["Arithmetic Sum Identity"] 
    F --> I["Harmonic Sum Identity"] 
    F --> J["Logarithmic Sum Identity"]
    G --> K["STEP 6 Substitute Depth and Simplify"]
    H --> K
    I --> K
    J --> K
    K --> L["STEP 7 Apply Asymptotic Bound O Omega or Theta"]
    L --> M["OUTPUT Final Complexity Class"]
```

### 4.2 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ (Merge Sort)

This Mermaid graph renders the **per-level cost structure** of Example 3.2. The root is the top-level call; each child represents one of the two recursive sub-calls. The leaf level corresponds to the base case.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root["Level 0: cost n, size n"] --> left1["Level 1 left: cost n over 2, size n over 2"]
    root --> right1["Level 1 right: cost n over 2, size n over 2"]
    left1 --> ll2["Level 2 left-left: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    left1 --> lr2["Level 2 left-right: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    right1 --> rl2["Level 2 right-left: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    right1 --> rr2["Level 2 right-right: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    ll2 --> base1["Base case size 1 cost 1"]
    lr2 --> base2["Base case size 1 cost 1"]
    rl2 --> base3["Base case size 1 cost 1"]
    rr2 --> base4["Base case size 1 cost 1"]
```

**Reading the tree:** There are $\log_{2} n + 1$ levels. At every level, the total work is $n$ (since $2 \cdot (n/2) = n$, $4 \cdot (n/4) = n$, etc.). The leaves contribute $\Theta(n)$ in total. Hence the total cost is $(\log_{2} n + 1) \cdot n = \Theta(n \log n)$.

### 4.3 Recursion Tree for $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + n$ (Example 3.3)

This visualization shows the **shrinking-then-dominating** behavior of Case 1 of the Master Theorem. The top levels have many leaves, but the cost decreases geometrically.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    r["Level 0: cost n, sub-problems 1 of size n"] --> a1["L1 child A: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    r --> a2["L1 child B: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    r --> a3["L1 child C: cost n over 4, size n over 4"]
    a1 --> a1a["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a1 --> a1b["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a1 --> a1c["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a2 --> a2a["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a2 --> a2b["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a2 --> a2c["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a3 --> a3a["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a3 --> a3b["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
    a3 --> a3c["L2 grandchild cost n over 16"]
```

**Reading the tree:** Top level cost is $n$, next level is $3 \cdot (n/4) = 0.75n$, next is $9 \cdot (n/16) = 0.5625n$, decaying geometrically by ratio $3/4$. The tree is balanced, depth is $\log_{4} n$, and the dominant cost is at the leaves: $n^{\log_{4} 3}$.

### 4.4 Comparison Flow — When to Use Iteration vs Other Methods

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start["Given Recurrence T of n"] --> q1{"Is it of form aT of n over b plus f of n?"}
    q1 -- Yes --> q2{"Does f of n match a regularity condition?"}
    q1 -- No --> iter["Use ITERATION METHOD directly"]
    q2 -- Yes --> mt["Apply MASTER THEOREM in O of 1"]
    q2 -- No --> iter
    iter --> verify["Verify result by MATHEMATICAL INDUCTION"]
    mt --> verify
```

> [!TIP]
> **Strategy Tip for KTU Exams:** Always start with the Master Theorem as a quick check. If it applies and matches, you can still be required to show the iteration method derivation. Use iteration to *verify* the result obtained by other means.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part A question must be answered in approximately **60–80 words** as per KTU ESE valuation norms. The model answers below are calibrated to that length.

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define a recurrence relation. What are the two essential components of a recurrence with respect to algorithm analysis?*

**Model Answer:**
A **recurrence relation** is an equation that expresses the value of a function $T(n)$ for a given input size $n$ in terms of its value on one or more smaller input sizes. The two essential components are: (i) the **recursive case**, which gives $T(n)$ in terms of $T(k)$ for $k < n$, and (ii) the **base case** (or boundary condition), which terminates the recursion by providing a direct value, typically $T(1) = \Theta(1)$ or $T(0) = c$.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
*List any three techniques for solving recurrence relations in algorithm analysis.*

**Model Answer:**
The three standard techniques for solving recurrences in algorithm analysis are: (1) the **Iteration Method** (also called substitution or unfolding), which repeatedly expands the recurrence and accumulates per-level costs; (2) the **Recursion Tree Method**, which visualizes the recursion as a tree and sums the cost at each level; and (3) the **Master Theorem**, which provides a direct $\Theta$-bound for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ under regularity conditions. A fourth, more general technique is the **Guess-and-Verify method using Mathematical Induction**.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions on Module 1 carry **14 marks** with internal choice. Each sub-part is typically worth **7 marks**. The model solutions below are exhaustive, with all working shown.

---

#### **Question A — Iteration on a Decrease-and-Conquer Recurrence**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q.** *(a)* Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 3T(n-1)$ with the base case $T(0) = 5$ using the **iteration method**. Express your final answer in Big-Oh notation. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n-1) + 2^{n}$ with the base case $T(0) = 1$ using the iteration method. State the asymptotic class. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Write the recurrence explicitly.**

$$T(n) = 3T(n-1), \quad T(0) = 5$$

**Step 2 — Unfold once.**

$$T(n) = 3 \cdot 3T(n-2) = 3^{2}T(n-2)$$

**Step 3 — Unfold again.**

$$T(n) = 3^{2} \cdot 3T(n-3) = 3^{3}T(n-3)$$

**Step 4 — General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = 3^{i} T(n-i)$$

**Step 5 — Termination.** $T(0)$ is reached when $n - i = 0 \Rightarrow i = n$.

**Step 6 — Substitute $i = n$.**

$$T(n) = 3^{n} T(0) = 3^{n} \cdot 5 = 5 \cdot 3^{n}$$

**Step 7 — Asymptotic bound.**

$$T(n) = \Theta(3^{n})$$

This is exponential growth — characteristic of recursive algorithms with branching factor 3 and no work reduction (e.g., a naive 3-way recursive enumeration).

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recurrence:** $T(n) = T(n-1) + 2^{n}$, $T(0) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Unfold once.**

$$T(n) = T(n-1) + 2^{n}$$

**Step 3 — Unfold twice.**

$$T(n) = \bigl[T(n-2) + 2^{n-1}\bigr] + 2^{n} = T(n-2) + 2^{n-1} + 2^{n}$$

**Step 4 — Unfold to level $i$.**

$$T(n) = T(n-i) + \sum_{k=0}^{i-1} 2^{n-k}$$

**Step 5 — Termination.** Set $n - i = 0 \Rightarrow i = n$.

**Step 6 — Substitute and use geometric sum.**

$$T(n) = T(0) + \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{n-k} = 1 + 2^{n} \sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{-k}$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate the inner sum.**

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1} 2^{-k} = \frac{1 - 2^{-n}}{1 - 2^{-1}} = 2\left(1 - 2^{-n}\right) = 2 - 2^{1-n}$$

**Step 8 — Final form.**

$$T(n) = 1 + 2^{n}\left(2 - 2^{1-n}\right) = 1 + 2^{n+1} - 2 = 2^{n+1} - 1$$

**Step 9 — Asymptotic class.**

$$T(n) = \Theta(2^{n})$$

---

#### **Question B — Iteration on a Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence (Internal Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2023 Style]**

**Q.** *(a)* Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ using the iteration method. Compare your result with the Master Theorem classification. *(7 marks)*

*(b)* Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^{2}$ using the iteration method. Identify which Master Theorem case it belongs to. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Step 1 — Recurrence:** $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$, $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Unfold once.**

$$T(n) = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n$$

**Step 3 — Unfold twice.**

$$T(n) = 4\left[4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n}{2}\right] + n = 16T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2n + n$$

**Step 4 — Unfold thrice.**

$$T(n) = 16\left[4T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n}{4}\right] + 3n = 64T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 4n + 3n$$

**Step 5 — General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = 4^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + n \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2^{j}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the geometric sum.**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2^{j} = 2^{i} - 1$$

So:

$$T(n) = 4^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + n(2^{i} - 1)$$

**Step 7 — Depth.** $n / 2^{i} = 1 \Rightarrow i = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 8 — Substitute $i = \log_{2} n$.**

$$T(n) = 4^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + n(2^{\log_{2} n} - 1)$$

**Step 9 — Simplify using $4^{\log_{2} n} = n^{2}$ and $2^{\log_{2} n} = n$.**

$$T(n) = n^{2} \cdot 1 + n(n - 1) = n^{2} + n^{2} - n = 2n^{2} - n$$

**Step 10 — Asymptotic bound and Master Theorem comparison.**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$$

**Master Theorem verification:** Here $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$. We compute $n^{\log_{b} a} = n^{\log_{2} 4} = n^{2}$. Since $f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - \varepsilon})$ for $\varepsilon = 1$, this is **Case 1** of the Master Theorem, giving $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$. Both methods agree. ✓

---

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^{2}$, $T(1) = 1$.

**Step 2 — Unfold once.**

$$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Unfold twice.**

$$T(n) = 2\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n^{2}}{4}\right] + n^{2} = 4T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + \frac{n^{2}}{2} + n^{2}$$

**Step 4 — Unfold thrice.**

$$T(n) = 4\left[2T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n^{2}}{16}\right] + \frac{3n^{2}}{2} = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{4n^{2}}{16} + \frac{3n^{2}}{2}$$

$$= 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + \frac{n^{2}}{4} + \frac{3n^{2}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — General pattern at level $i$.**

$$T(n) = 2^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + n^{2} \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{j}$$

**Step 6 — Evaluate the geometric sum.**

$$\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^{j} = \frac{1 - (1/2)^{i}}{1 - 1/2} = 2\left[1 - 2^{-i}\right]$$

So:

$$T(n) = 2^{i} T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + 2n^{2}\left[1 - 2^{-i}\right]$$

**Step 7 — Depth.** $i = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 8 — Substitute.**

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_{2} n} \cdot T(1) + 2n^{2}\left[1 - 2^{-\log_{2} n}\right] = n \cdot 1 + 2n^{2}\left[1 - \frac{1}{n}\right]$$

**Step 9 — Simplify.**

$$T(n) = n + 2n^{2} - 2n = 2n^{2} - n = \Theta(n^{2})$$

**Step 10 — Master Theorem classification.** Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^{2}$. We have $n^{\log_{b} a} = n^{\log_{2} 2} = n^{1} = n$. Since $f(n) = n^{2} = \Omega(n^{1 + \varepsilon})$ for $\varepsilon = 1$, this is **Case 3** of the Master Theorem. The regularity condition $a \cdot f(n/b) \le c \cdot f(n)$ becomes $2 \cdot (n/2)^{2} = n^{2}/2 \le c \cdot n^{2}$, which holds for $c = 1/2 < 1$. So $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$ is confirmed. ✓

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU ESE Valuation:**
> 1. **Skipping the generalization step.** Examiners expect to see the *general $i$-th level pattern* explicitly, not just 2–3 unfoldings. If you only unfold twice and jump to the answer, expect to lose **3–4 marks**.
> 2. **Forgetting to substitute the depth.** A frequent error is to leave the answer in the form $T(n) = 2^{i}T(n/2^{i}) + n \cdot i$ without substituting $i = \log_{2} n$. This yields an answer worth at most half marks.
> 3. **Mis-evaluating the geometric sum.** Students often write $\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2^{j} = i$ instead of $2^{i} - 1$. Always verify with small cases.
> 4. **Confusing $a^{i}$ with $i^{a}$.** In the term $a^{i} T(n/b^{i})$, the exponent is on $a$, not on $i$.
> 5. **Missing asymptotic notation.** A full-mark answer must end with $T(n) = \Theta(\cdot)$, $O(\cdot)$, or $\Omega(\cdot)$ as explicitly required by the question. Numerical closed forms alone are insufficient.
> 6. **Ignoring the base case.** Every iteration-method solution must identify and use the base case at the final substitution step.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (print-friendly):**

- A **recurrence relation** defines $T(n)$ in terms of $T(k)$ for $k < n$, plus a **base case**.
- The **Iteration Method** works by *unfolding* the recurrence to a general level $i$, then *substituting* the depth $i = \log_{b}(n/c)$ where $c$ is the base-case size.
- The two canonical forms are:
  - **Decrease-and-conquer:** $T(n) = T(n-b) + f(n)$ — depth is $n/b$.
  - **Divide-and-conquer:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — depth is $\log_{b} n$.
- The cost accumulated at each level follows one of four series: **constant**, **arithmetic**, **geometric**, or **harmonic** — each with a known closed form.
- Geometric sum identities:
  - $\sum_{j=0}^{i-1} x^{j} = \dfrac{x^{i} - 1}{x - 1}$ for $x \ne 1$.
  - $\sum_{j=0}^{\infty} x^{j} = \dfrac{1}{1 - x}$ for $\vert x \vert < 1$.
- Arithmetic sum: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} j = \dfrac{n(n+1)}{2} = \Theta(n^{2})$.
- Harmonic sum: $\sum_{j=1}^{n} \dfrac{1}{j} = \Theta(\log n)$.
- Always show: (1) Unfolding, (2) General $i$-level pattern, (3) Depth calculation, (4) Substitution, (5) Sum evaluation, (6) Asymptotic class.
- **Master Theorem cross-check** (for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$):
  - Case 1: $f(n) = O(n^{\log_{b} a - \varepsilon})$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$.
  - Case 2: $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \log^{k} n)$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
  - Case 3: $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_{b} a + \varepsilon})$ with regularity → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
- The iteration method is the **universal fallback** when the Master Theorem does not apply (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$).
- **Verification step** (optional but highly recommended for 14-mark answers): Confirm the result by mathematical induction.
- The iteration method, recursion-tree method, and Master theorem are all **mutually consistent** — they must give the same asymptotic class for a well-posed recurrence.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Recursion Tree Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHMS ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Recursion Tree Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Recursion Tree Method (KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition)**
> The *Recursion Tree Method* is a non-closed-form asymptotic technique used to solve **recurrence relations** of the form $T(n) = aT\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$. It models the recursive expansion of a recurrence as a rooted tree where each node represents the cost of a single subproblem, thereby transforming the recurrence into a summation of per-level costs which is then evaluated asymptotically using standard bounding techniques.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for *PCCAT415 / PECAT415 – Algorithm Analysis and Design*, the recursion tree method is listed alongside the **Substitution Method** and the **Master Method** as one of the three primary tools for solving the recurrences arising from divide-and-conquer and recursive algorithms (e.g., Merge Sort, Quick Sort, Binary Search, Strassen's Matrix Multiplication).

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Think of a family tree… but for math.**
> Imagine you have a big problem of size $n$ (the *root* ancestor). You ask it: *"How are you built?"* It says: *"I have $a$ children, each with a problem $\frac{n}{b}$ times smaller, and I personally do work worth $f(n)$."* Each child, when asked, gives the same answer. You keep expanding downward until the children are so small (size $1$ or constant) that they stop having children. The **total cost** of the entire family is simply the sum of work done at every generation. This family is your **recursion tree**, and counting its total work is the **Recursion Tree Method**.

The method is essentially a *visual accounting trick*. Instead of trying to guess and prove (Substitution Method) or look up a closed form (Master Method), we **draw** the recurrence, **add up** the work per level, and then bound the resulting sum.

## 1.3 Why the Method is Important in KTU Examinations

- It provides a **structured, graphical proof** of the asymptotic bound — examiners reward this highly.
- It works even when the Master Theorem's regularity condition is *violated*.
- It naturally introduces the engineer to the concept of **amortized per-level cost analysis**, used in real systems (e.g., load balancing, distributed job schedulers, garbage collectors like the G1 GC in Java HotSpot).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Standard Divide-and-Conquer Recurrence

Most recurrences in the KTU Module 1 syllabus follow the canonical divide-and-conquer form:

$$T(n) = a \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

where each symbol has a strict KTU interpretation:

| Symbol | KTU Interpretation | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| $a$ | Number of **recursive subproblems** generated | $\ge 1$ (integer) |
| $b$ | Factor by which the subproblem **size shrinks** | $> 1$ (real, usually integer) |
| $f(n)$ | **Non-recursive cost** of dividing + combining | Function of $n$ |
| $n$ | Current problem size (assumed $\ge 1$) | Positive integer |
| $T(1)$ or $T(c)$ | **Base case cost** | Constant $\Theta(1)$ |

## 2.2 The Three Phases of the Recursion Tree Method

> [!NOTE]
> **Phase 1 — Expand**: Recursively unroll the recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ into a tree until the leaves reach the base case $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.
>
> **Phase 2 — Per-Level Cost**: For every depth $i$ (where root is depth $0$), compute
> $$\text{Level } i \text{ cost} = a^{i} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$$
>
> **Phase 3 — Sum the Series**: Sum the level costs over all $L+1$ levels (where $L = \log_{b} n$ is the height of the tree) and apply asymptotic bounding.

## 2.3 General Tree Anatomy (Killer Cheat Sheet)

| Tree Component | Formula | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Number of nodes at level $i$ | $a^{i}$ | $a$-ary branching factor |
| Subproblem size at level $i$ | $\dfrac{n}{b^{i}}$ | Each subproblem shrinks by $b$ |
| Number of levels (height) | $L = \log_{b} n$ | Until $n/b^{L} = 1$ |
| Number of leaves | $a^{L} = n^{\log_{b} a}$ | Total leaf cost $= \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_{b} a}\right)$ |
| Cost of a node at level $i$ | $f\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$ | Per-call non-recursive work |
| Total cost at level $i$ | $a^{i} \cdot f\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b^{i}}\right)$ | Nodes $\times$ per-node cost |
| Total cost of the tree | $\displaystyle\sum_{i=0}^{L} a^{i} \cdot f\!\left(\frac{n}{b^{i}}\right) + \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_{b} a}\right)$ | Full summation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Identity**: The sum of the leaf-level cost is *always* $\Theta\!\left(n^{\log_{b} a}\right)$, regardless of $f(n)$. This is the single most-tested identity in Module 1.

## 2.4 Three Canonical Tree Shapes

The KTU board examiner's favourite question pattern reduces to one of these three tree shapes, distinguished by how the per-level cost behaves:

| Case | Tree Shape | Per-Level Cost Behaviour | Dominant Cost | Resulting Bound |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Root-heavy (increasing) | $a^{i} f(n/b^{i})$ **grows** with $i$ | Root level (top) | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
| **2** | Balanced (uniform) | $a^{i} f(n/b^{i})$ is **roughly constant** | All levels equal | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n) \log_{b} n)$ |
| **3** | Leaf-heavy (decreasing) | $a^{i} f(n/b^{i})$ **shrinks** with $i$ | Leaves (bottom) | $T(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{\log_{b} a}\right)$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Utility**: This per-level cost taxonomy is exactly the reasoning used by Google's *BigQuery* query planner and the *Tez* execution engine in Apache Hadoop to decide whether a distributed job is CPU-bound, I/O-bound, or communication-bound at the deepest recursion depth.

## 2.5 The "Geometric Series" Trick

The level-cost sum is often a geometric series. Memorize these three:

| Series Form | Closed Form (Infinite) | Closed Form (Truncated at $L$) |
|---|---|---|
| $\sum_{i=0}^{L} r^{i}$ | $\dfrac{1}{1-r}$ if $\vert r \vert < 1$ | $\dfrac{r^{L+1} - 1}{r - 1}$ |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{L} c \cdot r^{i}$ | $\dfrac{c}{1-r}$ | $c \cdot \dfrac{r^{L+1}-1}{r-1}$ |
| $\sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \frac{1}{2^{i}}$ | $2$ | $2$ |

The key metric is the **ratio** of successive level costs:
$$r = \frac{a^{i+1} f(n/b^{i+1})}{a^{i} f(n/b^{i})} = a \cdot \frac{f(n/b^{i+1})}{f(n/b^{i})}$$

If $r < 1$, the series **decreases** geometrically (leaf-dominated). If $r > 1$, it **increases** (root-dominated). If $r = 1$, it stays constant and the sum is multiplied by the number of levels.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Board Examiner's Mandate**: Every step of every derivation must be shown explicitly. Skipping the algebraic manipulation loses 3–5 marks per sub-part. We never abbreviate.

## 3.1 Example 1 — The "Textbook Classic" $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^{2}$

This is the *exact* recurrence KTU has asked in past university examinations. We solve it for $T(1) = \Theta(1)$.

### Step 1: Write the Recurrence

$$T(n) = 3T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + cn^{2}, \quad T(1) = \Theta(1)$$

where $a = 3$, $b = 4$, $f(n) = cn^{2}$. The base case cost is a constant.

### Step 2: Draw / Describe the Tree

**Level 0 (root):** 1 node of cost $cn^{2}$. Total level cost = $cn^{2}$.

**Level 1:** 3 nodes, each of size $n/4$, each costing $c(n/4)^{2} = cn^{2}/16$. Total = $3 \cdot cn^{2}/16 = (3/16) cn^{2}$.

**Level 2:** $3^{2} = 9$ nodes, each of size $n/4^{2}$, each costing $cn^{2}/4^{4} = cn^{2}/256$. Total = $9 \cdot cn^{2}/256 = (9/256) cn^{2}$.

**Level $i$ (general):** $3^{i}$ nodes, each of size $n/4^{i}$, each costing $cn^{2}/4^{2i}$. Total level cost:

$$3^{i} \cdot \frac{cn^{2}}{4^{2i}} = cn^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^{i}$$

### Step 3: Determine the Tree Height

The recursion bottoms out when $n/4^{L} = 1$, i.e., $4^{L} = n$, so

$$L = \log_{4} n$$

### Step 4: Sum the Per-Level Costs

The total cost is the sum of internal levels plus the leaf cost:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} cn^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^{i} \;+\; (\text{leaf cost})$$

Since $L = \log_{4} n$, the leaf cost is:

$$\Theta(3^{L}) = \Theta(3^{\log_{4} n}) = \Theta(n^{\log_{4} 3})$$

### Step 5: Bound the Geometric Series

The ratio of successive level costs is $3/16 = 0.1875 < 1$. Therefore the geometric series is *strictly decreasing* and is dominated by its **first term**:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} cn^{2} \cdot \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^{i} < cn^{2} \cdot \sum_{i=0}^{\infty} \left(\frac{3}{16}\right)^{i} = cn^{2} \cdot \frac{1}{1 - 3/16} = \frac{16}{13} cn^{2} = O(n^{2})$$

### Step 6: Combine and Conclude

Adding the leaf cost $O(n^{\log_{4} 3})$ (which is $\ll n^{2}$ because $\log_{4} 3 \approx 0.792$):

$$T(n) = O(n^{2}) + O(n^{0.792}) = O(n^{2})$$

Since each level is *non-negative*, the lower bound is trivially $\Omega(n^{2})$ from the root. Hence:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Cross-check with the Master Theorem**: Compare $n^{\log_{4} 3} = n^{0.792}$ with $f(n) = n^{2}$. Since $f(n) = \Omega(n^{0.792 + \epsilon})$ for $\epsilon = 1.2$ and $a \cdot f(n/b) = 3 \cdot c(n/4)^{2} = (3/16) cn^{2} \le c n^{2}$ (regularity holds), Master Theorem Case 3 gives $T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$. ✓

---

## 3.2 Example 2 — The "Balanced Tree" $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$

A more *adversarial* recurrence that the Master Theorem does **not** directly cover. KTU loves this for the "internal choice" question.

### Step 1: Identify Tree Parameters

The subproblems are *uneven*: one is $n/3$, the other is $2n/3$. This makes the tree **asymmetric** — the longest path (right spine) determines the height.

### Step 2: Identify the Longest Path

Starting at the root with subproblem of size $n$, repeatedly taking the *larger* subproblem:

$$n \;\to\; \frac{2n}{3} \;\to\; \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{2} n \;\to\; \cdots \;\to\; \left(\frac{2}{3}\right)^{k} n$$

We stop when $(2/3)^{k} n = 1$, i.e., $k = \log_{3/2} n$. So the tree height is:

$$L = \log_{3/2} n = \frac{\log n}{\log(3/2)} = \Theta(\log n)$$

### Step 3: Per-Level Cost

At the root, the cost is $cn$. At every node, the *sum of $cn$ over the level* is bounded between $cn$ (only one node active) and $2cn$ (two nodes). A more careful amortized argument: at each level, the total size of the subproblems in the level is *at most* $n$ (the original size shrinks, but it is partitioned across the level). Hence each level's cost is $\Theta(n)$.

### Step 4: Sum the Levels

There are $L = \Theta(\log n)$ levels, each of cost $\Theta(n)$. So:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} \Theta(n) = \Theta(n) \cdot L = \Theta(n \log n)$$

### Step 5: Final Answer

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

---

## 3.3 Example 3 — The "Leaf-Heavy" $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$

This is the *opposite* extreme: the leaves dominate because $a = 4 > 2$ and $f(n) = n$ is small.

### Step 1: Parameters

$a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$. Tree height $L = \log_{2} n$. Critical exponent: $\log_{b} a = \log_{2} 4 = 2$. So leaf cost is $n^{2}$.

### Step 2: Per-Level Cost

At level $i$, there are $4^{i}$ nodes, each of size $n/2^{i}$, each costing $n/2^{i}$. Total level cost:

$$4^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} = n \cdot \left(\frac{4}{2}\right)^{i} = n \cdot 2^{i}$$

This is **increasing** geometrically with ratio $r = 2$.

### Step 3: Sum the Levels

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} n \cdot 2^{i} + \Theta(n^{2}) = n(2^{L} - 1) + \Theta(n^{2}) = n(n - 1) + \Theta(n^{2}) = \Theta(n^{2})$$

### Step 4: Final Answer

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{2})}$$

This matches Master Theorem Case 3 since $f(n) = n = O(n^{2 - \epsilon})$ with $\epsilon = 1$. ✓

---

## 3.4 The Master Theorem ↔ Recursion Tree Cross-Reference (Killer Summary Table)

| Master Theorem Case | Condition | Recursion Tree Visual | Bound |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_{b} a - \epsilon})$ | Leaves dominate | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$ |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \log^{k} n)$ | All levels roughly equal | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a} \log^{k+1} n)$ |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_{b} a + \epsilon})$ + regularity | Root dominates | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The "Generic" Recursion Tree for $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$

```mermaid
graph TD
    rootA["LEVEL 0: 1 node, cost f(n)"]
    rootB["LEVEL 1: a nodes, cost a*f(n/b)"]
    rootC["LEVEL 2: a^2 nodes, cost a^2*f(n/b^2)"]
    rootD["LEVEL i: a^i nodes, cost a^i*f(n/b^i)"]
    rootE["LEVEL L: n^logb(a) leaves, total cost T(n^logb(a))"]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootB --> rootC
    rootC --> rootD
    rootD --> rootE
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: The Three-Phase Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PHASE1["PHASE 1: EXPAND RECURRENCE"]:::phase
    PHASE2["PHASE 2: COMPUTE PER-LEVEL COST"]:::phase
    PHASE3["PHASE 3: SUM AND ASYMPTOTICALLY BOUND"]:::phase
    RESULT["FINAL ANSWER Theta(...) or O(...) or Omega(...)"]:::result

    PHASE1 -->|"draw tree, identify a, b, f(n)"| PHASE2
    PHASE2 -->|"cost_i = a^i * f(n / b^i)"| PHASE3
    PHASE3 -->|"sum over i=0 to L, apply geometric series"| RESULT

```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper 1]**
> Define the **Recursion Tree Method** for solving recurrences. List the three steps involved in it. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The Recursion Tree Method is a technique used to find the asymptotic bound of a recurrence relation of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ by drawing a rooted tree representing the recursive expansion.

**Three steps:**
1. **Construct the recursion tree** by expanding the recurrence level by level, where each node corresponds to a subproblem of cost $f(\cdot)$.
2. **Determine the cost at each level** of the tree. The total cost at level $i$ is $a^{i} \cdot f(n/b^{i})$.
3. **Sum the costs over all levels** and apply asymptotic bounding (using geometric series identities) to obtain $T(n)$.

### Question A.2
> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 2]**
> For the recurrence $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$, find (a) the height of the recursion tree, and (b) the number of leaves. (3 marks)

**Model Answer:**

**(a) Height of the tree:**
The recursion bottoms out when $n/2^{L} = 1 \Rightarrow L = \log_{2} n$.

**(b) Number of leaves:**
Number of leaves $= a^{L} = 4^{\log_{2} n} = 2^{2 \log_{2} n} = n^{\log_{2} 4} = n^{2}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Question Paper]**
> **(a)** Solve the following recurrence using the **Recursion Tree Method** and prove that $T(n) = O(n \log n)$:
> $$T(n) = 2T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n, \quad T(1) = 1$$
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$ using the recursion tree method. Mention the case of the Master Theorem that applies. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for A(a):

**Step 1 — Identify parameters**: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n$.

**Step 2 — Build the recursion tree**:

**Level 0**: $1$ node, cost $n$. Total = $n$.

**Level 1**: $2$ nodes, each of size $n/2$, each cost $n/2$. Total = $2 \cdot n/2 = n$.

**Level 2**: $2^{2} = 4$ nodes, each of size $n/4$, each cost $n/4$. Total = $4 \cdot n/4 = n$.

**Level $i$**: $2^{i}$ nodes, each of size $n/2^{i}$, each cost $n/2^{i}$. Total = $2^{i} \cdot n/2^{i} = n$.

**Step 3 — Determine tree height**: $L = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 4 — Leaf cost**: Number of leaves $= 2^{L} = 2^{\log_{2} n} = n$, each with cost $T(1) = 1$. Total leaf cost $= n \cdot 1 = n$.

**Step 5 — Sum the levels**:

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} n + n = L \cdot n + n = n \log_{2} n + n$$

**Step 6 — Conclude**:

$$T(n) = n \log_{2} n + n = O(n \log n)$$

Hence **proved**. ∎

> [!NOTE]
> **Algorithm in context**: This is the recurrence for **Merge Sort**, confirming its $O(n \log n)$ complexity.

#### Model Solution for A(b):

**Step 1 — Identify the structure**: The recurrence has *two unequal subproblems*: $n/3$ and $2n/3$. The tree is **asymmetric**.

**Step 2 — Find the height of the longest path**:
Repeatedly taking the larger subproblem: $n \to (2n/3) \to (2/3)^{2} n \to \cdots$. We stop when $(2/3)^{L} n = 1$, giving $L = \log_{3/2} n = \Theta(\log n)$.

**Step 3 — Per-level cost**: At every level, the *sum of the subproblem sizes* is at most $n$ (the original size is split across the level). So each level's cost is bounded by $cn$ from above and is $\Omega(n)$ from below (since the root alone contributes $cn$). Hence each level costs $\Theta(n)$.

**Step 4 — Sum the levels**:
There are $\Theta(\log n)$ levels, each of cost $\Theta(n)$:

$$T(n) = \Theta(n) \cdot \Theta(\log n) = \Theta(n \log n)$$

**Step 5 — Master Theorem connection**:
This recurrence does *not* fit the canonical form $aT(n/b)$ because the subproblem sizes differ. It sits in a **generalized** form that the Master Theorem does not directly cover; the recursion tree method is the cleanest way to handle it.

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1]**
> **(a)** Using the recursion tree method, find the asymptotic bound of:
> $$T(n) = 8T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn^{3}, \quad T(1) = 1$$
> **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ using the recursion tree method. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for B(a):

**Step 1 — Parameters**: $a = 8$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = cn^{3}$. Critical exponent: $\log_{2} 8 = 3$. Note that $f(n) = n^{3}$ *equals* the leaf cost exponent exactly.

**Step 2 — Per-level cost at level $i$**:
$8^{i}$ nodes, each of size $n/2^{i}$, each cost $c(n/2^{i})^{3} = cn^{3}/2^{3i}$. Total:

$$8^{i} \cdot \frac{cn^{3}}{8^{i}} = cn^{3}$$

Every level costs exactly $cn^{3}$ — a *perfectly balanced* tree.

**Step 3 — Tree height**: $L = \log_{2} n$.

**Step 4 — Sum the levels**:
$\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} cn^{3} = cn^{3} \cdot L = cn^{3} \log_{2} n$. Adding the leaf cost $\Theta(n^{3})$:

$$T(n) = cn^{3} \log_{2} n + \Theta(n^{3}) = \Theta(n^{3} \log n)$$

**Step 5 — Master Theorem cross-check**:
$n^{\log_{b} a} = n^{3}$ and $f(n) = n^{3}$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{3} \log^{0} n)$, this is **Case 2** of the Master Theorem, giving $T(n) = \Theta(n^{3} \log n)$. ✓

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{3} \log n)}$$

#### Model Solution for B(b):

**Step 1 — Parameters**: $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n \log n$.

**Step 2 — Per-level cost at level $i$**:
$2^{i}$ nodes, each of size $n/2^{i}$, each cost $\frac{n}{2^{i}} \log\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) = \frac{n}{2^{i}} (\log n - i)$. Total:

$$2^{i} \cdot \frac{n}{2^{i}} (\log n - i) = n(\log n - i)$$

**Step 3 — Sum the levels** (with $L = \log_{2} n$):

$$T(n) = \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} n(\log n - i) = n \sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (\log n - i)$$

Let $j = \log n - i$. As $i$ ranges over $0, 1, \ldots, L-1$, $j$ ranges over $L, L-1, \ldots, 1$. So:

$$\sum_{i=0}^{L-1} (\log n - i) = \sum_{j=1}^{L} j = \frac{L(L+1)}{2} = \frac{(\log n)(\log n + 1)}{2} = \Theta((\log n)^{2})$$

**Step 4 — Final summation**:

$$T(n) = n \cdot \Theta((\log n)^{2}) = \Theta(n (\log n)^{2})$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n (\log n)^{2})}$$

This matches **Master Theorem Case 2** with $k = 1$. ✓

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes That Cost 3–7 Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the leaf-level cost** $\Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$ in the final sum. Many students sum only the internal levels and miss the $O(n^{\log_{b} a})$ term. *Always* include the leaves.
> 2. **Confusing "tree height" with "tree depth"**: Height $L = \log_{b} n$ is the number of recursive expansions, not the number of *levels of nodes*. The root is level 0; there are $L+1$ levels of nodes in total.
> 3. **Using the Master Theorem when the regularity condition fails** (e.g., $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \sin n$). The recursion tree method is the safer fallback.
> 4. **Treating non-integer subproblem sizes as a problem**: Use $T(\lceil n/b \rceil)$ or $T(\lfloor n/b \rfloor)$ — the asymptotic answer is *unchanged*. Examiners accept both notations.
> 5. **Dropping the constant $c$ prematurely** in $f(n) = cn^{k}$: keep the constant until the geometric series step, because the *ratio* of the geometric series is what determines the bound, and that ratio is dimensionless.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for the Recursion Tree Method:**
>
> - The method is for recurrences of the form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.
> - **Phase 1** — Expand the recurrence as a tree. **Phase 2** — Compute the cost per level $i$ as $a^{i} \cdot f(n/b^{i})$. **Phase 3** — Sum and asymptotically bound.
> - The **height** of the tree is $L = \log_{b} n$. The **number of leaves** is $a^{L} = n^{\log_{b} a}$. The **total leaf cost** is $\Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$ — this is fixed by the recurrence regardless of $f(n)$.
> - Three canonical cases: (i) **root-heavy** → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$; (ii) **balanced** → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n) \log n)$; (iii) **leaf-heavy** → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_{b} a})$.
> - The ratio of successive level costs is $r = a \cdot f(n/b^{i+1}) / f(n/b^{i})$: if $r < 1$, root dominates; if $r > 1$, leaves dominate; if $r = 1$, multiply by $\log n$.
> - The method works even when the Master Theorem fails (e.g., $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + cn$).
> - **Cross-check** the answer with the Master Theorem if applicable.
> - **Always** include the leaf cost in the final sum; **always** bound the geometric series carefully.
> - Worked classics to memorize: $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$ (Merge Sort), $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + n^{2} \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_{2} 7})$, $T(n) = 3T(n/4) + cn^{2} \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{2})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Substitution Method and Master's Theorem (Proof Not Required)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Substitution Method and Master's Theorem (Proof Not Required)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Substitution Method — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Substitution Method):** The Substitution Method is a non-mechanical, **inductive technique** for solving recurrences that arise in the analysis of recursive algorithms. It consists of two phases: **(1) GUESS** the asymptotic form of the solution, and **(2) VERIFY** that guess using **mathematical induction** by substituting it back into the recurrence and proving boundary conditions hold.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, this method is part of **Module 1: Introduction to Algorithm Analysis**, and it is the most *flexible* of all recurrence-solving techniques because it can handle recurrences that the Master Theorem cannot.

## 1.2 Master's Theorem — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (Master's Theorem):** A "cookbook" / lookup-style theorem that provides **asymptotic upper bounds** in Big-O notation for recurrences of the canonical **divide-and-conquer** form:
>
> $$T(n) = a\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$
>
> where constants satisfy $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is an asymptotically positive function. It expresses $T(n)$ in terms of a comparison between $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Two Accountants" Metaphor:**
>
> Imagine you are auditing a company's expenses for the year.
> - **The Substitution Method** is like a **detective accountant** who looks at past years' patterns, *guesses* "this year's expenses should be roughly quadratic," and then *verifies* month-by-month whether the books support that guess.
> - **The Master's Theorem** is like a **rule-book accountant** who asks: "Is the work outside the recursive calls $f(n)$ heavier, lighter, or equal to the work of the recursive calls $n^{\log_b a}$?" and immediately gives you a verdict — Case 1, 2, or 3.
>
> The detective is more powerful but slower; the rule-book is faster but only works for standard situations.

## 1.4 Why These Methods Matter in KTU Curriculum

Both methods are **high-weightage** in KTU End Semester Examinations (typically 7–14 mark questions from Module 1). They directly support **Course Outcomes** such as:
- **CO1:** *Apply asymptotic notation to analyze the complexity of algorithms.*
- **CO2:** *Solve recurrences using standard techniques to determine algorithmic efficiency.*

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Substitution Method — Operational Steps

The substitution method has **three canonical steps** which examiners reward if explicitly listed:

### Step 1 — GUESS the Form
- Look at similar recurrences you have already solved.
- Often, the form is $T(n) = O(n^k \log^p n)$ for some constants $k$ and $p$.
- Use **recursion trees** informally to generate the guess.

### Step 2 — SUBSTITUTE and VERIFY by Induction
- Assume the bound holds for smaller inputs: $T(n/2) \leq c \cdot g(n/2)$.
- Substitute into the recurrence and simplify to show $T(n) \leq c \cdot g(n)$.
- This proves the **upper bound** (Big-O). For Big-$\Omega$, the technique is identical with inequalities reversed.

### Step 3 — SOLVE for Constants
- Pick a sufficiently **large constant $c$** to absorb lower-order terms.
- Verify **base cases** at small $n$ (typically $n=1, 2, 3$).
- The base cases sometimes require a *larger* $c_0$ — this is a frequent KTU pitfall.

## 2.2 The Master Theorem — Operational Logic

Let $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$ and $b > 1$. Define the **critical exponent**:

$$n^{\log_b a}$$

This is the cost of the leaves of the recursion tree (number of subproblems $\times$ cost of each).

| Case | Condition (on $f(n)$ vs $n^{\log_b a}$) | Conclusion for $T(n)$ | Intuition |
|:----:|:----------------------------------------|:----------------------|:----------|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$ | **Recursion dominates** the cost |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for $k \geq 0$ | $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n)$ | **Balanced** — both levels contribute |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ AND regularity: $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ | $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$ | **Driver function** dominates |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the "gap" $\varepsilon$ matters:** Case 1 and Case 3 require a **polynomial gap** between $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$. If $f(n)$ is only smaller/larger by a logarithmic factor, the theorem **does not apply** — you must fall back on Case 2 (or the substitution method).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER'S THEOREM — QUICK-REFERENCE TABLE**
>
> | Recurrence Form | $a$ | $b$ | $\log_b a$ | $f(n)$ | Verdict |
> |:----------------|:---:|:---:|:-----------:|:-------|:--------|
> | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ | 2 | 2 | 1 | $n$ | Case 2 → $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
> | $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n$ | 4 | 2 | 2 | $n$ | Case 1 → $\Theta(n^2)$ |
> | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$ | 2 | 2 | 1 | $n^2$ | Case 3 → $\Theta(n^2)$ |
> | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + \sqrt{n}$ | 2 | 2 | 1 | $n^{1/2}$ | Case 1 → $\Theta(n)$ |
> | $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$ | 9 | 3 | 2 | $n$ | Case 1 → $\Theta(n^2)$ |
> | $T(n) = T(2n/3) + 1$ | 1 | 3/2 | 0 | 1 | Case 2 → $\Theta(\log n)$ |
> | $T(n) = 3T(n/3) + n \log n$ | 3 | 3 | 1 | $n \log n$ | Case 2 (k=1) → $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ |
> | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$ | 2 | 2 | 1 | $n \log n$ | **Does not apply** (gap is logarithmic) |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

- **Substitution Method:** Used in research papers to analyze novel recurrences (e.g., Strassen-like matrix multiplication, advanced FFT variants) where no closed-form library exists.
- **Master's Theorem:** Used by software engineers to *quickly estimate* the running time of standard divide-and-conquer algorithms — **Merge Sort** ($a{=}2, b{=}2, f{=}n \Rightarrow \Theta(n \log n)$), **Binary Search** ($a{=}1, b{=}2, f{=}1 \Rightarrow \Theta(\log n)$), **Karatsuba Multiplication** ($a{=}3, b{=}2, f{=}n \Rightarrow \Theta(n^{\log_2 3}) \approx \Theta(n^{1.585})$).

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Substitution Method (KTU Favourite)

> **Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$ using the substitution method, proving $T(n) = O(n \log n)$.

### Step 1 — Guess the Form
From the recursion tree (each level sums to $n$, with $\log_2 n$ levels), we guess:

$$T(n) \leq c \cdot n \log_2 n \quad \text{for some constant } c > 0$$

### Step 2 — Inductive Substitution
**Inductive Hypothesis:** Assume for all $m < n$, the bound $T(m) \leq c \cdot m \log_2 m$ holds.

Substitute into the recurrence:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n \\
     &\leq 2 \cdot \left[ c \cdot \frac{n}{2} \log_2\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) \right] + n \quad \text{(by IH)} \\
     &= c \cdot n \left( \log_2 n - \log_2 2 \right) + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - c \cdot n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - (c - 1)\,n
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Bound Selection
We want the result to be $\leq c \cdot n \log_2 n$. This requires:

$$-(c - 1)\,n \leq 0 \quad \Longleftrightarrow \quad c \geq 1$$

So we choose $c \geq 1$. The inductive step is **complete**.

### Step 4 — Base Case
For $n = 2$: $T(2) = 2T(1) + 2$. Choosing $c = 1$, we need $T(2) \leq 1 \cdot 2 \log_2 2 = 2$. This requires $T(1) \leq 0$, which may not hold. **Solution:** pick a larger constant (e.g., $c = 2$) and verify small base cases like $n = 3$ or $n = 4$ directly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(n \log n)$. (Combined with a lower-bound argument, $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.) This is the standard KTU textbook derivation.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Substitution Method for Big-$\Omega$

> **Problem:** Prove $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n = \Omega(n \log n)$.

We guess $T(n) \geq c \cdot n \log_2 n$ for $0 < c \leq 1$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + n \\
     &\geq 2 \cdot \left[ c \cdot \frac{n}{2} \log_2\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) \right] + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n - c \cdot n + n \\
     &= c \cdot n \log_2 n + (1 - c)\,n \\
     &\geq c \cdot n \log_2 n \quad \text{since } (1 - c)\,n \geq 0 \text{ for } c \leq 1
\end{aligned}
$$

Hence $T(n) = \Omega(n \log n)$. Combined with Section 3.1: $T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Master's Theorem (Case 1)

> **Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + n^2$.

Here $a = 8$, $b = 2$, so:

$$\log_b a = \log_2 8 = 3 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^{\log_b a} = n^3$$

Compare $f(n) = n^2$ with $n^3$. Since:

$$n^2 = O(n^{3 - 1}) \quad \text{(polynomially smaller, } \varepsilon = 1\text{)}$$

**Conclusion:** **Case 1** applies.

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^3)$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Master's Theorem (Case 2)

> **Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$.

Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^1 = n$. We have:

$$f(n) = n \log n = n^1 \cdot \log^1 n \quad \Rightarrow \quad k = 1$$

This matches Case 2 with $k = 1$.

**Conclusion:**

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 2} \cdot \log^{1+1} n) = \Theta(n \log^2 n)$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Master's Theorem (Case 3)

> **Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$.

Here $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $n^{\log_b a} = n$. We have $f(n) = n^2$.

**Check polynomial gap:** $n^2 = \Omega(n^{1 + 1})$, so $\varepsilon = 1 > 0$. ✓

**Check regularity condition:** Need $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$.

$$2 \cdot \left(\frac{n}{2}\right)^2 = 2 \cdot \frac{n^2}{4} = \frac{n^2}{2} \leq c \cdot n^2$$

Choosing $c = 1/2 < 1$ satisfies this. ✓

**Conclusion:** **Case 3** applies.

$$T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$$

---

## 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Case Where Master's Theorem Fails

> **Problem:** Solve $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n \log n$. *(Repeated for clarity.)*

Here $f(n) = n \log n$ and $n^{\log_b a} = n$. The ratio is:

$$\frac{f(n)}{n^{\log_b a}} = \frac{n \log n}{n} = \log n$$

This is **not polynomially larger** (Case 3 fails: $\log n$ is not $\Omega(n^\varepsilon)$) and **not polynomially smaller** (Case 1 fails). The recurrence sits in the **logarithmic gap** between Case 1 and Case 3.

> [!WARNING]
> **The Master's Theorem DOES NOT APPLY** to this recurrence in its standard form. (It is still solvable — it equals $\Theta(n \log^2 n)$ — but requires a recursion-tree expansion or the substitution method, NOT the Master Theorem.) This is a **favourite KTU trick question**: students who force-fit a case lose full marks.

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow Summary

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MASTER'S THEOREM — DECISION ALGORITHM:**
>
> 1. **Identify $a$, $b$, and $f(n)$.**
> 2. **Compute $n^{\log_b a}$.**
> 3. **Compare $f(n)$ to $n^{\log_b a}$:**
>    - $f(n)$ polynomially smaller → **Case 1** → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
>    - $f(n) \approx n^{\log_b a} \cdot \text{poly-log}$ → **Case 2** → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$
>    - $f(n)$ polynomially larger AND regularity holds → **Case 3** → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$
>    - None of the above → **Use substitution method / recursion tree.**

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Substitution Method Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start with recurrence T n] --> B[Examine structure]
    B --> C[Guess form T n = O g n]
    C --> D[Inductive hypothesis: assume T m le c g m for m lt n]
    D --> E[Substitute guess into RHS of recurrence]
    E --> F[Algebraic simplification]
    F --> G{Did RHS reduce to c g n?}
    G -- No --> H[Modify guess: change c, add log factor, or change exponent]
    H --> C
    G -- Yes --> I[Verify base case n small]
    I --> J{Base case holds?}
    J -- No --> K[Increase constant c and re-verify]
    K --> I
    J -- Yes --> L[Proved T n = O g n]
    L --> M[End]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Master's Theorem Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Input: T n = aT n over b + f n] --> Q[Compute critical exponent n to the power log base b of a]
    Q --> R{Is f n = O of n to log b a minus epsilon?}
    R -- Yes --> S[Case 1: T n = Theta n to log b a]
    R -- No --> T{Is f n = Theta of n to log b a times log to the k n?}
    T -- Yes --> U[Case 2: T n = Theta n to log b a log to k+1 n]
    T -- No --> V{Is f n = Omega of n to log b a plus epsilon?}
    V -- No --> X[Master Theorem does NOT apply - use substitution method]
    V -- Yes --> W{Regularity: a f n over b le c f n for c lt 1?}
    W -- Yes --> Y[Case 3: T n = Theta f n]
    W -- No --> X
    S --> Z[End]
    U --> Z
    Y --> Z
    X --> Z
```

## 4.3 Conceptual Map — Recurrence-Solving Strategy Selection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Rec[Given Recurrence] --> Q1{Is it divide and conquer of form aT n over b + f n?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> MT[Use Master Theorem]
    Q1 -- No --> Q2{Is the recurrence linear homogeneous with constant coefficients?}
    Q2 -- Yes --> RS[Use Recurrence Tree or Characteristic Equation]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Do you have a good guess from a similar problem?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> SM[Substitution Method]
    Q3 -- No --> RT[Build Recursion Tree and Sum Levels]
    RT --> SM
    MT --> Done[Final Asymptotic Bound T n]
    RS --> Done
    SM --> Done
```
