# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**State the Master's Theorem. List its three cases and the conditions for each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Master's Theorem Statement:** Let $T(n) = a\,T(n/b) + f(n)$ where $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ is asymptotically positive. Define the critical exponent $n^{\log_b a}$. Then:
>
> - **Case 1:** If $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
> - **Case 2:** If $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n)$ for some $k \geq 0$, then $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
> - **Case 3:** If $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ **and** the regularity condition $a\,f(n/b) \leq c\,f(n)$ holds for some constant $c < 1$, then $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.

**Mark split:** 1 mark for statement, 1 mark for Case 1 & 2, 1 mark for Case 3 with regularity.

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Briefly explain the substitution method for solving recurrences. What is the main limitation of the Master's Theorem that the substitution method overcomes?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **substitution method** involves:
1. Guessing the asymptotic form of the solution (e.g., $T(n) = O(n \log n)$).
2. Verifying the guess by **mathematical induction** — assuming it for smaller inputs, substituting back into the recurrence, and confirming the bound propagates.
3. Solving for constants and verifying **base cases**.

The **main limitation of the Master's Theorem** is that it applies **only** to recurrences of the strict form $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with a *single* division factor. The **substitution method** overcomes this — it can handle *any* recurrence, including non-standard ones like $T(n) = T(\sqrt{n}) + 1$, $T(n) = T(n/3) + T(2n/3) + n$, or recurrences with floor/ceiling functions and multiple subproblem sizes.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks)

### ⭐ Question A — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = 9T(n/3) + n$ using the **Master's Theorem**. Identify $a$, $b$, $f(n)$, the critical exponent, and state which case applies. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Use the **substitution method** to prove that $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + 1$ has solution $T(n) = \Theta(2^n)$. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Identify parameters:**

$$a = 9, \quad b = 3, \quad f(n) = n$$

**Step 2 — Compute critical exponent:**

$$\log_b a = \log_3 9 = 2 \quad \Rightarrow \quad n^{\log_b a} = n^2$$

**Step 3 — Compare $f(n)$ with $n^2$:**

$$f(n) = n = n^1 = O(n^{2-1}) = O(n^{2-\varepsilon}) \quad \text{with } \varepsilon = 1$$

This is polynomially smaller, so **Case 1** applies.

**Step 4 — Conclusion:**

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^2)}$$

**Mark split:** 1 mark for parameters, 1 mark for $\log_b a$, 1 mark for comparison & $\varepsilon$, 1 mark for case identification, 1 mark for final answer, 2 marks for justification.

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Guess the form:**

Unrolling the recurrence gives $T(n) = 4T(n-2) + 3$ → eventually $T(n) = 2^n - 1$. We guess:

$$T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n \quad \text{for some constant } c > 0$$

**Step 2 — Inductive substitution:**

**Hypothesis:** Assume $T(m) \leq c \cdot 2^m$ for all $m < n$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T(n-1) + 1 \\
     &\leq 2 \cdot \left[ c \cdot 2^{n-1} \right] + 1 \quad \text{(by IH)} \\
     &= c \cdot 2^{n} + 1
\end{aligned}
$$

We want $c \cdot 2^n + 1 \leq c \cdot 2^n$ — this **fails** by a constant 1!

**Step 3 — Strengthen the guess:**

We modify: $T(n) \leq c \cdot 2^n - 1$ for some $c > 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= 2\,T(n-1) + 1 \\
     &\leq 2 \cdot \left[ c \cdot 2^{n-1} - 1 \right] + 1 \\
     &= c \cdot 2^n - 2 + 1 \\
     &= c \cdot 2^n - 1 \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Base case:** For $n = 1$: $T(1) = 2T(0) + 1$. Choosing $c \geq 1$ works.

**Conclusion:** $T(n) = O(2^n)$. A symmetric argument (with $c \leq 1$) gives $T(n) = \Omega(2^n)$. Hence:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(2^n)}$$

**Mark split:** 1 mark for guess, 2 marks for IH substitution, 2 marks for handling the $+1$ (strengthening), 1 mark for base case, 1 mark for conclusion.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Many students stop at Step 2 and get a $-1$ deficit, then *incorrectly* conclude the method "doesn't work." The correct technique (worth full marks) is to **strengthen the inductive hypothesis** by subtracting a constant. Examiners specifically test this trap.

---

### ⭐ Question B — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** State the **Master's Theorem** clearly. Apply it to solve $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$. Identify which case applies and justify your choice. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Solve the recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/2) + n^2$ (i.e., $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n^2$) using the **Master's Theorem**, and verify your answer by sketching the **recursion tree sum**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Statement of Master's Theorem:** *(2 marks)*

Let $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ with $a \geq 1$, $b > 1$, and $f(n)$ asymptotically positive. Let $n^{\log_b a}$ be the critical exponent.

- **Case 1:** $f(n) = O(n^{\log_b a - \varepsilon})$ $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$
- **Case 2:** $f(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^k n)$ $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$
- **Case 3:** $f(n) = \Omega(n^{\log_b a + \varepsilon})$ **and** regularity $\Rightarrow$ $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$

**Application to $T(n) = 4T(n/2) + n^3$:** *(5 marks)*

- $a = 4$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^3$.
- $\log_b a = \log_2 4 = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^2$.
- Compare: $f(n) = n^3 = \Omega(n^{2+1}) = \Omega(n^{2+\varepsilon})$ with $\varepsilon = 1 > 0$. ✓ **Polynomial dominance confirmed.**
- **Regularity check:** $a \cdot f(n/b) = 4 \cdot (n/2)^3 = 4 \cdot n^3/8 = n^3/2 \leq c \cdot n^3$. Choosing $c = 1/2 < 1$. ✓
- **Case 3 applies.**

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^3)}$$

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Parameters:** $a = 2$, $b = 2$, $f(n) = n^2$, $\log_b a = 1$, $n^{\log_b a} = n$.

**Comparison:** $f(n) = n^2 = \Omega(n^{1+1}) = \Omega(n^{1+\varepsilon})$ with $\varepsilon = 1$.

**Regularity:** $2 \cdot (n/2)^2 = 2 \cdot n^2/4 = n^2/2 \leq c \cdot n^2$, choose $c = 1/2$. ✓

**Case 3 applies →** $T(n) = \Theta(n^2)$. *(3 marks for the Master Theorem part)*

**Recursion Tree Verification:** *(4 marks)*

| Level | Number of Subproblems | Subproblem Size | Cost per Subproblem | Total Cost at Level |
|:-----:|:---------------------:|:---------------:|:-------------------:|:-------------------:|
| 0 | 1 | $n$ | $n^2$ | $n^2$ |
| 1 | 2 | $n/2$ | $(n/2)^2$ | $2 \cdot n^2/4 = n^2/2$ |
| 2 | 4 | $n/4$ | $(n/4)^2$ | $4 \cdot n^2/16 = n^2/4$ |
| $i$ | $2^i$ | $n/2^i$ | $(n/2^i)^2$ | $2^i \cdot n^2/4^i = n^2/2^i$ |
| Last | $n$ | 1 | 1 | $n \cdot 1 = n$ |

**Total cost:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T(n) &= \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \frac{n^2}{2^i} + n \\
     &= n^2 \sum_{i=0}^{\log_2 n - 1} \frac{1}{2^i} + n \\
     &\leq n^2 \cdot 2 + n \quad \text{(geometric series bounded by 2)} \\
     &= \Theta(n^2) \quad \checkmark
\end{aligned}
$$

The dominant term is the **root level $n^2$**, which decays geometrically — consistent with **Case 3** of the Master Theorem.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls (KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Forgetting the regularity condition in Case 3** → lose 1–2 marks. Examiners *always* check this.
> 2. **Forcing a Master Theorem case when $f(n)$ and $n^{\log_b a}$ differ only logarithmically** (e.g., $f(n) = n \log n$ vs $n^1$) → this is the most common trap. Answer: "Master Theorem does not apply."
> 3. **Confusing $\log_b a$ with $\log a / \log b$ numerically** — write it as a **fraction** $\log a / \log b$ in your scratch work to avoid arithmetic errors.
> 4. **In substitution method, failing to strengthen the inductive hypothesis** when constants don't cancel cleanly (see Question A part b).
> 5. **Omitting the base case verification** in the substitution method → loses 1 mark guaranteed.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 1: Substitution Method & Master's Theorem**
>
> - **Substitution Method** = **Guess + Inductive Verification + Base Case**.
> - **Master Theorem Form:** $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$ — only this exact form qualifies.
> - **Critical Exponent** $n^{\log_b a}$ is the **cost of the leaves** of the recursion tree.
> - **Case 1:** $f(n)$ polynomially *smaller* → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a})$.
> - **Case 2:** $f(n) \approx n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^k n$ → $T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_b a} \log^{k+1} n)$.
> - **Case 3:** $f(n)$ polynomially *larger* **AND** regularity holds → $T(n) = \Theta(f(n))$.
> - **Regularity Condition** (Case 3): $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some constant $c < 1$.
> - **Gap Rule:** If $f(n)$ is smaller/larger than $n^{\log_b a}$ by only a **logarithmic** factor, **Master Theorem fails** — use substitution or recursion tree.
> - **Strengthening the guess** is a legitimate and *expected* substitution-method technique when initial bounds don't close.
> - **Memo:** If the recurrence has a non-constant split (e.g., $T(n/3) + T(2n/3)$), the **Master Theorem does NOT apply** — Master Theorem is only for **equal-sized** subproblems.
> - **Always state $a$, $b$, $f(n)$, $\log_b a$ explicitly** in your answer — examiners grade these as individual line items.
> - **Boundary cases** in substitution: pick $n_0$ large enough so the inequality holds; sometimes the first $n_0$ values need to be checked manually.

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Divide and Conquer — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Divide and Conquer (D&C)** is a top-down, recursive algorithmic paradigm in which a problem of size **n** is decomposed into a **set of smaller sub-problems** of the same (or similar) type, each sub-problem is **solved independently**, and finally the **solutions to the sub-problems are combined** to produce the solution to the original problem.

The KTU 2024 Scheme formally recognises **three mandatory stages** for every D&C algorithm:

1. **Divide** — Partition the original problem into one or more *disjoint* sub-instances of the same problem.
2. **Conquer** — Solve each sub-instance either *directly* (when the size is small enough — the **base case**) or *recursively* by applying the same D&C scheme.
3. **Combine** — Merge the sub-solutions appropriately to obtain the final answer.

## 1.2 Greedy Strategy — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Greedy Strategy** is an algorithm-design method that builds up a solution piece by piece, always choosing the option that offers the **largest immediate (local) benefit** with the **hope of reaching a globally optimum** solution. Unlike D&C, greedy algorithms do **not** partition the problem and do **not** combine partial solutions — they make **one irrevocable choice** at each step.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

### The "Tournament Bracket" Analogy for Divide and Conquer
Imagine a knockout tennis tournament with **1024 players**. To find the champion:
- **Divide:** The 1024 players are split into 512 matches; then 256, then 128… 
- **Conquer:** Each match decides a winner directly (base case of size 2).
- **Combine:** Winners advance upward until one champion remains.

You never re-play a match — the work at each level is only to **combine** winners. This is exactly how **Merge Sort** or **Binary Search** works: each level performs a fixed amount of "merge" work, and the depth of recursion is logarithmic.

### The "Coin-Change Trolley" Analogy for Greedy
A cashier returning change for ₹87 picks the **largest coin** that fits first (₹50), then the next largest (₹20), then (₹10), then (₹5), then (₹2). She **never revisits** a previous choice. Greedy algorithms operate with this same one-shot mentality.

## 1.4 Where Each Paradigm Fits

| Aspect | Divide and Conquer | Greedy Strategy |
|---|---|---|
| Decomposition | Yes, into sub-problems | No decomposition |
| Number of choices | Independent sub-problems solved separately | Single path of locally optimal picks |
| Optimality guarantee | Often provably optimal (e.g., Merge Sort) | Optimal only when problem has **greedy-choice property** and **optimal substructure** |
| Storage of sub-solutions | Combined only at the end | Built up incrementally |
| Example problems | Binary Search, Merge Sort, Strassen, Max-Min | Kruskal, Prim, Dijkstra, Huffman (covered later) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The remainder of this module focuses exclusively on **Divide and Conquer**. Greedy Strategy is introduced here only to *contrast* the two paradigms so that students can identify which tool fits which problem class.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Generic Divide-and-Conquer Template

A canonical D&C algorithm follows this skeleton (expressed in pseudocode):

```
Algorithm DAndC(P):
    if |P| <= n0:                   // BASE CASE
        return SolveDirectly(P)
    else:
        Divide P into P1, P2, ..., Pk    // k >= 2 sub-problems
        for i = 1 to k:
            Yi = DAndC(Pi)               // Recursive conquer
        return Combine(Y1, Y2, ..., Yk)  // Merge step
```

## 2.2 The General Recurrence for Divide and Conquer

Let the recurrence that models a generic D&C algorithm be:

$$T(n) = \begin{cases} \Theta(1) & \text{if } n \le n_0 \\ a \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + D(n) + C(n) & \text{if } n > n_0 \end{cases}$$

Where each symbol carries a precise engineering meaning:

| Symbol | Meaning | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| **n** | Size of the problem instance | Given |
| **a** | Number of sub-problems generated | $\geq 2$ |
| **n / b** | Size of each sub-problem (assumed equal) | $b \geq 2$ |
| **D(n)** | Cost of **dividing** the problem into sub-problems | Often $\Theta(1)$ or $\Theta(n)$ |
| **C(n)** | Cost of **combining** the sub-solutions | $\Theta(1)$, $\Theta(n)$, or $\Theta(n^{2})$ |
| **n₀** | Threshold size for the base case | Usually 1 or 2 |

If we absorb $D(n)$ and $C(n)$ into a single non-recursive function $f(n)$, the recurrence becomes the canonical form used by the **Master Theorem**:

$$T(n) = a \, T\!\left(\dfrac{n}{b}\right) + f(n)$$

## 2.3 The Master Theorem (Board-Exam Favourite)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Compare $f(n)$ with $n^{\log_b a}$. Let $c_{\text{crit}} = \log_b a$.

| Case | Condition on $f(n)$ | Asymptotic Solution $T(n)$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Case 1** | $f(n) = O\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}} - \varepsilon}\right)$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ | $\Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a}\right)$ |
| **Case 2** | $f(n) = \Theta\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}}} \cdot \log^{k} n\right)$ for some $k \geq 0$ | $\Theta\!\left(n^{\log_b a} \cdot \log^{k+1} n\right)$ |
| **Case 3** | $f(n) = \Omega\!\left(n^{c_{\text{crit}} + \varepsilon}\right)$ for some $\varepsilon > 0$ **and** the **regularity condition** $a \cdot f(n/b) \leq c \cdot f(n)$ for some $c < 1$ holds | $\Theta(f(n))$ |

### Quick Worked Examples of the Master Theorem

- **Merge Sort:** $a=2,\ b=2,\ f(n)=cn$. Here $\log_b a = 1$. Since $f(n)=\Theta(n^{1})$ ⇒ **Case 2** with $k=0$. Solution: $\Theta(n \log n)$.
- **Binary Search:** $a=1,\ b=2,\ f(n)=c$. Here $\log_b a = 0$. Since $f(n)=\Theta(n^{0})$ ⇒ **Case 2** with $k=0$. Solution: $\Theta(\log n)$.
- **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication:** $a=7,\ b=2,\ f(n)=cn^{2}$. Here $\log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.807$. Since $f(n)=O(n^{2.807-\varepsilon})$ for any $\varepsilon \le 0.807$ ⇒ **Case 1**. Solution: $\Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})$.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!TIP]
> Memorise this table — the recurrence form and its Master-Theorem solution appear in nearly every KTU 2024 ESE paper for this module.

| Algorithm | Recurrence $T(n)$ | $\log_b a$ | Master Case | Closed-Form $T(n)$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Binary Search** | $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ | $0$ | 2 | $\Theta(\log n)$ |
| **Finding Max-Min** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 2$ | $1$ | 2 | $\Theta(n)$ |
| **Merge Sort** | $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + cn$ | $1$ | 2 | $\Theta(n \log n)$ |
| **Standard Matrix Mult.** | $T(n) = 8T(n/2) + cn^{2}$ | $3$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{3})$ |
| **Strassen's Mult.** | $T(n) = 7T(n/2) + cn^{2}$ | $\approx 2.807$ | 1 | $\Theta(n^{2.807})$ |

## 2.5 Why These Equations Matter in Engineering

The recurrences above directly answer engineering questions like *"How does the wall-clock time of my server-side sorting routine scale as my dataset grows from 1 GB to 1 TB?"* Knowing that Merge Sort is $\Theta(n \log n)$ while a naive sort is $\Theta(n^{2})$ is the difference between a system that responds in seconds and one that times out. Similarly, Strassen's reduction from $n^{3}$ to $n^{2.807}$ is exploited by high-performance numerical libraries (BLAS-like routines) used in graphics, weather modelling and machine-learning backpropagation kernels.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Binary Search — Recurrence Derivation

**Problem:** Given a sorted array $A[\text{low} \ldots \text{high}]$ and a key $x$, determine whether $x$ is in $A$.

### 3.1.1 Derivation of the Recurrence

At each recursive step of Binary Search, the algorithm performs the following non-recursive operations:

1. Compute the midpoint: $m = \lfloor (\text{low} + \text{high})/2 \rfloor$.  →  $O(1)$ comparison.
2. Compare $A[m]$ with $x$. →  $O(1)$.
3. Discard **half** the array and recurse on a sub-problem of size $n/2$.

Therefore the **cost of the non-recursive work** per call is a constant $c$, and the algorithm makes **exactly one** recursive call on a problem of half the size:

$$T(n) = 1 \cdot T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c, \qquad T(1) = d$$

### 3.1.2 Solving the Recurrence (Repeated Substitution)

Unroll the recurrence step by step:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c + c = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2c$$

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3c$$

Continuing for $i$ iterations, the pattern is:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + i \cdot c$$

The recursion bottoms out when $n/2^{i} = 1$, i.e. $2^{i} = n$, so $i = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$T(n) = T(1) + c \cdot \log_2 n = d + c \log_2 n$$

Therefore:

$$\boxed{\,T(n) = \Theta(\log n)\,}$$

This confirms the Master Theorem result and matches the well-known fact that **binary search runs in logarithmic time**.

## 3.2 Finding Maximum and Minimum — Recurrence Derivation

**Problem:** Find both the maximum and minimum of $n$ numbers using the fewest comparisons.

### 3.2.1 Straight-Forward Bound (Naive)

Compare every pair: $n-1$ comparisons to find the maximum, then $n-1$ comparisons to find the minimum ⇒ total $2(n-1)$ comparisons.

### 3.2.2 Tight Bound via Divide and Conquer

Split the array into two halves of size $n/2$ each. Recursively find the (max, min) of each half, then combine:

- Compare the two sub-maxima → 1 comparison.
- Compare the two sub-minima → 1 comparison.

That is **2 comparisons** in the combine step. The recurrence is therefore:

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 2, \qquad T(2) = 1$$

### 3.2.3 Solving the Recurrence

Using the substitution method, unroll:

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 2$$

$$T(n) = 2\left[2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2\right] + 2 = 4 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 4 + 2$$

$$T(n) = 4\left[2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 2\right] + 6 = 8 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 8 + 6$$

At iteration $i$:

$$T(n) = 2^{i} \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + 2 \cdot \sum_{j=0}^{i-1} 2^{j} = 2^{i} \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + 2(2^{i} - 1)$$

The recursion stops when $n/2^{i} = 2$, so $2^{i} = n/2$, i.e. $i = \log_2(n/2)$. Substituting $2^{i} = n/2$ and $T(2)=1$:

$$T(n) = \frac{n}{2} \cdot 1 + 2\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) = \frac{n}{2} + n - 2 = \frac{3n}{2} - 2$$

$$\boxed{\,T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2 = \Theta(n)\,}$$

This is a $\mathbf{25\%}$ improvement over the naive $2(n-1)$ bound and is the **provably optimal** number of comparisons for the Max-Min problem.

## 3.3 Merge Sort — Recurrence Derivation

**Problem:** Sort an array $A$ of size $n$ in ascending order.

### 3.3.1 The Recurrence

The Merge Sort algorithm performs three stages:

1. **Divide** — split $A$ into two halves of size $n/2$.  Cost: $\Theta(1)$.
2. **Conquer** — recursively sort each half.  Cost: $2 T(n/2)$.
3. **Combine** — merge two sorted halves of size $n/2$ into one sorted array.  Cost: $\Theta(n)$.

Therefore the recurrence is:

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \cdot n, \qquad T(1) = d$$

### 3.3.2 Solving the Recurrence by Repeated Substitution

Step 1: Unroll once.

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + cn$$

Step 2: Substitute the recurrence for $T(n/2)$.

$$T(n) = 2\left[2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + c \cdot \frac{n}{2}\right] + cn = 4 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2cn$$

Step 3: Substitute for $T(n/4)$.

$$T(n) = 4\left[2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + c \cdot \frac{n}{4}\right] + 2cn = 8 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{8}\right) + 3cn$$

Step 4: After $i$ unrollings, the pattern is:

$$T(n) = 2^{i} \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + i \cdot cn$$

The recursion stops when $n/2^{i} = 1$, i.e. $2^{i} = n$, so $i = \log_2 n$. Substituting:

$$T(n) = 2^{\log_2 n} \cdot T(1) + c n \cdot \log_2 n = n \cdot d + c n \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{\,T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)\,}$$

This bound holds for **best, worst, and average cases** — one of the hallmarks of Merge Sort that makes it attractive for production-grade libraries (e.g., Java's `Arrays.sort` for object types, Python's `Timsort` hybrid).

## 3.4 Strassen's Matrix Multiplication — Recurrence Derivation

### 3.4.1 The Standard Algorithm

To multiply two $n \times n$ matrices $A$ and $B$, the classic formula needs **8 multiplications** of $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ sub-matrices and **4 additions** of $(n/2) \times (n/2)$ sub-matrices:

$$C_{ij} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} A_{ik} \cdot B_{kj}$$

The recurrence for the naive algorithm:

$$T(n) = 8 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^{2}), \qquad T(1) = \Theta(1)$$

By the Master Theorem, $a = 8,\ b = 2,\ \log_b a = 3$, so $f(n) = \Theta(n^{2}) = O(n^{3-\varepsilon})$ ⇒ **Case 1**.

$$\Rightarrow T(n) = \Theta(n^{3})$$

### 3.4.2 Strassen's Insight (1969)

Volker Strassen observed that the two $2 \times 2$ matrices

$$M_1 = (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22})$$
$$M_2 = (A_{21} + A_{22})B_{11}$$
$$M_3 = A_{11}(B_{12} - B_{22})$$
$$M_4 = A_{22}(B_{21} - B_{11})$$
$$M_5 = (A_{11} + A_{12})B_{22}$$
$$M_6 = (A_{21} - A_{11})(B_{11} + B_{12})$$
$$M_7 = (A_{12} - A_{22})(B_{21} + B_{22})$$

use only **7 multiplications** (instead of 8) and **18 scalar additions/subtractions**, yet still compute the four result blocks correctly:

$$C_{11} = M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7$$
$$C_{12} = M_3 + M_5$$
$$C_{21} = M_2 + M_4$$
$$C_{22} = M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6$$

### 3.4.3 Strassen's Recurrence

Because the combining cost is $\Theta(n^{2})$ (18 such additions on $n/2$-sized matrices) and there are 7 recursive calls:

$$T(n) = 7 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^{2}), \qquad T(1) = \Theta(1)$$

### 3.4.4 Solving the Recurrence

Apply the Master Theorem. Here $a = 7,\ b = 2$, so $\log_b a = \log_2 7 \approx 2.807$.

Compare $f(n) = \Theta(n^{2})$ with $n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$. Since $n^{2} = O(n^{2.807 - 0.807})$ ⇒ **Case 1**.

$$\boxed{\,T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})\,}$$

### 3.4.5 Why It Matters

Even though the asymptotic gain looks modest, on **large dense matrices** Strassen's algorithm is roughly **6× to 10× faster** than the naive cubic algorithm because the constant factors and the lower exponent compound. Modern linear-algebra libraries (e.g., LAPACK, BLAS) include Strassen-like routines for matrices above a few hundred dimensions. For very large matrices, **divide-and-conquer matrix multiplication** (which recursively applies Strassen's scheme) is the practical state-of-the-art.

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Side-by-Side Complexity Comparison

| Algorithm | Divide Step | Recursive Calls | Combine Step | $T(n)$ | Best Use-Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Binary Search | $m = (l+h)/2$ | 1 call, size $n/2$ | None | $\Theta(\log n)$ | Searching sorted arrays |
| Max-Min | $n/2 + n/2$ | 2 calls, size $n/2$ | 2 comparisons | $\Theta(n)$ | Tournament selection |
| Merge Sort | $n/2 + n/2$ | 2 calls, size $n/2$ | Linear merge | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | Stable external sort |
| Standard Matrix Mult. | Quadrants | 8 calls, size $n/2$ | Add sub-matrices | $\Theta(n^{3})$ | Small matrices only |
| Strassen | Quadrants | 7 calls, size $n/2$ | 18 add/sub | $\Theta(n^{2.807})$ | Large dense matrices |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Divide-and-Conquer — Top-Level Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([Start with problem P of size n]) --> checkA{Is size n at most n0}
    checkA -- Yes --> baseA[Return SolveDirectly P]
    baseA --> stopA([Stop])
    checkA -- No --> divideA[Divide P into k subproblems P1 P2 ... Pk]
    divideA --> loopA[For i from 1 to k]
    loopA --> recurseA[Recursively solve subproblem Pi]
    recurseA --> loopA
    loopA -- All subproblems solved --> combineA[Combine solutions Y1 Y2 ... Yk into Y]
    combineA --> stopA
```

## 4.2 Binary Search — Recursion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root1([Search 16 elements]) --> level1L([Search left half size 8])
    root1 --> level1R([Search right half size 8])
    level1L --> level2LL([Search size 4])
    level1L --> level2LR([Search size 4])
    level1R --> level2RL([Search size 4])
    level1R --> level2RR([Search size 4])
    level2LL --> leafLL([Base case size 1])
    level2LL --> leafLR([Base case size 1])
    level2LR --> leafRL2([Base case size 1])
    level2LR --> leafRR2([Base case size 1])
    level2RL --> leafLL3([Base case size 1])
    level2RL --> leafLR3([Base case size 1])
    level2RR --> leafRL4([Base case size 1])
    level2RR --> leafRR4([Base case size 1])
```

> [!NOTE]
> The depth of the binary-search recursion tree is exactly $\log_2 n$. The number of **active** nodes at any level is exactly 1 — only one branch of the recursion is ever explored. This is why the recurrence is $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ and **not** $2T(n/2) + c$.

## 4.3 Merge Sort — Recursion Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    root2([Sort 16 elements]) --> splitL([Sort left 8])
    root2 --> splitR([Sort right 8])
    splitL --> splitLL([Sort left 4])
    splitL --> splitLR([Sort right 4])
    splitR --> splitRL([Sort left 4])
    splitR --> splitRR([Sort right 4])
    splitLL --> baseLL([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitLL --> baseLR([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitLR --> baseRL2([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitLR --> baseRR2([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitRL --> baseLL3([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitRL --> baseLR3([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitRR --> baseRL4([Base size 1 or 2])
    splitRR --> baseRR4([Base size 1 or 2])
    baseLL --> mergeL1([Merge 4 elements])
    baseLR --> mergeL1
    baseRL2 --> mergeL2([Merge 4 elements])
    baseRR2 --> mergeL2
    baseLL3 --> mergeR1([Merge 4 elements])
    baseLR3 --> mergeR1
    baseRL4 --> mergeR2([Merge 4 elements])
    baseRR4 --> mergeR2
    mergeL1 --> mergeFinalL([Merge 8 elements])
    mergeL2 --> mergeFinalL
    mergeR1 --> mergeFinalR([Merge 8 elements])
    mergeR2 --> mergeFinalR
    mergeFinalL --> mergeRoot([Merge 16 elements])
    mergeFinalR --> mergeRoot
```

> [!TIP]
> **Key Visual Insight:** At each level of the Merge Sort tree, the total work across **all nodes** is $\Theta(n)$ (because the merge operation scans the whole array once). With $\log_2 n$ levels, the total work is $\Theta(n \log n)$. Compare this with Binary Search, where only **one node per level is active**.

## 4.4 Strassen's Matrix Multiplication — Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startMat([Two n by n matrices A and B]) --> partitionA[Partition A into 4 quadrants]
    startMat --> partitionB[Partition B into 4 quadrants]
    partitionA --> compM1[Compute M1 equals A11 plus A22 times B11 plus B22]
    partitionA --> compM2[Compute M2 equals A21 plus A22 times B11]
    partitionA --> compM3[Compute M3 equals A11 times B12 minus B22]
    partitionA --> compM4[Compute M4 equals A22 times B21 minus B11]
    partitionA --> compM5[Compute M5 equals A11 plus A12 times B22]
    partitionA --> compM6[Compute M6 equals A21 minus A11 times B11 plus B12]
    partitionA --> compM7[Compute M7 equals A12 minus A22 times B21 plus B22]
    compM1 --> asmC11[Assemble C11 from M1 M4 M5 M7]
    compM2 --> asmC21[Assemble C21 from M2 M4]
    compM3 --> asmC12[Assemble C12 from M3 M5]
    compM4 --> asmC11
    compM4 --> asmC21
    compM5 --> asmC12
    compM6 --> asmC22[Assemble C22 from M1 M2 M3 M6]
    compM7 --> asmC11
    asmC11 --> finalMat([Result matrix C])
    asmC12 --> finalMat
    asmC21 --> finalMat
    asmC22 --> finalMat
```

> [!NOTE]
> Notice that **only 7 multiplications** are issued at every recursion level, in contrast to the naive algorithm's 8. This single saved multiplication is what reduces the asymptotic exponent from $3$ to $\log_2 7 \approx 2.807$.
