# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Advantages and Disadvantages of Divide and Conquer

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Examiners often expect this as a short 3-mark or 7-mark sub-question. Memorise the items below.

### Advantages
- **Elegant reduction of large problems** to simpler sub-problems.
- Often yields **optimal or near-optimal asymptotic bounds** ($\Theta(n \log n)$ for sorting, $\Theta(\log n)$ for searching).
- Naturally **parallelisable** because sub-problems are independent — suitable for multi-core and distributed systems (e.g., MapReduce style).
- **Recursion trees** give a clear visual proof of correctness and complexity.
- Provides a structured way to design new algorithms for unfamiliar problems.

### Disadvantages
- **Recursion overhead**: deep recursion can lead to **stack overflow** for very large $n$ unless converted to iteration.
- **Memory usage**: algorithms like Merge Sort require $O(n)$ auxiliary space (unlike in-place Quicksort variants).
- **Sub-problems may overlap**, in which case D&C becomes inefficient and **Dynamic Programming** is preferred.
- **Constant factors** can be large; D&C algorithms sometimes lose to simpler iterative algorithms for small $n$ (hybrid algorithms switch to insertion-sort for small sub-arrays).
- For Strassen's algorithm, the numerical **stability is lower** than the naive method — inappropriate for ill-conditioned matrices.

---

## 5.2 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model 1]`
**Define the Divide and Conquer paradigm. List its three fundamental steps.**

**Model Answer:**
Divide and Conquer is an algorithm-design technique in which a problem of size $n$ is broken into smaller sub-problems of the same type, each sub-problem is solved recursively (or directly if small enough), and finally the sub-solutions are combined to form the solution to the original problem. The three steps are: **(i) Divide** the problem into one or more sub-problems, **(ii) Conquer** each sub-problem recursively or directly using a base case, and **(iii) Combine** the sub-solutions to obtain the final answer.

### Question 2. `[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model 1]`
**State and apply the Master Theorem to find the asymptotic complexity of the recurrence $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + n$.**

**Model Answer:**
Comparing with $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$, we have $a=2,\ b=2,\ f(n)=n$. Then $\log_b a = \log_2 2 = 1$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{1} \log^{0} n)$, we are in **Case 2** of the Master Theorem with $k=0$. Hence $T(n) = \Theta(n^{1} \cdot \log n) = \Theta(n \log n)$.

---

## 5.3 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) — `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the Binary Search algorithm. Derive its recurrence relation and solve it to find the time complexity. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the divide-and-conquer algorithm to find both the maximum and minimum of a set of $n$ elements. Derive its recurrence and show that it performs exactly $\dfrac{3n}{2} - 2$ comparisons. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Binary Search

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
Algorithm BinarySearch(A, low, high, x):
    if low > high:
        return -1                          // Not found
    mid = floor((low + high) / 2)
    if A[mid] == x:
        return mid
    else if A[mid] < x:
        return BinarySearch(A, mid+1, high, x)
    else:
        return BinarySearch(A, low, mid-1, x)
```

**Recurrence derivation:** At each call, one comparison with $A[m]$ is made and the algorithm recurses on a sub-array of size $\lfloor n/2 \rfloor$ in the worst case. The non-recursive cost is a constant $c$. Hence:

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c, \quad T(1) = d$$

**Solving by repeated substitution:**

$$T(n) = T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c = T\!\left(\frac{n}{4}\right) + 2c = \cdots = T(1) + c \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(\log n)}$$

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Max-Min Algorithm

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
Algorithm MaxMin(A, low, high):
    if high == low:                                 // single element
        return (A[low], A[low])
    if high == low + 1:                            // two elements
        if A[low] < A[high]:
            return (A[high], A[low])               // (max, min)
        else:
            return (A[low], A[high])
    mid = floor((low + high) / 2)
    (max1, min1) = MaxMin(A, low, mid)
    (max2, min2) = MaxMin(A, mid+1, high)
    return (max(max1, max2), min(min1, min2))      // 2 comparisons
```

**Recurrence derivation:** Two recursive calls on sub-problems of size $n/2$, plus exactly **2** comparisons in the combine step:

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + 2, \quad T(2) = 1$$

**Solving by repeated substitution:**

$$T(n) = 2^{i} \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + 2(2^{i} - 1)$$

Substituting $i = \log_2(n/2)$, so $2^{i} = n/2$:

$$T(n) = \frac{n}{2} \cdot 1 + 2\left(\frac{n}{2} - 1\right) = \frac{n}{2} + n - 2$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \frac{3n}{2} - 2}$$

This is provably optimal for the max-min problem.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) — `[KTU University Exam — June 2024]`

**(a)** Explain Merge Sort. Derive its recurrence and solve it to obtain the time complexity $\Theta(n \log n)$. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe Strassen's matrix multiplication. Write its recurrence relation and solve it using the Master Theorem. Compare it with the standard algorithm. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a): Merge Sort

**Algorithm (Pseudocode):**

```
Algorithm MergeSort(A, low, high):
    if low < high:
        mid = floor((low + high) / 2)
        MergeSort(A, low, mid)             // sort left half
        MergeSort(A, mid+1, high)          // sort right half
        Merge(A, low, mid, high)           // merge two halves
```

The `Merge` routine uses two sentinels/pointers to interleave the two sorted halves into a single sorted array in $\Theta(n)$ time.

**Recurrence derivation:**

- **Divide:** $\Theta(1)$ to compute the midpoint.
- **Conquer:** $2 T(n/2)$ for the two recursive calls.
- **Combine:** $\Theta(n)$ to merge.

$$T(n) = 2 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + c \, n, \quad T(1) = d$$

**Solving by repeated substitution:**

$$T(n) = 2^{i} \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2^{i}}\right) + i \cdot c n$$

Stopping at $i = \log_2 n$:

$$T(n) = n \cdot d + c \, n \log_2 n$$

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n \log n)}$$

This bound is uniform across best, worst, and average cases. Auxiliary space is $\Theta(n)$.

#### Model Solution — Part (b): Strassen's Matrix Multiplication

**The Seven Strassen Products** (as derived in §3.4.2):

$$M_1 = (A_{11} + A_{22})(B_{11} + B_{22})$$
$$M_2 = (A_{21} + A_{22}) \cdot B_{11}$$
$$M_3 = A_{11} \cdot (B_{12} - B_{22})$$
$$M_4 = A_{22} \cdot (B_{21} - B_{11})$$
$$M_5 = (A_{11} + A_{12}) \cdot B_{22}$$
$$M_6 = (A_{21} - A_{11}) \cdot (B_{11} + B_{12})$$
$$M_7 = (A_{12} - A_{22}) \cdot (B_{21} + B_{22})$$

**Result quadrants:**

$$C_{11} = M_1 + M_4 - M_5 + M_7, \quad C_{12} = M_3 + M_5$$
$$C_{21} = M_2 + M_4, \quad C_{22} = M_1 - M_2 + M_3 + M_6$$

**Recurrence derivation:** 7 recursive multiplications of size $n/2$, plus 18 additions/subtractions of size $n/2 \times n/2$, each costing $\Theta(n^{2})$:

$$T(n) = 7 \, T\!\left(\frac{n}{2}\right) + \Theta(n^{2}), \quad T(1) = \Theta(1)$$

**Applying the Master Theorem:** $a = 7,\ b = 2$, so $n^{\log_b a} = n^{\log_2 7} \approx n^{2.807}$. Since $f(n) = \Theta(n^{2}) = O(n^{2.807 - \varepsilon})$ for $\varepsilon = 0.807$, this is **Case 1**:

$$\boxed{T(n) = \Theta(n^{\log_2 7}) \approx \Theta(n^{2.807})}$$

**Comparison with the standard algorithm:** Standard matrix multiplication issues 8 recursive multiplications, leading to $T(n) = \Theta(n^{3})$. Strassen saves one multiplication at every recursion level, reducing the exponent from $3$ to approximately $2.807$. For large matrices this yields a substantial practical speedup, though at the cost of poorer numerical stability and increased implementation complexity.

---

## 5.4 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places where students lose marks on this module:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify the base case** of the recurrence (e.g., $T(1) = \Theta(1)$). Always state $T(n_0)$.
> 2. **Confusing the merge step's complexity**: Merge Sort's combine is $\Theta(n)$, not $\Theta(1)$ — many students write $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ and obtain a wrong answer.
> 3. **Mixing up Master Theorem Case 2 and Case 3**: when $f(n)$ exactly equals $n^{\log_b a}$ up to a poly-log factor, it is **Case 2**, not Case 3.
> 4. **Strassen's recurrence**: students often forget the $\Theta(n^{2})$ combine term or write $8T(n/2)$ instead of $7T(n/2)$.
> 5. **Max-Min**: writing $T(n) = 2T(n/2) + 1$ (1 comparison) instead of $2T(n/2) + 2$ (2 comparisons) — both maxima and both minima must be compared.
> 6. **Final answer formatting**: Always wrap the final closed-form in a box and state the asymptotic class explicitly.

---

## 5.5 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Divide and Conquer** has three stages — Divide, Conquer, Combine — and is described by the recurrence $T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n)$.
- **Greedy Strategy** differs from D&C because it does *not* partition the problem; it picks locally optimal choices irrevocably.
- **Binary Search** applies to a *sorted* array only; recurrence $T(n) = T(n/2) + c$ → $\Theta(\log n)$.
- **Max-Min via D&C** performs $3n/2 - 2$ comparisons — provably optimal.
- **Merge Sort** is the canonical stable, $\Theta(n \log n)$ comparison-based sort; combines in $\Theta(n)$ time and uses $\Theta(n)$ auxiliary space.
- **Strassen's Matrix Multiplication** uses 7 (not 8) recursive multiplications to drop the exponent from $3$ to $\log_2 7 \approx 2.807$.
- The **Master Theorem** has three cases based on how $f(n)$ compares to $n^{\log_b a}$.
- Advantages of D&C include **parallelisability** and **optimal asymptotic bounds**; disadvantages include **recursion overhead**, **auxiliary memory cost**, and **numerical-stability concerns** in Strassen-like methods.
- Always **state the base case** of a recurrence explicitly, and **always box the final closed-form answer** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Decrease and Conquer Approach - Topological Sort
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Decrease and Conquer Approach - Topological Sort

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 | Decrease and Conquer: Topological Sort

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!NOTE]
> **Topological Sorting** is a linear ordering of the vertices of a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** $G = (V, E)$ such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, the vertex $u$ appears **before** vertex $v$ in the ordering.

Mathematically, a topological ordering is a bijection $f: V \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, \vert V \vert\}$ such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$:

$$f(u) < f(v)$$

A graph admits a topological sort **if and only if** it contains no directed cycles (i.e., it is a DAG). A DAG may have **multiple valid topological orderings**, and the total number of distinct orderings can be **exponential** in the worst case.

### 1.2 Classification under the Decrease and Conquer Paradigm

Topological sort is presented in the KTU 2024 Algorithm Analysis syllabus as a canonical application of the **Decrease and Conquer** strategy, which is a special form of Divide and Conquer where the problem is reduced by either:

1. **Decrease by a constant** — Reduce the input size by a fixed amount (e.g., remove one vertex).
2. **Decrease by a constant factor** — Reduce the input size by a multiplicative factor.
3. **Decrease by a variable amount** — Reduce the input size based on the structure of the input.

Two principal decrease-and-conquer algorithms are used to produce a topological ordering:

| Algorithm Variant | Type of Decrease | Key Operation |
|------------------|------------------|---------------|
| **Source-Removal Algorithm (Kahn's Algorithm)** | Decrease by a constant | Repeatedly remove a vertex with **in-degree 0** |
| **DFS-Based Topological Sort** | Decrease by a variable (recursion) | Recursively process neighbours, then push to stack |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The University Course Prerequisite Chain

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are a B.Tech student planning your **course schedule**. Course *Data Structures* (DST) requires *Programming in C* (PCC) as a prerequisite. Course *Algorithms* (ALG) requires *DST*. Course *Operating Systems* (OSY) requires *DST* and *Computer Organization* (COA).
>
> You cannot attempt a course until **all** its prerequisites are completed. The act of finding a valid sequence in which to take all courses is **exactly** a topological sort of the *course-prerequisite DAG*.

Other classic applications:
- **Task scheduling in project management** (PERT/CPM charts).
- **Build order resolution** in compilers and dependency managers (e.g., `make`, `apt`, `npm`).
- **Instruction scheduling** and **deadlock detection** in operating systems.
- **Spreadsheet recalculation order** — cells depending on other cells must be re-evaluated after their dependencies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 PECAT415):** Topological sort is specifically listed under *Decrease and Conquer* and is typically examined through the **DFS-based** recursion or the **Source-Removal (Kahn's)** method. Students must be able to trace both algorithms on a given graph and state the asymptotic complexity as $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Source-Removal Algorithm (Kahn's Algorithm) — Operational Breakdown

The **Source-Removal Algorithm**, developed by **A. B. Kahn (1962)**, embodies the pure "decrease by a constant" flavour. At every step, it identifies a **source** — a vertex whose in-degree is zero — removes it from the graph, and appends it to the output. This reduces the problem size by exactly one vertex at each step.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1 (Initialization):** Compute the in-degree $d_{in}(v)$ for every vertex $v \in V$. This requires a single pass over all edges, costing $O(\vert E \vert)$ time. A queue (or list) $S$ is initialized with all vertices whose in-degree equals **zero**.
- **Step 2 (Decrease Step — Pick a Source):** Select a vertex $u$ from $S$. Remove $u$ from the graph conceptually by **decrementing the in-degree** of each of its outgoing neighbours $w \in Adj(u)$ by one. If after decrementing, $d_{in}(w) = 0$, then $w$ is enqueued into $S$.
- **Step 3 (Conquer Step — Record):** Append $u$ to the end of the output list $L$.
- **Step 4 (Termination Check):** Repeat Steps 2–3 until $S$ is empty. If $\vert L \vert = \vert V \vert$, the ordering in $L$ is a valid topological sort. If $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$, the graph contains a **cycle** and topological sort is impossible.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does this work?** A source has no incoming edges, so placing it first cannot violate the precedence constraint of any edge $(u, v)$. After removing $u$, the remaining graph $G - u$ is still a DAG (since removing a vertex cannot introduce a cycle), and the problem is structurally smaller — a textbook **decrease and conquer** situation.

## 2.2 DFS-Based Topological Sort — Operational Breakdown

The DFS-based approach reduces the problem by a **variable amount** — it recurses on all unvisited neighbours before pushing the current vertex. When the DFS call stack unwinds, vertices are pushed onto a stack. Popping all vertices from the stack (or reversing the push order) yields the topological sort.

**Step-by-step operational logic:**

- **Step 1:** Mark all vertices as **unvisited**. Initialize an empty stack $STK$.
- **Step 2 (Decrease Step — Recurse):** For each unvisited vertex $u$, call $\text{DFS-Visit}(u)$.
- **Step 3 (Recursive Conquer):** Inside $\text{DFS-Visit}(u)$:
    - Mark $u$ as visited.
    - For each neighbour $v \in Adj(u)$ that is still unvisited, recursively call $\text{DFS-Visit}(v)$. (This is the **decrease** — the recursion handles the smaller subproblem of $u$'s descendants.)
    - **After** all neighbours are processed, **push $u$ onto $STK$**. (This is the **conquer** — $u$ is placed only after all vertices reachable from $u$ are recorded.)
- **Step 4:** When DFS completes, **pop** all elements from $STK$. The sequence of popped vertices is a topological ordering.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why does this work?** A vertex is pushed onto the stack only when DFS has finished exploring *all* its descendants (all vertices reachable via outgoing edges). Therefore, for any edge $(u, v)$, $v$ is processed (and pushed) before $u$. Popping reverses this, putting $u$ before $v$. This is the **variable-decrease** flavour: each recursive call shrinks the problem by at least one vertex (the current $u$ is "settled" after recursion).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Property / Metric | Formula / Value | Notes |
|--------------------|-----------------|-------|
| **In-degree of vertex $v$** | $d_{in}(v) = \sum_{u \in V} \mathbb{1}[(u,v) \in E]$ | Count of edges entering $v$ |
| **Out-degree of vertex $v$** | $d_{out}(v) = \sum_{w \in V} \mathbb{1}[(v,w) \in E]$ | Count of edges leaving $v$ |
| **Time Complexity (Both Algorithms)** | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Linear in graph size |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(\vert V \vert)$ | For the output list, in-degree array, visited array, stack |
| **Existence Condition** | Graph is a **DAG** (no directed cycles) | A cycle produces $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$ |
| **Number of Orderings (Worst Case)** | Exponential — up to $\vert V \vert !$ | For a graph with no edges, every permutation is valid |
| **Edge Constraint in Ordering $L$** | If $(u,v) \in E$, then $\text{pos}(u) < \text{pos}(v)$ | Index of $u$ in $L$ is less than index of $v$ |
| **Cycle Detection (Kahn's)** | $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert \Rightarrow$ cycle exists | At termination |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Software Engineering:** Build systems (`make`, `Maven`, `Gradle`) and package managers (`apt`, `pip`, `npm`) resolve the dependency DAG to determine the order in which to compile, link, or install components.
- **Operating Systems:** The kernel uses topological sorting to detect **deadlocks** (a cycle in the resource-allocation graph means deadlock) and to determine a safe sequence for resource allocation.
- **VLSI & CAD:** Logic synthesis tools schedule gate evaluations in combinational circuits using topological order over the netlist DAG.
- **Data Science & ML:** DAGs in **Apache Airflow**, **Apache Spark**, and **TensorFlow** define execution pipelines; topological sort determines the evaluation order.
- **Compiler Design:** Instruction-level parallelism (ILP) schedulers use topological order to respect data dependencies when reordering instructions.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example Graph (Reference DAG)

Let us trace both algorithms on the same Directed Acyclic Graph $G = (V, E)$ where:

$$V = \{1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7\}$$

$$E = \{(1,2),\ (1,4),\ (2,3),\ (2,5),\ (3,5),\ (3,7),\ (4,5),\ (4,6),\ (5,7),\ (6,7)\}$$

**Initial in-degree table:**

| Vertex $v$ | Incoming Edges | $d_{in}(v)$ |
|------------|----------------|-------------|
| 1 | — | **0** |
| 2 | (1,2) | **1** |
| 3 | (2,3) | **1** |
| 4 | (1,4) | **1** |
| 5 | (2,5), (3,5), (4,5) | **3** |
| 6 | (4,6) | **1** |
| 7 | (3,7), (5,7), (6,7) | **3** |

## 3.2 Trace of Kahn's Source-Removal Algorithm

**Initial source queue $S$** = $\{1\}$ (only vertex with in-degree 0). Output list $L = [\ ]$.

**Iteration 1:**
- Dequeue $u = 1$. Append to $L$: $L = [1]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $1$: vertices $\{2, 4\}$.
- New $d_{in}(2) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 2. New $d_{in}(4) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 4.
- $S = \{2, 4\}$.

**Iteration 2:**
- Dequeue $u = 2$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $2$: vertices $\{3, 5\}$.
- New $d_{in}(3) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 3. $d_{in}(5) = 2$ (not yet 0).
- $S = \{4, 3\}$.

**Iteration 3:**
- Dequeue $u = 4$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2, 4]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $4$: vertices $\{5, 6\}$.
- $d_{in}(5) = 1$, $d_{in}(6) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 6.
- $S = \{3, 6\}$.

**Iteration 4:**
- Dequeue $u = 3$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2, 4, 3]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $3$: vertices $\{5, 7\}$.
- $d_{in}(5) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 5. $d_{in}(7) = 2$.
- $S = \{6, 5\}$.

**Iteration 5:**
- Dequeue $u = 6$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2, 4, 3, 6]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $6$: vertex $\{7\}$.
- $d_{in}(7) = 1$.
- $S = \{5\}$.

**Iteration 6:**
- Dequeue $u = 5$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2, 4, 3, 6, 5]$.
- Decrement in-degree of neighbours of $5$: vertex $\{7\}$.
- $d_{in}(7) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue 7.
- $S = \{7\}$.

**Iteration 7:**
- Dequeue $u = 7$. Append to $L$: $L = [1, 2, 4, 3, 6, 5, 7]$.
- $S = \emptyset$. **Algorithm terminates.**

**Verification:** $\vert L \vert = 7 = \vert V \vert$, so the graph is a DAG. The ordering

$$1 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 7$$

is a valid topological sort. Every edge $(u, v) \in E$ has $u$ appearing before $v$ in $L$. ✓

## 3.3 Trace of DFS-Based Topological Sort

**Pseudocode Skeleton (Algorithm Style):**

```
ALGORITHM TopologicalSortDFS(G)
    for each vertex v in V do
        visited[v] ← FALSE
    end for
    STK ← empty stack
    for each vertex v in V do
        if not visited[v] then
            DFS-Visit(v)
        end if
    end for
    while STK is not empty do
        output STK.pop()
    end while
END ALGORITHM

PROCEDURE DFS-Visit(u)
    visited[u] ← TRUE
    for each v in Adj(u) do
        if not visited[v] then
            DFS-Visit(v)
        end if
    end for
    STK.push(u)
END PROCEDURE
```

**Step-by-step trace** (start DFS from vertex $1$):

| Step | Action | Visited Set | Stack $STK$ (bottom → top) |
|------|--------|-------------|----------------------------|
| 1 | DFS-Visit(1) | $\{1\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 2 | DFS-Visit(2) (neighbour of 1) | $\{1,2\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 3 | DFS-Visit(3) (neighbour of 2) | $\{1,2,3\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 4 | DFS-Visit(5) (neighbour of 3) | $\{1,2,3,5\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 5 | DFS-Visit(7) (neighbour of 5) | $\{1,2,3,5,7\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 6 | Return from 7 → push 7 | $\{1,2,3,5,7\}$ | $[7]$ |
| 7 | Return from 5 → push 5 | $\{1,2,3,5,7\}$ | $[7, 5]$ |
| 8 | DFS-Visit(7) skipped (visited); back to 3 | — | — |
| 9 | Return from 3 → push 3 | $\{1,2,3,5,7\}$ | $[7, 5, 3]$ |
| 10 | Return from 2 → push 2 | $\{1,2,3,5,7\}$ | $[7, 5, 3, 2]$ |
| 11 | DFS-Visit(4) (next unvisited neighbour of 1) | $\{1,2,3,4,5,7\}$ | $[7, 5, 3, 2]$ |
| 12 | DFS-Visit(6) (neighbour of 4; 5 already visited) | $\{1,2,3,4,5,6,7\}$ | $[7, 5, 3, 2]$ |
| 13 | DFS-Visit(7) skipped; return from 6 → push 6 | — | $[7, 5, 3, 2, 6]$ |
| 14 | Return from 4 → push 4 | — | $[7, 5, 3, 2, 6, 4]$ |
| 15 | Return from 1 → push 1 | — | $[7, 5, 3, 2, 6, 4, 1]$ |

**Pop order (topological sort):**

$$1 \rightarrow 4 \rightarrow 6 \rightarrow 2 \rightarrow 3 \rightarrow 5 \rightarrow 7$$

This is a **different** valid topological ordering from the one produced by Kahn's algorithm — confirming the non-uniqueness of topological sorts on a DAG.

## 3.4 Cycle Detection — Analytical Derivation

Consider a graph with the cycle $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$ and no other vertices.

**Initial in-degree:** $d_{in}(1) = d_{in}(2) = d_{in}(3) = 1$.

**Kahn's source queue $S$** = $\emptyset$ (no vertex with in-degree 0).

**Iteration 1:** $S$ is empty, so the loop terminates immediately.

**Result:** $L = [\ ]$, so $\vert L \vert = 0 < 3 = \vert V \vert$.

**Conclusion:** The presence of a directed cycle leaves **no source vertex**, making topological sort impossible. This is the **definitive cycle detection** criterion used in Kahn's algorithm.

In the DFS variant, the same cycle would manifest as a **back edge** — an edge $(u, v)$ where $v$ is an ancestor of $u$ in the DFS tree — detected via the standard DFS color/ancestor marking scheme.

## 3.5 Complexity Derivation (Asymptotic Bound)

Let $n = \vert V \vert$ and $m = \vert E \vert$.

**For Kahn's Algorithm:**

- **Computing in-degrees:** Single pass over all edges.

$$T_{in-deg} = \sum_{(u,v) \in E} O(1) = O(m)$$

- **Initial source identification:** Single pass over all vertices.

$$T_{init} = O(n)$$

- **Main loop:** Each vertex is dequeued exactly once (each pop costs $O(1)$ using a queue). For each dequeue, the algorithm iterates over the outgoing edges of the popped vertex. The total work over all iterations equals the total number of edges.

$$T_{main} = O(n) + O(m)$$

- **Total:**

$$T_{Kahn}(n, m) = O(n) + O(m) + O(m) = O(n + m)$$

**For DFS-Based Topological Sort:**

- **DFS visits each vertex exactly once** and traverses **each edge exactly once** in the adjacency-list representation. Standard DFS complexity applies.

$$T_{DFS}(n, m) = O(n + m)$$

- **Stack operations** (push and pop) are $O(1)$ each and execute $n$ times.

$$T_{stack} = O(n)$$

- **Total:**

$$T_{DFS-Topo}(n, m) = O(n + m)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board-Standard Answer:** Both algorithms run in $\boxed{O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)}$ time and $O(\vert V \vert)$ auxiliary space (for the visited/in-degree arrays, queue, and stack). This is **optimal** for the topological sort problem because any algorithm must at least read the input graph, which requires $\Omega(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Reference DAG (Input Graph)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["1"] --> node2["2"]
    node1 --> node4["4"]
    node2 --> node3["3"]
    node2 --> node5["5"]
    node3 --> node5
    node3 --> node7["7"]
    node4 --> node5
    node4 --> node6["6"]
    node5 --> node7
    node6 --> node7
```

*Figure 4.1: The reference DAG $G$ used throughout the notes. Vertices 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7 with the ten directed edges listed in Section 3.1.*

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Kahn's Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Input DAG G with V vertices and E edges"] --> B["Compute in-degree array din for all v in V"]
    B --> C["Initialize queue S with all v where din v equals 0"]
    C --> D{"Is queue S empty?"}
    D -- "Yes" --> E["Check: is L complete?"]
    D -- "No" --> F["Dequeue source vertex u from S"]
    F --> G["Append u to output list L"]
    G --> H["For each w in Adj of u: decrement din w by 1"]
    H --> I{"Is din w now equal to 0?"}
    I -- "Yes" --> J["Enqueue w into S"]
    I -- "No" --> K["Continue to next neighbour"]
    J --> K
    K --> L{"More neighbours?"}
    L -- "Yes" --> H
    L -- "No" --> D
    E --> M{"Is size of L equal to size of V?"}
    M -- "Yes" --> N["SUCCESS: L is a valid topological ordering"]
    M -- "No" --> O["FAILURE: Graph contains a directed cycle"]
```

*Figure 4.2: Functional flow of Kahn's Source-Removal Algorithm. Each iteration corresponds to one **decrease step** (vertex removal), and the output list is built incrementally — a textbook decrease-and-conquer execution trace.*

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of DFS-Based Topological Sort

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["START: Input DAG G with V vertices and E edges"] --> B["Mark all vertices as unvisited"]
    B --> C["Initialize empty stack STK"]
    C --> D{"All vertices processed?"}
    D -- "No" --> E["Pick an unvisited vertex u"]
    E --> F["Call DFS Visit u"]
    F --> G["Mark u as visited"]
    G --> H["For each v in Adj of u: is v unvisited?"]
    H -- "Yes" --> I["Recursively call DFS Visit v"]
    I --> H
    H -- "No" --> J["Push u onto STK"]
    J --> D
    D -- "Yes" --> K["Pop all elements from STK to produce ordering L"]
    K --> L["OUTPUT: L is a valid topological ordering"]
```

*Figure 4.3: Functional flow of the DFS-based Topological Sort. The recursive DFS-Visit is the **decrease step** — each call shrinks the unvisited subgraph — and the stack push is the **conquer step** that records the current vertex after all its descendants are settled.*

## 4.4 Decrease-and-Conquer Recursion Tree (DFS-Based)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R["DFS-Visit 1: conquer on stack after recursion"] --> A["DFS-Visit 2: neighbour of 1"]
    R --> B["DFS-Visit 4: neighbour of 1"]
    A --> C["DFS-Visit 3: neighbour of 2"]
    A --> D["DFS-Visit 5: neighbour of 2 (also of 3,4)"]
    C --> E["DFS-Visit 5 already visited skip"]
    C --> F["DFS-Visit 7: neighbour of 3 (also of 5,6)"]
    D --> G["DFS-Visit 7 already visited skip"]
    B --> H["DFS-Visit 5 already visited skip"]
    B --> I["DFS-Visit 6: neighbour of 4"]
    I --> J["DFS-Visit 7 already visited skip"]
    F --> K["push 7 onto stack"]
    G --> L["push 5 onto stack after backtrack"]
    J --> M["push 6 onto stack after backtrack"]
    H --> N["push 4 onto stack after backtrack"]
    E --> O["push 3 onto stack after backtrack"]
    L --> P["push 2 onto stack after backtrack"]
    N --> Q["push 1 onto stack after backtrack"]
    M --> N
    O --> P
    P --> Q
    K --> R2["Pop order: 1, 4, 6, 2, 3, 5, 7"]
    L --> R2
    M --> R2
    N --> R2
    O --> R2
    P --> R2
    Q --> R2
```

*Figure 4.4: Conceptual recursion tree of DFS-based topological sort. Each level shows the **decrease step** (recurse into smaller subproblems) and the leaves correspond to **base cases** (no unvisited neighbours). The push/pop sequence yields the topological ordering $1, 4, 6, 2, 3, 5, 7$.*
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**Define topological sorting. Under what conditions does a directed graph admit a topological sort?**

**Model Answer:**

Topological sorting is the process of arranging the vertices of a directed graph $G = (V, E)$ in a linear order $L$ such that for every directed edge $(u, v) \in E$, the vertex $u$ appears before $v$ in $L$. Formally, if $f: V \to \{1, \dots, \vert V \vert\}$ denotes the position function, then $(u, v) \in E \implies f(u) < f(v)$.

A directed graph admits a topological sort **if and only if** it is a **Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG)** — i.e., it contains no directed cycle. This equivalence is a foundational theorem: any DAG possesses at least one valid topological ordering, and conversely, if a graph has a cycle, no such ordering can exist because the cycle would impose contradictory precedence constraints.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**
**Classify topological sort under the algorithm-design paradigms. Which two decrease-and-conquer variants are commonly used?**

**Model Answer:**

Topological sort is a canonical example of the **Decrease and Conquer** paradigm, in which a problem is solved by reducing it to a smaller instance of the same problem and combining the result. Unlike Divide and Conquer (which partitions into multiple independent subproblems), Decrease and Conquer reduces to a single smaller subproblem.

Two principal decrease-and-conquer algorithms implement topological sort:

1. **Source-Removal Algorithm (Kahn's Algorithm):** Repeatedly identifies and removes a vertex with in-degree 0 (a "source"). Each iteration decreases the problem size by **one vertex**, exemplifying the **decrease-by-a-constant** variant.

2. **DFS-Based Topological Sort:** Recursively explores all unvisited neighbours of a vertex, then pushes the vertex onto a stack. The recursion reduces the problem by a **variable amount** (all descendants of the current vertex), exemplifying the **decrease-by-a-variable** variant.

Both run in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ time.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Paper]**

**Apply Kahn's Source-Removal Algorithm to find a topological sort of the following DAG. Show each iteration explicitly and verify the result. State the time and space complexity.**

$$V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$$

$$E = \{(A, C),\ (A, D),\ (B, C),\ (B, E),\ (C, D),\ (C, F),\ (D, E),\ (D, F),\ (E, F)\}$$

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Trace the algorithm step-by-step.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute Initial In-Degrees:**

| Vertex | Incoming Edges | $d_{in}$ |
|--------|----------------|----------|
| A | — | 0 |
| B | — | 0 |
| C | (A,C), (B,C) | 2 |
| D | (A,D), (C,D) | 2 |
| E | (B,E), (D,E) | 2 |
| F | (C,F), (D,F), (E,F) | 3 |

**Initial source queue:** $S = \{A, B\}$, $L = [ \ ]$.

**Iteration 1:** Dequeue $u = A$. $L = [A]$. Decrement in-degree of neighbours $\{C, D\}$: $d_{in}(C) = 1$, $d_{in}(D) = 1$. $S = \{B\}$.

**Iteration 2:** Dequeue $u = B$. $L = [A, B]$. Decrement in-degree of neighbours $\{C, E\}$: $d_{in}(C) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue $C$; $d_{in}(E) = 1$. $S = \{C\}$.

**Iteration 3:** Dequeue $u = C$. $L = [A, B, C]$. Decrement in-degree of neighbours $\{D, F\}$: $d_{in}(D) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue $D$; $d_{in}(F) = 2$. $S = \{D\}$.

**Iteration 4:** Dequeue $u = D$. $L = [A, B, C, D]$. Decrement in-degree of neighbours $\{E, F\}$: $d_{in}(E) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue $E$; $d_{in}(F) = 1$. $S = \{E\}$.

**Iteration 5:** Dequeue $u = E$. $L = [A, B, C, D, E]$. Decrement in-degree of neighbour $\{F\}$: $d_{in}(F) = 0 \Rightarrow$ enqueue $F$. $S = \{F\}$.

**Iteration 6:** Dequeue $u = F$. $L = [A, B, C, D, E, F]$. $S = \emptyset$. **Algorithm terminates.**

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Verify result, state complexity, classify algorithm.

**Verification:** $\vert L \vert = 6 = \vert V \vert$, so the graph is a DAG. The ordering $A, B, C, D, E, F$ is valid because for every edge $(u, v) \in E$, vertex $u$ precedes $v$ in $L$. ✓

**Time Complexity:**

$$T(n, m) = O(n + m) = O(6 + 9) = O(15)$$

**Space Complexity:** $O(n) = O(6)$ for the in-degree array, source queue, and output list.

**Classification:** This is a **Decrease-by-a-Constant** algorithm — at each step, exactly one vertex (a source) is removed, reducing the problem size by 1. The recurrence is:

$$T(n) = T(n - 1) + O(1) \quad \text{per edge processed, summing to } O(n + m)$$

---

### Question B — 14 Marks (Alternative Choice)

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper]**

**Apply the DFS-based topological sort algorithm to the DAG in Question A. Show the complete DFS traversal, the stack contents at each step, and the final topological ordering. Compare with the Source-Removal method.**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks: Trace DFS-Based Topological Sort.

**Model Solution:**

Starting DFS from vertex $A$:

| Step | Action | Visited | Stack (bottom → top) |
|------|--------|---------|----------------------|
| 1 | DFS-Visit(A): mark visited | $\{A\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 2 | Recurse DFS-Visit(C) (neighbour of A) | $\{A, C\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 3 | Recurse DFS-Visit(D) (neighbour of C) | $\{A, C, D\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 4 | Recurse DFS-Visit(E) (neighbour of D) | $\{A, C, D, E\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 5 | Recurse DFS-Visit(F) (neighbour of E) | $\{A, C, D, E, F\}$ | $[ \ ]$ |
| 6 | Return from F → **push F** | — | $[F]$ |
| 7 | Return from E → **push E** | — | $[F, E]$ |
| 8 | DFS-Visit(F) already visited; back to D | — | $[F, E]$ |
| 9 | Return from D → **push D** | — | $[F, E, D]$ |
| 10 | DFS-Visit(F) already visited; back to C | — | $[F, E, D]$ |
| 11 | Return from C → **push C** | — | $[F, E, D, C]$ |
| 12 | Return from A → **push A** | — | $[F, E, D, C, A]$ |
| 13 | Next unvisited vertex: B → DFS-Visit(B) | $\{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ | $[F, E, D, C, A]$ |
| 14 | C, E already visited; return from B → **push B** | — | $[F, E, D, C, A, B]$ |

**Pop order (topological sort):** $B, A, C, D, E, F$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks: Verify, classify, and compare.

**Verification:** For each edge $(u, v) \in E$, $u$ appears before $v$:

| Edge | $u$ position | $v$ position | Valid? |
|------|--------------|--------------|--------|
| (A, C) | A = 2 | C = 3 | ✓ |
| (A, D) | A = 2 | D = 4 | ✓ |
| (B, C) | B = 1 | C = 3 | ✓ |
| (B, E) | B = 1 | E = 5 | ✓ |
| (C, D) | C = 3 | D = 4 | ✓ |
| (C, F) | C = 3 | F = 6 | ✓ |
| (D, E) | D = 4 | E = 5 | ✓ |
| (D, F) | D = 4 | F = 6 | ✓ |
| (E, F) | E = 5 | F = 6 | ✓ |

**Classification:** This is a **Decrease-by-a-Variable** algorithm — the recursion depth varies depending on the graph's depth. The implicit recurrence is:

$$T(n, m) = O(n + m) \text{ (standard DFS cost)}$$

**Comparison with Source-Removal:**

| Aspect | Kahn's Algorithm | DFS-Based |
|--------|------------------|-----------|
| Type of decrease | By a constant (remove 1 vertex) | By a variable (recurse on all descendants) |
| Data structure | Queue (or list) of sources | Stack (DFS call stack + explicit push) |
| Cycle detection | $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$ | Back edge in DFS tree |
| Ordering produced | $A, B, C, D, E, F$ | $B, A, C, D, E, F$ |
| Determinism | Depends on queue tie-breaking | Depends on vertex-visit order |

Both are correct; the difference in output reflects the non-uniqueness of topological orderings on a DAG.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to recompute in-degrees after each removal** in Kahn's algorithm. The in-degree of a neighbour must be decremented, not simply checked.
> 2. **Misidentifying source vertices.** A source has in-degree 0 in the *current* (already-reduced) graph, not necessarily the original graph.
> 3. **Confusing push order with pop order in DFS.** The stack is built bottom-up; the *popped* sequence (reversed push order) is the topological sort.
> 4. **Not checking for cycles.** If $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$ at termination, the graph is not a DAG and no valid topological sort exists. Always state this conclusion explicitly.
> 5. **Stating the wrong complexity.** The bound is $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$, **not** $O(\vert V \vert \cdot \vert E \vert)$. Examiners deduct heavily for this.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Topological sort** is a linear ordering of the vertices of a **DAG** such that for every edge $(u, v)$, $u$ appears before $v$.
- It **exists if and only if** the graph is a **DAG** (no directed cycles).
- A DAG may have **multiple valid topological orderings**; the number can be exponential in $\vert V \vert$.
- **Two decrease-and-conquer algorithms:**
    - **Kahn's Source-Removal** (decrease by a constant): remove in-degree-0 vertex, repeat.
    - **DFS-Based** (decrease by a variable): recursively visit descendants, then push to stack.
- **Time complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for both algorithms — **optimal** because reading the input alone requires this much time.
- **Space complexity:** $O(\vert V \vert)$ for the in-degree/visited arrays, queue/stack, and output list.
- **Cycle detection:**
    - In Kahn's algorithm: $\vert L \vert < \vert V \vert$ at termination.
    - In DFS-based approach: a **back edge** in the DFS tree signals a cycle.
- **Real-world applications:** course scheduling, software build systems, deadlock detection, instruction scheduling, project management (PERT/CPM), and pipeline execution in data engineering.
- **Key classification under KTU 2024 syllabus:** Topological sort is a **canonical Decrease and Conquer** problem — *not* Divide and Conquer — because at each step the problem is reduced to a **single** smaller subproblem.
- **Common exam trap:** stating $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ complexity — always use the linear bound $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ for adjacency-list representations.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: The Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy - Fractional Knapsack Problem, Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Computation - Kruskal's Algorithms, Analysis
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** The Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy - Fractional Knapsack Problem, Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Computation - Kruskal's Algorithms, Analysis

---

### 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

#### 1.1 The Greedy Strategy – Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Greedy Algorithm (KTU 2024 Definition):** A greedy algorithm is a problem-solving methodology that builds up a solution piece by piece, always choosing the next piece that offers the most **immediate, local optimal benefit** at that step, under the assumption that a sequence of locally optimal choices will yield a **globally optimal solution**.

In the KTU 2024 syllabus, the *control abstraction* of the greedy strategy is the **generic algorithmic skeleton** (a template procedure) into which any specific greedy problem (Knapsack, MST, Job Sequencing, Huffman, Dijkstra, etc.) can be plugged.

> [!NOTE]
> **Control Abstraction:** A generalized procedure that captures the universal "select → check-feasibility → commit" loop used by every greedy algorithm, independent of the specific optimization function.

#### 1.2 The Three Pillars of Every Greedy Algorithm

| Pillar | Role |
|---|---|
| **Candidate Set (C)** | The pool of all feasible choices available at the start |
| **Selection Function (S)** | The greedy criterion that picks the "best" candidate (e.g., max value/weight, min weight edge) |
| **Feasibility Check (F)** | A boolean test that verifies whether adding the candidate keeps the partial solution valid |

#### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy – "The Tiffin-Box Packer"

Imagine you are packing a lunch box of capacity **W = 5 kg** with food items of varying weights and tastiness (value). 

- **Greedy by Value/Weight ratio** is like a smart canteen manager: at every step, he picks the food that gives the *most taste per gram* first (say, a chocolate bar with ratio 8), then the next-best (juice with ratio 5), and so on — until the box is exactly full. Unlike the **0/1 knapsack**, he is allowed to *break* a roti in half to fit it perfectly. This is the **Fractional Knapsack Problem**.

#### 1.4 The Fractional Knapsack Problem (FKP)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fractional Knapsack Problem Definition (KTU Module 2):** Given $n$ items, each with a value $v_i > 0$ and a weight $w_i > 0$, and a knapsack of total capacity $W$, find the subset of items (where items **may be taken fractionally**, i.e., $0 \le x_i \le 1$) that **maximizes total value** $\sum v_i x_i$ subject to the weight constraint $\sum w_i x_i \le W$.

#### 1.5 Minimum Cost Spanning Tree (MCST / MST)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Minimum Cost Spanning Tree Definition (KTU):** Given a connected, undirected, weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = n$ vertices and edge weights $w(u,v)$, a **spanning tree** is an acyclic subgraph that connects all $n$ vertices using exactly $(n-1)$ edges. The **Minimum Cost Spanning Tree** is the spanning tree whose sum of edge weights $\sum_{e \in T} w(e)$ is **minimum among all possible spanning trees**.

#### 1.6 Kruskal's Algorithm – Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kruskal's Algorithm (KTU 2024):** A greedy algorithm for MST that sorts all edges of the graph in **non-decreasing order of weight** and adds them one by one to the growing forest. An edge is **accepted** only if its inclusion does **NOT form a cycle** with the already selected edges (verified using a **Disjoint Set Union / Union-Find** data structure). The process stops when exactly $(n-1)$ edges have been accepted.

#### 1.7 Intuitive Analogy – "The Cable-Laying Engineer"

Picture a telecom engineer connecting 7 villages with underground cable at minimum cost. He lays cables in **increasing order of cost per kilometre**. Before laying each cable, he asks: *"Will this cable create a loop?"*. If yes (a village already connected), he skips it; if no, he commits it. He stops when all villages are connected by exactly 6 cables (a tree). That is **Kruskal's algorithm** in action.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** The KTU examiner expects you to (1) state the *control abstraction*, (2) apply it to **Fractional Knapsack** and **Kruskal's MST**, and (3) provide a rigorous **asymptotic analysis** of both.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

### 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

#### 2.1 The Control Abstraction of Greedy Strategy – Universal Template

The control abstraction is a generic procedure (see Section 3 for the full pseudocode). The structural flow is:

1. **Initialize** the solution set $A \leftarrow \emptyset$.
2. **Initialize** the candidate set $C \leftarrow$ the set of all possible objects (items / edges).
3. **Repeat** while $A$ has not yet formed a complete solution:
   - **Select** the best candidate $u \in C$ using the **greedy selection function** $S(C)$ (e.g., max profit-to-weight ratio, min edge weight).
   - **Remove** $u$ from $C$ : $C \leftarrow C \setminus \{u\}$.
   - **Check Feasibility** $F(A \cup \{u\})$. If adding $u$ is feasible, **accept** it: $A \leftarrow A \cup \{u\}$.
   - **Else** (infeasible), **discard** $u$ permanently.
4. **Return** the final solution set $A$.

> [!NOTE]
> The **key insight** for KTU: every greedy algorithm differs ONLY in three places — the *greedy criterion (S)*, the *feasibility test (F)*, and the *termination condition*. The outer loop structure is identical for Fractional Knapsack, Kruskal, Prim, Dijkstra, Job Sequencing, and Huffman Coding.

#### 2.2 Fractional Knapsack – Algorithmic Analysis

**Greedy Criterion:** Sort items by **decreasing value-to-weight ratio** $r_i = \dfrac{v_i}{w_i}$, i.e., $r_1 \ge r_2 \ge \ldots \ge r_n$.

**Why this works:** Choosing the highest $r_i$ first packs maximum value per unit weight. Because fractional items are allowed, the optimality proof relies on the **Greedy-Choice Property** (a locally optimal choice is part of some globally optimal solution) and the **Optimal-Substructure Property** (an optimal solution to the subproblem is part of an optimal solution to the original).

**Step-by-step Logic:**
- Compute $r_i$ for every item.
- Sort items in decreasing order of $r_i$.
- Iterate $i = 1 \to n$:
  - If $w_i \le$ remaining capacity $W_r$: take the whole item, $x_i = 1$.
  - Else: take a fraction $x_i = W_r / w_i$, set $W_r = 0$, and stop.
- Total profit = $\sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i x_i$.

#### 2.3 Kruskal's Algorithm – Algorithmic Analysis

**Greedy Criterion:** Pick the **minimum-weight edge** remaining in the graph (global minimum, not local to a vertex — this distinguishes Kruskal from **Prim's**, which picks a local minimum).

**Feasibility Function:** The edge $(u, v)$ is feasible **iff** vertices $u$ and $v$ belong to **different connected components** of the current forest. This is checked in $O(\alpha(n))$ amortized time using the **Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union — DSU)** data structure with **path compression** and **union by rank**.

**Step-by-step Logic:**
- Sort all $m$ edges of $G$ in non-decreasing order of weight: $O(m \log m)$.
- Initialize a DSU of $n$ singleton sets: $O(n)$.
- Initialize accepted-edges set $T \leftarrow \emptyset$.
- Iterate through the sorted edge list:
  - For each edge $(u, v, w)$: if $\text{Find}(u) \ne \text{Find}(v)$, then $\text{Union}(u, v)$ and $T \leftarrow T \cup \{(u,v)\}$.
  - If $|T| = n - 1$, break and output $T$.
- Return $T$ as the MST.

#### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Complexity Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is the **single most important reference** for KTU Module 2 numerical/derivation questions. Memorise the cost model and the DSU inverse-Ackermann term $\alpha(n)$.

| **Algorithm / Quantity** | **Formula / Expression** | **Time Complexity** | **Space Complexity** | **Optimality Guarantee** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sorting step (Knapsack) | Sort by $r_i = v_i / w_i$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(1)$ auxiliary | — |
| Greedy selection loop (Knapsack) | Single pass over sorted list | $O(n)$ | $O(1)$ | Yes (Fractional only) |
| **Total – Fractional Knapsack** | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(n)$ | **Provably Optimal** |
| Edge sorting (Kruskal) | Sort $m$ edges | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(m)$ | — |
| DSU initialization (Kruskal) | $n$ MakeSet calls | $O(n)$ | $O(n)$ | — |
| Per-edge DSU ops (Kruskal) | $2$ Find + 1 Union | $O(\alpha(n))$ | — | — |
| **Total – Kruskal (with DSU)** | $\Theta(m \log m)$ | $O(m \log m)$ | $O(n+m)$ | **Provably Optimal** |
| Number of edges in any spanning tree | $n - 1$ | — | — | — |
| Cycle-check in Kruskal | $\text{Find}(u) \ne \text{Find}(v)$ | $O(\alpha(n))$ | — | — |
| $\alpha(n)$ – Inverse Ackermann | Grows slower than $\log^{*} n$ | $< 5$ for all $n < 10^{600}$ | — | — |
| MST weight objective | $\min \sum_{e \in T} w(e)$ | — | — | — |
| Knapsack objective | $\max \sum v_i x_i$ s.t. $\sum w_i x_i \le W$ | — | — | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Note on Complexity:** $O(m \log m)$ dominates $O(m \cdot \alpha(n))$ because $\alpha(n) \le 4$ for all practical $n$. Thus the asymptotic bottleneck of Kruskal is the **initial edge sort**, NOT the DSU operations. This is a high-yield KTU question.

#### 2.5 Why the Greedy Choice is Optimal — Intuition

For **Fractional Knapsack**: Suppose the optimal OPT takes a fraction of item $j$ (with $r_j < r_i$ for some unpicked item $i$). Replacing that fraction with the same weight from item $i$ strictly **increases** the total value, contradicting OPT's optimality. Hence the greedy must be optimal.

For **Kruskal's MST**: The standard exchange (cut) argument — at every step, the chosen minimum-weight edge $e$ crossing some cut is **strictly less than or equal** to the weight of any edge of OPT that also crosses that cut. Swapping preserves minimality.

#### 2.6 Engineering Real-World Utility

- **Fractional Knapsack** models: cargo loading, resource allocation in cloud servers, bandwidth slicing in 5G networks, and investment diversification with liquid assets.
- **Minimum Cost Spanning Tree** models: laying minimal-cost electrical/telecom/fibre-optic cables, designing minimal-latency VLSI routing, building low-cost road/railway networks, and clustering in machine learning (single-linkage clustering).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

### 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

#### 3.1 Generalised Control Abstraction – Full Pseudocode

```
Algorithm Greedy(C, S, F)
Input :  Candidate set C
         Selection function S : 2^C → C
         Feasibility test    F : 2^C → Boolean
Output : A feasible solution A ⊆ C

1.  A ← ∅
2.  while  C ≠ ∅  and  A is not a complete solution  do
3.        u ← S(C)              // pick best candidate (the GREEDY CHOICE)
4.        C ← C \ {u}           // remove it from the pool
5.        if  F(A ∪ {u})  then  // is it safe to add ?
6.               A ← A ∪ {u}    // YES → commit (ACCEPT)
7.        end if
8.  end while
9.  return  A
```

**Line-by-line reasoning:**

- **Line 1:** Start with an empty solution — no commitment has been made.
- **Line 2:** The loop runs until either there are no more candidates or the solution is structurally complete (e.g., $n-1$ edges in MST, knapsack full, all jobs scheduled).
- **Line 3:** $S(C)$ is the **problem-specific greedy criterion** — for Fractional Knapsack it returns the item with the highest residual $v_i/w_i$ ratio; for Kruskal it returns the minimum-weight edge from the still-unprocessed sorted list.
- **Line 4:** Once a candidate is examined, it is **never reconsidered** (Greedy is irrevocable).
- **Lines 5–7:** This is the **only place** a wrong greedy choice is prevented — e.g., in Kruskal, $F$ returns FALSE when $\text{Find}(u) = \text{Find}(v)$ (cycle detected), and the edge is rejected.
- **Line 9:** Return the final solution. No backtracking is ever performed.

#### 3.2 Worked Example — Fractional Knapsack

**Problem Instance:**

| Item $i$ | Value $v_i$ | Weight $w_i$ | Capacity $W$ |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | 60 | 10 | — |
| 2 | 100 | 20 | — |
| 3 | 120 | 30 | **W = 50** |

**Step 1 — Compute the value-to-weight ratio $r_i = v_i / w_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_1 &= \frac{60}{10} = 6.0 \\
r_2 &= \frac{100}{20} = 5.0 \\
r_3 &= \frac{120}{30} = 4.0
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Sort items in non-increasing order of $r_i$:**

The sorted order is **Item 1 (6.0) > Item 2 (5.0) > Item 3 (4.0)**. This order is already natural in the given data.

**Step 3 — Greedy filling with remaining capacity $W_r = 50$:**

**Iteration 1 — Take Item 1:**
$w_1 = 10 \le W_r = 50$, so we take the whole item.
$x_1 = 1$, value gained $= 60$, capacity remaining $W_r = 50 - 10 = 40$.

**Iteration 2 — Take Item 2:**
$w_2 = 20 \le W_r = 40$, so we take the whole item.
$x_2 = 1$, value gained $= 100$, capacity remaining $W_r = 40 - 20 = 20$.

**Iteration 3 — Take Item 3 (partially):**
$w_3 = 30 > W_r = 20$, so we take only a fraction.
$x_3 = \dfrac{W_r}{w_3} = \dfrac{20}{30} = \dfrac{2}{3}$.
Value gained $= v_3 \times x_3 = 120 \times \dfrac{2}{3} = 80$.
Capacity remaining $W_r = 20 - 30 \times \dfrac{2}{3} = 0$.

**Step 4 — Compute the final optimal value:**

$$
V_{\text{opt}} = 60 \times 1 + 100 \times 1 + 120 \times \frac{2}{3} = 60 + 100 + 80 = \boxed{240}
$$

**Step 5 — Complexity tally for this instance:**

Sorting cost $= O(n \log n) = O(3 \log 3) \approx 4.75$ operations. The greedy loop is $O(n) = O(3)$. Total asymptotic cost $= \Theta(n \log n)$.

#### 3.3 Worked Example — Kruskal's MST

**Problem Instance:** Connected undirected weighted graph $G = (V, E)$ with $|V| = 6$ vertices labelled $A, B, C, D, E, F$ and the following $|E| = 10$ edges:

| Edge | Weight | Edge | Weight |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| (A, B) | 4 | (C, D) | 7 |
| (A, F) | 2 | (D, E) | 9 |
| (B, F) | 5 | (B, C) | 8 |
| (B, D) | 1 | (E, F) | 6 |
| (C, F) | 4 | (D, F) | 2 |

**Step 1 — Sort edges in non-decreasing order of weight:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(B, D) &= 1 \\
(A, F) &= 2 \\
(D, F) &= 2 \\
(A, B) &= 4 \\
(C, F) &= 4 \\
(B, F) &= 5 \\
(E, F) &= 6 \\
(C, D) &= 7 \\
(B, C) &= 8 \\
(D, E) &= 9
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Initialise DSU:** Each vertex is in its own component:
$\{A\}, \{B\}, \{C\}, \{D\}, \{E\}, \{F\}$.

**Step 3 — Iteratively process edges; accept if $\text{Find}(u) \ne \text{Find}(v)$:**

| # | Edge $(u,v)$ | Weight | Find $(u)$ | Find $(v)$ | Cycle ? | Action | Components After |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | (B, D) | 1 | B | D | No | **Accept** | {A}, {B,D}, {C}, {E}, {F} |
| 2 | (A, F) | 2 | A | F | No | **Accept** | {A,F}, {B,D}, {C}, {E} |
| 3 | (D, F) | 2 | B,D | A,F | No | **Accept** | {A,F,B,D}, {C}, {E} |
| 4 | (A, B) | 4 | A,F,B,D | A,F,B,D | **Yes** | Reject | {A,F,B,D}, {C}, {E} |
| 5 | (C, F) | 4 | C | A,F,B,D | No | **Accept** | {A,F,B,D,C}, {E} |
| 6 | (B, F) | 5 | A,F,B,D,C | A,F,B,D,C | **Yes** | Reject | {A,F,B,D,C}, {E} |
| 7 | (E, F) | 6 | E | A,F,B,D,C | No | **Accept** | {A,F,B,D,C,E} ✓ |

**Termination:** We have accepted 5 edges. Since $|V| - 1 = 6 - 1 = 5$, the MST is complete.

**Step 4 — The MST and its total weight:**

$$
T = \{(B,D),\,(A,F),\,(D,F),\,(C,F),\,(E,F)\}
$$

$$
W_{\text{MST}} = 1 + 2 + 2 + 4 + 6 = \boxed{15}
$$

#### 3.4 Complexity Analysis – Kruskal's Algorithm (Detailed)

**Cost Decomposition:**

$$
T_{\text{Kruskal}}(n, m) = \underbrace{T_{\text{sort}}}_{\text{Step 1}} + \underbrace{T_{\text{init}}}_{\text{Step 2}} + \underbrace{T_{\text{process}}}_{\text{Step 3}}
$$

**Step 1 – Edge sorting:**

There are $m$ edges. Using a comparison-based sort (merge sort / heap sort):

$$
T_{\text{sort}} = \Theta(m \log m)
$$

**Step 2 – DSU initialisation:**

We call $\text{MakeSet}(v)$ for every vertex. Each call is $O(1)$, and there are $n$ vertices:

$$
T_{\text{init}} = \Theta(n)
$$

**Step 3 – Edge processing:**

For each of the $m$ edges, we perform exactly **two Find operations** and **one Union operation** (the latter is conditional, but in the worst case still $O(1)$ amortised plus a Find). With path compression and union by rank, the amortised cost of each Find/Union is $\alpha(n)$, where $\alpha$ is the **inverse Ackermann function**:

$$
T_{\text{process}} = m \cdot O(\alpha(n)) = O(m \cdot \alpha(n))
$$

**Step 4 – Total asymptotic complexity:**

$$
T_{\text{Kruskal}} = \Theta(m \log m) + \Theta(n) + O(m \cdot \alpha(n))
$$

Since $\alpha(n) \le 4$ for all practically encountered $n$, $m \cdot \alpha(n) \le 4m = O(m)$, which is strictly dominated by $m \log m$ for $m \ge 2$. Therefore:

$$
\boxed{T_{\text{Kruskal}}(n, m) = \Theta(m \log m)}
$$

**Space complexity:** We store the sorted edge list ($m$ edges) and the DSU arrays (`parent` and `rank` of length $n$):

$$
S_{\text{Kruskal}} = O(n + m)
$$

#### 3.5 Complexity Analysis – Fractional Knapsack (Detailed)

**Cost Decomposition:**

$$
T_{\text{FKP}}(n) = \underbrace{T_{\text{ratio}}}_{\text{compute } r_i} + \underbrace{T_{\text{sort}}}_{\text{sort by } r_i} + \underbrace{T_{\text{greedy-loop}}}_{\text{iterate and pack}}
$$

**Step 1 – Ratio computation:** A single pass over $n$ items:

$$
T_{\text{ratio}} = \Theta(n)
$$

**Step 2 – Sorting by ratio:** Comparison-based sort:

$$
T_{\text{sort}} = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Step 3 – Greedy loop:** A single linear pass:

$$
T_{\text{greedy-loop}} = \Theta(n)
$$

**Total asymptotic complexity:**

$$
T_{\text{FKP}} = \Theta(n) + \Theta(n \log n) + \Theta(n) = \Theta(n \log n)
$$

**Space complexity:** Storing the items and the sorted order:

$$
S_{\text{FKP}} = O(n)
$$

#### 3.6 Master Comparison Matrix (KTU Viva Favourite)

| **Parameter** | **Fractional Knapsack** | **Kruskal's MST** |
|---|---|---|
| Problem Class | Optimisation (maximisation) | Optimisation (minimisation) |
| Greedy Criterion | Max $r_i = v_i / w_i$ | Min $w(u,v)$ edge weight |
| Data Structure Used | Simple array (sorted) | Disjoint Set Union (DSU) |
| Sorting Cost | $O(n \log n)$ | $O(m \log m)$ |
| Per-iteration cost | $O(1)$ | $O(\alpha(n))$ |
| Total Time | $\Theta(n \log n)$ | $\Theta(m \log m)$ |
| Space | $O(n)$ | $O(n + m)$ |
| Optimal for $0/1$ Knapsack? | **No** (counter-example exists) | **N/A** |
| Produces unique output? | Not necessarily | Not necessarily (ties exist) |
| Polynomial-time? | Yes | Yes |
| Requires graph to be connected? | No | **Yes** (else returns a Minimum Spanning **Forest**) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Fractional Knapsack's greedy **fails spectacularly** for the $0/1$ Knapsack variant. Classic counter-example: items $(v, w) = (60, 10), (100, 20), (120, 30)$ with $W = 50$. Greedy gives $240$, but the optimal $0/1$ choice is to drop the low-ratio item 1 and take items 2 + 3 = $220$… wait, that is **worse**. A true counter-example: items $(v, w) = (30, 5), (50, 10), (60, 15)$ with $W = 20$. Greedy picks $r_1 = 6, r_2 = 5, r_3 = 4$ → takes item 1 fully ($6$ units) then item 2 fully ($10$ units) = $80$ value, weight $15$; then $5$ units of item 3 = $20$ value, total $100$. But the $0/1$ optimum is items 2 + 3 = $50 + 60 = 110$. **The greedy FAILS for $0/1$ knapsack.** Dynamic Programming is required for $0/1$.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

### 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

#### 4.1 Generic Greedy Control Abstraction – Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA([START Greedy C S F]) --> initA["A equals empty set"]
    initA --> checkA{"C not empty AND A not complete"}
    checkA -- NO --> returnA[/"RETURN A"\]
    checkA -- YES --> selectA["u = SC  Greedy Choice"]
    selectA --> removeA["C = C minus u"]
    removeA --> feasA{"F of A union u  = TRUE"}
    feasA -- YES --> acceptA["A = A union u  ACCEPT"]
    feasA -- NO --> rejectA["Discard u permanently"]
    acceptA --> checkA
    rejectA --> checkA
    returnA --> stopA([END])
```

#### 4.2 Fractional Knapsack – Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startB([START FKP]) --> inputB[/"READ n, v_i, w_i, W"/]
    inputB --> ratioB["FOR i = 1 to n: compute r_i = v_i / w_i"]
    ratioB --> sortB["SORT items by r_i in DECREASING order"]
    sortB --> initB["W_r = W  remaining capacity"]
    initB --> loopB{"i <= n AND W_r > 0"}
    loopB -- NO --> outputB[/"PRINT total value V_opt"/]
    loopB -- YES --> compareB{"w_i <= W_r"}
    compareB -- YES --> fullB["x_i = 1  take whole item"]
    fullB --> updFullB["W_r = W_r - w_i; V = V + v_i"]
    updFullB --> incrB["i = i + 1"]
    compareB -- NO --> fracB["x_i = W_r / w_i  take fraction"]
    fracB --> updFracB["W_r = 0; V = V + v_i * x_i"]
    updFracB --> incrB
    incrB --> loopB
    outputB --> stopB([END])
```

#### 4.3 Kruskal's Algorithm – Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startC([START Kruskal G]) --> readC[/"READ V, E, weights"/]
    readC --> sortC["SORT all m edges by weight ASCENDING"]
    sortC --> dsC["INIT DSU: parent v = v, rank v = 0 for all v in V"]
    dsC --> tC["T = empty set  accepted edges"]
    tC --> iterC{"i <= m AND size of T < n-1"}
    iterC -- NO --> outC[/"PRINT T and total weight"/]
    iterC -- YES --> pickC["Pick next edge e = u, v, w from sorted list"]
    pickC --> findC["root_u = Find u; root_v = Find v"]
    findC --> cycC{"root_u == root_v  CYCLE ?"}
    cycC -- YES --> skipC["REJECT edge e  forms cycle"]
    cycC -- NO --> unionC["Union u v  merge components"]
    unionC --> addC["T = T union e  ACCEPT"]
    skipC --> nextC["i = i + 1"]
    addC --> nextC
    nextC --> iterC
    outC --> stopC([END])
```

#### 4.4 DSU (Union-Find) Subsystem Referenced by Kruskal

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph DSU["Disjoint Set Union Subsystem"]
        make["MakeSet x  parent x = x, rank x = 0"]
        find["Find x  WITH path compression"]
        union["Union x y  BY RANK  with path compression"]
        make --> find
        find --> union
    end
```

#### 4.5 Greedy vs. Dynamic Programming – Conceptual Map

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PARADIGMS["Algorithmic Paradigms Map"]
        direction TB
        greedy["GREEDY  locally optimal sequence = global optimum"]
        dp["DP  overlapping subproblems + optimal substructure"]
        dnc["DIVIDE AND CONQUER  non-overlapping subproblems"]
        bt["BACKTRACKING  exhaustive search with pruning"]
    end
    greedy -.->|"APPLIES TO"| fkp["Fractional Knapsack"]
    greedy -.->|"APPLIES TO"| kru["Kruskal MST"]
    greedy -.->|"APPLIES TO"| prim["Prim MST"]
    greedy -.->|"APPLIES TO"| dijk["Dijkstra SSSP"]
    greedy -.->|"APPLIES TO"| huff["Huffman Coding"]
    dp -.->|"APPLIES TO"| kn01["0 by 1 Knapsack"]
    dp -.->|"APPLIES TO"| floyd["Floyd Warshall APSP"]
```
