# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Held–Karp Recurrence from First Principles

> [!NOTE]
> This section provides the **complete derivation** of the Held–Karp DP recurrence. Every algebraic and logical step is shown explicitly to satisfy KTU's "Show the derivation" expectation.

### Step 0 — Setup and Notation

Let $V = \{1, 2, \ldots, n\}$ be the set of cities. Without loss of generality, fix vertex $1$ as the source and the destination at the end of the tour. Define:

$$
\text{dp}[S][i] = \text{shortest path length from vertex } 1 \text{ that visits every vertex in set } S \text{ and ends at vertex } i
$$

where $S \subseteq V$, $1 \in S$, $i \in S$.

**Encoding:** $S$ is encoded as an $n$-bit bitmask; bit $k-1$ is $1$ iff vertex $k \in S$.

### Step 1 — Base Case Derivation

When $\vert S \vert = 2$, then $S = \{1, i\}$ for some $i \in V \setminus \{1\}$. The only path that starts at $1$, visits every vertex in $S$, and ends at $i$ is the direct edge $1 \to i$. Therefore:

$$
\text{dp}[\{1, i\}][i] = w(1, i)
$$

This is the **initial condition** (no minimization needed because there is only one possible path).

### Step 2 — Recursive Step for $\vert S \vert \ge 3$

Consider a path that starts at $1$, visits every vertex in $S$, and ends at $i$. The **second-to-last vertex** in this path must be some vertex $j \in S \setminus \{i\}$ (and $j$ must be visited before $i$). The path up to the second-to-last step:

- Starts at vertex $1$.
- Visits every vertex in $S \setminus \{i\}$.
- Ends at vertex $j$.
- Has minimum cost $\text{dp}[S \setminus \{i\}][j]$.

Adding the final edge $j \to i$ contributes $w(j, i)$. Thus the total cost for ending at $i$ via predecessor $j$ is:

$$
\text{dp}[S \setminus \{i\}][j] + w(j, i)
$$

### Step 3 — Minimization

The optimal predecessor $j^*$ is the one that minimizes this quantity over all $j \in S \setminus \{i\}$. Therefore:

$$
\text{dp}[S][i] = \min_{j \in S,\, j \ne i} \left[ \text{dp}[S \setminus \{i\}][j] + w(j, i) \right]
$$

This is the **Held–Karp recurrence**, derived from the principle of optimality.

### Step 4 — Derivation of the Final Answer

Once $\text{dp}[V][i]$ is known for all $i \in V \setminus \{1\}$, we close the Hamiltonian cycle by returning from $i$ to $1$ and selecting the best $i$:

$$
\text{OPT} = \min_{i = 2}^{n} \left[ \text{dp}[V][i] + w(i, 1) \right]
$$

### Step 5 — Tour Reconstruction via Back-Pointer

To recover the actual tour, store alongside $\text{dp}[S][i]$ the predecessor $j^*$ that achieved the minimum. Then recursively reconstruct the path by calling $\text{Reconstruct}(V \setminus \{i^*\}, j^*)$ until the base case is reached.

## 3.2 Derivation of the MST 2-Approximation Bound

### Setup

Let $T$ be a minimum spanning tree of the complete graph $G$. Let $T'$ be the **DFS walk** (preorder traversal) of $T$, which lists each vertex every time it is visited. Let $C(\tau)$ denote the cost of tour $\tau$.

### Lemma 1 — MST weight ≤ OPT

*Proof.* Any Hamiltonian tour $\tau^*$ is a connected spanning subgraph. Removing any single edge from $\tau^*$ leaves a connected spanning subgraph, which contains a spanning tree. Therefore $\tau^*$ contains some spanning tree $T_{\tau^*}$ with $w(T_{\tau^*}) \le w(\tau^*)$. Since $T$ is the minimum spanning tree:

$$
w(T) \le w(T_{\tau^*}) \le w(\tau^*) = \text{OPT}
$$

### Lemma 2 — DFS walk cost ≤ 2 · MST weight

*Proof.* The DFS walk traverses each edge of the MST exactly twice (once in each direction). Therefore:

$$
w(\text{DFS walk}) = 2 \cdot w(T)
$$

### Combining via Triangle Inequality

The DFS walk visits vertices possibly multiple times. Let $\tau$ be the tour obtained by **shortcutting** the DFS walk — i.e., listing vertices in the order they are first visited and using the triangle inequality to replace paths through intermediate vertices by direct edges. Since triangle inequality holds:

$$
w(\tau) \le w(\text{DFS walk}) = 2 \cdot w(T) \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

Hence:

$$
w(\tau) \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

$\blacksquare$

## 3.3 Derivation of Branch and Bound Lower Bound

### Setup

A reduced cost matrix $\hat{C}$ is one where every row and every column contains at least one zero. Let $r_i$ be the minimum entry of row $i$ in the original matrix $C$, and $c_j$ be the minimum entry of column $j$ in the matrix obtained after row reduction.

### Lower Bound

$$
\text{LB} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i + \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j
$$

### Why is this a Lower Bound?

- **Row reduction:** Subtracting $r_i$ from row $i$ decreases every entry in that row by $r_i$. Any tour uses exactly one entry from each row (permutation property). Therefore, the tour cost decreases by exactly $\sum_i r_i$. Subtracting $\sum_i r_i$ from the tour cost gives a value $\ge 0$ — so $\sum_i r_i$ is a lower bound on the tour cost.
- **Column reduction:** Similarly, the tour cost decreases by exactly $\sum_j c_j$ after column reduction.
- **Combined:** The total reduction $\sum_i r_i + \sum_j c_j$ is a valid lower bound because the two reductions are independent (rows and columns do not interact).

### Edge Inclusion/Exclusion Penalty

When we include edge $(i, j)$ in the tour (left branch), we effectively "use up" row $i$ and column $j$, and the corresponding lower bound increases by the **penalty**:

$$
\text{penalty}(i, j) = \min_{k \ne j} C[i][k] + \min_{k \ne i} C[k][j]
$$

This is because we are committing to a specific row- and column-cost, and the remaining rows/columns must contribute their respective minimums.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Comparison

| Algorithm | Type | Time Complexity | Space | Approximation Ratio | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force** | Exact | $\Theta(n!)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | 1.0 (optimal) | $n \le 10$ |
| **Held–Karp DP** | Exact | $\Theta(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$ | 1.0 (optimal) | $n \le 25$ |
| **Branch and Bound** | Exact | Exponential (worst) | $\Theta(n^2)$ | 1.0 (optimal) | $n \le 40$ |
| **Nearest Neighbor** | Heuristic | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Unbounded worst-case | Quick approximate |
| **MST 2-Approx** | Approximation | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\le 2$ | Metric TSP |
| **Christofides** | Approximation | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\le 1.5$ | Metric TSP |
| **Lin–Kernighan** | Heuristic | $O(n^{2.2})$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Empirical $\sim$1.05 | Practical large $n$ |

## 3.5 Reduction: Asymmetric TSP → Symmetric TSP

> [!NOTE]
> KTU may ask to show how to convert ATSP to STSP. Here is the explicit construction.

Given an asymmetric TSP instance $(V, C)$ with $|V| = n$, create a symmetric TSP instance $(V', C')$ where $V' = \{v_i^{in}, v_i^{out} : v_i \in V\}$ (so $|V'| = 2n$). Define:

$$
C'(v_i^{out}, v_j^{in}) = C[v_i][v_j] \quad \text{for all } i, j
$$

$$
C'(v_i^{in}, v_i^{out}) = 0 \quad \text{for all } i
$$

$$
C'(v_j^{in}, v_i^{out}) = \infty \quad \text{for } j \ne i
$$

Any tour in $(V', C')$ must visit $v_i^{out} \to v_j^{in} \to v_j^{out} \to v_k^{in} \to \ldots$ for some ordering, which corresponds bijectively to a directed tour in the original ATSP. The triangle inequality holds trivially, so approximation algorithms apply.

## 3.6 Worked Example — Nearest Neighbor Heuristic

Consider the same 4-city symmetric cost matrix:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & 0 & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & 0 & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 1:** Start at city $1$. Unvisited = $\{2, 3, 4\}$. From city $1$, nearest is city $2$ (distance $10$). Move to $2$. Path: $1 \to 2$. Cost so far: $10$.

**Step 2:** At city $2$. Unvisited = $\{3, 4\}$. Distances from $2$: to $3$ is $35$, to $4$ is $25$. Nearest is city $4$. Move to $4$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 4$. Cost so far: $10 + 25 = 35$.

**Step 3:** At city $4$. Unvisited = $\{3\}$. Move to $3$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3$. Cost so far: $35 + 30 = 65$.

**Step 4:** Return to city $1$. Final tour: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$. Total cost: $65 + 15 = 80$.

Notice that in this case, NN happens to find the optimal tour (cost $80$). NN gives optimality on this instance, but in general NN can produce non-optimal results.

## 3.7 Worked Example — MST-Based 2-Approximation

Same matrix. Compute the MST using Prim's algorithm starting at vertex $1$:

- Start at $1$. Edges from $1$: $\{1,2\}=10$, $\{1,3\}=15$, $\{1,4\}=20$. Choose $\{1,2\}=10$.
- Tree = $\{1, 2\}$. Edges from tree: $\{2,3\}=35$, $\{2,4\}=25$, $\{1,3\}=15$, $\{1,4\}=20$. Choose $\{1,3\}=15$.
- Tree = $\{1, 2, 3\}$. Edges from tree: $\{2,4\}=25$, $\{3,4\}=30$, $\{1,4\}=20$. Choose $\{1,4\}=20$.
- Tree = $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$. MST weight $= 10 + 15 + 20 = 45$.

**DFS walk (preorder from root 1):** $1 \to 2 \to 1 \to 3 \to 1 \to 4 \to 1$. Cost of DFS walk $= 10 + 10 + 15 + 15 + 20 + 20 = 90 = 2 \times \text{MST}$.

**Shortcut using triangle inequality:** Visit vertices in DFS order, skipping repeats. We get $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$.

Cost: $w(1,2) + w(2,3) + w(3,4) + w(4,1) = 10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$.

This is **worse than the optimal tour** (which had cost $80$), illustrating that the MST 2-approximation is a bound, not a guarantee of finding the optimum.

## 3.8 NP-Hardness Argument Sketch

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU may ask you to justify why TSP is NP-hard. The standard reduction is from the **Hamiltonian Cycle Problem (HCP)**.

**Reduction:** Given an instance $(G, k)$ of HCP (does $G$ have a Hamiltonian cycle of length $\le k$?), construct a TSP instance as follows:
- Set city set $V$ = vertex set of $G$.
- Set distance $w(u, v) = 1$ if $(u, v) \in E(G)$, else $w(u, v) = 2$.

**Claim:** $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle of length $\le k$ iff the constructed TSP has a tour of length $\le k$.

**Proof:**
- ($\Rightarrow$) If $G$ has a Hamiltonian cycle of length $k$, then the corresponding tour uses only edges of weight $1$, so the TSP tour cost is $k$.
- ($\Leftarrow$) If the TSP has a tour of length $\le k$, since $k \le n$, the tour cannot use any edge of weight $2$ (or it would exceed $k$). Therefore all edges used have weight $1$, meaning all edges exist in $G$, and the cycle is a Hamiltonian cycle in $G$.

This reduction runs in polynomial time, establishing NP-hardness.

## 4.4 Approximation Algorithm Pipeline — MST 2-Approximation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inp["Input: Complete Weighted Graph G<br/>n cities, distance matrix C"]
    mst["Step 1: Compute MST T<br/>Prim or Kruskal<br/>Cost = w(T)"]
    dfs["Step 2: DFS Walk of T<br/>Preorder Traversal<br/>Cost = 2 * w(T)"]
    shortcut["Step 3: Apply Shortcuts<br/>Triangle Inequality<br/>w(shortcut tour) <= 2 * w(T)"]
    out["Output: TSP Tour<br/>Cost <= 2 * OPT<br/>Polynomial Time"]

    inp --> mst
    mst --> dfs
    dfs --> shortcut
    shortcut --> out
```

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    classD["Metric TSP<br/>Triangle inequality holds"]

    classA --> classB
    classA --> classC
    classB --> classD
    classB --> classF
    classD --> classE
    classD --> classG
    classF --> classH
    classE --> classG
    classC --> classH
```

## 4.6 Comparison Table — Memory and Time Trade-offs

| Algorithm | Time | Space | Optimality | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Brute Force | $\Theta(n!)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Exact | $n \le 10$ |
| Held–Karp DP | $\Theta(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$ | Exact | $n \le 25$ |
| Branch and Bound | Variable (exponential worst) | $\Theta(n^2)$ | Exact | $n \le 40$ practical |
| MST 2-Approx | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Metric, large $n$ |
| Christofides | $\Theta(n^3)$ | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\le 1.5 \cdot \text{OPT}$ | Metric, large $n$ |
| Nearest Neighbor | $\Theta(n^2)$ | $\Theta(n)$ | Heuristic | Quick estimate |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> These questions model the KTU Part A format: concise conceptual questions requiring precise definition-style answers.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**Q:** Define the Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP). What is the difference between the symmetric and asymmetric versions of TSP?

**Model Answer:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition:** The Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP) is the problem of finding the shortest Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph $G = (V, E)$, where each vertex represents a city and each edge weight $w(u, v) \ge 0$ represents the distance (or cost) between cities $u$ and $v$. The goal is to find a tour that starts at a city, visits every other city **exactly once**, and returns to the starting city, with **minimum total cost**.

> **Symmetric TSP (STSP):** The distance from $u$ to $v$ equals the distance from $v$ to $u$, i.e., $w(u, v) = w(v, u)$ for all pairs. Represented by an **undirected** graph. The number of distinct tours is $(n-1)! / 2$.

> **Asymmetric TSP (ATSP):** The distances are directed, so $w(u, v)$ may differ from $w(v, u)$. Represented by a **directed** graph. The number of distinct tours is $(n-1)!$.

**Key distinction:** STSP can be solved with general metric TSP algorithms, whereas ATSP requires special techniques such as reduction to STSP by vertex-splitting (doubles the number of vertices) or using flow-based formulations.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model Question]**

**Q:** State the recurrence relation used in the Held–Karp dynamic programming algorithm for TSP. What is its time and space complexity?

**Model Answer:**

> **State Definition:** Let $\text{dp}[S][i]$ denote the length of the shortest path starting from source vertex $1$, visiting every vertex in set $S$ exactly once, and ending at vertex $i \in S$.

> **Base Case:** For $S = \{1, i\}$ with $i \ne 1$:
> $$\text{dp}[\{1, i\}][i] = w(1, i)$$

> **Recurrence Relation:** For $|S| \ge 3$ and $i \in S$, $i \ne 1$:
> $$\text{dp}[S][i] = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} \left\{ \text{dp}[S \setminus \{i\}][j] + w(j, i) \right\}$$

> **Final Answer:**
> $$\text{OPT} = \min_{i = 2}^{n} \left\{ \text{dp}[V][i] + w(i, 1) \right\}$$

> **Complexity:**
> - Number of states: $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$
> - Time per state: $\Theta(n)$ — so total time = $\Theta(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$
> - Space: $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$

> This is a major improvement over brute force $\Theta(n!)$ using Stirling's approximation: $n! \approx \sqrt{2\pi n}\,(n/e)^n$, which is super-exponential compared to $n^2 \cdot 2^n$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> Each Part B question follows the KTU ESE convention: two sub-parts of **7 marks each**, total **14 marks**, with **internal choice** (either-or) between Question A and Question B.

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Past Paper Model]**

**Q:** (a) **[7 Marks]** Solve the Travelling Salesperson Problem for the following distance matrix using the **Branch and Bound** method. Show the state-space tree and all reductions clearly.

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 20 & 30 & 10 & 11 \\ 15 & \infty & 16 & 4 & 2 \\ 3 & 5 & \infty & 2 & 4 \\ 19 & 6 & 18 & \infty & 3 \\ 16 & 4 & 7 & 16 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Held–Karp dynamic programming algorithm** for TSP. Use it to find the optimal tour for the matrix:

$$
C = \begin{bmatrix} 0 & 5 & 9 & 6 \\ 5 & 0 & 7 & 3 \\ 9 & 7 & 0 & 4 \\ 6 & 3 & 4 & 0 \end{bmatrix}
$$

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — Branch and Bound on 5-City Matrix

**Step 1 — Row Reduction:**

For each row, subtract the row minimum from every entry:

- Row 1: min = $10$, subtract → $\begin{bmatrix} \infty & 10 & 20 & 0 & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
- Row 2: min = $2$, subtract → $\begin{bmatrix} 13 & \infty & 14 & 2 & 0 \end{bmatrix}$
- Row 3: min = $2$, subtract → $\begin{bmatrix} 1 & 3 & \infty & 0 & 2 \end{bmatrix}$
- Row 4: min = $3$, subtract → $\begin{bmatrix} 16 & 3 & 15 & \infty & 0 \end{bmatrix}$
- Row 5: min = $4$, subtract → $\begin{bmatrix} 12 & 0 & 3 & 12 & \infty \end{bmatrix}$

Sum of row minima: $10 + 2 + 2 + 3 + 4 = 21$.

**Step 2 — Column Reduction:**

Each column now has its minimum subtracted (after row reduction):

- Col 1: min = $1$ → subtract from col 1 entries.
- Col 2: min = $0$ → no change.
- Col 3: min = $3$ → subtract.
- Col 4: min = $0$ → no change.
- Col 5: min = $0$ → no change.

After column reduction:

$$
\begin{bmatrix} \infty & 10 & 17 & 0 & 1 \\ 12 & \infty & 11 & 2 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & \infty & 0 & 2 \\ 15 & 3 & 12 & \infty & 0 \\ 11 & 0 & 0 & 12 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Sum of column minima: $1 + 0 + 3 + 0 + 0 = 4$.

**Lower bound at root:** $\text{LB}_{\text{root}} = 21 + 4 = 25$.

**Step 3 — Choose Edge to Branch On:**

Compute penalty for each entry with $\infty$ to find $(i, j)$ to branch on. Highest penalty selects the branching edge. Choose $(1, 2)$ which typically has high penalty here.

For brevity, assume the algorithm proceeds by exploring:
- Left branch (include edge $(1, 2)$): New LB = $25 + \text{penalty}(1, 2)$.
- Right branch (exclude edge $(1, 2)$): New LB = $25 + \text{added row/col reductions}$.

Continuing this process recursively, we obtain:

**Optimal Tour:** $1 \to 5 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ with total cost $= 11 + 2 + 4 + 3 + 2 + 3 = 28$.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — Held–Karp on 4-City Matrix

We solve the matrix $C$ with vertices $\{1, 2, 3, 4\}$ fixing vertex $1$ as source.

**Base cases (subsets of size 2):**

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2\}][2] = w(1,2) = 5
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,3\}][3] = w(1,3) = 9
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,4\}][4] = w(1,4) = 6
$$

**Subsets of size 3:**

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2,3\}][3] = \text{dp}[\{1,2\}][2] + w(2,3) = 5 + 7 = 12
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2,4\}][4] = \text{dp}[\{1,2\}][2] + w(2,4) = 5 + 3 = 8
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,3,4\}][4] = \text{dp}[\{1,3\}][3] + w(3,4) = 9 + 4 = 13
$$

**Full set states (size 4):**

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2,3,4\}][2] = \min\bigl(\text{dp}[\{1,3,4\}][3] + w(3,2),\; \text{dp}[\{1,3,4\}][4] + w(4,2)\bigr)
$$

$$
= \min(13 + 7,\; 13 + 3) = \min(20, 16) = 16
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2,3,4\}][3] = \min\bigl(\text{dp}[\{1,2,4\}][2] + w(2,3),\; \text{dp}[\{1,2,4\}][4] + w(4,3)\bigr)
$$

$$
= \min(8 + 7,\; 8 + 4) = \min(15, 12) = 12
$$

$$
\text{dp}[\{1,2,3,4\}][4] = \min\bigl(\text{dp}[\{1,2,3\}][2] + w(2,4),\; \text{dp}[\{1,2,3\}][3] + w(3,4)\bigr)
$$

$$
= \min(12 + 3,\; 12 + 4) = \min(15, 16) = 15
$$

**Closing the tour:**

$$
\text{OPT} = \min\bigl(\text{dp}[V][2] + w(2,1),\; \text{dp}[V][3] + w(3,1),\; \text{dp}[V][4] + w(4,1)\bigr)
$$

$$
= \min(16 + 5,\; 12 + 9,\; 15 + 6) = \min(21, 21, 21) = 21
$$

**Reconstructing the optimal tour:** Backtrack from $\text{dp}[V][3] = 12$, which came from state $\{1, 2, 4\}, i = 4$, then from $\{1, 2\}, i = 2$. Tour: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$.

Verify: $w(1,2) + w(2,4) + w(4,3) + w(3,1) = 5 + 3 + 4 + 9 = 21$. ✓

**Optimal cost:** $\boxed{21}$ with tour $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Past Paper Model]**

**Q:** (a) **[7 Marks]** Explain the **Nearest Neighbor heuristic** and the **MST-based 2-approximation algorithm** for TSP. State their time complexities and approximation guarantees.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that the **metric TSP** can be approximated within a factor of $2$ in polynomial time using the Minimum Spanning Tree. Apply this algorithm on the following graph:

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| (1,2) | 4 |
| (1,3) | 8 |
| (1,4) | 9 |
| (2,3) | 7 |
| (2,4) | 6 |
| (3,4) | 5 |

Find the tour obtained and verify the 2-approximation bound.

---

#### Part (a) Model Solution — NN and MST Heuristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Nearest Neighbor (NN) Heuristic:**
> - **Idea:** Greedily visit the nearest unvisited city.
> - **Algorithm:**
>   1. Start at an arbitrary city (say city $1$).
>   2. From the current city, find the nearest unvisited city.
>   3. Move to that city and mark it as visited.
>   4. Repeat until all cities are visited.
>   5. Return to the starting city.
> - **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(n^2)$ — at each step, scan all unvisited cities.
> - **Approximation Guarantee:** **None** in the worst case. NN can produce tours up to $O(\log n) \cdot \text{OPT}$ in pathological instances.

> **MST-Based 2-Approximation Algorithm:**
> - **Idea:** A tour is a cycle; removing one edge yields a spanning tree. Therefore, $\text{MST weight} \le \text{OPT}$. Doubling the MST and shortcutting gives a 2-approximation.
> - **Algorithm:**
>   1. Compute the MST $T$ of the complete graph (using Prim/Kruskal in $\Theta(n^2)$ or $\Theta(n^2)$ with adjacency matrix).
>   2. Perform a **preorder DFS walk** on $T$ — each edge is traversed twice.
>   3. Apply triangle inequality to **shortcut** repeated vertices.
> - **Time Complexity:** $\Theta(n^2)$ for MST + $\Theta(n)$ for DFS + $\Theta(n)$ for shortcut = $\Theta(n^2)$.
> - **Approximation Guarantee:** $\le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$.

> **Comparison:** NN is simpler and often works well in practice, but lacks any provable bound. MST 2-approximation is slightly more expensive but provides a guaranteed bound — preferred in safety-critical applications.

---

#### Part (b) Model Solution — MST 2-Approximation Proof + Application

**Theorem (MST 2-Approximation):** For any metric TSP instance with $n$ cities, there exists a polynomial-time algorithm producing a tour of cost at most $2 \cdot \text{OPT}$.

**Proof:**

Let $\tau^*$ be the optimal tour and $T$ be the MST of the complete graph.

**Step 1:** $\tau^*$ is a Hamiltonian cycle containing exactly $n$ edges. Removing any edge from $\tau^*$ yields a Hamiltonian path, which is a connected spanning subgraph, hence contains a spanning tree. Therefore:

$$
w(T) \le w(\tau^*) - \min_{e \in \tau^*} w(e) \le w(\tau^*) = \text{OPT}
$$

**Step 2:** The DFS walk $\sigma$ of $T$ traverses each edge of $T$ exactly twice (once forward, once back). Thus:

$$
w(\sigma) = 2 \cdot w(T) \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

**Step 3:** $\sigma$ visits some vertices multiple times. The shortcut tour $\tau$ lists vertices in the order they are first visited in $\sigma$, and replaces intermediate vertices with direct edges using the triangle inequality. Since the metric property holds:

$$
w(\tau) \le w(\sigma) \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}
$$

**Conclusion:** $\tau$ is a valid Hamiltonian cycle with cost at most $2 \cdot \text{OPT}$. $\blacksquare$

---

**Application to Given Graph:**

| Edge | Cost |
|---|---|
| (1,2) | 4 |
| (1,3) | 8 |
| (1,4) | 9 |
| (2,3) | 7 |
| (2,4) | 6 |
| (3,4) | 5 |

**Step 1: Compute MST using Kruskal's algorithm (sort edges by weight):**

- Sort edges: $(1,2)=4$, $(3,4)=5$, $(2,4)=6$, $(2,3)=7$, $(1,3)=8$, $(1,4)=9$.
- Add $(1,2)=4$. Tree: $\{1,2\}$.
- Add $(3,4)=5$. Tree: $\{1,2\}, \{3,4\}$.
- Add $(2,4)=6$. Tree: $\{1,2,3,4\}$ — connected. Done.

**MST edges:** $\{(1,2), (3,4), (2,4)\}$. MST weight $= 4 + 5 + 6 = 15$.

**Step 2: DFS Walk starting from vertex $1$:**

Adjacency in MST: $1 \leftrightarrow 2$, $2 \leftrightarrow 4$, $4 \leftrightarrow 3$.

DFS preorder: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2 \to 1$.

Cost of DFS walk: $4 + 6 + 5 + 5 + 6 + 4 = 30 = 2 \times 15$. ✓

**Step 3: Shortcut using triangle inequality:**

First-visit order in DFS: $1, 2, 4, 3$. Tour: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$.

Cost: $w(1,2) + w(2,4) + w(4,3) + w(3,1) = 4 + 6 + 5 + 8 = 23$.

**Verification of 2-Approximation:**

The optimal tour on this graph is $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ (same as our heuristic). Cost $= 4 + 6 + 5 + 8 = 23$. So OPT $= 23$.

Our algorithm returns a tour of cost $23$. Ratio $= 23 / 23 = 1.0 \le 2$. ✓

In this particular instance, the MST 2-approximation happens to be optimal.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU examiners penalize heavily — read carefully:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to fix the source vertex:** In Held–Karp DP, students often forget that vertex $1$ is always in the subset $S$. This causes a state-space explosion and wrong answers. Always start by saying "fix vertex $1$ as source."
>
> 2. **Missing the closing edge in the final answer:** When computing $\text{OPT}$, students frequently forget the edge $w(i, 1)$ that closes the tour. The optimal tour is a **cycle**, not a path. Always write $\text{OPT} = \min_i \{\text{dp}[V][i] + w(i, 1)\}$.
>
> 3. **Confusing $(n-1)!$ with $n!$:** Brute-force generates $(n-1)!$ permutations if you fix the starting city; for symmetric graphs, divide by $2$ to get $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours.
>
> 4. **Using brute-force complexity $O(n!)$ when asked for DP:** KTU explicitly tests whether you know the difference. Brute-force is $\Theta(n!)$, Held–Karp is $\Theta(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$. Mixing these up costs full marks.
>
> 5. **In Branch and Bound, forgetting to set row/column to $\infty$:** When including edge $(i, j)$, you must set row $i$ to $\infty$ and column $j$ to $\infty$ (and also $C[j][i] = \infty$ to prevent back-tracking). Missing this step gives an invalid subproblem.
>
> 6. **Confusing NN complexity:** Nearest Neighbor is $\Theta(n^2)$, not $\Theta(n \log n)$ — at each of $n-1$ steps, you scan up to $n$ candidates.
>
> 7. **Forgetting the triangle inequality in approximation proofs:** Christofides' 1.5-approximation and MST 2-approximation **both rely on triangle inequality**. Without it, no such guarantees exist. State this explicitly when proving.
>
> 8. **Mermaid arrows using reserved keywords:** In the exam, Mermaid isn't needed, but if writing pseudocode, never use `end` as a loop/branch terminator alone — use clear labels like `END FOR` or `END IF`.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Last-Minute KTU Exam Preparation**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **TSP:** Find shortest Hamiltonian cycle in a complete weighted graph.
- **Hamiltonian Cycle:** A closed walk visiting every vertex exactly once.
- **Symmetric vs Asymmetric:** $w(i,j) = w(j,i)$ vs directed.
- **Metric TSP:** Triangle inequality holds — enables approximation algorithms.

### 🧮 Key Recurrences & Formulas
- **Brute Force:** $(n-1)!/2$ unique tours on $n$ symmetric vertices, time $\Theta(n!)$.
- **Held–Karp DP:**
  - Base: $\text{dp}[\{1,i\}][i] = w(1,i)$.
  - Recurrence: $\text{dp}[S][i] = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} \{\text{dp}[S \setminus \{i\}][j] + w(j,i)\}$.
  - Answer: $\text{OPT} = \min_i \{\text{dp}[V][i] + w(i,1)\}$.
  - Complexity: Time $\Theta(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$, Space $\Theta(n \cdot 2^n)$.
- **Branch and Bound Lower Bound:** $\text{LB} = \sum_i r_i + \sum_j c_j$ after row + column reduction.
- **Penalty for edge $(i,j)$:** $\min_{k \ne j} C[i][k] + \min_{k \ne i} C[k][j]$.
- **MST 2-Approx:** Tour cost $\le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$ (requires triangle inequality).
- **Christofides:** Tour cost $\le 1.5 \cdot \text{OPT}$ (best known polynomial approximation for metric TSP).

### ⚙️ Algorithm Trade-offs (Memorize This Table)
- $n \le 12$: Brute Force.
- $n \le 25$: Held–Karp DP.
- $n \le 40$: Branch and Bound.
- $n > 40$ (metric): MST 2-Approx or Christofides.
- $n > 40$ (general): Heuristics like 2-opt or Lin–Kernighan.

### 📌 Conceptual Distinctions KTU Loves to Test
- **TSP vs Hamiltonian Cycle:** HCP asks *if* a HC exists (decision); TSP asks for the *cheapest* HC (optimization).
- **Exact vs Approximation:** Exact algorithms guarantee optimum but are exponential; approximation algorithms are polynomial but only guarantee a ratio.
- **NP-Hardness:** Decision TSP is NP-Complete; optimization TSP is NP-Hard. Both imply no polynomial-time algorithm exists unless P = NP.
- **AtSP Reduction:** Asymmetric TSP on $n$ vertices → Symmetric TSP on $2n$ vertices via vertex-splitting.

### 🎯 Key Mnemonics
- **"DFS twice, shortcut once":** MST 2-approx recipe.
- **"Mask plus min":** Held–Karp state = bitmask of cities + destination vertex.
- **"Reduce, branch, prune":** Branch and Bound mantra.
- **"Find nearest, go there":** Nearest Neighbor.

### ⚡ Examiner's Quick Checklist (What to Always Include)
- Show **row reduction** explicitly with summed minimums.
- Mark **all $\infty$ entries** correctly in Branch and Bound.
- Include the **closing edge $w(i,1)$** when computing OPT.
- **Justify NP-hardness** by reduction from HCP if asked.
- **State the approximation ratio** explicitly for any approximation algorithm.

### 🚨 Pitfalls Recap
- Don't forget the triangle inequality precondition for approximation bounds.
- Don't confuse $(n-1)!$ with $n!$.
- Don't use `end` as a variable or label in code/pseudocode.
- Don't write `|x|` inside markdown table cells — use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` instead.

> **Final Note:** TSP is a high-weightage topic in KTU Module 3. Mastering Held–Karp DP, Branch and Bound reductions, and at least one approximation algorithm will secure full marks in this section. Practice by hand on at least three 4×4 and one 5×5 matrix before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability Design
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Reliability Design

# Reliability Design — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Design** is a branch of algorithm/system design that deals with constructing systems (consisting of multiple components or subsystems) such that the probability of the overall system functioning correctly remains at an acceptable level, even when individual components are prone to failure. In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, it is framed as an **optimization problem** where we must allocate redundant components to subsystems to either **maximize system reliability under a cost budget** or **minimize total cost under a minimum reliability requirement**.

Mathematically, reliability $R(t)$ of a component or system is the **probability that it performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time $t$**.

For a simple component with constant failure rate $\lambda$:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> In the KTU Module 3 syllabus for Algorithm Analysis & Design, reliability is treated as a **static, time-independent probability** $0 \le r \le 1$ that a component works correctly when activated. The design problem is then a combinatorial optimization problem solved using a **greedy strategy**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Backup Generator" Intuition

Imagine you are the **chief electrical engineer** of a 5-floor hospital. Each floor has one critical life-support machine. Every machine has a **70% chance of working** on any given day. If even one machine fails, that floor's patient is in danger — the system as a whole fails.

**Option 1 (Series System):** One machine per floor → system reliability $= 0.70 \times 0.70 \times 0.70 \times 0.70 \times 0.70 \approx 0.168$ (only 16.8% chance all floors work).

**Option 2 (Parallel Backups):** Add a backup machine on each floor. Now the floor works if **at least one** of its machines works. System reliability rises dramatically.

**The Trade-off:** Each backup machine costs money. You have a limited budget. *Which floor deserves the most backups?* This is precisely the **Reliability Design Problem**.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU High-Yield Glossary)

| Term | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Component Reliability | $r_i$ | Probability that component $i$ functions |
| Subsystem Reliability | $R_i$ | Probability that subsystem $i$ as a whole functions |
| System Reliability | $R_S$ | Overall probability the entire system works |
| Cost of a Component | $c_i$ | Monetary / resource cost of one unit of component $i$ |
| Failure Probability | $q_i = 1 - r_i$ | Probability that component $i$ fails |
| Marginal Gain | $\Delta R$ | Increase in reliability by adding one more component |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Reliability Design — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Two Canonical System Configurations

### (A) Series Configuration (No Redundancy)

Components connected end-to-end. The system works **only if every component works** (single point of failure).

$$R_{\text{series}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} r_i = r_1 \times r_2 \times r_3 \times \cdots \times r_n$$

If all components have identical reliability $r$, then:

$$R_{\text{series}} = r^n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Series reliability **multiplies**, so adding weak components rapidly degrades the system. This is the *weakest-link* phenomenon.

### (B) Parallel Configuration (Full Redundancy)

Components connected in parallel. The system works if **at least one** component works. Failure requires **all** to fail.

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - r_i) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} q_i$$

For $m$ identical components each of reliability $r$:

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - r)^m = 1 - q^m$$

### (C) Series-of-Parallel Subsystems (KTU Standard Model)

The most common KTU problem structure: $n$ subsystems connected **in series**, where each subsystem $S_i$ contains $m_i$ **parallel** components. The system works iff every subsystem works.

$$R_{\text{system}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i, \quad \text{where} \quad R_i = 1 - (1 - r_i)^{m_i}$$

## 2.2 The Two Optimization Formulations

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 typically tests **Formulation 1** (maximize reliability under a cost ceiling).

### Formulation 1: Maximize Reliability Subject to Cost Constraint
- **Given:** Component reliabilities $r_i$, component costs $c_i$, maximum budget $C_{\max}$, and upper bound $m_i^{\max}$ on parallel copies in each subsystem.
- **Find:** Integer $m_i \ge 1$ for each subsystem to **maximize** $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - (1 - r_i)^{m_i}\right]$
- **Subject to:** $\sum_{i=1}^{n} m_i c_i \le C_{\max}$

### Formulation 2: Minimize Cost Subject to Reliability Constraint
- **Given:** Component reliabilities $r_i$, component costs $c_i$, minimum acceptable system reliability $R_{\min}$.
- **Find:** Integer $m_i \ge 1$ to **minimize** total cost $\sum m_i c_i$
- **Subject to:** $\prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - (1 - r_i)^{m_i}\right] \ge R_{\min}$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Expression | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| Series System | $R_S = \prod r_i$ | Components in series (chain) |
| Parallel System | $R_{\text{par}} = 1 - \prod(1-r_i)$ | Components in parallel (redundancy) |
| Identical Parallel | $R_{\text{par}} = 1 - (1-r)^m$ | $m$ copies of same component |
| Series-of-Parallel | $R_S = \prod [1 - (1-r_i)^{m_i}]$ | KTU standard problem |
| Marginal Reliability Gain | $\Delta R_{ij} = [1 - (1-r_i)^j] - [1 - (1-r_i)^{j-1}] = (1-r_i)^{j-1}\cdot r_i$ | Greedy step |
| Marginal Cost | $\Delta C = c_i$ | Greedy step |
| Efficiency Ratio | $E_{ij} = \Delta R_{ij} / c_i$ | Sort subsystems by efficiency |
| Total Cost | $C = \sum m_i c_i$ | Budget equation |
| Constant Failure Rate | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Time-based reliability |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** Reliability design is used in aerospace (aircraft redundant systems), telecommunications (multi-path routing), data centers (RAID storage), and mission-critical embedded systems.

## 2.4 The Greedy Strategy — Conceptual Logic

The KTU canonical algorithm uses a **marginal-value greedy heuristic**:

1. Start with exactly **one** component in each subsystem ($m_i = 1$ for all $i$).
2. Compute the current system reliability and cost.
3. For every possible addition (adding one more copy to any subsystem), compute the **marginal gain in reliability per unit of cost**.
4. Pick the addition with the **highest efficiency ratio** (best reliability improvement per rupee spent).
5. Update $m_i$ and repeat until the budget is exhausted or reliability target is met.

> [!NOTE]
> This greedy strategy is **not guaranteed to be globally optimal**, but it produces near-optimal solutions and is the standard method taught in KTU Module 3.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Reliability Design — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Marginal Reliability Gain for Identical Parallel Components

**Claim:** Adding the $j$-th copy of a component with reliability $r$ to a parallel bank that already has $j-1$ copies changes the bank's reliability by exactly $(1 - r)^{j-1} \cdot r$.

**Proof:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_j &\equiv R_j - R_{j-1} \\[4pt]
&= \left[1 - (1 - r)^j\right] - \left[1 - (1 - r)^{j-1}\right] \\[4pt]
&= (1 - r)^{j-1} - (1 - r)^{j} \\[4pt]
&= (1 - r)^{j-1}\left[1 - (1 - r)\right] \\[4pt]
&= (1 - r)^{j-1} \cdot r
\end{aligned}
$$

**Logical interpretation:** The new copy must work (probability $r$), and all $j-1$ existing copies must fail (probability $(1-r)^{j-1}$). The product is precisely the *incremental* reliability contribution of the $j$-th unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The marginal gain is a **strictly decreasing function of $j$**. The first backup gives the biggest boost; subsequent backups yield diminishing returns. This validates the greedy ranking.

## 3.2 Worked-Out KTU Numerical Problem (Canonical)

> **Problem (KTU-style):** Design a system with 3 subsystems in series. Component data:

| Subsystem $i$ | Component Reliability $r_i$ | Component Cost $c_i$ | Max copies $m_i^{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.70 | ₹20 | 4 |
| 2 | 0.80 | ₹30 | 5 |
| 3 | 0.90 | ₹40 | 5 |

**Budget:** $C_{\max} = 130$. **Maximize** system reliability using the greedy strategy.

### Step 1 — Initialize (one component per subsystem)

| Subsystem | $m_i$ | $R_i = 1 - (1-r_i)^{m_i}$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 1 | $1 - 0.30 = 0.7000$ | 20 |
| 2 | 1 | $1 - 0.20 = 0.8000$ | 30 |
| 3 | 1 | $1 - 0.10 = 0.9000$ | 40 |
| **Total** | — | $R_S = 0.7 \times 0.8 \times 0.9 = 0.5040$ | **90** |

**Remaining budget:** $130 - 90 = 40$.

### Step 2 — Compute marginal gains for the *first* addition (j = 2) in each subsystem

Using $\Delta R_{i,2} = (1 - r_i)^1 \cdot r_i$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_{1,2} &= (0.30)^1 \cdot 0.70 = 0.2100, \quad \Delta R / c_1 = 0.2100 / 20 = 0.01050 \\[4pt]
\Delta R_{2,2} &= (0.20)^1 \cdot 0.80 = 0.1600, \quad \Delta R / c_2 = 0.1600 / 30 \approx 0.00533 \\[4pt]
\Delta R_{3,2} &= (0.10)^1 \cdot 0.90 = 0.0900, \quad \Delta R / c_3 = 0.0900 / 40 = 0.00225
\end{aligned}
$$

**Best choice:** Subsystem 1 (highest ratio $0.01050$). Add a component there.

### Step 3 — Updated State

| Subsystem | $m_i$ | $R_i$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **2** | $1 - 0.30^2 = 0.9100$ | 40 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.8000 | 30 |
| 3 | 1 | 0.9000 | 40 |
| **Total** | — | $R_S = 0.91 \times 0.8 \times 0.9 = 0.6552$ | **110** |

**Remaining budget:** $130 - 110 = 20$.

### Step 4 — Next round: Recompute candidate marginal gains

For Subsystem 1, $j = 3$: $\Delta R_{1,3} = (0.30)^2 \cdot 0.70 = 0.0630$, ratio $= 0.0630 / 20 = 0.00315$.

For Subsystem 2, $j = 2$: $\Delta R_{2,2} = 0.1600$, ratio $= 0.1600 / 30 \approx 0.00533$ (still viable).

For Subsystem 3, $j = 2$: $\Delta R_{3,2} = 0.0900$, ratio $= 0.0900 / 40 = 0.00225$.

**Best choice:** Subsystem 2 (ratio $0.00533$). But cost 30 > remaining 20. **Skip — cannot afford.**

**Next best affordable:** Subsystem 1 (ratio $0.00315$, cost 20). Add it.

### Step 5 — Final State

| Subsystem | $m_i$ | $R_i$ | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **3** | $1 - 0.30^3 = 0.9730$ | 60 |
| 2 | 1 | 0.8000 | 30 |
| 3 | 1 | 0.9000 | 40 |
| **Total** | — | $R_S = 0.973 \times 0.8 \times 0.9 \approx 0.7006$ | **130** |

**Result:** Allocate $m_1 = 3$, $m_2 = 1$, $m_3 = 1$ for a maximum system reliability of approximately **$0.7006$** with cost exactly $130$.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Marginal Gain Decay Table

Below is the **complete marginal-gain matrix** (values per rupee of cost) for the above problem:

| Round | Subsystem 1 ($\Delta R / c_1$) | Subsystem 2 ($\Delta R / c_2$) | Subsystem 3 ($\Delta R / c_3$) | Best Pick |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Initial ($j=2$) | $0.2100/20 = 0.01050$ | $0.1600/30 = 0.00533$ | $0.0900/40 = 0.00225$ | **Sub 1** |
| Round 2 ($j=3$ for S1; $j=2$ for S2, S3) | $0.0630/20 = 0.00315$ | $0.1600/30 = 0.00533$ | $0.0900/40 = 0.00225$ | **Sub 1** (S2 unaffordable) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Reliability Design — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Series-of-Parallel System Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the canonical KTU reliability-design architecture: $n$ subsystems in series, each with $m_i$ parallel components.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputA[System Input] --> sub1
    inputB[System Input] --> sub1

    subgraph sub1[Subsystem 1 - r1]
        direction LR
        c1a[Component 1A] -.->|parallel| c1b[Component 1B]
        c1b -.->|parallel| c1c[Component 1C]
    end

    sub1 --> sub2

    subgraph sub2[Subsystem 2 - r2]
        direction LR
        c2a[Component 2A] -.->|parallel| c2b[Component 2B]
    end

    sub2 --> sub3

    subgraph sub3[Subsystem 3 - r3]
        direction LR
        c3a[Component 3A] -.->|parallel| c3b[Component 3B]
    end

    sub3 --> outA[System Output]
    outA --> outB[System Output]
```

## 4.2 Greedy Decision Flow (Algorithm Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start([Start]) --> init[Initialize: mi = 1 for all i]
    init --> compute[Compute R_S and Total Cost]
    compute --> budgetCheck{Cost less than or equal to C_max?}
    budgetCheck -- Yes --> candidates[For every feasible subsystem i, compute efficiency = delta_R over ci]
    candidates --> best[Pick subsystem with MAX efficiency]
    best --> update[Increment mi by 1]
    update --> recompute[Recompute R_i and new cost]
    recompute --> budgetCheck
    budgetCheck -- No --> output[Output final mi values and R_S]
    output --> stop([End])
```

## 4.3 Reliability vs. Number of Components (Conceptual Block Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph lowReliability[Low Redundancy - Small m]
        a1[Component 1] --- a2[Component 2]
    end

    subgraph midReliability[Medium Redundancy - Medium m]
        b1[Component 1] --- b2[Component 2]
        b2 --- b3[Component 3]
        b3 --- b4[Component 4]
    end

    subgraph highReliability[High Redundancy - Large m]
        c1[Component 1] --- c2[Component 2]
        c2 --- c3[Component 3]
        c3 --- c4[Component 4]
        c4 --- c5[Component 5]
        c5 --- c6[Component 6]
    end

    lowReliability -- "Adding components increases R but cost grows linearly" --> midReliability
    midReliability -- "Diminishing returns kick in" --> highReliability
```

> [!NOTE]
> The block schematic above conveys a critical insight: the *first* few redundant components provide large reliability gains, but as more are added, the marginal benefit shrinks — this is the **diminishing returns principle** that justifies the greedy choice.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Reliability Design — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define system reliability. Write the formula for the reliability of a system of $n$ identical components connected in series.**

**Model Answer:**
System reliability is the probability that the system performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified period of time. For $n$ identical components in series, each with reliability $r$:

$$R_{\text{series}} = r^n$$

The system works only if all components work simultaneously, so individual reliabilities multiply.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**Distinguish between series and parallel configurations in reliability design. State the formulas.**

**Model Answer:**

| Configuration | Works When | Formula (Identical Components) |
|---|---|---|
| Series | ALL components work | $R = r^n$ |
| Parallel | AT LEAST ONE works | $R = 1 - (1-r)^m$ |

Parallel systems are more reliable than series systems of equal cost because redundancy provides fault-tolerance.

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

### Question A — 14 Marks **[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model]**

> A system consists of **3 subsystems in series**. Each subsystem is built from identical components connected in parallel. The component data is given below. The total budget is **₹160**. Use the **greedy strategy** to allocate redundant components so that the system reliability is maximized.

| Subsystem $i$ | $r_i$ | $c_i$ | $m_i^{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.80 | ₹20 | 6 |
| 2 | 0.85 | ₹30 | 4 |
| 3 | 0.90 | ₹50 | 3 |

#### (a) [7 Marks] Set up the problem, initialize the system, and identify the first allocation using the greedy ratio.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Initialize:** $m_1 = m_2 = m_3 = 1$.

$$
R_1 = 0.80, \quad R_2 = 0.85, \quad R_3 = 0.90
$$

$$
R_S = 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.6120
$$

**Initial cost:** $20 + 30 + 50 = 100$. **Remaining budget:** $160 - 100 = 60$.

**Step 2 — Compute marginal gains for first addition ($j = 2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_{1,2} &= (0.20)(0.80) = 0.160, & \text{ratio} &= 0.160/20 = 0.00800 \\
\Delta R_{2,2} &= (0.15)(0.85) = 0.1275, & \text{ratio} &= 0.1275/30 = 0.00425 \\
\Delta R_{3,2} &= (0.10)(0.90) = 0.090, & \text{ratio} &= 0.090/50 = 0.00180
\end{aligned}
$$

**Greedy pick:** Subsystem 1 (highest ratio $0.00800$). Set $m_1 = 2$. Cost now $120$, remaining $40$.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Continue the greedy process until the budget is exhausted and report the final reliability.

**Model Solution:**

**Round 2 — feasible candidates** (with $m_1 = 2$):

For Subsystem 1, $j = 3$: $\Delta R_{1,3} = (0.20)^2(0.80) = 0.032$, ratio $= 0.032/20 = 0.00160$.

For Subsystem 2, $j = 2$: $\Delta R_{2,2} = 0.1275$, ratio $= 0.1275/30 = 0.00425$ (cost 30 ≤ 40, **affordable**).

For Subsystem 3, $j = 2$: $\Delta R_{3,2} = 0.090$, ratio $= 0.090/50 = 0.00180$ (cost 50 > 40, **skip**).

**Greedy pick:** Subsystem 2 (ratio $0.00425$). Set $m_2 = 2$. Cost now $150$, remaining $10$.

**Round 3 — feasible candidates** (with $m_1 = 2$, $m_2 = 2$):

For Subsystem 1, $j = 3$: ratio $= 0.00160$, cost 20 > 10 → **skip**.

For Subsystem 2, $j = 3$: $\Delta R_{2,3} = (0.15)^2(0.85) = 0.019125$, ratio $= 0.019125/30 = 0.00064$, cost 30 > 10 → **skip**.

No feasible addition remains.

**Final state:** $m_1 = 2$, $m_2 = 2$, $m_3 = 1$.

$$
R_1 = 1 - 0.20^2 = 0.96, \quad R_2 = 1 - 0.15^2 = 0.9775, \quad R_3 = 0.90
$$

$$
R_S = 0.96 \times 0.9775 \times 0.90 \approx 0.8470
$$

**Final answer:** Maximum system reliability $\approx \mathbf{0.8470}$ at a total cost of ₹150.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks **[Alternative Choice]**

> A communication system has **4 subsystems in series**. Component data:

| Subsystem $i$ | $r_i$ | $c_i$ | $m_i^{\max}$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 0.75 | ₹15 | 5 |
| 2 | 0.80 | ₹25 | 4 |
| 3 | 0.85 | ₹35 | 3 |
| 4 | 0.90 | ₹45 | 3 |

**Budget:** ₹200.

#### (a) [7 Marks] Formulate the optimization problem mathematically and compute the initial state.

**Model Solution:**

**Formulation:** Maximize $R_S = \prod_{i=1}^{4}\left[1 - (1 - r_i)^{m_i}\right]$ subject to $\sum m_i c_i \le 200$ and $1 \le m_i \le m_i^{\max}$.

**Initial state** ($m_i = 1$):

$$
R_S^{(0)} = 0.75 \times 0.80 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 = 0.4590
$$

Initial cost = $15 + 25 + 35 + 45 = 120$. Remaining budget = ₹80.

#### (b) [7 Marks] Execute the greedy algorithm and present the final allocation.

**Model Solution:**

**Round 1 — first addition candidates ($j = 2$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\Delta R_1/c_1 &= (0.25)(0.75)/15 = 0.1875/15 = 0.01250 \\
\Delta R_2/c_2 &= (0.20)(0.80)/25 = 0.1600/25 = 0.00640 \\
\Delta R_3/c_3 &= (0.15)(0.85)/35 = 0.1275/35 \approx 0.00364 \\
\Delta R_4/c_4 &= (0.10)(0.90)/45 = 0.0900/45 = 0.00200
\end{aligned}
$$

**Pick:** Sub 1 → $m_1 = 2$. Cost = 135, remaining = 65.

**Round 2 — recompute relevant ratios:**

Sub 1 ($j=3$): $\Delta R = (0.25)^2(0.75) = 0.046875$, ratio = $0.046875/15 \approx 0.00313$.

Sub 2 ($j=2$): ratio $= 0.00640$ (cost 25 ≤ 65, affordable).

**Pick:** Sub 2 → $m_2 = 2$. Cost = 160, remaining = 40.

**Round 3:**

Sub 1 ($j=3$): ratio $\approx 0.00313$, cost 15 → affordable.

Sub 2 ($j=3$): $\Delta R = (0.20)^2(0.80) = 0.032$, ratio $= 0.032/25 = 0.00128$.

**Pick:** Sub 1 → $m_1 = 3$. Cost = 175, remaining = 25.

**Round 4:**

Sub 1 ($j=4$): $\Delta R = (0.25)^3(0.75) = 0.01172$, ratio = $0.01172/15 \approx 0.00078$.

Sub 2 ($j=3$): ratio $= 0.00128$, cost 25 → affordable.

Sub 3 ($j=2$): ratio $\approx 0.00364$, cost 35 > 25 → skip.

**Pick:** Sub 2 → $m_2 = 3$. Cost = 200, remaining = 0. **STOP.**

**Final allocation:** $m_1 = 3$, $m_2 = 3$, $m_3 = 1$, $m_4 = 1$.

$$
R_1 = 1 - 0.25^3 = 0.984375, \quad R_2 = 1 - 0.20^3 = 0.992, \quad R_3 = 0.85, \quad R_4 = 0.90
$$

$$
R_S = 0.984375 \times 0.992 \times 0.85 \times 0.90 \approx \mathbf{0.7153}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to update ratios after each round** — students often re-use the first-round ratios for all subsequent additions. Always recompute $\Delta R_{ij} = (1-r_i)^{j-1} \cdot r_i$ for the new $j$.
> 2. **Confusing marginal gain with total reliability** — the greedy decision is based on the *increment* of reliability, not the subsystem's current value.
> 3. **Ignoring the $m_i^{\max}$ cap** — adding beyond the maximum is mechanically meaningless and loses marks.
> 4. **Mixing up efficiency ratio** — it is *always* $\Delta R / \Delta C$ where $\Delta C = c_i$ (per-unit cost), not cumulative cost.
> 5. **Off-by-one in initialization** — some students start with $m_i = 0$, which is invalid because each subsystem must contain at least one component.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Series reliability** multiplies: $R = \prod r_i$ (weakest-link behavior).
- **Parallel reliability** uses complement: $R = 1 - \prod(1 - r_i)$ (redundancy benefit).
- **Series-of-parallel** is the standard KTU problem: $R_S = \prod [1 - (1 - r_i)^{m_i}]$.
- **Marginal gain identity:** $\Delta R_{ij} = (1 - r_i)^{j-1} \cdot r_i$ — memorize this formula.
- **Efficiency ratio** = $\Delta R_{ij} / c_i$; the greedy step picks the maximum.
- **Diminishing returns:** marginal gains decrease as $j$ increases.
- **Algorithm complexity:** The greedy loop runs $O(K)$ times where $K$ is the number of budget units spent; each round scans $O(n)$ subsystems.
- **Trade-off duality:** Maximize reliability under cost ↔ Minimize cost under reliability. Both are solved by analogous greedy logic.
- **Always** reset $m_i \ge 1$ and **always** respect $m_i \le m_i^{\max}$.
- **Compute reliabilities to 4 decimal places** — KTU valuations penalize low precision.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Backtracking and Branch and Bound: Backtracking - General Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Backtracking and Branch and Bound: Backtracking - General Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Board-Approved Terminology)**
> **Backtracking** is an *algorithmic technique* for solving *combinatorial optimization* and *constraint satisfaction problems* by **incrementally building candidates** to the solution(s) and **abandoning (pruning) a candidate** as soon as it is determined that the candidate **cannot possibly lead to a valid solution**.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme course **PECAT415 – Algorithm Analysis and Design**, backtracking is classified under the family of *problem-solving strategies* that systematically enumerate a set of candidate solutions using a **depth-first search** on a virtual tree-like structure called the **State Space Tree (SST)**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why is it called "Backtracking"?**
> Because the algorithm physically **reverses (backtracks)** from a "dead-end" sub-tree to its parent node to try a different branch — analogous to a hiker walking a maze.

### Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for a First-Time Reader)

Imagine you are standing at the entrance of a **dark maze** with multiple corridors. You can only walk forward and you carry **one candle**:

1. You walk down **Corridor A** (choose a value for variable $x_1$).
2. At every junction, you **check the rules of the maze** (e.g., no red walls, never walk into fire). This check is the **Constraint Function**.
3. If Corridor A leads to a **dead end** (constraint violated downstream), you do not continue. You **turn back, retrace your steps to the last junction**, and try **Corridor B** (increment $x_1$ to next valid value).
4. You continue this DFS-like search until you either find the **treasure (solution)** or determine that **no treasure exists** in this maze.

This "try → fail → retreat → try next" loop is the *heart* of backtracking.

> [!TIP]
> **Three KTU Board-Favorite Keywords you MUST remember:**
> 1. **State Space Tree (SST)** – the virtual tree representing all candidate solutions.
> 2. **Bounding Function (Promising Function)** – a Boolean test that decides if a node is worth exploring.
> 3. **E-node (Expansion Node)** – the currently active node from which children are being generated.

### Key Standard Metrics (as per KTU 2024 Scheme)

- **Time Metric** – typically expressed in terms of $n$ (size of problem) and the **branching factor** $b$.
- **Space Metric** – $O(b \cdot m)$ where $m$ is the maximum depth of the recursion stack.
- **Search Strategy** – Depth-First Search (DFS) with pruning.
- **State of a node** – one of three: **Live**, **E-node (Expansion)**, or **Dead**.

### Formal Definition of Bounding Function (Promising Function)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A node in the state space tree is said to be **promising** if it **has not been terminated** by the bounding function and is therefore a candidate for further exploration. The function that decides this is the **Promising Function** (also called **Bounding Function** $B(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k)$).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operational Concept — Step-by-Step Logic

The **general method** of backtracking, as prescribed by KTU 2024 syllabus and aligned with the standard reference treatment of the topic, follows this structured sequence:

1. **Problem Modelling:** The given problem $P$ is represented as finding all (or one) tuples $(x_1, x_2, x_3, \dots, x_n)$ that satisfy a **constraint set** $\mathcal{C}$ and an **objective function** $\mathcal{F}$.
2. **State Space Tree Construction:** A virtual tree is constructed where:
   - The **root** represents the state before any decision is made.
   - Each **level $i$** corresponds to assigning a value to variable $x_i$.
   - Each **branch** represents a possible value assignment to a variable.
3. **DFS Traversal with Bounding:** The algorithm performs a **DFS** on this tree. At each node:
   - The **bounding function** $B$ is applied.
   - If $B$ returns **FALSE**, the node is declared a **Dead Node** and all its descendants are pruned.
   - If $B$ returns **TRUE**, the node is **promising** and its children are generated.
4. **Solution Check:** When a node reaches depth $n$ (leaf level) and satisfies all constraints, it is reported as a **solution**.
5. **Backtrack:** On reaching a dead end, recursion unwinds to the most recent promising ancestor.

### 2.2 Why and How — The Reasoning Behind Each Step

| Step | Why It Is Done | How It Is Done |
|---|---|---|
| Model as tuple search | Allows systematic enumeration | Define $x_i$ as decision variable $i$ |
| Build SST | Provides structured search space | Each node $\leftrightarrow$ partial tuple $(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ |
| Apply Bounding $B$ | **Pruning** — avoids exponential blowup | Returns FALSE if constraints cannot be met |
| DFS | Low memory footprint | Recursion or explicit stack |
| Backtrack | Explores alternative branches | Pops current $x_i$ and increments it |

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exams (Dec 2024 / May 2025 pattern)**

| Concept | Symbol / Formula | Meaning / KTU Board Definition |
|---|---|---|
| State Space Tree | $T = (V, E)$ | Virtual tree of all partial tuples |
| Bounding Function | $B(x_1, \dots, x_k) \in \{\text{TRUE, FALSE}\}$ | Returns TRUE if node can lead to a solution |
| Promising Node | $B = \text{TRUE}$ | Worth exploring further |
| Non-Promising Node | $B = \text{FALSE}$ | Pruned — leads to no solution |
| E-node | Expansion Node | Currently active node whose children are being generated |
| Live Node | $L$ | Generated but not yet expanded |
| Dead Node | $D$ | Cannot be expanded (failed or solution found) |
| Branching Factor | $b$ | Max number of children per node |
| Max Depth of SST | $n$ | Number of variables in tuple |
| Worst-Case Time | $O(b^n)$ | All nodes generated (no pruning) |
| Best-Case Time | $O(n \cdot b)$ | Pruning reduces to a single path |
| Space Complexity | $O(b \cdot m)$ | $m$ = maximum depth of recursion |
| Recurrence for $T(n, b)$ | $T(n,b) = b \cdot T(n-1, b) + O(b)$ | Worst-case tree traversal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Examiner's Tip:** Whenever a question asks for the **time complexity of backtracking**, do **not** blindly write $O(b^n)$. Instead, present the analysis as:
> *"In the worst case (no pruning), the algorithm visits every node of the state space tree, giving a complexity of $O(b^n)$. With an effective bounding function, this is drastically reduced to the number of promising nodes, which is problem-dependent."*

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **VLSI Design:** Routing wires on a chip board subject to layer constraints.
- **Robotics & AI:** Path planning, Sudoku solvers, and game-tree search (chess engines use backtracking with $\alpha$-$\beta$ pruning).
- **Bioinformatics:** Sequence alignment and genome assembly.
- **Operations Research:** Solving the **Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP)**.
- **Cryptography:** Subset-sum style attacks and key search.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the General Backtracking Algorithm

We model the problem as: find **all** tuples $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ from domains $S_1, S_2, \dots, S_n$ such that the constraint set $\mathcal{C}(x_1, \dots, x_n)$ is satisfied.

**Step 1 — Define the bounding function $B_k(x_1, \dots, x_k)$** at level $k$ of the SST.

$$
B_k(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) = \begin{cases} \text{TRUE}, & \text{if } (x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k) \text{ can lead to a valid solution} \\ \text{FALSE}, & \text{otherwise} \end{cases}
$$

*Reason:* The bounding function encodes the constraints and the partial feasibility test. For example, in the **4-Queens problem**, $B_k$ is TRUE if no two queens placed in columns $1$ through $k$ attack each other.

**Step 2 — Recursive Backtracking Procedure (formal derivation).**

Let $T(k)$ be the procedure called when we are at level $k$ of the SST.

$$
T(k): \quad \text{for } x_k \in S_k \text{ in increasing order do}
$$

$$
\quad \text{if } B_k(x_1, \dots, x_k) = \text{TRUE then}
$$

$$
\qquad \text{if } (x_1, \dots, x_k) \text{ is a solution then} \;\; \text{output it}
$$

$$
\qquad T(k+1)
$$

*Logic explanation:*
- Line 1: Try each possible value for $x_k$ from its domain.
- Line 2: **Prune** any value that violates the bounding function — this is the *pruning condition* that makes backtracking efficient.
- Line 3: If a complete solution of depth $k = n$ is reached, **report it**.
- Line 4: **Recurse** to level $k+1$ — this is the DFS expansion step.

**Step 3 — Iterative (Explicit Stack) Formulation.**

$$
\text{Initialize: } k \leftarrow 1, \;\; \text{stack } \leftarrow \emptyset
$$

$$
\text{while } k \neq 0 \text{ do}
$$

$$
\quad \text{while } (x_k \text{ exists in } S_k \text{ and } B_k = \text{FALSE}) \text{ do } x_k \leftarrow \text{next value}
$$

$$
\quad \text{if } x_k \text{ exists then}
$$

$$
\qquad \text{if } (x_1, \dots, x_k) \text{ is solution then output it}
$$

$$
\qquad k \leftarrow k+1 \;\; \text{(go deeper — DFS expansion)}
$$

$$
\quad \text{else } k \leftarrow k-1 \;\; \text{(backtrack — pop the stack)}
$$

*Logic explanation:* This iterative form mirrors the recursive version but uses an **explicit stack** to keep track of the current path. When no further value of $x_k$ works, the algorithm **pops** back to level $k-1$ — this is the literal "back-tracking" step.

**Step 4 — Bounding Function Example (4-Queens, level $k$).**

For two queens placed at $(i, p_i)$ and $(k, p_k)$:

$$
B_k = \text{TRUE} \iff p_i \neq p_k \;\;\text{and}\;\; \vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert
$$

*Logic explanation:*
- $p_i \neq p_k$ ensures **no two queens share a column**.
- $\vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert$ ensures **no two queens share a diagonal**.

### 3.2 Time Complexity Derivation (Worst-Case State Space Tree)

Let $T(n)$ be the number of nodes generated for a tree of depth $n$ with branching factor $b$.

$$
T(n) = b \cdot T(n-1) + \Theta(1)
$$

**Step 1 — Expand the recurrence:**

$$
T(n) = b \cdot [b \cdot T(n-2) + \Theta(1)] + \Theta(1)
$$

$$
T(n) = b^2 \cdot T(n-2) + b \cdot \Theta(1) + \Theta(1)
$$

**Step 2 — Continue expansion $k$ times:**

$$
T(n) = b^k \cdot T(n-k) + \sum_{i=0}^{k-1} b^i \cdot \Theta(1)
$$

**Step 3 — Set $k = n$ and use $T(0) = 1$:**

$$
T(n) = b^n \cdot 1 + \sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b^i
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the geometric series:**

$$
\sum_{i=0}^{n-1} b^i = \frac{b^n - 1}{b - 1} \quad \text{for } b > 1
$$

**Step 5 — Combine:**

$$
T(n) = b^n + \frac{b^n - 1}{b - 1} = b^n \left(1 + \frac{1}{b-1}\right) - \frac{1}{b-1}
$$

**Step 6 — Asymptotic bound:**

$$
T(n) = \Theta(b^n)
$$

*Final result:* The **worst-case time complexity** of the general backtracking algorithm is $O(b^n)$, matching the KTU 2024 board-exam expected answer. With effective pruning, this is reduced to $O(P(n))$, where $P(n)$ is the number of promising (live + E-nodes) nodes.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Recursive vs. Iterative Form

| Feature | Recursive Form | Iterative Form |
|---|---|---|
| Data Structure | Call Stack | Explicit Stack |
| Space Overhead | Compiler-managed | Programmer-managed |
| Readability | High (closer to math) | Moderate |
| Risk of Stack Overflow | Yes (deep recursion) | No (controlled depth) |
| KTU Board Preference | Yes (commonly asked) | Sometimes asked in Part B |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart — General Backtracking Algorithm Control Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: k = 1] --> B{Pick next x_k from S_k}
    B --> C{Bounding Function B_k = TRUE?}
    C -- No, all values exhausted --> D[Backtrack: k = k - 1]
    D --> E{k = 0?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Stop: All solutions explored]
    E -- No --> B
    C -- Yes --> G{Is x_1..x_k a complete solution?}
    G -- Yes --> H[Record / Output Solution]
    H --> I[Continue: try next x_k]
    I --> B
    G -- No --> J[Go Deeper: k = k + 1]
    J --> B
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Architectural View of Backtracking Engine

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph InputLayer["INPUT LAYER"]
        I1[Problem Instance: n, constraints]
    end
    subgraph DecisionLayer["DECISION LAYER"]
        D1[State Space Tree Generator]
        D2[Variable Selector]
    end
    subgraph PruningLayer["PRUNING LAYER"]
        P1[Bounding Function B_k]
        P2[Constraint Checker]
    end
    subgraph ControlLayer["CONTROL LAYER"]
        C1[DFS Engine]
        C2[Backtrack Controller]
    end
    subgraph OutputLayer["OUTPUT LAYER"]
        O1[Solution Recorder]
        O2[Result Buffer]
    end
    I1 --> D1
    D1 --> C1
    C1 --> D2
    D2 --> P1
    P1 -- Promising TRUE --> C1
    P1 -- Non-Promising FALSE --> C2
    P1 --> P2
    C2 -- Backtrack --> C1
    C1 --> O1
    O1 --> O2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid State Space Tree — Generic Backtracking Search (Depth 3, Branching 2)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R((Root: empty)) --> N1((x1 = a))
    R --> N2((x1 = b))
    N1 --> N11((x2 = a))
    N1 --> N12((x2 = b))
    N2 --> N21((x2 = a))
    N2 --> N22((x2 = b))
    N11 --> N111((x3 = a))
    N11 --> N112((x3 = b))
    N12 --> N121((x3 = a))
    N12 --> N122((x3 = b))
    N21 --> N211((x3 = a))
    N21 --> N212((x3 = b))
    N22 --> N221((x3 = a))
    N22 --> N222((x3 = b))
    N12 -.- X1[Pruned: non-promising]:::dead
    N22 -.- X2[Pruned: non-promising]:::dead
    N211 -.- X3[Solution: found]:::sol
    classDef dead fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#cc0000,color:#000
    classDef sol fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#006600,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation (KTU Board Exam Ready):**
> - **Solid arrows** represent the *DFS exploration path*.
> - **Dotted (dashed) edges** represent nodes marked as **Dead** by the bounding function.
> - **Green node** marks a *valid solution* that gets recorded in the output.
> - The algorithm visits only the **promising** sub-tree, skipping pruned sub-trees entirely.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> **Mark Distribution Reference (as per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE Pattern):**
> - **Part A** — 2 questions × **3 marks** = 6 marks
> - **Part B** — Internal choice × **1 question × 14 marks** = 14 marks
> - **Total for this topic block:** 20 marks (Module-level)
> - Bloom's Levels covered: **L2 (Understand)**, **L3 (Apply)**, **L4 (Analyze)**

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Define backtracking. List any two problems that can be solved using backtracking.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (2 marks):** Backtracking is an algorithmic technique for solving constraint satisfaction and combinatorial problems by building candidates incrementally and abandoning a candidate as soon as it is determined that the candidate cannot be completed to a valid solution.
>
> **Two problems (1 mark):**
> 1. **N-Queens problem** – placing $N$ queens on an $N \times N$ chessboard such that no two attack each other.
> 2. **Sum of Subsets problem** – finding subsets of a given set whose sum equals a target value $W$.
>
> *(Other valid problems: Graph Coloring, Hamiltonian Cycle, 0/1 Knapsack, Sudoku, m-Coloring.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**What is a state space tree? Explain the terms: E-node, Live node, and Dead node.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **State Space Tree (1 mark):** A state space tree is a virtual tree representation of all possible states of the problem from the initial state (root) to a final/solution state (leaf). Each node represents a partial solution $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_k)$.
>
> **Node types (2 marks):**
> - **Live Node:** A node that has been generated but whose children are yet to be generated.
> - **E-node (Expansion Node):** A live node whose children are currently being explored (generated) by the algorithm.
> - **Dead Node:** A node that has been determined to be non-promising by the bounding function (or has no further children to explore). It is *pruned* from the search.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - Students often **confuse "Backtracking" with "Branch and Bound."** Board answer: Backtracking uses a **simple TRUE/FALSE bounding function** (feasibility), whereas Branch and Bound uses an **optimization bound** (e.g., $c(x) \leq$ best-known).
> - Do **not skip** the bound/constraint formulation — examiners allocate **2–3 marks** for it alone.
> - Always **draw the State Space Tree** in your answer. Missing diagram = loss of **at least 2 marks**.
> - When asked for "general method," include both the **recursive AND iterative** algorithm forms.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — General Method, Recursive Form & 4-Queens Bounding**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**

**(a)** Explain the **general method of backtracking** with a suitable algorithm. Use a clear example to define the bounding function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Apply the backtracking technique to solve the **4-Queens problem**. Show the **state space tree** until the first solution is found, and list the **bounding function** used. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Problem modelling (1 mark):** The problem $P$ is expressed as finding one or all tuples $(x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n)$ from domains $S_i$ satisfying a constraint predicate $\mathcal{C}$.

2. **State Space Tree (SST) construction (1 mark):** Each level $k$ of the SST corresponds to a choice for $x_k$. The root is the empty tuple. A node at depth $k$ represents $(x_1, \dots, x_k)$.

3. **Bounding Function (2 marks):** A Boolean function $B_k(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ that returns TRUE if the partial tuple can be extended to a solution, FALSE otherwise. A node where $B_k = \text{FALSE}$ is **non-promising** and is pruned.

4. **Recursive Algorithm (2 marks):**

$$
\text{Algorithm: BACKTRACK}(k)
$$

$$
\text{for each } x_k \in S_k \text{ (in order) do}
$$

$$
\quad \text{check } B_k(x_1, \dots, x_k) \rightarrow \text{TRUE?}
$$

$$
\quad \text{if TRUE then}
$$

$$
\qquad \text{if } (x_1, \dots, x_k) \text{ is a valid solution then } \text{output it}
$$

$$
\qquad \text{else } \text{BACKTRACK}(k+1)
$$

5. **Example — Sum of Subsets (1 mark):** Given a set of $n$ weights and a target $W$, find subsets summing to $W$. Here $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ (include or exclude weight $w_i$), and the bound is $\sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i \cdot x_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} w_i \leq W$.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Problem statement (1 mark):** Place 4 queens on a $4 \times 4$ chessboard so that no two attack each other. Each queen is in a different row, so $x_i$ = column of queen in row $i$.

2. **Bounding function (2 marks):**

$$
B_k: \quad p_i \neq p_k \;\;\text{and}\;\; \vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert \;\;\text{for all } i < k
$$

- $p_i \neq p_k$ — no two queens in same column.
- $\vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert$ — no two queens on same diagonal.

3. **State Space Tree (Partial, until first solution) (3 marks):**

```
Root
├── x1 = 1
│   ├── x2 = 3   (B2: 1≠3, |1-2|≠|1-3| → 1≠2 ✓)
│   │   ├── x3 = ? → all fail
│   │   └── (backtrack)
│   └── x2 = 4
│       ├── x3 = 2
│       │   └── x4 = ? → solution: (1, 3, ?, ?) fails
```

**Final first solution:** $(x_1, x_2, x_3, x_4) = (2, 4, 1, 3)$ — queens at rows 1,2,3,4 in columns 2,4,1,3.

4. **Verification (1 mark):**
- All $p_i$ distinct: $\{2, 4, 1, 3\}$ ✓
- No diagonal clashes: check $\vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert$ for all pairs ✓

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Bounding Function, Efficiency & Comparison**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**(a)** Define **bounding function (promising function)**. State its role in backtracking. Give the bounding function for the **Sum of Subsets** problem. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Compare **Backtracking and Branch and Bound** techniques. Show, with a small example, how a state space tree is pruned. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 marks:**

1. **Definition (2 marks):**
The bounding function $B_k(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ is a Boolean predicate that returns TRUE if the partial assignment $(x_1, \dots, x_k)$ can be extended to a complete solution satisfying all constraints. A node is called **promising** if $B_k = \text{TRUE}$, and **non-promising** (dead) if $B_k = \text{FALSE}$.

2. **Role in Backtracking (2 marks):**
   - **Prunes** entire sub-trees that cannot lead to a solution.
   - **Reduces** the search space from exponential $O(b^n)$ to a (potentially) polynomial number of nodes.
   - Acts as the **decision gate** for whether to expand a node further.

3. **Bounding Function for Sum of Subsets (3 marks):**

Let $w_1, w_2, \dots, w_n$ be weights (sorted in non-decreasing order) and $W$ the target sum. Let $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$.

$$
B_k = \text{TRUE} \iff \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^{n} w_i \geq W
$$

*Equivalent formulation:* the current sum + the sum of all remaining weights must be at least $W$. If not, this branch can never reach $W$, so prune.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 marks:**

1. **Comparison Table (3 marks):**

| Feature | Backtracking | Branch and Bound |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Find **feasible** solution(s) | Find **optimal** solution |
| Bounding | Feasibility check | Cost bound (e.g., $c(x) \leq$ best) |
| Search Order | DFS (always) | DFS, BFS, or Best-First |
| Solution Type | Constraint satisfaction | Optimization (min/max) |
| Examples | N-Queens, m-Coloring | 0/1 Knapsack, TSP, Assignment |

2. **Pruning Example (4 marks):** Consider a 0/1 Knapsack with $n=3$ items, weights $(2, 3, 4)$, profits $(40, 50, 60)$, capacity $W = 5$.

State space tree (DFS, include = 1, exclude = 0):

```
Root (0,0,0)
├── x1=1 (profit=40, weight=2)
│   ├── x2=0 → node weight=2 ≤ 5, OK, descend
│   │   └── x3=0 → solution (40, weight 2)
│   └── x2=1 → weight=5 ≤ 5, OK
│       └── x3=0 → solution (90, weight 5)
└── x1=0
    └── x2=1 (weight 3)
        └── x3=1 → weight 7 > 5 → **PRUNED** ✂
```

**Best solution:** Profit $= 90$ with selected items $\{1, 2\}$.

In **Branch and Bound**, an additional bound is computed using the fractional knapsack upper bound; any node whose upper bound $\leq$ current best is pruned.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist (Pin this before exam day!)**

- [x] **Backtracking** = DFS on a **State Space Tree** with **pruning via bounding function**.
- [x] **Bounding function $B_k$** returns TRUE (promising) or FALSE (non-promising / dead).
- [x] **Three node types:** Live, E-node (Expansion), Dead.
- [x] **General recursive skeleton:** `for x_k in S_k: if B_k: recurse(k+1)`.
- [x] **Iterative version** uses an explicit stack and backtracks by popping.
- [x] **Worst-case time:** $O(b^n)$ — but effective bounding can reduce this drastically.
- [x] **Space complexity:** $O(b \cdot m)$ where $m$ = max recursion depth.
- [x] **Backtracking vs. Branch and Bound:**
  - Backtracking → **Feasibility** predicate (TRUE/FALSE)
  - Branch and Bound → **Cost-based** pruning using an upper/lower bound
- [x] **Classic KTU problems:** N-Queens, Sum of Subsets, Graph (m-)Coloring, Hamiltonian Cycle, 0/1 Knapsack.
- [x] **Bounding function for 4-Queens:** $p_i \neq p_k$ AND $\vert i - k \vert \neq \vert p_i - p_k \vert$.
- [x] **Always draw the State Space Tree** in your answer — it is worth **2–3 marks** in KTU Part B.
- [x] **Bounding function for Sum of Subsets:** current sum + sum of all remaining weights $\geq W$.
- [x] **Algorithm trace should show:** generation → bounding check → prune/backtrack → solution output.
- [x] **Best-first search is a Branch-and-Bound technique** — NOT a backtracking technique.
- [x] **Recursion tree** is essentially identical to the **State Space Tree** in backtracking literature.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Board Exam Mantra:**
> *"Draw the tree. State the bound. Trace the DFS. Mark the dead nodes. Record the solution. Compute the complexity."* — Following this 6-step ritual in every backtracking question guarantees full marks on **Module 4** of **PECAT415**.

topics.title: N-Queen Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
