<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **N-Queen Problem (KTU 2024 Scheme Definition)**
> The **N-Queen Problem** is a classical *constraint satisfaction* and *combinatorial optimization* problem in which the objective is to place **N queens** on an **N × N chessboard** such that **no two queens attack each other**. Since a queen attacks along its row, column, and both diagonals, the solution requires that no two queens share the same row, column, or diagonal. The problem is the canonical example used to teach **Backtracking** as a complete search algorithm design strategy.

The N-Queen problem belongs to the broader class of **NP-Hard** problems (decision version) in its general form. For small N (typically N ≤ 14), backtracking with constraint propagation solves it efficiently. For larger N, heuristic and search-based techniques are employed.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Mapping (Module 4 – Backtracking Strategy):**
> The N-Queen problem is listed in Module 4 of PECAT415 as the **primary illustration** of the backtracking paradigm. Students are expected to understand the recursive formulation, trace the state-space tree, and derive the asymptotic complexity.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hosting **N very powerful queens** for a royal banquet in an **N × N hall**. Each queen refuses to sit on the same row, same column, or same diagonal as another queen — because she will *immediately launch a chess attack* on the other. Your task as the host is to find seating arrangements where **all N queens are placed safely**.

Now consider the host's strategy:
1. Seat **one queen per row** (a smart simplification — if no two share a row, the row-conflict is automatically eliminated).
2. Try column positions row by row.
3. As soon as a queen threatens another, **backtrack** — undo the last placement and try the next column.

This "try, fail, undo, try again" methodology is precisely what the **Backtracking Algorithm** does for the N-Queen problem. It is *organized exhaustive search* with intelligent early termination.

## 1.3 Why Study the N-Queen Problem?

| Domain | Utility |
|--------|---------|
| **Algorithm Pedagogy** | Canonical example for backtracking, recursion, and pruning |
| **Artificial Intelligence** | Foundation for CSP (Constraint Satisfaction Problems) solvers |
| **Operations Research** | Models scheduling, placement, and assignment problems |
| **VLSI Design** | Used in routing and component placement heuristics |
| **Parallel Computing** | Solved in parallel for large N using GPU/distributed search |

> [!TIP]
> **Key Insight:** The N-Queen problem is **not solved by a greedy method** because placing queens locally optimally (e.g., minimum attack) does not guarantee a global solution. Backtracking's *systematic enumeration with pruning* is required.

## 1.4 Standard Terminology (KTU Board-Examiner Vocabulary)

- **State Space Tree (SST):** The tree representing all partial queen placements during search.
- **Live Node:** A node whose children are yet to be explored.
- **E-Node (Expansion Node):** The live node currently being expanded.
- **Dead Node:** A node that cannot lead to a solution (constraint violated).
- **Bounding Function:** The function that tests whether a partial placement is still safe — **the core pruning mechanism** in N-Queen.
- **Solution Node:** A leaf node in the SST representing a complete valid placement.
- **Chromatic Variant / Mod-N form:** An advanced version where queens are placed such that each row, column, and diagonal contains exactly one queen and the queens form specific patterns.

## 1.5 Definition of Attack Constraints (Three Rules)

A queen at position `(row, col)` will attack another queen at `(r2, c2)` if and only if any of the following holds:

$$
\text{row}_1 = \text{row}_2 \quad \text{(same row)}
$$

$$
\text{col}_1 = \text{col}_2 \quad \text{(same column)}
$$

$$
\vert \text{row}_1 - \text{row}_2 \vert = \vert \text{col}_1 - \text{col}_2 \vert \quad \text{(same diagonal)}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often forget the **diagonal condition**. The diagonal attack condition is *NOT* `row₁ − row₂ = col₁ − col₂` alone — it is the **absolute-value equality**. Always use `|Δrow| == |Δcol|`.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Decomposition of the Backtracking Algorithm

The N-Queen solver operates in **five structured logical phases**:

### Phase 1 — Initialization
- A global 2D array `board[N][N]` is initialized with `0` (empty).
- An integer `row = 0` indicates the current row under consideration.
- An empty solution list is initialized.

### Phase 2 — Recursive Placement
- The recursive procedure `solveNQueens(row)` is invoked starting from `row = 0`.
- **Base Case:** If `row == N`, a valid placement has been found; record and print the solution.

### Phase 3 — Column Iteration with Constraint Check
- For each column `col` from `0` to `N−1`:
  - Call the **isSafe(row, col)** bounding function.
  - If safe → place the queen (`board[row][col] = 1`), recurse on `row + 1`.
  - After recursion returns, **backtrack** by resetting `board[row][col] = 0`.

### Phase 4 — Bounding (isSafe) Function
The `isSafe` function checks three conditions against all previously placed queens (rows `0` to `row − 1`):

- **Column Clash:** No queen in any earlier row has the same column.
- **Left Diagonal Clash:** `(row − i) != (col − placedCol[i])`.
- **Right Diagonal Clash:** `(row − i) != (placedCol[i] − col)`.

### Phase 5 — Solution Accumulation and Termination
- Each complete placement (all N queens placed safely) is appended to the solution vector.
- The recursion unwinds when the entire tree has been traversed.

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Each Phase

| Phase | Why it Exists |
|-------|---------------|
| **Initialization** | Establishes global state; the 2D board is the visualization surface for placement. |
| **Recursive Placement** | Naturally mirrors the row-by-row decision tree; recursion eliminates explicit stack management. |
| **Constraint Check** | The *only* mechanism that converts brute-force O(N!) search into a tractable pruned search. |
| **Backtrack on Return** | Removes the queen so subsequent column trials in the parent row are not poisoned by stale state. |
| **Termination** | Ensures all valid configurations are enumerated (counting) or the first is reported (existence). |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Term | Expression | Meaning / Use |
|---|---------------|------------|---------------|
| 1 | **Total Configurations (Brute Force)** | $N^N$ | Place one queen per row, any column → upper bound of brute force |
| 2 | **Permutation Bound (one per row & column)** | $N!$ | After eliminating row & column attacks |
| 3 | **Time Complexity (Worst Case)** | $O(N!)$ | Without pruning, full permutation enumeration |
| 4 | **Time Complexity (Average with Pruning)** | $O(N!)$ asymptotically, but with a **drastically smaller constant** | Backtracking prunes the majority of the SST |
| 5 | **Space Complexity (Board)** | $O(N^2)$ | Storing the N × N chessboard |
| 6 | **Space Complexity (Column Arrays Only)** | $O(N)$ | Optimized variant using three 1D arrays |
| 7 | **Diagonal Index Mapping (Left /)** | $d_1 = \text{row} - \text{col} + (N - 1)$ | Maps (row, col) to a single unique diagonal ID |
| 8 | **Diagonal Index Mapping (Right \\)** | $d_2 = \text{row} + \text{col}$ | Maps (row, col) to the other diagonal ID |
| 9 | **Range of $d_1$** | $0 \le d_1 \le 2N - 2$ | Used to size `diag1[]` array |
| 10 | **Range of $d_2$** | $0 \le d_2 \le 2N - 2$ | Used to size `diag2[]` array |
| 11 | **Known Solution Counts** | N=1: 1, N=2: 0, N=3: 0, **N=4: 2**, N=5: 10, N=6: 4, N=7: 40, **N=8: 92** | Standard values KTU examiners expect for N ≤ 8 |
| 12 | **Symmetry Factor** | $\dfrac{\text{Total}}{\text{Unique}}$ | Rotations and reflections produce 8-symmetry families |
| 13 | **Bounded Search Nodes** | $< N!$ | Strictly less than $N!$ due to pruning at each row |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Compiler Register Allocation:** Models variable-to-register assignment where registers attack (conflict) along shared resources.
- **Robot Motion Planning:** Path planning with non-overlapping reachability constraints.
- **Antenna Placement:** Maximum coverage with no mutual interference (analogous to diagonal non-interference).
- **SAT Solvers:** N-Queen solutions serve as benchmarks for DPLL-style backtracking SAT engines.
- **GPU/Parallel Search:** Modern GPU implementations achieve O(N) parallel reductions of the SST for N up to 27.

## 2.5 Lower Bound on Number of Solutions

For all $N \ge 4$, the number of solutions $S(N)$ satisfies:

$$
S(N) \ge 1
$$

This follows from constructive proofs (e.g., for $N = 4k$ and $N = 4k + 1$, explicit pattern-based solutions exist).

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Tip:** When asked "How many solutions does N=4 have?", the answer is **2**. For N=8, it is **92**. These are the most-tested values.

# 3. Step-by-Step Algorithm, Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Formal Pseudocode (Algorithm Box)

The following is **language-agnostic pseudocode** (NOT Python) suitable for KTU board answers:

```
ALGORITHM  : N-Queen using Backtracking
INPUT      : Integer N (board dimension, N ≥ 1)
OUTPUT     : All valid N-Queen configurations

GLOBAL DATA:
    board[0..N-1][0..N-1] : array of integers, initialized to 0
    solutions             : list of valid placements

PROCEDURE  solveNQueens(row)
    IF row == N THEN
        solutions.append(copy of board)
        RETURN
    END IF

    FOR col FROM 0 TO N-1 DO
        IF isSafe(row, col) THEN
            board[row][col] ← 1
            solveNQueens(row + 1)
            board[row][col] ← 0     // BACKTRACK
        END IF
    END FOR
END PROCEDURE

FUNCTION isSafe(row, col) → boolean
    FOR i FROM 0 TO row-1 DO
        IF board[i][col] == 1 THEN
            RETURN false            // Column clash
        END IF
        IF (row - i) == ABS(col - getColOfQueenInRow(i)) THEN
            RETURN false            // Diagonal clash
        END IF
    END FOR
    RETURN true
END FUNCTION

MAIN:
    solveNQueens(0)
    PRINT count and configurations from solutions
```

## 3.2 Exhaustive Trace for N = 4 (State Space Tree)

Below is the **complete backtracking trace** for N = 4. The state-space tree demonstrates pruning in action.

### Step-by-Step Tree Expansion

```
                BACKTRACK to Level 1

   BACKTRACK to Level 0

            Try col = 3 → SAFE
                └─ Place Q at (1,3)
                Level 2: Try col = 0 → SAFE
                            └─ Place Q at (2,0)

                                     Try col = 3 → CLASH (diag)

                         Try col = 1 → CLASH (diag)

               └─ Place Q at (1,0)

                        Try col = 2 → CLASH (col)
                        Try col = 3 → SAFE

                                         [(0,2),(1,0),(2,3),(3,1)]

                        Try col = 0 → CLASH (col)

            Try col = 1 → CLASH (diag)

Level 0: Try col = 3

                           └─ Place Q at (2,1)

                                         ** row == N → SOLUTION #2 **
                                         [(0,3),(1,0),(2,1),(3,2)]
                                    Try col = 3 → CLASH (diag)
                        Try col = 2 → CLASH (diag)
                        Try col = 3 → CLASH (col)
            Try col = 1 → CLASH (diag)
            Try col = 2 → CLASH (diag)

```

### Final Solutions for N = 4

$$
\text{Solutions} = \{ \; [(0,1),(1,3),(2,0),(3,2)], \; [(0,2),(1,0),(2,3),(3,1)] \; \}
$$

Wait — correcting the trace above: The **two canonical N=4 solutions** are:

$$
S_1: \; Q_0=(0,1), \; Q_1=(1,3), \; Q_2=(2,0), \; Q_3=(3,2)
$$

$$
S_2: \; Q_0=(0,2), \; Q_1=(1,0), \; Q_2=(2,3), \; Q_3=(3,1)
$$

### Visual Board Representations (N=4)

**Solution 1** $(S_1)$ — displayed as a 4×4 grid where `1` = Queen:

\text{Row 1} &: \quad . \; . \; . \; Q \\
\text{Row 2} &: \quad Q \; . \; . \; . \\
\text{Row 3} &: \quad . \; . \; Q \; .
\end{aligned}
$$

**Solution 2** $(S_2)$:

\text{Row 1} &: \quad Q \; . \; . \; . \\
\text{Row 2} &: \quad . \; . \; . \; Q \\
\text{Row 3} &: \quad . \; Q \; . \; .
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Both solutions are **mirror images** of each other (rotation + reflection under the 8-fold symmetry of the square). This is why N=4 has exactly **2 unique solutions** (not 4).

## 3.3 Derivation of Asymptotic Complexity

### Worst-Case Time Complexity

$$
T(N) = \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} T(\text{remaining}) \le \sum_{i=0}^{N-1} T(N - i - 1)
$$

By induction / substitution:

$$
T(N) = O(N!)
$$

Each row has at most $N$ column choices → first row $N$ choices, second row $\le N$ choices, ... → bound $N!$. Pruning reduces the constant factor but not the asymptotic class.

### Optimization — Diagonal Hashing (Bitmask Variant)

Instead of looping through previous rows in `isSafe`, use three boolean arrays of size $N$, $2N-1$, $2N-1$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cols}[c]      &= \text{true} \iff \text{column } c \text{ occupied} \\
\text{diag1}[r+c]   &= \text{true} \iff \text{right diagonal } r+c \text{ occupied} \\
\text{diag2}[r-c+N-1] &= \text{true} \iff \text{left diagonal } r-c \text{ occupied}
\end{aligned}
$$

This reduces each `isSafe` call from $O(N)$ to $O(1)$, but the overall complexity remains $O(N!)$.

## 3.4 Complexity Comparison Matrix

| Variant | Time (worst) | Space | Constant Factor |
|---------|-------------|-------|----------------|
| Brute force (any cell) | $O(N^{2N})$ | $O(N^2)$ | Huge |
| Brute force (1 queen/row) | $O(N!)$ | $O(N^2)$ | Large |
| Backtracking (row-based) | $O(N!)$ | $O(N^2)$ | Small (pruning) |
| Backtracking + bitmask | $O(N!)$ | $O(N)$ | Very small |
| Backtracking + symmetry breaking | $O(N!/8)$ | $O(N)$ | Smallest practical |

## 3.5 Analytical Decision Matrix — When Does Backtracking Fail?

| Condition | Outcome |
|-----------|---------|
| $N = 2$ or $N = 3$ | No solution exists (proven) |
| $N \ge 4$ | At least one solution exists |
| $N$ very large ($> 25$) | Algorithm takes hours in serial; parallel required |
| Symmetric variants required | Need constraint injection at root level |
| Counting all solutions | Exponential in output size — only feasible for $N \le 14$ |

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the N-Queen Solver

This block diagram shows the **runtime architecture** of the backtracking algorithm — mapping how data flows from input through recursion to the solution collector.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Input["Input: N value"] --> Init["Initialize Board N x N to 0"]
    Init --> CallSolver["Call solveNQueens(row=0)"]
    CallSolver --> CheckRow{"row == N ?"}

    CheckRow -- Yes --> Record["Record Solution in solutions list"]
    Record --> ReturnSolver["Return to Parent Call"]

    CheckRow -- No --> IterateCol["Iterate col from 0 to N-1"]
    IterateCol --> CheckSafe{"isSafe(row, col) ?"}

    CheckSafe -- No --> NextCol["col = col + 1"]
    NextCol --> IterateCol

    CheckSafe -- Yes --> PlaceQueen["Set board row col = 1"]
    PlaceQueen --> Recurse["Call solveNQueens(row + 1)"]
    Recurse --> Backtrack["Set board row col = 0  (BACKTRACK)"]
    Backtrack --> NextCol

    ReturnSolver --> OutputCheck{"More subtrees ?"}
    OutputCheck -- Yes --> IterateCol
    OutputCheck -- No --> FinalOutput["Output: Print all solutions and total count"]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Q: Define the N-Queen problem. Why is backtracking the preferred algorithmic strategy over brute force?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The N-Queen problem requires placing N queens on an N×N chessboard such that no two queens attack each other (no two share a row, column, or diagonal). **(1 mark)**

Brute force would try all $N^{N^2}$ or $N!$ configurations exhaustively, with no early termination when conflicts are detected. **(1 mark)**

Backtracking is preferred because it uses a **bounding function (`isSafe`)** that prunes entire subtrees the moment a conflict is detected, reducing the effective search space from $O(N!)$ with a huge constant to a much smaller practical search tree while guaranteeing completeness. **(1 mark)**

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024]
**Q: List the number of distinct solutions for the N-Queen problem when N = 4, N = 5, N = 6, N = 7, and N = 8. State why N = 2 and N = 3 have no solutions.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| N | Number of Solutions |
|---|---------------------|
| 4 | **2** |
| 5 | **10** |
| 6 | **4** |
| 7 | **40** |
| 8 | **92** |

**(2 marks for the table)**

For N = 2: A 2×2 board cannot accommodate two non-attacking queens because any two distinct cells either share a row, column, or diagonal. **(0.5 marks)**

For N = 3: Similarly, a 3×3 board exhausts all placements — the third queen always conflicts with one of the first two. **(0.5 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> As per KTU 2024 Scheme ESE regulation, Module 4 long questions carry 14 marks with sub-parts (a) and (b) typically of 7 marks each.

### Question A (14 Marks) — Algorithm + Trace [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]

**Q: Solve the N-Queen problem for N = 4 using backtracking.**

**(a)** Draw the state space tree showing all live nodes, E-nodes, dead nodes, and the two solution nodes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write the backtracking algorithm pseudocode and explain the role of the `isSafe` bounding function. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Initial Setup (1 mark)**
The board is 4×4. Place queens row by row. Root = empty board, level 0 corresponds to row 0 decisions.

**Step 2: Trace (5 marks)** — refer to the complete SST in Section 4.2 above. The two solutions are:

- **Solution 1:** $Q_0=(0,1), Q_1=(1,3), Q_2=(2,0), Q_3=(3,2)$
- **Solution 2:** $Q_0=(0,2), Q_1=(1,0), Q_2=(2,3), Q_3=(3,1)$

**Step 3: Dead Nodes Identification (1 mark)** — annotate nodes where `isSafe` rejects all columns (e.g., subtree rooted at Q at (1,2) in path (0,0)→(1,2) — all four Level 2 columns clash).

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Algorithm Pseudocode (4 marks)** — see Section 3.1.

**Step 2: Explanation of `isSafe` (3 marks)**

The `isSafe(row, col)` function is the **bounding function** of the algorithm. It checks three conditions for each candidate position `(row, col)`:

1. **Column Safety:** Iterates rows `i = 0` to `row − 1`; returns `false` if `board[i][col] == 1`. **(1 mark)**
2. **Left Diagonal Safety:** Returns `false` if `row − i == col − placedCol[i]` (i.e., `\vert row − i \vert == \vert col − placedCol[i] \vert`). **(1 mark)**
3. **Right Diagonal Safety:** Returns `false` if `row − i == placedCol[i] − col`. **(1 mark)**

If all three pass, `isSafe` returns `true`, permitting the placement. This function is what converts brute-force search into efficient backtracking — it prunes subtrees early.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Complexity + Optimization [KTU University Exam – May 2023]

**Q: Discuss the time and space complexity of the backtracking solution to the N-Queen problem.**

**(a)** Derive the worst-case time complexity with proper justification, and compute the precise number of recursive calls for N = 4. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the bitmask / diagonal-indexing optimization and discuss its effect on asymptotic complexity and constant factors. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Part (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Tree Structure (2 marks)**
The recursive calls form a tree of depth N (one level per row). Each internal node at depth $k$ has at most $N$ children (column choices for row $k$).

**Step 2: Worst-Case Bound (3 marks)**
Without pruning, the number of leaves equals $N \cdot (N-1) \cdot (N-2) \cdots 1 = N!$. Total nodes in a tree with branching factor N and depth N is bounded by:

$$
T(N) \le \sum_{i=0}^{N} N^{i} = \frac{N^{N+1} - 1}{N - 1} = O(N^N)
$$

However, since one queen per row is enforced (a stronger invariant), the bound tightens to:

$$
T(N) = O(N!)
$$

This is the **worst-case asymptotic time complexity**. **(3 marks)**

**Step 3: Recursive Calls for N = 4 (2 marks)**
From the trace in Section 4.2:

- `solveNQueens(0)` invoked 1 time
- `solveNQueens(1)` invoked at most 4 times (4 different parents)
- `solveNQueens(2)` invoked at most 16 times
- `solveNQueens(3)` invoked at most 64 times

Actual recursive calls in our trace (counting only those that passed `isSafe`): **12 total** calls. With pruning the call count drops to ~32 instead of the brute-force 341 (i.e., $4^4 + 4^3 + ...$). **(2 marks)**

---

#### Part (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1: Bitmask Idea (2 marks)**
Maintain three Boolean arrays (or bitmasks for speed):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{cols}[c]      &\quad \text{for } 0 \le c < N \\
\text{diag1}[r+c]   &\quad \text{for } 0 \le r+c < 2N-1 \quad \text{(right diagonal)} \\
\text{diag2}[r-c+N-1] &\quad \text{for } 0 \le r-c+N-1 < 2N-1 \quad \text{(left diagonal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: isSafe becomes O(1) (2 marks)**
Instead of looping over previous rows, we check:

$$
\neg \text{cols}[col] \;\wedge\; \neg \text{diag1}[row+col] \;\wedge\; \neg \text{diag2}[row-col+N-1]
$$

This is a **constant-time** safety check — three array accesses.

**Step 3: Complexity Effect (3 marks)**

- **Time:** Still $O(N!)$ asymptotically, but the constant factor per call drops from $O(N)$ to $O(1)$. For N = 14, this gives a **~14× speedup**.
- **Space:** Drops from $O(N^2)$ (board) to $O(N)$ (three 1D arrays).
- **Diagonal indexing derivation:** A cell `(r, c)` lies on the unique right-diagonal with index `r + c` (since the line `r + c = const` is a "\" diagonal). For the "/" diagonals, `r − c = const`, so we shift by `N − 1` to make it non-negative: index = `r − c + N − 1`. The range `−(N−1) ≤ r − c ≤ N−1` gives `0 ≤ r−c+N−1 ≤ 2N−2`, hence array size `2N − 1`. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in N-Queen Answers:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting the diagonal absolute-value condition** — writing `row − i == col − placedCol[i]` instead of `|row − i| == |col − placedCol[i]|`. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 2. **Missing the backtrack step** — students often place a queen but forget `board[row][col] = 0` after the recursive call. This breaks subsequent exploration. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 3. **Confusing `isSafe` direction** — the function checks against *previous rows only* (rows `0` to `row − 1`), not future rows. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 4. **Reporting wrong solution count** — claiming N = 8 has 12 or 76 solutions; the correct value is **92**. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 5. **Stating time complexity as $O(N^N)$** — after applying the one-queen-per-row invariant, the bound is $O(N!)$, not $O(N^N)$. Loss: **1 mark**.
> 6. **Not annotating the SST with E-nodes / dead nodes** — examiners explicitly look for these annotations. Loss: **1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Confusing row vs column indexing** — using 1-based indexing in code but 0-based in trace, causing off-by-one errors. Loss: **1 mark**.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The N-Queen problem places **N queens** on an **N×N board** with no two attacking — i.e., no shared row, column, or diagonal.
- **Backtracking** is the standard solution strategy: place one queen per row, recurse, and undo on conflict.
- The **bounding function `isSafe(row, col)`** checks three conditions: column clash, left-diagonal clash, and right-diagonal clash, all using the **absolute-value diagonal condition** $\vert \Delta\text{row} \vert = \vert \Delta\text{col} \vert$.
- **Worst-case time complexity is $O(N!)$** because we are permuting columns across N rows in the worst case.
- **Space complexity** is $O(N^2)$ for the board representation, reducible to $O(N)$ with bitmask optimization.
- The **state space tree (SST)** has the root at the empty board, N levels (one per row), and pruning at every level via `isSafe`.
- **Standard solution counts:** N=4 → **2**, N=5 → **10**, N=6 → **4**, N=7 → **40**, N=8 → **92**, N=1 → **1**, N=2 and N=3 → **0**.
- **Diagonal indexing** (optimization): right diagonal `d₁ = r + c` (range `[0, 2N−2]`); left diagonal `d₂ = r − c + N − 1` (range `[0, 2N−2]`).
- The **state space tree contains three node types**: Live (children yet to be explored), E-node (currently being expanded), Dead (pruned), and Solution (leaf with valid configuration).
- **Brute force** tries all $N^{N^2}$ or $N!$ permutations — backtracking is far more efficient due to **early pruning**.
- **No greedy algorithm** solves N-Queen optimally because local placement decisions do not guarantee a global solution — the recursive backtracking framework is essential.
- **Symmetry reduction** can cut search by a factor of up to 8 (rotations + reflections), making it the most powerful practical optimization.
- **KTU examiners frequently test:** (i) algorithm pseudocode, (ii) state-space tree for N=4 or N=8, (iii) complexity derivation, and (iv) solution count recitation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Sum of Subsets Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Sum of Subsets Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus)

The **Sum of Subsets Problem** is a classical combinatorial optimization problem that, given a set of **n** positive integers (weights or values)

$$W = \{w_1, w_2, w_3, \ldots, w_n\}$$

and a positive integer target sum **M**, asks to identify **all subsets** of **W** whose element sum is exactly equal to **M**. Formally, we must compute every subset $X \subseteq W$ such that:

$$\sum_{w_i \in X} w_i = M$$

where each weight satisfies the constraint $w_i > 0$ and the ordering $w_1 \le w_2 \le w_3 \le \ldots \le w_n$ is enforced (after sorting) to enable efficient pruning during the search.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Emphasis:** The Sum of Subsets problem is canonically solved using the **Backtracking** paradigm (a refined form of brute-force Depth-First Search with intelligent pruning). It directly maps to **CO4 (Design Backtracking and Branch & Bound Algorithms)** under the KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education framework.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Treasure Chest Puzzle

> [!NOTE]
> **Imagine you are a treasure hunter** standing in front of **six locked chests**, each containing a different number of gold coins: **5, 7, 10, 12, 15, and 20**. A mysterious note tells you: *"Exactly 30 coins will open the gate to the next chamber."* You must now try every possible combination of chests (open some, leave others closed) until you find every group whose coins total precisely **30**.

This is precisely the Sum of Subsets problem:
- The **chests** are the weights $w_i$.
- The **magic number 30** is the target sum $M$.
- The **act of trying combinations one by one** mirrors **backtracking**, where we either *include* an element in the candidate subset or *exclude* it, then move on.

## 1.3 Why Sorting is Mandatory

Before applying backtracking, the weights **must be sorted in non-decreasing order** ($w_1 \le w_2 \le \ldots \le w_n$). This is the master key that unlocks the bounding (pruning) function, allowing the algorithm to know *in advance* when adding more elements would overshoot the target $M$.

---

## 1.4 Two Equivalent Problem Formulations

| Formulation | What is Searched |
|-------------|------------------|
| **All-Subsets Variant** | Find *every* subset whose sum exactly equals $M$. |
| **Existence Variant** | Find *one* subset (typically of size $k$ or simply any one) whose sum equals $M$. |

Both variants share the same algorithmic skeleton; the only difference is *when the algorithm halts or reports*.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid for Exams:** Think *Subset = Sub-Tree*. The entire solution space of size $2^n$ can be visualized as a **binary tree of depth n**, where the left branch denotes *inclusion* and the right branch denotes *exclusion* of $w_i$.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The State Space Tree (Subset Tree)

The state space tree for Sum of Subsets is a **binary tree of depth $n$**. At each level $i$ (for the $i^{th}$ element), two branches emerge:

- **Left child** (decision = **include** $w_i$): Tentatively add $w_i$ to the running subset.
- **Right child** (decision = **exclude** $w_i$): Skip $w_i$ and move to the next element.

A **node is a leaf** when all $n$ items have been considered (either included or excluded), i.e., we have descended to depth $n$.

## 2.2 The Bounding (Pruning) Function — The Core Engine

To avoid exploring futile subtrees, two pruning conditions are imposed on the current running sum $s$ after processing $w_i$:

### Upper-Bound Pruning
> If we include $w_{i+1}$, the new sum must not exceed $M$:

$$s + w_{i+1} \le M$$

If violated, the entire "include-$w_{i+1}$" branch is **dead** and pruned.

### Lower-Bound Pruning
> Even if we include *all remaining* items ($w_{i+1}, w_{i+2}, \ldots, w_n$), the running sum $s$ must still be capable of reaching $M$:

$$s + \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_j \ge M$$

Let $r_i = w_{i+1} + w_{i+2} + \ldots + w_n$ denote the **remaining weight** at level $i$. The condition becomes:

$$s + r_i \ge M$$

If $s + r_i < M$, no matter which subset of remaining elements we pick, we can **never** hit $M$; the entire subtree is pruned.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why $r_i$ is precomputed in advance:** Because the weights are sorted, $r_i = r_{i-1} - w_i$, so the sum of remaining weights can be updated in $O(1)$ during traversal — a classic KTU trick worth **3 marks** in exams.

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Expression | Engineering Meaning |
|---------|-------------------------|---------------------|
| **Problem Statement** | $\sum_{w_i \in X} w_i = M$ | Find subset $X$ with exact target sum |
| **State-Space Size** | $2^n$ | Total nodes in the worst-case tree |
| **Running Sum** | $s = \sum_{j=1}^{i} x_j w_j$ | Current accumulated weight at depth $i$ |
| **Inclusion Vector** | $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ | 1 = include $w_i$, 0 = exclude |
| **Upper-Bound Pruning** | $s + w_{i+1} \le M$ | Cannot exceed target |
| **Lower-Bound Pruning** | $s + r_i \ge M$ where $r_i = \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_j$ | Must be able to reach target |
| **Final Acceptance Test** | $s = M$ (at a leaf) | Report subset as a valid solution |
| **Backtracking Time** | $O(2^n)$ worst-case | Same as brute force; gains come from pruning |
| **Backtracking Space** | $O(n)$ | Stack depth for recursion |
| **Precomputation** | $r_n = 0$, $r_{i-1} = r_i + w_i$ | Reverse-sum the sorted array |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Tip:** Always **precompute** $r_i$ values in a separate array before invoking the recursive backtracking routine. This avoids redundant summations and demonstrates to the examiner that you understand the $O(1)$ incremental update principle.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

1. **Resource Allocation in Cloud Computing:** Assigning virtual machines (weights = RAM/CPU demand) to physical servers (capacity = M) without exceeding capacity.
2. **Cryptographic Knapsack Systems:** The Subset Sum problem underlies the Merkle–Hellman *knapsack cryptosystem*; subset-sum decisions are bit-decisions on the plaintext.
3. **Load Balancing in Power Grids:** Distributing a fixed power load $M$ across generating units $w_i$ where each unit is either ON or OFF.
4. **Subset-Sum-Based Feature Selection in Machine Learning:** Selecting a subset of features whose "total importance score" equals a chosen budget $M$.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example — Complete Backtracking Trace

**Given:**
$$W = \{5,\ 7,\ 10,\ 12,\ 15,\ 20\}, \quad n = 6, \quad M = 30$$

**Step 0 — Precompute $r_i$ (sum of weights strictly after index $i$):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_6 &= 0 \\
r_5 &= w_6 = 20 \\
r_4 &= w_5 + w_6 = 15 + 20 = 35 \\
r_3 &= w_4 + w_5 + w_6 = 12 + 15 + 20 = 47 \\
r_2 &= w_3 + w_4 + w_5 + w_6 = 10 + 12 + 15 + 20 = 57 \\
r_1 &= w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 + w_6 = 7 + 10 + 12 + 15 + 20 = 64 \\
r_0 &= 5 + 7 + 10 + 12 + 15 + 20 = 69
\end{aligned}
$$

The algorithm starts at level $i = 0$ with running sum $s = 0$.

## 3.2 State Space Tree Trace — Branch-by-Branch

At each node we apply the **two pruning rules**:
- **Rule A:** $s + w_{i+1} \le M$  (upper bound)
- **Rule B:** $s + r_i \ge M$  (lower bound)

| Node | $i$ | Include? | New $s$ | Rule A: $s+w_{i+1} \le 30$? | Rule B: $s+r_i \ge 30$? | Action |
|:----:|:---:|:--------:|:-------:|:----------------------------:|:-----------------------:|:-------|
| Root | 0 | – | 0 | $0+5=5 \le 30$ ✓ | $0+64=64 \ge 30$ ✓ | Descend |
| A | 1 | +5 | 5 | $5+7=12 \le 30$ ✓ | $5+57=62 \ge 30$ ✓ | Descend |
| B | 2 | +7 | 12 | $12+10=22 \le 30$ ✓ | $12+47=59 \ge 30$ ✓ | Descend |
| C | 3 | +10 | 22 | $22+12=34 \le 30$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| C | 3 | –10 | 12 | $12+12=24 \le 30$ ✓ | $12+35=47 \ge 30$ ✓ | Descend |
| D | 4 | +12 | 24 | $24+15=39 \le 30$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| D | 4 | –12 | 12 | $12+15=27 \le 30$ ✓ | $12+20=32 \ge 30$ ✓ | Descend |
| E | 5 | +15 | 27 | $27+20=47 \le 30$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| E | 5 | –15 | 12 | $12+20=32 \le 30$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| … (continue for all branches) | | | | | | |

> [!NOTE]
> The complete trace generates **two valid solutions** for this instance: $\{5, 10, 15\}$ and $\{10, 20\}$. Students should be able to reproduce this tree node-by-node on the exam sheet to score full marks.

## 3.3 Complete Pseudocode Algorithm (Backtracking)

```
ALGORITHM SumOfSubsets(i, s, r)
// i : current index (0 to n)
// s : running sum so far
// r : remaining sum r_i = w[i+1] + ... + w[n]

BEGIN
    // Generate left child (include w[i+1])
    s_new = s + w[i+1]

    IF (s_new == M) THEN
        PRINT  "Found Subset including w[i+1]";  RETURN
    END IF

    IF (s_new + w[i+2] <= M) AND (s_new + r >= M) THEN
        SumOfSubsets(i + 1, s_new, r - w[i+1])
    END IF

    // Generate right child (exclude w[i+1])
    IF (s + w[i+2] <= M) AND (s + r - w[i+2] >= M) THEN
        SumOfSubsets(i + 1, s, r - w[i+1])
    END IF
END
```

**Initial Call:** $\texttt{SumOfSubsets}(0,\ 0,\ \sum_{j=1}^{n} w_j)$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Solved Subset for $W = \{5,7,10,12,15,20\},\ M = 30$

| Solution # | Subset | Verification: $\sum w_i$ | Equals $M$? |
|:----------:|:------:|:------------------------:|:-----------:|
| 1 | $\{5,\ 10,\ 15\}$ | $5+10+15$ | $30$ ✓ |
| 2 | $\{10,\ 20\}$ | $10+20$ | $30$ ✓ |
| — | $\{5,\ 7,\ 10,\ 8\}$ | Invalid (8 not in set) | ✗ |
| — | $\{7,\ 12,\ 20\}$ — wait, $7+12+20 = 39$ | Exceeds $M$ | ✗ |
| — | $\{5,\ 7,\ 18\}$ | 18 not in set | ✗ |

## 3.5 Complexity Derivation

**Worst Case:** No pruning is effective; the entire binary tree of $2^n$ nodes is explored.

$$
T(n) = 2 \cdot T(n-1) + O(1) \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; T(n) = O(2^n)
$$

**Space Complexity:** Recursion depth is at most $n$, hence stack space is $O(n)$. Plus the auxiliary $r_i$ array of size $n+1$, giving overall auxiliary space:

$$
S(n) = O(n)
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Comparison point for exams:** Brute-force enumeration is also $O(2^n)$ time, but backtracking's *practical* runtime is dramatically lower because of the bounding rules. In KTU valuation, emphasize that the **theoretical worst-case bound remains $O(2^n)$** even after pruning.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Backtracking State-Space Tree (Mermaid Block)

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the pruned state-space tree for the example $W = \{5, 7, 10, 12, 15, 20\}$ and $M = 30$. Solid arrows indicate traversed edges; dashed lines labelled **PRUNED** indicate subtrees cut by the bounding function.

```mermaid
graph TD
    root["Root : s=0 i=0"]:::rootStyle
    nodeA["Include 5 : s=5 i=1"]:::visited
    nodeB["Include 7 : s=12 i=2"]:::visited
    nodeC1["Include 10 : s=22 i=3"]:::pruned
    nodeC2["Exclude 10 : s=12 i=3"]:::visited
    nodeD1["Include 12 : s=24 i=4"]:::pruned
    nodeD2["Exclude 12 : s=12 i=4"]:::visited
    nodeE1["Include 15 : s=27 i=5"]:::pruned
    nodeE2["Exclude 15 : s=12 i=5"]:::pruned
    leafGood1["Subset 5 10 15 : s=30 i=6"]:::solution
    leafBad1["Subset 5 7 12 6 : s=30 invalid"]:::deadend
    leafBad2["Subset 5 7 10 8 : s=30 invalid"]:::deadend
    branch2["Include 10 from C2 : s=22 i=4"]:::visited
    branch3["Exclude 10 from C2 : s=12 i=4"]:::pruned
    branch4["Include 20 from branch2 : s=42 i=5"]:::pruned
    leafGood2["Subset 10 20 : s=30 i=6"]:::solution

    root --> nodeA
    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC1
    nodeB --> nodeC2
    nodeC1 -.PRUNED X.-> leafBad1
    nodeC2 --> nodeD1
    nodeC2 --> nodeD2
    nodeD1 -.PRUNED X.-> leafBad2
    nodeD2 --> nodeE1
    nodeD2 --> nodeE2
    nodeE1 -.PRUNED X.-> deadend1["Dead end s=27 less than 30"]
    nodeE2 -.PRUNED X.-> deadend2["Dead end s=12 less than 30"]
    nodeC2 --> branch2
    branch2 --> leafGood1
    branch2 -.PRUNED X.-> branch4
    branch2 --> branch3
    branch3 -.PRUNED X.-> deadend3["Dead end s=12 less than 30"]
    nodeB -.ALTERNATE PATH.-> branch2
    branch2 --> leafGood2

    classDef rootStyle fill:#1f3a5f,stroke:#fff,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef visited fill:#4a90e2,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef pruned fill:#e74c3c,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    classDef solution fill:#2ecc71,stroke:#222,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    classDef deadend fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#222,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow of the Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Input Weights W and Target M"] --> B["Sort W in non-decreasing order"]
    B --> C["Precompute r array from right to left"]
    C --> D{"Call SumOfSubsets 0,0,total"}
    D --> E["Try Include w i plus 1"]
    D --> F["Try Exclude w i plus 1"]
    E --> G{"s equals M"}
    G -- Yes --> H["Print Solution Subset"]
    G -- No --> I{"s plus w i plus 2 <= M AND s plus r >= M"}
    I -- Yes --> J["Recurse to SumOfSubsets i plus 1"]
    I -- No --> K["Prune this branch"]
    F --> L{"s plus w i plus 2 <= M AND s plus r minus w i plus 1 >= M"}
    L -- Yes --> M["Recurse to SumOfSubsets i plus 1"]
    L -- No --> N["Prune this branch"]
    J --> O{"i equals n"}
    O -- Yes --> P["Backtrack to parent node"]
    O -- No --> E
    M --> O
```

> [!TIP]
> **How to read this diagram:** The recursive call **SumOfSubsets(i+1, ...)** corresponds to descending one level deeper into the state-space tree. Each recursive return corresponds to **backtracking** to the parent node, which is the very heart of the backtracking paradigm.

## 4.3 Decision Logic Bubble Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    P1["Decision Point at Node i"]:::dp
    P2["Include w i plus 1"]:::include
    P3["Exclude w i plus 1"]:::exclude
    P4["Test Upper Bound s plus w i plus 1 <= M"]:::test
    P5["Test Lower Bound s plus r i >= M"]:::test
    P6["Accept: s equals M"]:::accept
    P7["Prune: Both bounds failed"]:::prune
    P8["Backtrack to parent"]:::backtrack

    P1 --> P2
    P1 --> P3
    P2 --> P4
    P4 -- Yes --> P5
    P5 -- Yes --> P6
    P5 -- No --> P7
    P4 -- No --> P7
    P6 --> P8
    P3 --> P5
    P7 --> P8

    classDef dp fill:#34495e,stroke:#fff,color:#fff
    classDef include fill:#27ae60,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef exclude fill:#c0392b,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef test fill:#f39c12,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef accept fill:#16a085,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef prune fill:#7f8c8d,stroke:#222,color:#fff
    classDef backtrack fill:#8e44ad,stroke:#222,color:#fff
```

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]
**Define the Sum of Subsets problem and state its formal mathematical formulation.**

**Model Answer:**
The Sum of Subsets problem is a combinatorial decision problem: given a set of $n$ positive integers $W = \{w_1, w_2, \ldots, w_n\}$ (assumed sorted non-decreasing) and a target integer $M$, find all subsets $X \subseteq W$ such that
$$\sum_{w_i \in X} w_i = M.$$
Each element can be either *included* (decision variable $x_i = 1$) or *excluded* ($x_i = 0$). The problem is canonically solved using backtracking, exploiting the bounding function $s + w_{i+1} \le M$ and $s + r_i \ge M$. **[3 Marks: 1 def + 1 formula + 1 method]**

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]
**Why must the input weights be sorted before applying the backtracking algorithm for Sum of Subsets? Mention any two pruning rules used.**

**Model Answer:**
**Reason for Sorting:** Sorting arranges the weights in non-decreasing order ($w_1 \le w_2 \le \ldots \le w_n$). This is essential because:

1. The **upper-bound pruning** $s + w_{i+1} \le M$ assumes that $w_{i+1}$ is the smallest remaining weight. If unsorted, this bound is invalid and we may incorrectly prune a valid solution.
2. The precomputation of $r_i = w_{i+1} + \ldots + w_n$ becomes meaningful only after sorting; the *lower-bound pruning* $s + r_i \ge M$ depends on knowing the maximum possible contribution of remaining elements.

**Two Pruning Rules:**
- **Rule A (Upper Bound):** $s + w_{i+1} \le M$ — adding $w_{i+1}$ must not overshoot.
- **Rule B (Lower Bound):** $s + r_i \ge M$ — even after consuming all remaining weights, the running sum must still be capable of reaching $M$.

**[3 Marks: 1 sort reason + 2 pruning rules]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Solve the Sum of Subsets problem using backtracking for the instance $W = \{3, 5, 7, 10, 12\}$ and $M = 18$. Generate the state-space tree, show all pruning decisions, and list the valid solutions.**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Sort and Precompute $r_i$:**
Weights are already sorted. Compute $r_i$ from the right:

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_5 &= 0 \\
r_4 &= w_5 = 12 \\
r_3 &= w_4 + w_5 = 10 + 12 = 22 \\
r_2 &= w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 7 + 10 + 12 = 29 \\
r_1 &= 5 + 7 + 10 + 12 = 34 \\
r_0 &= 3 + 5 + 7 + 10 + 12 = 37
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Trace the State-Space Tree:**

| Node | $i$ | Action | New $s$ | Upper: $s+w_{i+1}\le 18$? | Lower: $s+r_i\ge 18$? | Outcome |
|:----:|:---:|:------:|:--------:|:-------------------------:|:---------------------:|:--------|
| Root | 0 | Include 3 | 3 | $3+5=8 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+34=34 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| A | 1 | Include 5 | 8 | $8+7=15 \le 18$ ✓ | $3+29=32 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| B | 2 | Include 7 | 15 | $15+10=25 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| B | 2 | Exclude 7 | 8 | $8+10=18 \le 18$ ✓ | $8+22=30 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| C | 3 | Include 10 | 18 | $s=M$ ✓ | — | **SOLUTION {3,5,10}** |
| C | 3 | Exclude 10 | 8 | $8+12=20 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| A | 1 | Exclude 5 | 3 | $3+7=10 \le 18$ ✓ | $3+29=32 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| D | 2 | Include 7 | 10 | $10+10=20 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| D | 2 | Exclude 7 | 3 | $3+10=13 \le 18$ ✓ | $3+22=25 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| E | 3 | Include 10 | 13 | $13+12=25 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| E | 3 | Exclude 10 | 3 | $3+12=15 \le 18$ ✓ | $3+12=15 < 18$ ✗ | **Prune Both** |
| Root | 0 | Exclude 3 | 0 | $0+5=5 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+34=34 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| F | 1 | Include 5 | 5 | $5+7=12 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+29=29 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| G | 2 | Include 7 | 12 | $12+10=22 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| G | 2 | Exclude 7 | 5 | $5+10=15 \le 18$ ✓ | $5+22=27 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| H | 3 | Include 10 | 15 | $15+12=27 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| H | 3 | Exclude 10 | 5 | $5+12=17 \le 18$ ✓ | $5+12=17 < 18$ ✗ | **Prune Both** |
| F | 1 | Exclude 5 | 0 | $0+7=7 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+29=29 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| I | 2 | Include 7 | 7 | $7+10=17 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+22=22 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| J | 3 | Include 10 | 17 | $17+12=29 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| J | 3 | Exclude 10 | 7 | $7+12=19 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| I | 2 | Exclude 7 | 0 | $0+10=10 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+22=22 \ge 18$ ✓ | Descend |
| K | 3 | Include 10 | 10 | $10+12=22 \le 18$ ✗ | — | **Prune Right** |
| K | 3 | Exclude 10 | 0 | $0+12=12 \le 18$ ✓ | $0+12=12 < 18$ ✗ | **Prune Both** |

**Valid Solutions Identified:**
- $\{3, 5, 10\}$ — sum $= 18$ ✓
- $\{5, 7\}$ ... let me re-check: from F include 5 ($s=5$), then I include 7 → $s=12$, then J include 10 → $s=22$ (pruned). Actually from F, we need to check exclude-5 path...

**Re-examining:** From node $F$ ($i=1$, $s=0$, exclude 5 → $s=0$):
- Include 7 ($i=2$, $s=7$) → Include 10 ($s=17$) pruned → Exclude 10 ($s=7$) → Include 12 ($s=19$) pruned.
- Exclude 7 ($i=2$, $s=0$) → Include 10 ($s=10$) pruned → Exclude 10 ($s=0$) → Include 12 ($s=12$, $r=0$, $12 \ne 18$).

**Final Answer:** The valid solution is $\boxed{\{3, 5, 10\}}$ where $3 + 5 + 10 = 18 = M$.

**[7 Marks: 2 for precompute + 3 for tree trace + 2 for final answer]**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the bounding function used in Sum of Subsets backtracking. Why is the lower bound $s + r_i \ge M$ necessary in addition to the upper bound $s + w_{i+1} \le M$?**

**Model Answer:**

**Bounding Function — Two Components:**

**1. Upper Bound (Forward Check):**
$$s + w_{i+1} \le M$$
This checks that adding the *next* (smallest remaining) weight does not exceed the target. If this fails, including $w_{i+1}$ is impossible, and we prune the left subtree.

**2. Lower Bound (Reachability Check):**
$$s + r_i \ge M, \quad \text{where } r_i = \sum_{j=i+1}^{n} w_j$$
This checks that even if we *include every remaining weight*, we can still hit $M$. If $s + r_i < M$, no subset of the remaining elements can possibly sum to $M - s$, so the entire subtree is hopeless.

**Why Both Are Needed:**

| Bound Alone | Limitation |
|-------------|------------|
| Upper bound only | Fails to prune subtrees where we can never reach $M$ (sum too small) |
| Lower bound only | Fails to prune subtrees that overshoot $M$ immediately |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Combined Application:** Both conditions must hold simultaneously for the recursion to proceed. If either fails, we backtrack immediately, saving exponential work in practice. The theoretical worst-case complexity remains $O(2^n)$ but practical runtimes improve dramatically — a key KTU Board point.

**Worked Numeric Illustration:** For $W = \{5, 7, 10, 12, 15, 20\}$, $M = 30$, at the node where $s = 5$, $i = 3$:
- Upper: $5 + 10 = 15 \le 30$ ✓
- Lower: $5 + r_3 = 5 + (12 + 15 + 20) = 5 + 47 = 52 \ge 30$ ✓ → continue recursion.

If only the upper bound were checked, subtrees where the running sum is already too small would still be fully explored, wasting exponential time.

**[7 Marks: 3 for upper bound + 3 for lower bound + 1 for combined justification]**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**For the instance $W = \{2, 4, 6, 8, 10\}$ and $M = 14$, write the backtracking algorithm and trace its execution. List all valid subsets.**

**Model Solution:**

**Algorithm Recap (Pseudocode):**
```
SumOfSubsets(i, s, r):
1. If (s + w[i+1] <= M) then
2.     s_new = s + w[i+1]
3.     If (s_new == M) then print solution; else
4.         If (s_new + r - w[i+1] >= M) then
5.             SumOfSubsets(i+1, s_new, r - w[i+1])
6. If (s + r - w[i+1] >= M) AND (i+1 < n) then
7.     SumOfSubsets(i+1, s, r - w[i+1])
```

**Precompute $r_i$:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
r_5 &= 0 \\
r_4 &= 10, \quad r_3 = 8+10=18, \quad r_2 = 6+8+10=24 \\
r_1 &= 4+6+8+10=28, \quad r_0 = 2+4+6+8+10=30
\end{aligned}
$$

**Trace:**

| Decision | $s$ before | $i$ | $s+w_{i+1}$ | Upper | $s+r_i$ | Lower | Result |
|----------|-----------|-----|-------------|-------|---------|-------|--------|
| Root → Include 2 | 0 | 0 | $2$ | $2 \le 14$ ✓ | $0+28=28$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 4 | 2 | 1 | $6$ | $6 \le 14$ ✓ | $2+24=26$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 6 | 6 | 2 | $12$ | $12 \le 14$ ✓ | $6+18=24$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 12 | 3 | $20$ | $20 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune Include** |
| → Exclude 8 | 12 | 3 | — | — | $12+10=22$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 10 | 12 | 4 | $22$ | $22 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune Include** |
| → Exclude 10 | 12 | 4 | — | — | $12+0=12$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune Exclude** |
| → Exclude 6 | 6 | 2 | — | — | $6+18=24$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 6 | 3 | $14$ | $s = M$ ✓ | — | — | **SOLUTION {2,4,8}** |
| → Exclude 4 | 2 | 1 | — | — | $2+24=26$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 6 | 2 | 2 | $8$ | $8 \le 14$ ✓ | $2+18=20$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 8 | 3 | $16$ | $16 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 8 | 8 | 3 | — | — | $8+10=18$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 10 | 8 | 4 | $18$ | $18 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 10 | 8 | 4 | — | — | $8+0=8$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 6 | 2 | 2 | — | — | $2+18=20$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 2 | 3 | $10$ | $10 \le 14$ ✓ | $2+10=12$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune Include** |
| → Exclude 8 | 2 | 3 | — | — | $2+10=12$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune Exclude** |
| → Exclude 2 | 0 | 0 | — | — | $0+28=28$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 4 | 0 | 1 | $4$ | $4 \le 14$ ✓ | $0+24=24$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 6 | 4 | 2 | $10$ | $10 \le 14$ ✓ | $4+18=22$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 10 | 3 | $18$ | $18 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 8 | 10 | 3 | — | — | $10+10=20$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 10 | 10 | 4 | $20$ | $20 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 10 | 10 | 4 | — | — | $10+0=10$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 6 | 4 | 2 | — | — | $4+18=22$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 8 | 4 | 3 | $12$ | $12 \le 14$ ✓ | $4+10=14$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 10 | 12 | 4 | $22$ | $22 \le 14$ ✗ | — | — | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 10 | 12 | 4 | — | — | $12+0=12$ | $<14$ ✗ | **Prune** |
| → Exclude 8 | 4 | 3 | — | — | $4+10=14$ | $\ge 14$ ✓ | Descend |
| → Include 10 | 4 | 4 | $14$ | $s = M$ ✓ | — | — | **SOLUTION {4,10}** |

**Valid Solutions:**
- $\boxed{\{2,\ 4,\ 8\}}$ with $2+4+8 = 14$ ✓
- $\boxed{\{4,\ 10\}}$ with $4+10 = 14$ ✓

**[7 Marks: 2 algorithm + 3 tree trace + 1 prunes + 1 final answer]**

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare brute-force enumeration with backtracking for Sum of Subsets. Discuss time complexity, space complexity, and practical performance for $n = 30$.**

**Model Answer:**

| Metric | Brute-Force Enumeration | Backtracking |
|--------|------------------------|--------------|
| **Approach** | Generate all $2^n$ subsets explicitly; test each sum | Generate subsets recursively with pruning rules |
| **Time Complexity (Worst)** | $O(2^n \cdot n)$ — generate + sum each subset | $O(2^n)$ — exponential, but pruning reduces real work |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n)$ for storing subsets + $O(n)$ for sums | $O(n)$ for recursion stack + $O(n)$ for $r_i$ array |
| **Pruning?** | None — every subset tested | Two bounding rules eliminate dead subtrees |
| **For $n = 30$** | $2^{30} \approx 10^9$ subsets — **infeasible** | Practically fast on typical instances; worst still $10^9$ |

**Detailed Comparison:**

**1. Time Complexity:**
- Brute force must generate all $2^n$ subsets and compute their sums, each in $O(n)$. Total: $O(n \cdot 2^n)$.
- Backtracking in the worst case still visits $O(2^n)$ nodes, but in **average cases** the bounding rules cut more than 50% of branches. The savings depend on the structure of $W$ and the value of $M$.

**2. Space Complexity:**
- Both algorithms use $O(n)$ auxiliary space.
- Backtracking additionally maintains the array $r[0 \ldots n]$ of size $n+1$.

**3. Practical Performance for $n = 30$:**
- $2^{30} = 1{,}073{,}741{,}824 \approx 10^9$ nodes.
- Brute force on a 3 GHz machine: roughly $10^9 \times 30 \approx 3 \times 10^{10}$ operations → **hours** of compute.
- Backtracking on a *typical* instance may visit only $10^6$ to $10^8$ nodes → **seconds** of compute, because pruning is aggressive.

> [!WARNING]
> **Important Caveat:** Backtracking's worst case is still exponential. For pathological inputs (e.g., $W$ values much smaller than $M$), the algorithm degenerates to brute-force enumeration. Therefore, backtracking is *practically efficient* but not asymptotically superior.

**Engineering Takeaway:** For $n \le 25$, backtracking is feasible. For $n > 35$, even backtracking struggles, and one must resort to **dynamic programming** (the $O(nM)$ pseudo-polynomial algorithm) or **approximation schemes**.

**[7 Marks: 2 comparison table + 2 complexity + 2 practical + 1 takeaway]**

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Typically Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to Sort (–2 marks):** Many students apply backtracking directly on unsorted input. The bounding function *only* works on sorted weights. Always sort first!
>
> 2. **Forgetting to Precompute $r_i$ (–2 marks):** Recomputing the remaining sum at every recursive call adds unnecessary $O(n)$ overhead and signals to the examiner that you don't understand the $O(1)$ incremental update.
>
> 3. **Misapplying the Bounding Function (–3 marks):** A frequent mistake is to write the condition as $s + w_i \le M$ (using the current index instead of $i+1$) or to omit the lower-bound check entirely.
>
> 4. **Not Drawing the State-Space Tree (–3 marks):** KTU examiners expect a *visual tree trace* showing include/exclude branches and clearly marking **PRUNED** nodes. A textual-only answer forfeits significant marks.
>
> 5. **Confusing Sum of Subsets with 0/1 Knapsack (–2 marks):** The two problems look similar, but Sum of Subsets demands *exact* equality, whereas 0/1 Knapsack demands *maximization* under a capacity constraint. Do not mix the bounding functions.
>
> 6. **Wrong Complexity Statement (–1 mark):** Always state the *worst-case* time complexity as $O(2^n)$. Never write $O(n \log n)$ or similar — those are wrong even with pruning.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Sum of Subsets Problem**

- **Problem Goal:** Find all subsets $X \subseteq W$ with $\sum_{w_i \in X} w_i = M$.
- **Algorithm Used:** Backtracking (a refinement of brute-force DFS).
- **Data Structure:** Sorted array $W[1 \ldots n]$ with $w_1 \le w_2 \le \ldots \le w_n$.
- **Two Pruning Rules (Bounding Function):**
  - *Upper Bound:* $s + w_{i+1} \le M$ — cannot overshoot.
  - *Lower Bound:* $s + r_i \ge M$ where $r_i = w_{i+1} + \ldots + w_n$ — must remain reachable.
- **Recurrence:** $T(n) = 2T(n-1) + O(1) \Rightarrow O(2^n)$ worst case.
- **Space:** $O(n)$ for recursion stack and $r_i$ array.
- **Precomputation:** Compute $r_n = 0$ and decrementally update $r_{i-1} = r_i + w_i$.
- **State-Space Tree:** Binary tree of depth $n$ with $2^n$ nodes worst case; left = include, right = exclude.
- **Acceptance Test:** When $s == M$ at a leaf (or partial depth), print the subset as a solution.
- **Sorting is Mandatory:** Without sorted weights, the bounding function is invalid.
- **Distinguish from 0/1 Knapsack:** Sum of Subsets demands *exact* sum; 0/1 Knapsack demands *optimal* (max) value under capacity.
- **Engineering Applications:** Cloud resource allocation, cryptographic knapsack systems, power-grid load balancing, feature selection in ML.
- **Examiner's Expectation:** Always draw the state-space tree, mark pruned nodes with an **X**, and write down the recursion trace.

> **Final Note:** This topic is consistently tested for **7–14 marks** in KTU End Semester Examinations. Master the tree-trace methodology above and you will secure full marks consistently.

---
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Graph Coloring
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Graph Coloring ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition **Graph Coloring** is an assignment of labels (traditionally called *colors*) to elements of a graph — most commonly to **vertices**, but also to **edges** or **faces** of a planar graph — subject to a set of prescribed constraints. A **proper vertex coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a function $$c : V \rightarrow \{1, 2, 3, \dots , k\}$$ such that for every edge $(u, v) \in E$: $$c(u) \neq c(v)$$ In other words, **no two adjacent vertices may share the same color**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Chromatic Number $\chi(G)$:** The *minimum* number of colors required to obtain a proper vertex coloring of $G$ is known as the **chromatic number** of the graph. A graph is called **k-colorable** if and only if $\chi(G) \le k$. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Map Coloring" View Imagine you are a cartographer coloring a political map. Your only rule is that **no two countries sharing a border may receive the same color**. The question you ask is: *"What is the smallest number of colored crayons I need to finish the job?"* That minimum is exactly the **chromatic number** of the region's adjacency graph. This same rule governs: - **University Exam Scheduling:** Courses (vertices) sharing a common student (edges) cannot be scheduled at the same time slot (color). - **Radio Frequency Assignment:** Transmitter towers (vertices) within interference range (edges) must broadcast on different frequencies (colors). - **Sudoku Solving:** Each row, column, and 3×3 box is a clique — every number (color) must be unique within each constraint. > [!NOTE] > The **Four Color Theorem** (Appel & Haken, 1976) proved that every planar graph can be properly colored with at most **4** colors — i.e., $\chi(G) \le 4$ for any planar $G$. This is one of the most famous results in graph theory. ### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Key Terminology | Term | Notation | Meaning | |---|---|---| | **Chromatic Number** | $\chi(G)$ | Minimum colors needed for a proper coloring | | **Chromatic Polynomial** | $P_G(k)$ | Number of distinct proper colorings using $\le k$ colors | | **Clique Number** | $\omega(G)$ | Size of the largest complete subgraph | | **Independent Set** | $\alpha(G)$ | Largest set of mutually non-adjacent vertices | | **k-Colorable** | — | Graph can be properly colored with $k$ colors | ### 1.4 Syllabus Highlights (KTU 2024 — Module 4) - Vertex coloring, edge coloring, and face coloring concepts. - Computation of the **chromatic number** for small graphs. - The **Greedy Coloring Algorithm** and its worst-case complexity. - **Brooks' Theorem** as an upper bound for non-complete, non-odd-cycle graphs. - Real-world engineering applications. --- ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Types of Graph Coloring Graph coloring is a family of related problems classified by which element receives the color label: 1. **Vertex Coloring** — Assign colors to vertices so adjacent vertices differ. (Most common form.) 2. **Edge Coloring** — Assign colors to edges so that no two edges sharing a vertex receive the same color. The minimum number of edge colors required is the **chromatic index** $\chi'(G)$. 3. **Face Coloring** — Apply colors to faces of a planar embedding such that no two faces sharing an edge share a color. By the Four Color Theorem, every planar graph has $\chi_f(G) \le 4$. 4. **Total Coloring** — A simultaneous vertex + edge coloring where no two *adjacent or incident* elements share a color. ### 2.2 Properties of the Chromatic Number Let $G = (V, E)$ be any simple undirected graph with $n = \vert V \vert$ vertices: - **Lower Bound (Trivial):** $\chi(G) \ge \omega(G)$ where $\omega(G)$ is the clique number. - **Trivial Upper Bound:** $\chi(G) \le n$ (color every vertex uniquely). - **Odd Cycles:** $\chi(C_{2k+1}) = 3$ for any odd cycle. - **Even Cycles & Trees:** $\chi = 2$ (bipartite graphs). - **Complete Graphs:** $\chi(K_n) = n$. - **Empty Graphs:** $\chi(\overline{K_n}) = 1$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Brooks' Theorem (1941):** For any connected, undirected graph $G$ that is neither a complete graph $K_n$ nor an odd cycle, the chromatic number satisfies $\chi(G) \le \Delta(G)$, where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum vertex degree. For $K_n$ and odd cycles, $\chi(G) = \Delta(G) + 1$. ### 2.3 The Chromatic Polynomial For a graph $G$ and a positive integer $k$, the **chromatic polynomial** $P_G(k)$ counts the number of distinct proper colorings of $G$ using at most $k$ colors. **Fundamental Properties:** | Graph $G$ | $P_G(k)$ | |---|---| | $n$ isolated vertices ($\overline{K_n}$) | $k^n$ | | Tree on $n$ vertices | $k(k-1)^{n-1}$ | | Cycle $C_n$ | $(k-1)^n + (-1)^n (k-1)$ | | Complete graph $K_n$ | $k(k-1)(k-2)\dots(k-n+1)$ | | Empty edge (single vertex + edge) | $k(k-1)$ | **Recursive Deletion-Contraction Rule:** If $e = (u, v)$ is an edge of $G$: $$P_G(k) = P_{G - e}(k) - P_{G / e}(k)$$ where $G - e$ deletes the edge and $G / e$ contracts it (merging $u$ and $v$). ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Theorem | Description | |---|---|---| | 1 | $\chi(G) \ge \omega(G)$ | Chromatic number $\ge$ clique number | | 2 | $\chi(G) \le \Delta(G) + 1$ | Trivial upper bound | | 3 | $\chi(G) \le \Delta(G)$ | **Brooks' Theorem** (non-complete, non-odd-cycle) | | 4 | $\chi(G) \le 4$ | Four Color Theorem (planar graphs) | | 5 | $\chi(C_{2k+1}) = 3$, $\chi(C_{2k}) = 2$ | Odd/even cycles | | 6 | $\chi(K_n) = n$ | Complete graphs | | 7 | $P_{K_n}(k) = \prod_{i=0}^{n-1}(k - i)$ | Chromatic poly of complete graph | | 8 | $P_G(k) = P_{G-e}(k) - P_{G/e}(k)$ | Deletion-contraction | | 9 | $\chi'(G) \ge \Delta(G)$ | Vizing's lower bound for edge coloring | | 10 | $\chi'(G) \le \Delta(G) + 1$ | Vizing's Theorem upper bound | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** Graph coloring underpins **VLSI channel routing**, **register allocation in compilers**, **frequency assignment in wireless networks**, and **Sudoku-like constraint satisfaction problems** used in AI planning. ### 2.5 Decision Complexity The decision problem **"Is $G$ k-colorable?"** is **NP-Complete** for any fixed $k \ge 3$ (Karp, 1972). This means: - For $k = 1, 2$: solvable in **polynomial time** ($O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ via BFS/DFS bipartiteness check). - For $k \ge 3$: NP-Complete — no known polynomial-time algorithm unless **P = NP**. --- ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Manual Coloring of a Sample Graph **Problem.** Find the chromatic number $\chi(G)$ of the graph $G$ with $V = \{A, B, C, D, E, F\}$ and edges: $$E = \{(A,B), (A,C), (B,C), (B,D), (C,E), (D,E), (D,F), (E,F)\}$$ **Step 1: Visualize the graph.** The graph contains triangle $A$–$B$–$C$ and triangle $D$–$E$–$F$, connected by edges $B$–$D$ and $C$–$E$. **Step 2: Lower bound derivation.** Triangle $\{A, B, C\}$ is a $K_3$ subgraph, so $\omega(G) \ge 3$. Hence: $$\chi(G) \ge \omega(G) \ge 3$$ **Step 3: Attempt a 3-coloring.** We try assigning colors $\{1, 2, 3\}$ via the greedy algorithm (process vertices in order $A, B, C, D, E, F$). | Vertex | Adjacent (already colored) | Used colors | Assigned color | |---|---|---|---| | $A$ | — | $\{\}$ | **1** | | $B$ | $A$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | $C$ | $A, B$ | $\{1, 2\}$ | **3** | | $D$ | $B$ | $\{2\}$ | **1** | | $E$ | $C, D$ | $\{3, 1\}$ | **2** | | $F$ | $D, E$ | $\{1, 2\}$ | **3** | **Step 4: Verify the coloring.** Final assignment: $c(A)=1,\ c(B)=2,\ c(C)=3,\ c(D)=1,\ c(E)=2,\ c(F)=3$. Check each edge: $$\begin{aligned} (A,B):\ 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\ (A,C):\ 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\ (B,C):\ 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\ (B,D):\ 2 \neq 1 \quad &\checkmark \\ (C,E):\ 3 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\ (D,E):\ 1 \neq 2 \quad &\checkmark \\ (D,F):\ 1 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \\ (E,F):\ 2 \neq 3 \quad &\checkmark \end{aligned}$$ All edges satisfied with **3 colors** ⇒ $\chi(G) = 3$. **Step 5: Cross-check via Brooks' theorem.** Compute degree of each vertex: $$\deg(A)=2,\ \deg(B)=3,\ \deg(C)=2,\ \deg(D)=3,\ \deg(E)=3,\ \deg(F)=2$$ Maximum degree $\Delta(G) = 3$. $G$ is neither $K_n$ nor an odd cycle. By Brooks' Theorem: $$\chi(G) \le \Delta(G) = 3$$ Combined with lower bound, $\chi(G) = 3$. ✓ --- ### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Computing the Chromatic Polynomial $P_G(k)$ **Problem.** Compute $P_G(k)$ for the path graph $P_3$ on vertices $\{v_1, v_2, v_3\}$ with edges $(v_1, v_2)$ and $(v_2, v_3)$. **Step 1: Apply deletion-contraction.** Let $e = (v_1, v_2)$. - **$G - e$:** Now $v_1$ is isolated. We have $\{v_1\}$ (single vertex) + edge $(v_2, v_3)$. - Edge on 2 vertices: $P = k(k-1)$. - One isolated vertex: multiply by $k$. - $P_{G-e}(k) = k \cdot k(k-1) = k^2(k-1)$. - **$G / e$:** Contract $e$ — merge $v_1, v_2$ into a single vertex. Result is an edge between the merged vertex and $v_3$. - $P_{G/e}(k) = k(k-1)$. **Step 2: Apply the recursion.** $$\begin{aligned} P_G(k) &= P_{G-e}(k) - P_{G/e}(k) \\ &= k^2(k-1) - k(k-1) \\ &= k(k-1)(k - 1) \\ &= k(k-1)^2 \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3: Sanity check.** For $k = 2$: $P_G(2) = 2 \cdot 1^2 = 2$. Indeed, a path of length 2 has exactly 2 proper 2-colorings (color endpoints the same, middle different). ✓ For $k = 3$: $P_G(3) = 3 \cdot 4 = 12$ proper 3-colorings. ✓ --- ### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Greedy Algorithm Walkthrough on a Complex Graph **Problem.** Apply the **Greedy Coloring Algorithm** to the following graph in vertex order $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_7$: Edges: $(v_1,v_2), (v_1,v_3), (v_2,v_4), (v_3,v_4), (v_3,v_5), (v_4,v_6), (v_5,v_6), (v_5,v_7), (v_6,v_7)$. | Step | Vertex | Already-colored neighbors | Forbidden colors | Assigned | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $v_1$ | — | $\emptyset$ | **1** | | 2 | $v_2$ | $v_1$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | 3 | $v_3$ | $v_1$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | 4 | $v_4$ | $v_2, v_3$ | $\{2\}$ | **1** | | 5 | $v_5$ | $v_3$ | $\{2\}$ | **1** | | 6 | $v_6$ | $v_4, v_5$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | 7 | $v_7$ | $v_5, v_6$ | $\{1, 2\}$ | **3** | **Total colors used: 3.** Since the graph contains the 4-cycle $v_3$–$v_5$–$v_7$–$v_6$–$v_4$–$v_3$ (even), the graph is bipartite plus some triangles — let us verify $\chi(G) = 3$. Lower bound check: Triangle $\{v_3, v_5, v_7\}$? Edges: $(v_3,v_5)$ ✓, $(v_5,v_7)$ ✓, but $(v_3,v_7)$ ✗ — *no triangle*. So lower bound is 2. But the cycle $v_4$–$v_6$–$v_7$–$v_5$–$v_3$–$v_4$ has length 4 (even) ⇒ bipartite. So $\chi(G) \le 3$ from greedy, and no odd cycle forces $\ge 3$. However, edge $(v_4,v_6), (v_6,v_7), (v_7,v_5), (v_5,v_3), (v_3,v_4)$ is a 5-cycle (odd)! Therefore $\chi(G) \ge 3$. Combined: $\chi(G) = 3$. ✓ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Algorithm Complexity | Algorithm | Input | Output | Time Complexity | Optimality | |---|---|---|---|---| | Greedy Coloring | Graph $G$, vertex ordering | Proper $k$-coloring | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Not always optimal | | BFS 2-Coloring | Graph $G$ | Bipartite check + 2-coloring | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | Optimal for $k=2$ | | Backtracking | Graph $G$, integer $k$ | k-coloring or "NO" | $O(k^n)$ worst case | Exact | | DSATUR (Brélaz) | Graph $G$ | Heuristic coloring | Polynomial heuristic | Often near-optimal | | Chromatic Poly. | Graph $G$ | $P_G(k)$ | Exponential in $\vert V \vert$ | Exact | --- ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Greedy Coloring Algorithm Flow (Mermaid) ```mermaid flowchart TD startA([START]) --> inputA[/Input: Graph G = V, E
and vertex ordering pi/] inputA --> initA[Initialize color array c of size n
Set all entries to 0] initA --> loopA{For each vertex v
in order pi} loopA --> getNeigh[Collect colors of
already-colored neighbors] getNeigh --> pickMin[Find smallest positive
integer k not in neighbor colors] pickMin --> assignA[Assign c at v = k] assignA --> loopA loopA --> doneA[/Output: coloring c
and chi = max of c/] doneA --> stopA([END]) style startA fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style stopA fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff style initA fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style pickMin fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style assignA fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d ``` ### 4.2 Block Diagram of Graph Coloring Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[INPUT STAGE] A[Real World Problem] B[Graph Model Construction] end subgraph ENGINE[CORE ENGINE] C[Constraint Representation] D[Greedy / Heuristic Solver] E[Verification Module] end subgraph OUTPUT[OUTPUT STAGE] F[Color Assignment] G[Performance Metric: chi G] end A --> B --> C --> D --> E --> F E --> G F --> H([Deployment in
Scheduling / Routing / Allocation]) style INPUT fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style ENGINE fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style OUTPUT fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style H fill:#065f46,stroke:#022c22,color:#ffffff ``` ### 4.3 Conceptual Map — Branch Structure of Coloring ```mermaid flowchart TD root[Graph Coloring] --> vtx[Vertex Coloring] root --> edg[Edge Coloring] root --> fcs[Face Coloring] root --> tot[Total Coloring] vtx --> vtxA[Proper vertex coloring] vtx --> vtxB[Chromatic Number chi G] vtx --> vtxC[Chromatic Polynomial P G k] edg --> edgA[Proper edge coloring] edg --> edgB[Chromatic Index chi prime G] edg --> edgC[Vizing Theorem] fcs --> fcsA[Planar embedding] fcs --> fcsB[Four Color Theorem] tot --> totA[Vertex + Edge jointly] style root fill:#1e3a8a,stroke:#0f172a,color:#ffffff style vtx fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style edg fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style fcs fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style tot fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e ``` ### 4.4 Reduction Pathway — Why NP-Completeness Matters ```mermaid flowchart LR A[3-SAT Instance] --> B[Reduce to] B --> C[Graph H] C --> D{Is H 3-colorable?} D -- YES --> E[3-SAT Satisfiable] D -- NO --> F[3-SAT Unsatisfiable] style A fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style C fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style E fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style F fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d ``` > [!NOTE] > The above reduction demonstrates that **3-colorability** is **NP-Complete** because it inherits the hardness of **3-SAT** via Karp's classical 21 reduction list. This is why KTU places strong emphasis on heuristics like the Greedy Algorithm and DSATUR. --- ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (2 Marks Each) --- #### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **Define proper vertex coloring of a graph. What is the chromatic number?** **Model Answer:** A **proper vertex coloring** of a graph $G = (V, E)$ is an assignment of colors $c : V \rightarrow \{1, 2, \dots, k\}$ such that for every edge $(u, v) \in E$, $c(u) \neq c(v)$. The **chromatic number** $\chi(G)$ is the minimum number of colors required for a proper vertex coloring of $G$. Formally: $$\chi(G) = \min\{k : G \text{ admits a proper } k\text{-coloring}\}$$ **Example:** $\chi(K_3) = 3$, $\chi(C_4) = 2$. --- #### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]** **State Brooks' Theorem. When does its equality hold?** **Model Answer:** **Brooks' Theorem:** For any connected undirected graph $G$ that is **not** a complete graph $K_n$ and **not** an odd cycle, the chromatic number satisfies: $$\chi(G) \le \Delta(G)$$ where $\Delta(G)$ is the maximum degree. **Equality** $\chi(G) = \Delta(G) + 1$ holds if and only if $G$ is either: - A complete graph $K_n$ (where $\chi(K_n) = n = \Delta(K_n) + 1$), or - An odd cycle $C_{2k+1}$ (where $\chi = 3 = \Delta + 1$). --- ### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Apply the Greedy Coloring Algorithm to the graph shown below using the vertex ordering $v_1, v_2, v_3, v_4, v_5, v_6$. Determine the number of colors used. **Edges:** $(v_1, v_2), (v_1, v_3), (v_2, v_4), (v_3, v_4), (v_3, v_5), (v_4, v_6), (v_5, v_6), (v_1, v_5)$. **(b) [7 Marks]** Prove that the chromatic number of any tree on $n \ge 2$ vertices is exactly 2. --- #### **Model Solution — Question A:** **Part (a):** | Step | Vertex | Colored Neighbors | Forbidden | Assigned | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | $v_1$ | — | $\emptyset$ | **1** | | 2 | $v_2$ | $v_1 \to 1$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | 3 | $v_3$ | $v_1 \to 1$ | $\{1\}$ | **2** | | 4 | $v_4$ | $v_2 \to 2,\ v_3 \to 2$ | $\{2\}$ | **1** | | 5 | $v_5$ | $v_1 \to 1,\ v_3 \to 2$ | $\{1, 2\}$ | **3** | | 6 | $v_6$ | $v_4 \to 1,\ v_5 \to 3$ | $\{1, 3\}$ | **2** | **Colors used: 3.** Since the graph contains odd cycle $v_1 \to v_3 \to v_5 \to v_1$ (length 3), $\chi(G) \ge 3$. Hence the greedy result is optimal: $\chi(G) = 3$. ✓ **Part (b): Proof that $\chi(T) = 2$ for any tree $T$ with $n \ge 2$.** **Step 1: Show $\chi(T) \ge 2$.** Any tree with $n \ge 2$ contains at least one edge. The two endpoints of that edge are adjacent and must receive different colors. Hence at least 2 colors are required: $\chi(T) \ge 2$. **Step 2: Show $\chi(T) \le 2$ via 2-coloring construction.** - Pick any vertex $r$ as root. - Perform BFS/DFS from $r$. Assign color 1 to all vertices at even depth, color 2 to all vertices at odd depth. - Consider any edge $(u, v)$. In a tree, every edge connects a vertex at depth $d$ with a vertex at depth $d+1$ (no cycles). Therefore $u$ and $v$ have different parities of depth, hence different colors. - This yields a proper 2-coloring. **Step 3: Conclude.** From Steps 1 and 2: $$\chi(T) = 2 \quad \text{for any tree with } n \ge 2. \blacksquare$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Modified]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the chromatic polynomial $P_G(k)$. Compute $P_G(k)$ for the cycle graph $C_4$ using the deletion-contraction method. **(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the time complexity of graph coloring as a decision problem. Why is it classified as NP-Complete for $k \ge 3$? --- #### **Model Solution — Question B:** **Part (a):** The **chromatic polynomial** $P_G(k)$ of a graph $G$ is the function (in variable $k$) that counts the number of distinct proper vertex colorings of $G$ using colors from a palette of $k$ colors. **Computing $P_{C_4}(k)$:** Let $G = C_4$ with edges in cyclic order $(v_1,v_2), (v_2,v_3), (v_3,v_4), (v_4,v_1)$. Pick edge $e = (v_1, v_2)$. **Case 1 — $G - e$:** Deleting $e$ gives a path $v_1 - v_3 - v_4 - v_2$ of length 3 (4 vertices, 3 edges). For any tree on $n$ vertices: $P_T(k) = k(k-1)^{n-1}$. $$P_{G-e}(k) = k(k-1)^3$$ **Case 2 — $G / e$:** Contracting $e$ merges $v_1$ and $v_2$ into a single vertex $w$. The resulting graph has vertices $\{w, v_3, v_4\}$ and edges $\{w, v_3\}, \{v_3, v_4\}, \{v_4, w\}$ — which is a triangle $K_3$. $$P_{G/e}(k) = k(k-1)(k-2)$$ **Apply deletion-contraction:** $$\begin{aligned} P_{C_4}(k) &= P_{G-e}(k) - P_{G/e}(k) \\ &= k(k-1)^3 - k(k-1)(k-2) \\ &= k(k-1)\left[(k-1)^2 - (k-2)\right] \\ &= k(k-1)\left[k^2 - 2k + 1 - k + 2\right] \\ &= k(k-1)(k^2 - 3k + 3) \end{aligned}$$ **Verification at $k=2$:** $P_{C_4}(2) = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot (4 - 6 + 3) = 2 \cdot 1 \cdot 1 = 2$. Indeed, $C_4$ has exactly 2 proper 2-colorings (alternating patterns). ✓ **Part (b):** The decision problem **"Given a graph $G$ and integer $k$, is $G$ k-colorable?"** has the following complexity profile: - **For $k = 1$:** Trivially solvable — $G$ must be edgeless. Time $O(1)$ given $\vert E \vert$. - **For $k = 2$:** Equivalent to checking if $G$ is **bipartite**, which is solvable in $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ using BFS/DFS 2-coloring. - **For $k \ge 3$:** The problem is **NP-Complete**. **Why NP-Complete for $k \ge 3$?** 1. **Membership in NP:** Given a proposed coloring $c$, verification that $c$ is proper takes $O(\vert E \vert)$ time — we just check every edge. 2. **NP-Hardness:** Karp (1972) showed that **3-SAT** (a canonical NP-Complete problem) **polynomial-time reduces** to **3-Colorability**. The reduction constructs a graph $G$ whose variables and clauses are encoded as gadgets (variable gadgets, clause gadgets, edge gadgets) such that $G$ is 3-colorable **if and only if** the original 3-SAT formula is satisfiable. 3. **Implication:** Unless **P = NP**, there exists no polynomial-time algorithm that solves $k$-coloring for $k \ge 3$. Practical algorithms (Greedy, DSATUR, Backtracking, ILP formulations) are heuristic or exponential. **Time Complexities of Practical Approaches:** | Approach | Time Complexity | |---|---| | Greedy | $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ | | Backtracking (exact) | $O(k^{\vert V \vert})$ | | DSATUR heuristic | $O(\vert V \vert^2)$ | | Chromatic polynomial | Exponential in $\vert V \vert$ | --- > [!WARNING] > **🔴 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Confusing $\chi(G)$ with $\omega(G)$:** Chromatic number is *not* equal to clique number in general — equality only holds for perfect graphs (König's Theorem, Chudnovsky et al.). Students who write $\chi(G) = \omega(G)$ for arbitrary graphs lose full marks on proof questions. > 2. **Forgetting Brooks' equality cases:** Always state that equality $\chi(G) = \Delta(G) + 1$ holds ONLY for complete graphs and odd cycles. Omitting this loses 2 marks. > 3. **Deletion-contraction sign error:** The correct recursion is $P_G(k) = P_{G-e}(k) - P_{G/e}(k)$. Reversing the order gives wrong polynomial. This is a frequent 3-mark deduction. > 4. **Claiming Greedy is optimal:** The greedy algorithm does NOT always yield $\chi(G)$ — it can over-color by $O(\log n)$ colors in pathological orderings. Always qualify the answer. > 5. **Skipping the lower bound:** When asked to find $\chi(G)$, students jump to a coloring without proving $\chi(G) \ge k$ via odd cycles, cliques, or Brooks' theorem. --- ### 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Graph Coloring (Module 4)** - **Definition:** Proper coloring $c : V \to \{1, \dots, k\}$ with $c(u) \neq c(v)$ for every $(u,v) \in E$. - **Chromatic Number $\chi(G)$:** Minimum $k$ for a proper coloring. - **Key Theorems:** * Four Color Theorem — $\chi(\text{planar}) \le 4$. * Brooks' Theorem — $\chi(G) \le \Delta(G)$ unless $G = K_n$ or odd cycle. * Vizing's Theorem — $\Delta(G) \le \chi'(G) \le \Delta(G) + 1$ for edge coloring. - **Special Graphs:** $\chi(K_n) = n$; $\chi(\text{tree}) = 2$; $\chi(C_{2k}) = 2$; $\chi(C_{2k+1}) = 3$. - **Chromatic Polynomial:** $P_G(k) = P_{G-e}(k) - P_{G/e}(k)$. - **Algorithmic Complexity:** * 2-coloring — $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$. * $k$-coloring for $k \ge 3$ — **NP-Complete**. - **Greedy Coloring:** $O(\vert V \vert + \vert E \vert)$ but not optimal. - **Applications:** Map coloring, exam timetabling, register allocation, frequency assignment, Sudoku, VLSI routing. - **Lower Bounds Always Required:** When asked $\chi(G)$, prove $\chi(G) \ge k$ via cliques, odd cycles, or Brooks' complement. - **KTU Marks Pattern:** Part A — direct definitions (2 marks); Part B — apply algorithm + prove theorem (14 marks split 7+7). ---

topics.title: Hamiltonian Cycles. Branch and Bound - General Method
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hamiltonian Cycles. Branch and Bound - General Method

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Hamiltonian Cycles & Branch and Bound: Foundational Concepts

## 1.1 Hamiltonian Cycle — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Rigor-Grade):**
