> A **Hamiltonian Cycle (HC)** in a connected, undirected graph $G = (V, E)$ is a simple cycle that visits **every vertex exactly once** and returns to the starting vertex. A graph possessing at least one such cycle is called a **Hamiltonian Graph**.

Formally, a Hamiltonian cycle is a sequence of vertices:

$$v_1, v_2, v_3, \ldots, v_n, v_1$$

such that every vertex $v \in V$ appears **exactly once** in the sequence, and each consecutive pair $(v_i, v_{i+1})$ is an edge in $E$ for $1 \le i \le n-1$, with the closing edge $(v_n, v_1) \in E$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Origin & Historical Note:** Named after the Irish mathematician **Sir William Rowan Hamilton** (1805–1865), who introduced the problem in 1856 via his *Icosian Game*. Unlike the famous Eulerian cycle (which traverses every *edge* once), the Hamiltonian cycle traverses every *vertex* once.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Tourist Who Visits Every City Once"

Imagine a **traveling tourist** who lands in a city, must visit every other city in the country **exactly once** (no revisits, no skipping), and then **fly back home** to the starting city. The question the tourist asks is:

> *"Does there exist a closed route that touches every city exactly once, using only the available direct flights?"*

- The **cities** = vertices of the graph.
- The **direct flight paths** = edges of the graph.
- The **tourist's complete tour** = Hamiltonian cycle.
- If the tourist wants the **cheapest possible tour** (each flight has a cost), then the problem transforms into the famous **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP)** — which is the weighted version of HC.

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision vs Optimization:**
> - **Decision form:** *"Does a Hamiltonian cycle exist in $G$?"* — **NP-Complete** (proved by Karp, 1972).
> - **Optimization form:** *"Find the minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle in a weighted graph"* — the **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP)** — **NP-Hard**.

## 1.3 Branch and Bound — Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> **Branch and Bound (B&B)** is a general algorithmic paradigm designed to solve **combinatorial optimization problems** (NP-Hard). It systematically enumerates candidate solutions by means of **state space tree** exploration, using **bounding functions** to discard large subsets of fruitless candidates, thereby achieving significant pruning of the search space.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The "Smart Treasure Hunt"

Think of a **treasure hunter** exploring a vast mansion with hundreds of rooms. Each room branches into more rooms, and the goal is to find a path that **minimizes total cost** (time, fuel, etc.) to reach the treasure.

- A **naïve** hunter explores **every single room** (exhaustive search — exponential time).
- A **smart B&B hunter** keeps a running **best-cost-so-far** estimate and a **lower-bound estimate** for any unexplored branch.
- The moment the **lower bound** of an unexplored branch **exceeds** the best known solution, the hunter **prunes** that branch and walks away.
- This is the essence of **branch (split), bound (estimate), prune (discard)**.

## 1.5 The Three Pillars of Branch and Bound

| Pillar | Description | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| **Branching** | Split the problem into 2 or more disjoint sub-problems (state space tree expansion). | Reduce a hard problem into smaller, manageable instances. |
| **Bounding** | Compute a **lower bound** (for minimization) or **upper bound** (for maximization) on the best achievable solution within that sub-problem. | Provide a *promise* of what the subtree can deliver. |
| **Pruning** | Discard a node whose bound is **worse** than the current best-known feasible solution. | Eliminate provably inferior candidates without exploration. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hamiltonian Cycle — Theoretical Properties

### 2.1.1 Necessary vs Sufficient Conditions

Unlike Eulerian graphs, **no simple necessary and sufficient condition** is known for a graph to be Hamiltonian. However, several **sufficient conditions** exist:

> **Ore's Theorem (1960):** If a graph $G$ on $n \ge 3$ vertices satisfies
> $$\deg(u) + \deg(v) \ge n \quad \forall u, v \in V, \; u \neq v, \; (u, v) \notin E$$
> then $G$ is Hamiltonian.

> **Dirac's Theorem (1952):** If a graph $G$ on $n \ge 3$ vertices has minimum degree
> $$\delta(G) \ge \frac{n}{2}$$
> then $G$ is Hamiltonian.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Quick Memory Aid:**
> - **Dirac** → "Degree condition alone suffices" (≥ n/2).
> - **Ore** → "Sum of non-adjacent degrees suffices" (≥ n).

### 2.1.2 Complexity Classification

| Problem Variant | Complexity Class | Decision Form |
|---|---|---|
| Hamiltonian Cycle (unweighted) | **NP-Complete** | YES/NO existence |
| TSP (weighted, optimization) | **NP-Hard** | — |
| Hamiltonian Path | **NP-Complete** | YES/NO existence |
| Hamiltonian Cycle on Directed Graphs | **NP-Complete** | YES/NO existence |

## 2.2 Branch and Bound — Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 State Space Tree (Live Nodes vs Dead Nodes vs E-Node)

In the state space tree, every node can be in one of three states:

- **Live Node:** A node that has been generated but whose children have not yet been explored. It *may* still lead to an optimal solution.
- **Dead Node:** A node that has been **pruned** (its children will never be explored) because it cannot yield a better solution than the current best.
- **E-Node (Expanding Node):** A live node whose children are currently being **expanded** (generated).

### 2.2.2 Three Search Strategies in Branch and Bound

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 — Direct Exam Question Zone:** Students must memorize these three strategies and their associated data structures.

| Strategy | Order of Node Exploration | Data Structure Used | Cost |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FIFO Branch and Bound** | Breadth-First (oldest first) | **Queue** | Lower memory; possibly more exploration |
| **LIFO Branch and Bound** | Depth-First (newest first) | **Stack** | Fast discovery of deep solutions; risk of going down wrong path |
| **LC-Search (Least-Cost Search)** | Always expand node with **smallest lower bound** | **Priority Queue (Min-Heap)** | **Optimal node selection**, fewer expanded nodes |

### 2.2.3 General Algorithm Skeleton

The general B&B algorithm for **minimization** works as follows:

1. Initialize: Compute the **initial lower bound** $\hat{c}$ of the root node.
2. Place the root in the list of live nodes (queue/stack/heap depending on strategy).
3. **Repeat** until the live node list is empty:
   a. Select a live node $X$ using the chosen strategy (E-Node).
   b. If the lower bound of $X$ is $\ge$ current best feasible solution $U$ (upper bound) → **prune** $X$ (becomes dead).
   c. Otherwise, **branch**: generate all children of $X$.
   d. For each child $Y$:
      - Compute lower bound $\hat{c}(Y)$.
      - If $\hat{c}(Y) \ge U$ → prune $Y$.
      - Else if $Y$ represents a **complete feasible solution** → update $U \leftarrow \hat{c}(Y)$.
      - Else add $Y$ to the live node list.
4. Return $U$ as the optimal solution.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & High-Yield Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam-Day Cheat Sheet (memorize before entering the hall):**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hamiltonian Cycle Length | $n$ vertices visited, $n$ edges traversed | Returns to start |
| 2 | Hamiltonian Path | $n$ vertices, $n-1$ edges, no return | Not a cycle |
| 3 | Dirac's Theorem (sufficient) | $\delta(G) \ge n/2$ | For $n \ge 3$ |
| 4 | Ore's Theorem (sufficient) | $\deg(u)+\deg(v) \ge n$ | For every non-adjacent pair |
| 5 | State Space Tree — Node Count | Up to $2^n$ (subset) or $n!$ (permutation) | Exponential worst case |
| 6 | B&B Lower Bound (TSP) | Sum of row minima + column minima of reduced cost matrix | Matrix reduction technique |
| 7 | Pruning Condition (min) | $\hat{c}(Y) \ge U_{\text{best}}$ | Discard node $Y$ |
| 8 | TSP Branch Factor | At each node, branch by choosing next unvisited vertex | $n-1$ branches from root |
| 9 | LC-Search Selection | Always pick live node with minimum $\hat{c}$ | Uses min-heap |
| 10 | TSP Complexity (naïve) | $O(n!)$ | Enumerate all tours |
| 11 | TSP Complexity (DP, Held–Karp) | $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ | Pseudo-polynomial |
| 12 | B&B Worst Case | $O(n!)$ | No pruning possible |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix: TSP via Branch and Bound

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this section is critical for KTU 2024:**
> The **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP) solved via Branch and Bound using the Reduced Cost Matrix technique** is the **single most frequently asked 14-mark question** in this module. The exact step-by-step methodology below has appeared in past KTU university examinations with high repetition.

## 3.1 The Reduced Cost Matrix Method — Derivation

Given a cost matrix $C$ of size $n \times n$ (entry $C[i][j]$ = cost to travel from city $i$ to city $j$), the **lower bound** of a node is computed through two-stage matrix reduction:

### 3.1.1 Step 1 — Row Reduction

For each row $i$, find the **minimum element** $r_i = \min_j C[i][j]$. Subtract $r_i$ from every entry in row $i$:

$$C'[i][j] = C[i][j] - r_i \quad \forall j$$

The sum of all row reductions gives a partial lower bound:

$$\text{LB}_{\text{row}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i$$

### 3.1.2 Step 2 — Column Reduction

After row reduction, for each column $j$, find the **minimum element** $c_j = \min_i C'[i][j]$. Subtract $c_j$ from every entry in column $j$:

$$C''[i][j] = C'[i][j] - c_j \quad \forall i$$

The total lower bound at this node is:

$$\hat{c} = \text{LB}_{\text{row}} + \text{LB}_{\text{col}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i + \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j$$

### 3.1.3 Why This Works (Intuitive Proof)

> **Reasoning:** In **any** Hamiltonian tour starting at vertex $v_1$, the salesman must leave $v_1$ via some outgoing edge. The minimum outgoing cost from $v_1$ is at least $r_1$. Similarly for every other vertex. Hence $\sum r_i$ is a valid lower bound on any tour's cost. Column reduction refines this bound further by accounting for the fact that the salesman must **enter** every vertex exactly once, contributing an additional minimum per column.

## 3.2 The Branching Step — Selecting an Edge

After reduction, we pick the **most promising edge to branch on**. Standard procedure:

1. For each entry $C''[i][j] = 0$ (the "zero-cost" edges), compute the **promise** of forcing this edge:
$$\text{promise}(i, j) = \text{(min in row } i, \text{ excluding col } j) + \text{(min in col } j, \text{ excluding row } i)$$
2. Pick the edge $(i, j)$ with **maximum promise** (i.e., most expensive to bypass).
3. **Branch:**
   - **Branch 1 (Include edge):** Set row $i$ and column $j$ to $\infty$ (invalidate them), set $C''[j][i] = \infty$ (prevent return edge, since tour is a cycle). Lower bound: $\hat{c}_{\text{include}} = \hat{c} + \text{promise}(i,j)$.
   - **Branch 2 (Exclude edge):** Set $C''[i][j] = \infty$. Re-reduce matrix. Lower bound: $\hat{c}_{\text{exclude}} = $ new reduction sum.

## 3.3 Worked-Out Example (KTU Exam-Standard)

**Problem:** Solve TSP for the following 4-city cost matrix using Branch and Bound:

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 20 & 30 & 10 \\ 15 & \infty & 16 & 4 \\ 3 & 5 & \infty & 2 \\ 19 & 6 & 18 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 1 — Row Reduction:**

Row minima: $r_1 = 10$, $r_2 = 4$, $r_3 = 2$, $r_4 = 6$.

$$C' = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 10 & 20 & 0 \\ 11 & \infty & 12 & 0 \\ 1 & 3 & \infty & 0 \\ 13 & 0 & 12 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

$\text{LB}_{\text{row}} = 10 + 4 + 2 + 6 = 22$.

**Step 2 — Column Reduction:**

Column minima of $C'$: $c_1 = 1$, $c_2 = 0$, $c_3 = 12$, $c_4 = 0$.

$$C'' = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 10 & 8 & 0 \\ 10 & \infty & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & 3 & \infty & 0 \\ 12 & 0 & 0 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

$\text{LB}_{\text{col}} = 1 + 0 + 12 + 0 = 13$.

**Root Lower Bound:** $\hat{c}_{\text{root}} = 22 + 13 = 35$.

**Step 3 — Branching on the Edge with Maximum Promise:**

We examine each zero entry $(i,j)$ and compute the promise:

| Edge $(i,j)$ | Min row $i$ (excl. $j$) | Min col $j$ (excl. $i$) | Promise |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,4)$ | 8 | 0 | 8 |
| $(2,3)$ | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| $(2,4)$ | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| $(3,1)$ | 0 | 10 | 10 |
| $(3,4)$ | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| $(4,2)$ | 0 | 0 | **0** |
| $(4,3)$ | 0 | 8 | 8 |

The edge with the **maximum promise** is $(3, 1)$ with promise = $0 + 10 = 10$.

**Step 4 — Create Two Branches:**

**Branch A (Include edge 3→1):**
- Set row 3 and column 1 to $\infty$.
- Set entry $(1, 3) = \infty$ (no return).
- Lower bound = $35 + 10 = 45$.

**Branch B (Exclude edge 3→1):**
- Set $C''[3][1] = \infty$.
- Re-reduce matrix.
- New lower bound = $35 + 0 = 35$ (no additional reduction needed since the matrix is already reduced).

**Step 5 — Expand the Smaller Bound (Branch B, $\hat{c} = 35$):**

Repeat the process recursively. The search continues until all branches are pruned or a complete tour is found with a finite cost. The **minimum-cost Hamiltonian cycle** is the optimal TSP tour.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The full recursion tree is lengthy for 4+ cities. KTU 2024 typically provides a **4-city matrix** and asks the student to compute the **first 2–3 levels** of the B&B tree, identify the **live nodes**, and bound the next search.

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: B&B Strategy Selection

| Scenario | Best B&B Strategy | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Deep, narrow search space with cheap deep solutions | **LIFO (Stack)** | Fast descent, low memory |
| Shallow, wide search space; solution expected near root | **FIFO (Queue)** | Breadth-wise exploration |
| Optimal node ordering required; cost-aware pruning | **LC-Search (Min-Heap)** | Best pruning efficiency |
| Very small $n$ ($\le 6$); memory abundant | Any | Trivial difference |
| Asymmetric TSP with $n \ge 10$ | **LC-Search** | Industry standard |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 State Space Tree for TSP with 4 Cities (Visualizing B&B Expansion)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA["Root Node: Cost Matrix C, LB=35"]
    branchA1["Branch A: INCLUDE edge 3 to 1, LB=45"]
    branchA2["Branch B: EXCLUDE edge 3 to 1, LB=35"]
    
    expandB1["Expand B: Pick max-promise edge, INCLUDE 4 to 2, LB=42"]
    expandB2["Expand B: Pick max-promise edge, EXCLUDE 4 to 2, LB=35"]
    
    leafTour["Leaf: Complete Tour 1 to 4 to 2 to 3 to 1, Cost=35"]
    leafPrune["Pruned: LB exceeds current best 35"]
    leafTour2["Leaf: Complete Tour Alternative, Cost=38"]
    
    rootA --> branchA1
    rootA --> branchA2
    branchA2 --> expandB1
    branchA2 --> expandB2
    expandB2 --> leafTour
    expandB2 --> leafTour2
    expandB1 --> leafPrune
    branchA1 --> leafPrune
```

## 4.2 The Branch and Bound Operational Pipeline (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    inputBlock["Input: Cost Matrix C of size n x n"]
    reduceBlock["Step 1: Matrix Reduction (Row + Column)"]
    lbCompute["Step 2: Compute Lower Bound c-hat"]
    branchSelect["Step 3: Select Branching Edge (max promise)"]
    includeNode["Step 4a: INCLUDE Branch Node"]
    excludeNode["Step 4b: EXCLUDE Branch Node"]
    
    checkLeaf{"Step 5: Is node a complete tour?"}
    pruneCheck{"Step 6: c-hat node greater than U-best?"}
    
    updateBest["Step 7: Update U-best if feasible tour found"]
    pruneAction["Step 8: Prune this node"]
    expandNext["Step 9: Push/Pop from Live Node list"]
    
    finalOutput["Step 10: Output Optimal Tour and Cost U-best"]
    
    inputBlock --> reduceBlock --> lbCompute --> branchSelect
    branchSelect --> includeNode
    branchSelect --> excludeNode
    includeNode --> checkLeaf
    excludeNode --> checkLeaf
    checkLeaf -- "YES" --> updateBest --> expandNext
    checkLeaf -- "NO" --> pruneCheck
    pruneCheck -- "YES (c-hat greater than U-best)" --> pruneAction --> expandNext
    pruneCheck -- "NO (promising)" --> expandNext
    expandNext --> finalOutput
```

## 4.3 The Pruning Decision Flowchart (LC-Search View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startNode["Live Node X with c-hat X"]
    currentBest["Current U-best (best known feasible solution)"]
    
    compareOp{"Is c-hat X greater than or equal to U-best?"}
    expandDecision["Expand X (generate children)"]
    pruneFinal["Mark X as DEAD (pruned)"]
    
    liveList["Insert/Keep X in Min-Heap of live nodes"]
    popMin["Extract-Min from heap (next E-node)"]
    emptyCheck{"Heap empty?"}
    
    outputTour["Output: U-best is optimal Hamiltonian Cycle"]
    haltOp["HALT"]
    
    startNode --> compareOp
    currentBest --> compareOp
    compareOp -- "NO" --> liveList --> popMin --> emptyCheck
    compareOp -- "YES" --> pruneFinal
    emptyCheck -- "NO" --> expandDecision
    emptyCheck -- "YES" --> outputTour --> haltOp
    expandDecision --> startNode
```

## 4.4 Comparative Schematic: Three B&B Strategies

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FIFO["FIFO Branch and Bound"]
        f1["Queue: Front at position 0"]
        f2["Insert: Rear append"]
        f3["Delete: Front dequeue"]
    end
    
    subgraph LIFO["LIFO Branch and Bound"]
        l1["Stack: Top pointer"]
        l2["Insert: Push to top"]
        l3["Delete: Pop from top"]
    end
    
    subgraph LC["LC-Search (Least-Cost)"]
        c1["Min-Heap: Key is c-hat value"]
        c2["Insert: Heapify-up"]
        c3["Delete: Extract-min c-hat node"]
    end
    
    rootCompare["Root Problem Instance"]
    rootCompare --> FIFO
    rootCompare --> LIFO
    rootCompare --> LC
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A1, 3 Marks]**
> **Q1.** Define a Hamiltonian cycle. How does it differ from an Eulerian cycle?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Hamiltonian cycle** in a graph $G = (V, E)$ is a closed walk that visits **every vertex exactly once** and returns to the starting vertex, traversing exactly $n = \vert V \vert$ edges.

The differences are:

| Feature | Hamiltonian Cycle | Eulerian Cycle |
|---|---|---|
| Visits every **vertex** exactly once? | ✅ Yes | ❌ No (may visit vertices multiple times) |
| Traverses every **edge** exactly once? | ❌ No (only $n$ edges used) | ✅ Yes ($\vert E \vert$ edges used) |
| Existence Check | NP-Complete (no simple condition) | Polynomial (Euler's theorem: all degrees even) |
| Named after | Sir William Rowan Hamilton | Leonhard Euler |

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question A2, 3 Marks]**
> **Q2.** List and briefly explain the three search strategies used in Branch and Bound.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **FIFO (First-In-First-Out) B&B:** Uses a **queue**. Live nodes are expanded in the order they are generated (breadth-first order). Simple to implement, moderate memory.
2. **LIFO (Last-In-First-Out) B&B:** Uses a **stack**. Live nodes are expanded in reverse order of generation (depth-first order). Quick discovery of deep solutions but may miss the optimal path.
3. **LC-Search (Least-Cost Search):** Uses a **priority queue (min-heap)** keyed by lower bound $\hat{c}$. Always expands the live node with the **smallest lower bound**, yielding the most efficient pruning.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks)

**Q-A(a).** [7 Marks] Explain the **general method of Branch and Bound** with a clear state space tree and a discussion of bounding functions.

**Model Answer:**

The **Branch and Bound (B&B)** method is a general algorithmic paradigm for solving discrete optimization problems. It works as follows:

**1. Branching:** The problem is recursively decomposed into sub-problems. The decomposition is represented as a **state space tree**, where the root represents the original problem and each child represents a restricted sub-problem.

**2. Bounding:** For every node $X$ in the tree, a **bounding function** $\hat{c}(X)$ computes:
- A **lower bound** (for minimization problems), which is a value $\le$ the optimal cost achievable from that node.
- An **upper bound** (for maximization problems), which is a value $\ge$ the optimal.

**3. Pruning:** Two pruning rules:
- If $\hat{c}(X) \ge U_{\text{best}}$ (current best feasible cost) for a minimization problem → **prune $X$**.
- If $X$ cannot lead to a feasible solution (e.g., subtour detection in TSP) → **prune $X$**.

**State Space Tree Diagram (conceptual):**

```
                [Root: P, LB = 0]
                /        |        \
           [P1, LB=5] [P2, LB=2] [P3, LB=8]
           /    \       |
        [...]  [...]  [P22, LB=4]  ← (prune P3: LB=8 ≥ 4)
                      /     \
                 [Complete tour, cost=4]  ← Update U-best
```

**Key Data Structures:**
- **Queue** for FIFO, **Stack** for LIFO, **Min-Heap** for LC-Search.
- Three node states: **Live** (not yet expanded), **E-Node** (currently being expanded), **Dead** (pruned or fully explored).

**Q-A(b).** [7 Marks] Differentiate between Branch and Bound and Backtracking. When would you prefer one over the other?

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Backtracking | Branch and Bound |
|---|---|---|
| **Problem type** | Satisfies constraints (feasibility) | Optimization (finds min/max cost) |
| **Pruning criterion** | Constraint violation | Bound worse than best known solution |
| **Bound function** | Not required | Mandatory (lower/upper bound) |
| **Search order** | Depth-first (LIFO stack) | Any (FIFO/LIFO/LC) |
| **Output** | Any/all feasible solutions | Optimal solution with cost |
| **Typical use** | N-Queens, Graph Coloring, Sudoku | TSP, 0/1 Knapsack, Assignment |
| **Complexity** | Can be exponential | Can be exponential, but tighter pruning |

**When to prefer which:**
- Use **Backtracking** when the problem asks *"does a solution exist?"* or *"find all solutions"* (e.g., N-Queens).
- Use **Branch and Bound** when the problem asks *"find the best (cheapest/shortest/maximum) solution"* (e.g., TSP, Assignment Problem).

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**Q-B(a).** [7 Marks] State and prove **Dirac's Theorem** on Hamiltonian graphs. Is the condition necessary?

**Model Answer:**

**Dirac's Theorem (1952):** *If $G$ is a simple graph with $n \ge 3$ vertices and the minimum degree satisfies $\delta(G) \ge n/2$, then $G$ is Hamiltonian.*

**Proof Sketch (using closure/extension arguments):**

Assume for contradiction that $G$ satisfies $\delta(G) \ge n/2$ but is **not** Hamiltonian. Then $G$ has a maximal non-Hamiltonian path $P = v_1, v_2, \ldots, v_n$. Since $P$ is maximal, both $v_1$ and $v_n$ are **not adjacent** to each other (else cycle exists, and due to $\delta(G) \ge n/2$ we can extend the cycle to a Hamiltonian one — by pigeonhole on neighbors). The neighbors of $v_1$ and $v_n$ each lie strictly in the interior of $P$, and the cardinalities $\vert N(v_1)\vert \ge n/2$ and $\vert N(v_n)\vert \ge n/2$ exceed $n-1$ available interior vertices, forcing a contradiction via a counting/pigeonhole argument on index overlaps. Hence $G$ must be Hamiltonian.

**Is the condition necessary?**

**No.** Dirac's condition is **sufficient but not necessary**. There exist Hamiltonian graphs with $\delta(G) < n/2$.

**Counterexample:** The cycle graph $C_5$ (pentagon) is Hamiltonian with $n=5$ and $\delta(C_5) = 2 < n/2 = 2.5$. So $C_5$ is Hamiltonian despite not satisfying Dirac's strict condition.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall (lose up to 2 marks):**
> Many students write "Dirac's Theorem is necessary and sufficient" — this is **wrong**. The theorem is **only sufficient**. Similarly, students often confuse $\delta(G) \ge n/2$ (Dirac) with $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \ge n$ (Ore). **Memorize both separately.**

**Q-B(b).** [7 Marks] Solve the following TSP instance using Branch and Bound with the **reduced cost matrix** technique:

$$C = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 5 & \infty & 9 & 10 \\ 6 & 13 & \infty & 12 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

Show the state space tree up to two levels and identify the optimal tour.

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

**Level 0 — Root Reduction:**

Row reduction (subtract row minimums $r_1=10, r_2=5, r_3=6, r_4=8$):

$$C' = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & \infty & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & 7 & \infty & 6 \\ 0 & 0 & 1 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

Column reduction (column minimums after row reduction: $c_1=0, c_2=0, c_3=1, c_4=5$):

$$C'' = \begin{pmatrix} \infty & 0 & 4 & 5 \\ 0 & \infty & 3 & 0 \\ 0 & 7 & \infty & 1 \\ 0 & 0 & 0 & \infty \end{pmatrix}$$

$\hat{c}_{\text{root}} = (10+5+6+8) + (0+0+1+5) = 29 + 6 = \mathbf{35}$.

**Branching on edge with max promise:**

Compute promise for each zero entry:

| Edge | Row min (excl.) | Col min (excl.) | Promise |
|---|---|---|---|
| $(1,2)$ | 4 | 0 | 4 |
| $(2,1)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $(2,4)$ | 0 | 1 | 1 |
| $(3,1)$ | 1 | 0 | 1 |
| $(4,1)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $(4,2)$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| $(4,3)$ | 0 | 4 | 4 |

Maximum promise = **4** (ties at $(1,2)$ and $(4,3)$). Choose $(1,2)$.

**Branch A — Include edge $1 \to 2$:** $\hat{c}_A = 35 + 4 = 39$.
**Branch B — Exclude edge $1 \to 2$:** Set $C''[1][2] = \infty$, re-reduce → $\hat{c}_B = 35$ (no further reduction needed at this step, recurse).

Continuing this process recursively, the **optimal tour** is:

$$1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1, \quad \text{Total Cost} = 15 + 9 + 8 + 8 = \mathbf{40}$$

or any other tour with minimum cost after full B&B exploration.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in B&B TSP Questions:**
> 1. Forgetting to set the **reverse edge** to $\infty$ when including a directed edge (this prevents invalid two-way cycles).
> 2. Not re-reducing the matrix after setting an entry to $\infty$ in the "exclude" branch.
> 3. Confusing "include" and "exclude" cost computation: include adds the **promise**, exclude adds **0** plus new reductions.
> 4. Forgetting to check for **subtour formation** (e.g., 1→2→1 is invalid).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Read Before Every Exam Attempt:**

- ✅ A **Hamiltonian cycle** visits every **vertex** exactly once and returns; do not confuse with Eulerian (edges).
- ✅ Decision form of HC is **NP-Complete**; optimization form is the **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP)** which is **NP-Hard**.
- ✅ **Dirac's Theorem:** $\delta(G) \ge n/2 \Rightarrow G$ is Hamiltonian. (Sufficient, **not necessary**.)
- ✅ **Ore's Theorem:** $\deg(u) + \deg(v) \ge n$ for all non-adjacent $u, v$ $\Rightarrow G$ is Hamiltonian.
- ✅ **Branch and Bound** is for **optimization**; **Backtracking** is for **feasibility**.
- ✅ Three search strategies: **FIFO (Queue)**, **LIFO (Stack)**, **LC-Search (Min-Heap / Priority Queue)**.
- ✅ LC-Search is the **most efficient** in terms of nodes expanded.
- ✅ State space tree node states: **Live**, **E-Node** (expanding), **Dead** (pruned).
- ✅ Pruning condition (min): If $\hat{c}(Y) \ge U_{\text{best}}$, prune $Y$.
- ✅ **Reduced cost matrix** for TSP: row reduction first, then column reduction; $\hat{c} = \sum r_i + \sum c_j$.
- ✅ Branch on the edge $(i,j)$ where the **promise** $\big(\text{min row } i \text{ excl. } j\big) + \big(\text{min col } j \text{ excl. } i\big)$ is **maximum**.
- ✅ In the "include" branch, set row $i$ and column $j$ to $\infty$ and reverse edge $(j, i) = \infty$ for directed TSP.
- ✅ In the "exclude" branch, set $C[i][j] = \infty$ and **re-reduce** the matrix.
- ✅ TSP naive complexity: $O(n!)$; Held–Karp DP: $O(n^2 \cdot 2^n)$ (pseudo-polynomial).
- ✅ Watch out for **subtour elimination** constraint: the tour must form a single cycle, not disjoint mini-cycles.

> **End of Notes — PECAT415 Module 4 — Hamiltonian Cycles & Branch and Bound**

topics.title: Travelling Salesperson Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Travelling Salesperson Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP) — KTU 2024 Definition**
> Given a set of $n$ cities and a cost (or distance) matrix $C = [c_{ij}]$ representing the cost of travelling from city $i$ to city $j$, the **Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP)** asks for the **shortest Hamiltonian cycle** — i.e., a tour that:
> 1. **Visits every city exactly once,**
> 2. **Returns to the starting city**, and
> 3. **Minimizes the total travel cost** $\sum c_{i_k, i_{k+1}}$ for the chosen sequence.

Mathematically, we seek a permutation $\pi$ of $\{1, 2, \dots, n\}$ that minimizes:
$$
\text{Cost}(\pi) \;=\; c_{\pi(1),\pi(2)} + c_{\pi(2),\pi(3)} + \dots + c_{\pi(n-1),\pi(n)} + c_{\pi(n),\pi(1)}
$$

If $c_{ij} = c_{ji}$ for all $i,j$, the problem is **symmetric TSP**; otherwise it is **asymmetric TSP (ATSP)**.

### Classification in the Complexity Hierarchy
| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| **Problem Class** | NP-Hard (optimization) / NP-Complete (decision form) |
| **Decision Form** | "Is there a tour of cost $\le K$?" — NP-Complete |
| **Brute-Force Cost** | $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ permutations |
| **Best Exact Algo.** | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ — Held–Karp DP |
| **Best Known Approx.** | $1.5$-approx. for metric TSP (Christofides, 1976) |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Kerala Postman"**
> Imagine a postal delivery driver in Kerala who must deliver parcels to **n different post offices** spread across districts (e.g., Thiruvananthapuram, Kochi, Kozhikode, Thrissur, Kannur). He starts from the central sorting hub in the morning, must visit **each office exactly once**, and return to the hub by evening. The total fuel cost is proportional to the total distance travelled. The TSP asks: *in which order should he visit the offices so that the total distance is minimum?*

For **n = 5 cities**, there are $(5-1)!/2 = 12$ unique tours (symmetric case). For **n = 20 cities**, there are about **$6 \times 10^{16}$** tours — more than the number of grains of sand on Earth. This is why TSP is the *flagship NP-Hard problem* in Algorithm Design.

---

## 1.3 Why TSP Matters in Engineering

- **Logistics & Supply Chain** — Amazon, FedEx, UPS route optimization.
- **VLSI Circuit Design** — Drilling holes in a printed circuit board.
- **DNA Sequencing** — Reconstructing genome order from fragments.
- **Robotics & Autonomous Vehicles** — Path planning for warehouse robots.
- **Astronomy** — Ordering telescope observations to minimize slewing time.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithmic Approaches — At a Glance

There are **three broad families** of algorithms for TSP, each trading off **optimality vs. time vs. space**:

| Approach | Optimality | Time | Space | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Brute Force (Exhaustive)** | Exact | $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n)$ | $n \le 10$ |
| **Dynamic Programming (Held–Karp)** | Exact | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot 2^{n})$ | $n \le 25$ |
| **Branch & Bound (Reduced Matrix)** | Exact | Worst $\mathcal{O}(n!)$, Avg. much faster | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ | $n \le 40$ |
| **Nearest Neighbour (Greedy)** | Approximate | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | Large $n$ |
| **MST-Based (2-Approximation)** | Approximate | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \log n)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ | Metric TSP, large $n$ |
| **Christofides Algorithm** | $1.5$-Approx. | Polynomial | Polynomial | Metric TSP |

---

## 2.2 Mathematical Formulation

### Decision Version (for NP-Completeness proof)
$$
\exists \text{ a tour } T \text{ with } \text{Cost}(T) \le K \text{ ?}
$$

### Optimization Version (ILP Form)
$$
\min \sum_{i=1}^{n}\sum_{j=1}^{n} c_{ij} \cdot x_{ij}
$$
subject to:
$$
\sum_{j=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} x_{ij} = 1, \quad x_{ij} \in \{0,1\}, \quad \text{(no sub-tours)}
$$

The "no sub-tour" constraints make the problem hard — they are exponential in number.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formulas — Memorize These for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Concept | Formula / Condition |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Tour cost (permutation form) | $\text{Cost}(\pi) = \sum_{k=1}^{n} c_{\pi(k),\pi((k \bmod n)+1)}$ |
| 2 | Symmetric tours count | $(n-1)!/2$ |
| 3 | Asymmetric tours count | $(n-1)!$ |
| 4 | Held–Karp subproblem | $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S}\bigl[g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{ji}\bigr]$ |
| 5 | Held–Karp base case | $g(i, \emptyset) = c_{1,i}$ |
| 6 | Final Held–Karp cost | $g(1, V) = \min_{j \neq 1}\bigl[g(j, V \setminus \{1\}) + c_{j,1}\bigr]$ |
| 7 | Nearest Neighbour cost bound | $\text{Cost}_{\text{NN}} \le \lceil \log_{2} n \rceil \cdot \text{OPT}$ (general) |
| 8 | MST 2-Approximation bound | $\text{Cost}_{\text{MST-tour}} \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$ |
| 9 | Christofides bound | $\text{Cost}_{\text{Christofides}} \le 1.5 \cdot \text{OPT}$ (metric) |
| 10 | Triangle inequality (metric) | $c_{i,k} \le c_{i,j} + c_{j,k}$ for all $i,j,k$ |
| 11 | Lower Bound (root node) | $\text{LB} = \sum (\text{row mins}) + \sum (\text{column mins})$ |
| 12 | Edge (i,j) new LB | $\text{LB}_{i \to j} = \text{LB}_{\text{current}} + c_{ij} + r_{i} + c_{j}$ |
| 13 | Total tour edges | Exactly $n$ edges in any Hamiltonian cycle |
| 14 | Any tour has degree | $\deg(v) = 2$ for every vertex $v$ |

> **Legend:** $g(i, S)$ = minimum cost of a path starting at city $1$, visiting all cities in set $S$, ending at city $i$. $\;V$ = set of all cities.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Operations Research** — Vehicle Routing Problem (VRP) is a generalization.
- **Compiler Design** — Instruction scheduling across pipelines.
- **Network Design** — Topology of low-latency networks.
- **Manufacturing** — Minimizing tool movement on CNC machines.
- **Bioinformatics** — Genome assembly (overlap-layout-consensus paradigm).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Reduced-Cost-Matrix Method (Branch & Bound)

This is the most-frequently-asked technique in **KTU 2024 ESE Module 4** papers. We derive it step-by-step.

### 3.1.1 Why Reduce?
A cost matrix entry $c_{ij}$ tells us the *actual* cost. If we subtract a constant $r_i$ from row $i$ and a constant $c_j$ from column $j$ such that every entry stays $\ge 0$, then **the difference between any two tours is unchanged** — only the *absolute* numbers shift. We can subtract the *minimum* of each row/column to get a "tighter" matrix with many zeros.

### 3.1.2 The Reduction Algorithm

**Step 1 — Row Reduction.** For every row $i$, find $r_i = \min_{j} c_{ij}$. Subtract $r_i$ from every entry in row $i$. Define:
$$
R_{\text{row}} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} r_i
$$

**Step 2 — Column Reduction.** For every column $j$ (of the *row-reduced* matrix), find $c_j = \min_{i} c_{ij}$. Subtract $c_j$ from every entry in column $j$. Define:
$$
R_{\text{col}} = \sum_{j=1}^{n} c_j
$$

**Step 3 — Root Lower Bound.**
$$
\text{LB}_{\text{root}} = R_{\text{row}} + R_{\text{col}}
$$

### 3.1.3 Branching on an Edge (i, j)

When we commit to edge $(i, j)$ in the tour, we set row $i$ and column $j$ to $\infty$ (city $i$ has left, city $j$ has been reached). To prevent the reverse edge $j \to i$, we set $c_{ji} = \infty$.

Then we **reduce the modified matrix again** to obtain a new lower bound. Equivalently, the new lower bound can be computed without re-reducing:
$$
\text{LB}_{i \to j} = \text{LB}_{\text{parent}} + c_{ij} + r_{i}^{\text{new}} + c_{j}^{\text{new}}
$$
where $r_{i}^{\text{new}}$ and $c_{j}^{\text{new}}$ are the *additional* row/column minimums needed after the modification. (If a row/column is *already* all zeros except $\infty$, $r_i^{\text{new}} = 0$.)

### 3.1.4 Complete Worked Example (4-City Symmetric TSP)

Let the cost matrix be:
$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & \infty & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & \infty & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

We are starting from city $1$.

#### Phase A — Root Reduction

**Step A.1 — Row Reduction:**
- Row 1: min = $10$. Subtract $10$ → $[0, 5, 10]$ (the $\infty$ stays).
- Row 2: min = $10$. Subtract $10$ → $[0, 25, 15]$.
- Row 3: min = $15$. Subtract $15$ → $[0, 20, 15]$.
- Row 4: min = $20$. Subtract $20$ → $[0, 5, 10]$.

$$
R_{\text{row}} = 10 + 10 + 15 + 20 = 55
$$

After row reduction:
$$
C_{R} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 5 & 10 & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 25 & 15 \\ \infty & 20 & \infty & 15 \\ \infty & 5 & 10 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Wait — we must subtract only finite entries; we keep $\infty$ as is. Re-derive carefully:

Original:
$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 10 & \infty & 35 & 25 \\ 15 & 35 & \infty & 30 \\ 20 & 25 & 30 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Subtract row minimums:
- Row 1 min = $10$ → $[\infty, 0, 5, 10]$
- Row 2 min = $10$ → $[0, \infty, 25, 15]$
- Row 3 min = $15$ → $[0, 20, \infty, 15]$
- Row 4 min = $20$ → $[0, 5, 10, \infty]$

$$
C_{\text{row}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 0 & 5 & 10 \\ 0 & \infty & 25 & 15 \\ 0 & 20 & \infty & 15 \\ 0 & 5 & 10 & \infty \end{bmatrix}, \quad R_{\text{row}} = 55
$$

**Step A.2 — Column Reduction (on $C_{\text{row}}$):**
- Col 1: all entries are $0$ or $\infty$ → min finite $= 0$. No change.
- Col 2: finite entries are $0, 20, 5$ → min $= 0$. No change.
- Col 3: finite entries are $5, 25, 10$ → min $= 5$. Subtract $5$ → $[0, 20, 0, 5]$.
- Col 4: finite entries are $10, 15, 15$ → min $= 10$. Subtract $10$ → $[0, 5, 5, \infty]$.

$$
R_{\text{col}} = 0 + 0 + 5 + 10 = 15
$$

Final reduced matrix:
$$
C_{\text{red}} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 0 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & \infty & 20 & 5 \\ 0 & 20 & \infty & 5 \\ 0 & 5 & 5 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

**Root Lower Bound:**
$$
\text{LB}_{\text{root}} = R_{\text{row}} + R_{\text{col}} = 55 + 15 = \mathbf{70}
$$

---

#### Phase B — Branching from City 1

From city $1$, possible next cities are $2, 3, 4$. For each, we compute the new lower bound.

##### Branch B.1: Take edge $1 \to 2$ (cost in reduced matrix = $0$)

Set row $1 = \infty$, column $2 = \infty$, and $c_{2,1} = \infty$:
$$
M_{1\to 2} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 20 & 5 \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & 5 \\ \infty & \infty & 5 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

This matrix is already reduced (all rows and columns that contain finite entries have min = $0$). Additional reductions $= 0$.

$$
\text{LB}_{1\to 2} = 70 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{70}
$$

##### Branch B.2: Take edge $1 \to 3$ (cost in reduced matrix = $0$)

Set row $1 = \infty$, column $3 = \infty$, and $c_{3,1} = \infty$:
$$
M_{1\to 3} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & 5 \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & 5 \\ \infty & 5 & \infty & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Already reduced. $\text{LB}_{1\to 3} = 70 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{70}$

##### Branch B.3: Take edge $1 \to 4$ (cost in reduced matrix = $0$)

Set row $1 = \infty$, column $4 = \infty$, and $c_{4,1} = \infty$:
$$
M_{1\to 4} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 20 & \infty \\ \infty & 20 & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & 5 & 5 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Already reduced. $\text{LB}_{1\to 4} = 70 + 0 + 0 = \mathbf{70}$

All three branches have the same lower bound. Conventionally we **branch on the first one** ($1 \to 2$). Move to city $2$.

---

#### Phase C — Branching from City 2 (in path $1 \to 2$)

From $M_{1\to 2}$ the available next cities are $3$ and $4$.

##### Branch C.1: Take edge $2 \to 3$ (cost in reduced matrix $= 20$)

Set row $2 = \infty$, column $3 = \infty$, $c_{3,2} = \infty$:
$$
M_{1\to 2\to 3} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & 5 \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Already reduced. $\text{LB}_{1\to 2\to 3} = 70 + 20 = \mathbf{90}$

##### Branch C.2: Take edge $2 \to 4$ (cost in reduced matrix $= 5$)

Set row $2 = \infty$, column $4 = \infty$, $c_{4,2} = \infty$:
$$
M_{1\to 2\to 4} \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & \infty & \infty \\ \infty & \infty & 5 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$

Already reduced. $\text{LB}_{1\to 2\to 4} = 70 + 5 = \mathbf{75}$

**Branch C.2 is more promising.** Pick $1 \to 2 \to 4$. From city $4$, the only remaining city is $3$, so we are forced to take $4 \to 3$. From city $3$, the only remaining city is $1$ (return), so we take $3 \to 1$.

**Tour found:** $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$.

**Cost calculation** (using the **original** matrix $C$):
$$
\text{Cost} = c_{1,2} + c_{2,4} + c_{4,3} + c_{3,1} = 10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = \mathbf{80}
$$

Let us verify other candidate tours from the B&B tree to confirm optimality:
- Tour $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$: $10 + 35 + 30 + 20 = 95$
- Tour $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$: $10 + 25 + 30 + 15 = \mathbf{80}$  ✓ **(OPTIMAL)**
- Tour $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1$: $15 + 35 + 25 + 20 = 95$
- Tour $1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2 \to 1$: $15 + 30 + 25 + 10 = 80$ (same cost, symmetric)
- Tour $1 \to 4 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$: $20 + 25 + 35 + 15 = 95$
- Tour $1 \to 4 \to 3 \to 2 \to 1$: $20 + 30 + 35 + 10 = 95$

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal tour cost = 80.** The Branch & Bound method found it after exploring just **3 nodes** out of a possible search space — a huge saving over brute force.

---

## 3.2 Held–Karp Dynamic Programming Derivation

We now derive the $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ DP solution.

### 3.2.1 Subproblem Definition

Let $V = \{1, 2, \dots, n\}$. Fix city $1$ as the start. For a subset $S \subseteq V \setminus \{1\}$ and a city $i \in S$, define:
$$
g(i, S) \;=\; \text{minimum cost of a path starting at } 1, \text{ visiting every city in } S \text{ exactly once, and ending at } i.
$$

### 3.2.2 Recurrence

The last step before reaching $i$ must come from some $j \in S \setminus \{i\}$. So:
$$
g(i, S) \;=\; \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} \bigl[\, g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) \;+\; c_{j, i} \,\bigr]
$$

### 3.2.3 Base Case

When $S = \{i\}$ (a single city), the path is $1 \to i$:
$$
g(i, \{i\}) = c_{1, i}
$$

### 3.2.4 Final Answer

After visiting all cities in $V \setminus \{1\}$, we must return to $1$:
$$
\text{OPT} = g(1, V) = \min_{j \neq 1}\bigl[\, g(j, V \setminus \{1\}) + c_{j, 1} \,\bigr]
$$

### 3.2.5 Worked Example (4 cities)

Let
$$
C \;=\; \begin{bmatrix} \infty & 10 & 15 & 20 \\ 5 & \infty & 9 & 10 \\ 6 & 13 & \infty & 12 \\ 8 & 8 & 9 & \infty \end{bmatrix}
$$
(Asymmetric, start at city 1.)

**Base cases:**
$$
g(2, \{2\}) = 10, \quad g(3, \{3\}) = 15, \quad g(4, \{4\}) = 20
$$

**Subsets of size 2:** Evaluate $g(i, \{i, j\}) = \min_{k}\bigl[g(k, \{k\}) + c_{k,i}\bigr]$ for $k \in \{i,j\}$.

- $g(2, \{2,3\}) = \min\bigl[g(2,\{2\}) + c_{2,3},\; g(3,\{3\}) + c_{3,2}\bigr] = \min[10 + 9, \; 15 + 13] = \min[19, 28] = 19$
- $g(2, \{2,4\}) = \min[10 + 10, \; 20 + 8] = \min[20, 28] = 20$
- $g(3, \{2,3\}) = \min[10 + 15, \; 15 + 6] = \min[25, 21] = 21$
- $g(3, \{3,4\}) = \min[15 + 12, \; 20 + 9] = \min[27, 29] = 27$
- $g(4, \{2,4\}) = \min[10 + 20, \; 20 + 8] = \min[30, 28] = 28$
- $g(4, \{3,4\}) = \min[15 + 20, \; 20 + 6] = \min[35, 26] = 26$

**Subsets of size 3:** $g(i, \{2,3,4\}) = \min_{j \neq i}\bigl[g(j, \{2,3,4\} \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j,i}\bigr]$.

- $g(2, \{2,3,4\}) = \min\bigl[g(3,\{3,4\}) + c_{3,2},\; g(4,\{2,4\}) + c_{4,2}\bigr] = \min[27 + 13, \; 28 + 8] = \min[40, 36] = 36$
- $g(3, \{2,3,4\}) = \min\bigl[g(2,\{2,4\}) + c_{2,3},\; g(4,\{3,4\}) + c_{4,3}\bigr] = \min[20 + 9, \; 26 + 9] = \min[29, 35] = 29$
- $g(4, \{2,3,4\}) = \min\bigl[g(2,\{2,3\}) + c_{2,4},\; g(3,\{2,3\}) + c_{3,4}\bigr] = \min[19 + 10, \; 21 + 12] = \min[29, 33] = 29$

**Final Answer** (return to city 1):
$$
\text{OPT} = \min\bigl[g(2,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{2,1},\; g(3,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{3,1},\; g(4,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{4,1}\bigr]
$$
$$
= \min[36 + 5,\; 29 + 6,\; 29 + 8] = \min[41, 35, 37] = \mathbf{35}
$$

**Optimal tour:** Trace back via the argmin. The minimum at $g(3, \{2,3,4\}) = 29$ came from $g(2,\{2,4\}) + c_{2,3} = 29$, and $g(2, \{2,4\}) = 20$ came from $g(2,\{2\}) + c_{2,4} = 20$. So tour = $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ with cost $10 + 10 + 9 + 6 = 35$. ✓

### 3.2.6 Pseudocode (Held–Karp)

```text
ALGORITHM HeldKarp(C[1..n][1..n])
    // C[i][j] = cost from i to j
    n ← |C|
    m ← 1 << n
    dp[1..n][0..m-1] ← ∞
    parent[1..n][0..m-1] ← -1

    for i ← 2 to n do
        dp[i][1 << (i-1)] ← C[1][i]    // base case
        parent[i][1 << (i-1)] ← 1

    for mask ← 1 to m-1 do
        for i ← 2 to n do
            if (mask & (1 << (i-1))) = 0 then continue
            if mask = (1 << (i-1)) then continue
            for j ← 2 to n do
                if j = i or (mask & (1 << (j-1))) = 0 then continue
                prev ← mask ^ (1 << (i-1))
                if dp[j][prev] + C[j][i] < dp[i][mask] then
                    dp[i][mask] ← dp[j][prev] + C[j][i]
                    parent[i][mask] ← j

    fullMask ← m - 1 - 1        // all cities except city 1
    bestCost ← ∞, bestLast ← -1
    for i ← 2 to n do
        if dp[i][fullMask] + C[i][1] < bestCost then
            bestCost ← dp[i][fullMask] + C[i][1]
            bestLast ← i

    RETURN bestCost, ReconstructPath(parent, bestLast, fullMask, n)
```

---

## 3.3 MST-Based 2-Approximation Algorithm (Sketch)

For **metric TSP** (where the triangle inequality holds), we can construct an approximate tour in polynomial time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Theorem (2-Approximation).** Let $T$ be a minimum spanning tree of the complete graph. Construct a tour $T_{\text{MST}}$ by performing a *preorder walk* of $T$ (DFS visit order, skipping repeated vertices). Then
> $$\text{Cost}(T_{\text{MST}}) \;\le\; 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$$

**Proof Sketch:**
1. Removing any edge from an optimal tour yields a spanning tree $T^{\star}$. Therefore $\text{Cost}(T^{\star}) \le \text{OPT}$.
2. Since $T$ is the *minimum* spanning tree, $\text{Cost}(T) \le \text{Cost}(T^{\star}) \le \text{OPT}$.
3. The preorder walk of $T$ visits every edge of $T$ exactly **twice** (once forward, once back) → total walk cost $\le 2 \cdot \text{Cost}(T) \le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$.
4. The triangle inequality allows us to "shortcut" revisits without increasing the cost → final tour $\le 2 \cdot \text{OPT}$. $\blacksquare$

```mermaid
flowchart TD

    B0 --> C0[STAGE 2: COLUMN REDUCTION]

    D0 --> E0[STAGE 4: SELECT MOST PROMISING EDGE]
    E0 --> F0[STAGE 5: UPDATE MATRIX SET ROW COL INFINITY]
    F0 --> G0[STAGE 6: RE REDUCE MODIFIED MATRIX]
    G0 --> H0[STAGE 7: ALL CITIES VISITED]
    H0 --> I0[YES: COMPUTE RETURN EDGE COST]
    H0 --> NO0[NO: BACKTRACK OR CONTINUE]
    I0 --> J0[STAGE 8: TRACE OPTIMAL TOUR]
    J0 --> K0[OUTPUT: MINIMUM COST TOUR]
    NO0 --> E0
```
