# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
*Define the Travelling Salesperson Problem (TSP). State its time complexity using brute force and mention one real-world application.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** The Travelling Salesperson Problem is a classic combinatorial optimization problem in which a salesperson must find the **shortest possible route** that visits a given set of $n$ cities **exactly once** and **returns to the starting city**, given the pairwise distances (or costs) between them.

> **Complexity (1 mark):** The brute-force approach enumerates all $(n-1)!$ permutations of cities (with start fixed). Hence the time complexity is $\mathcal{O}(n!)$. This factorial growth makes brute force infeasible for $n > 15$.

> **Application (0.5 mark):** TSP is widely applied in **logistics and supply-chain management** (e.g., Amazon's delivery routing), **VLSI circuit-board drilling**, and **robotic path planning** in warehouses.

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*What is the difference between the Nearest Neighbour heuristic and the Held–Karp algorithm for TSP? When would you prefer one over the other?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Nearest Neighbour | Held–Karp |
|---|---|---|
| **Type** | Greedy heuristic | Exact dynamic programming |
| **Optimality** | Approximate (not optimal) | Optimal (guaranteed) |
| **Time Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ | $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ |
| **Space Complexity** | $\mathcal{O}(1)$ | $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot 2^{n})$ |
| **Best Use Case** | Large $n$ ($\ge 100$ cities) | Small $n$ ($\le 25$ cities) |

> **When to use:** Use **Nearest Neighbour** when $n$ is large and a *quick* good-enough solution suffices (e.g., real-time route planning). Use **Held–Karp** when $n$ is small and an *optimal* solution is mandatory (e.g., mission-critical logistics, research benchmarks).

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module-4 Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> - **Forgetting to set $c_{ji} = \infty$** when including edge $(i,j)$ → 2-mark deduction.
> - **Using original-matrix cost in lower-bound update** instead of reduced-matrix cost → entire B&B tree wrong.
> - **Ignoring column reduction** after row reduction → lower bound is not tight, tree blows up.
> - **In Held–Karp, using $c_{1,1}=0$** instead of $\infty$ → 1-mark deduction.
> - **For asymmetric TSP, not dividing by 2 for tours** — there are $(n-1)!$ tours, not $(n-1)!/2$.

---

### Q3. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Module 4 Typical Pattern]**

**Question A (14 marks):**
> Solve the following Travelling Salesperson Problem using the **Branch and Bound method with reduced cost matrix**. Show all reduction steps and the search tree clearly. Start from city **1**.

| | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\infty$ | 8 | 13 | 18 |
| **2** | 12 | $\infty$ | 22 | 24 |
| **3** | 9 | 16 | $\infty$ | 21 |
| **4** | 14 | 17 | 19 | $\infty$ |

#### Model Solution (14 marks)

**Step 1 — Row Reduction (2 marks):**
- Row 1 min = $8$, Row 2 min = $12$, Row 3 min = $9$, Row 4 min = $14$.
- $R_{\text{row}} = 8 + 12 + 9 + 14 = 43$

| | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\infty$ | 0 | 5 | 10 |
| **2** | 0 | $\infty$ | 10 | 12 |
| **3** | 0 | 7 | $\infty$ | 12 |
| **4** | 0 | 3 | 5 | $\infty$ |

**Step 2 — Column Reduction (2 marks):**
- Col 1: min = $0$, Col 2: min = $0$, Col 3: min = $5$, Col 4: min = $10$.
- $R_{\text{col}} = 0 + 0 + 5 + 10 = 15$.

| | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\infty$ | 0 | 0 | 0 |
| **2** | 0 | $\infty$ | 5 | 2 |
| **3** | 0 | 7 | $\infty$ | 2 |
| **4** | 0 | 3 | 0 | $\infty$ |

**Root Lower Bound (1 mark):** $\text{LB}_{\text{root}} = 43 + 15 = \mathbf{58}$

**Step 3 — Branching from City 1 (3 marks):**

For each edge $(1, j)$, the matrix is modified. Since row 1 of the reduced matrix is $[∞, 0, 0, 0]$, all edges have *equal* cost $0$ in the reduced matrix, so all give LB $= 58$. We branch on $1 \to 2$ first (convention).

After $1 \to 2$: set row 1, col 2, and $c_{2,1} = ∞$. New reduced matrix is *already* reduced (no further reductions needed). $\text{LB}_{1 \to 2} = 58 + 0 = 58$.

**Step 4 — Branching from City 2 in path $1 \to 2$ (3 marks):**

Modified matrix (after $1 \to 2$):
| | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ |
| **2** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 5 | 2 |
| **3** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 2 |
| **4** | $\infty$ | $\infty$ | 0 | $\infty$ |

- Edge $2 \to 3$: cost $= 5$, no further reductions. $\text{LB} = 58 + 5 = 63$.
- Edge $2 \to 4$: cost $= 2$, no further reductions. $\text{LB} = 58 + 2 = 60$.

**Choose $1 \to 2 \to 4$** (lower LB = 60).

**Step 5 — Branching from City 4 in path $1 \to 2 \to 4$ (2 marks):**

Forced: $4 \to 3$, then $3 \to 1$.

**Step 6 — Final Tour Cost (1 mark):**
$$
\text{Cost} = c_{1,2} + c_{2,4} + c_{4,3} + c_{3,1} = 8 + 24 + 19 + 9 = \mathbf{60}
$$

**Optimal Tour: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3 \to 1$ with cost 60.** ✓

---

**Question B (14 marks):**
> Explain the **Held–Karp Dynamic Programming** algorithm for solving the Travelling Salesperson Problem. Write the recurrence relation and compute the optimal tour for the following 4-city asymmetric TSP (start from city 1):

| | **1** | **2** | **3** | **4** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $\infty$ | 8 | 5 | 12 |
| **2** | 10 | $\infty$ | 6 | 9 |
| **3** | 7 | 11 | $\infty$ | 14 |
| **4** | 6 | 8 | 13 | $\infty$ |

#### Model Solution (14 marks)

**Part (a) — Algorithm Explanation (7 marks):**

The Held–Karp algorithm is a **bottom-up dynamic programming** approach. It avoids the $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ cost of brute force by exploiting **overlapping subproblems**.

**Subproblem:** $g(i, S)$ = minimum cost to start at city 1, visit every city in set $S$ exactly once, and end at city $i$. $|S|$ counts include $i$ itself.

**Recurrence Relation (3 marks):**
$$
g(i, S) = \begin{cases}
c_{1,i} & \text{if } S = \{i\} \\
\min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}} \bigl[\, g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j,i} \,\bigr] & \text{otherwise}
\end{cases}
$$

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
$$
\text{OPT} = \min_{j \neq 1}\bigl[\, g(j, V \setminus \{1\}) + c_{j,1} \,\bigr]
$$

**Complexity (1 mark):** There are $n \cdot 2^{n}$ subproblems; each takes $\mathcal{O}(n)$ to compute → total time $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$, space $\mathcal{O}(n \cdot 2^{n})$.

**Why it works (2 marks):** The principle of optimality holds — the optimal sub-tour from $1$ to $j$ visiting a subset $S \setminus \{i\}$ is independent of the choice of the final destination $i$. This *decomposes* the problem and shares work across subproblems.

**Part (b) — Computation (7 marks):**

**Base cases (1 mark):**
$$
g(2,\{2\}) = 8, \quad g(3,\{3\}) = 5, \quad g(4,\{4\}) = 12
$$

**Subsets of size 2 (3 marks):**
- $g(2, \{2,3\}) = \min[8 + 6, \; 5 + 11] = \min[14, 16] = 14$ via $j=2$
- $g(2, \{2,4\}) = \min[8 + 9, \; 12 + 8] = \min[17, 20] = 17$ via $j=2$
- $g(3, \{2,3\}) = \min[8 + 5, \; 5 + 7] = \min[13, 12] = 12$ via $j=3$ (i.e., $1\to 2 \to 3$)
- $g(3, \{3,4\}) = \min[5 + 14, \; 12 + 13] = \min[19, 25] = 19$ via $j=3$
- $g(4, \{2,4\}) = \min[8 + 12, \; 12 + 6] = \min[20, 18] = 18$ via $j=4$ (i.e., $1\to 3 \to 4$)
- $g(4, \{3,4\}) = \min[5 + 12, \; 12 + 7] = \min[17, 19] = 17$ via $j=3$

**Subsets of size 3 (2 marks):**
- $g(2,\{2,3,4\}) = \min[g(3,\{3,4\}) + 11, \; g(4,\{2,4\}) + 8] = \min[19+11, \; 18+8] = \min[30, 26] = 26$ via $j=4$ ($1\to 3 \to 4 \to 2$)
- $g(3,\{2,3,4\}) = \min[g(2,\{2,4\}) + 6, \; g(4,\{3,4\}) + 13] = \min[17+6, \; 17+13] = \min[23, 30] = 23$ via $j=2$ ($1\to 2 \to 4 \to 3$)
- $g(4,\{2,3,4\}) = \min[g(2,\{2,3\}) + 9, \; g(3,\{2,3\}) + 14] = \min[14+9, \; 12+14] = \min[23, 26] = 23$ via $j=2$ ($1\to 2 \to 3 \to 4$)

**Final Answer (1 mark):**
$$
\text{OPT} = \min[g(2,\{2,3,4\}) + 10, \; g(3,\{2,3,4\}) + 7, \; g(4,\{2,3,4\}) + 6]
$$
$$
= \min[26 + 10, \; 23 + 7, \; 23 + 6] = \min[36, 30, 29] = \mathbf{29}
$$

**Optimal Tour:** Trace back. $29$ came from $g(4,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{4,1} = 23 + 6$. $g(4, \{2,3,4\}) = 23$ via $j = 2$, so before $4$ we visited via $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$. **Tour: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ with cost $8 + 6 + 14 + 6 = 34$? Wait — recompute: $8 + 6 + 14 + 6 = 34$, which contradicts $29$.**

Let us recheck: $c_{4,1} = 6$ from the table. The path is $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4 \to 1$ with costs $c_{1,2}+c_{2,3}+c_{3,4}+c_{4,1} = 8+6+14+6 = 34$. Hmm — but our DP says $29$. Let us re-examine $g(4,\{2,3,4\})$.

$g(4, \{2,3,4\}) = \min[g(2,\{2,3\}) + c_{2,4}, \; g(3,\{2,3\}) + c_{3,4}] = \min[14 + 9, \; 12 + 14] = \min[23, 26] = 23$. Yes $23$.

Path: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$. Costs: $c_{1,2} + c_{2,3} + c_{3,4} = 8 + 6 + 14 = 28$. But $g(3, \{2,3\}) = 12$ which is $c_{1,2} + c_{2,3} = 8 + 6 = 14$? No — $g(3, \{2,3\}) = 12$ via $j=3$, meaning we come from city 3 itself — but $j$ must be in $S \setminus \{i\}$. Let me recheck.

For $g(3, \{2,3\})$: $S = \{2,3\}$, $i = 3$. We choose $j \in S \setminus \{3\} = \{2\}$. So $g(3, \{2,3\}) = g(2, \{2\}) + c_{2,3} = 8 + 6 = 14$. The answer $12$ was wrong!

I must correct this. Let me redo $g(3, \{2,3\}) = 8 + 6 = 14$, not $12$.

Re-compute $g(4, \{2,3,4\}) = \min[14 + 9, \; 14 + 14] = \min[23, 28] = 23$. Same answer via $j=2$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 4$ with cost $8 + 9 = 17$, plus $c_{2,4} = 9$? Wait, the formula is $g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j,i}$ where $i = 4$. With $j = 2$, $S \setminus \{4\} = \{2,3\}$, so $g(2, \{2,3\}) = 14$. Then $g(2, \{2,3\}) + c_{2,4} = 14 + 9 = 23$. ✓

So path $1 \to 2 \to 4$ with $g(2, \{2,3\}) = 14$ means we visited $\{2,3\}$ and ended at $2$, costing $14$. Tracing back: $g(2, \{2,3\}) = g(2, \{2\}) + c_{2,?}$... wait, the recurrence is $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}}[g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j,i}]$. For $g(2, \{2,3\})$ with $i=2$, $S \setminus \{2\} = \{3\}$, so $j = 3$. Thus $g(2, \{2,3\}) = g(3, \{3\}) + c_{3,2} = 5 + 11 = 16$. Hmm.

Let me redo carefully. I'll define the recurrence more carefully and recompute everything.

For $g(i, S)$ where $i \in S$:
- If $S = \{i\}$: $g(i, \{i\}) = c_{1, i}$.
- Else: $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S, j \neq i} [g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j, i}]$.

Base cases (size 1 subsets):
- $g(2, \{2\}) = c_{1,2} = 8$
- $g(3, \{3\}) = c_{1,3} = 5$
- $g(4, \{4\}) = c_{1,4} = 12$

Size 2 subsets: $g(i, S)$ where $|S| = 2$. We need $S \setminus \{i\}$ to be size 1.

- $g(2, \{2,3\})$: $S = \{2,3\}$, $i = 2$, $S \setminus \{2\} = \{3\}$, so $j = 3$. $g(2,\{2,3\}) = g(3, \{3\}) + c_{3,2} = 5 + 11 = 16$.
- $g(2, \{2,4\})$: $S \setminus \{2\} = \{4\}$, $j = 4$. $g(2,\{2,4\}) = g(4, \{4\}) + c_{4,2} = 12 + 8 = 20$.
- $g(3, \{2,3\})$: $S \setminus \{3\} = \{2\}$, $j = 2$. $g(3,\{2,3\}) = g(2, \{2\}) + c_{2,3} = 8 + 6 = 14$.
- $g(3, \{3,4\})$: $S \setminus \{3\} = \{4\}$, $j = 4$. $g(3,\{3,4\}) = g(4, \{4\}) + c_{4,3} = 12 + 13 = 25$.
- $g(4, \{2,4\})$: $S \setminus \{4\} = \{2\}$, $j = 2$. $g(4,\{2,4\}) = g(2, \{2\}) + c_{2,4} = 8 + 9 = 17$.
- $g(4, \{3,4\})$: $S \setminus \{4\} = \{3\}$, $j = 3$. $g(4,\{3,4\}) = g(3, \{3\}) + c_{3,4} = 5 + 14 = 19$.

Size 3 subsets: $g(i, \{2,3,4\})$, $|S| = 3$. We try $j \in \{2,3,4\} \setminus \{i\}$.

- $g(2, \{2,3,4\})$: try $j = 3$ and $j = 4$.
  - $j=3$: $g(3, \{3,4\}) + c_{3,2} = 25 + 11 = 36$.
  - $j=4$: $g(4, \{2,4\}) + c_{4,2} = 17 + 8 = 25$.
  - $g(2, \{2,3,4\}) = 25$ via $j = 4$. Path so far: $1 \to ? \to 4 \to 2$. The $g(4, \{2,4\}) = 17 = 8 + 9 = c_{1,2} + c_{2,4}$. So full path: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 2$ — but $2$ is visited twice! 

This is a known issue with asymmetric TSP. The DP I wrote is technically for the **shortest path visiting all vertices**, not strictly a tour. For ATSP, we need the standard Held-Karp formulation.

Let me restate the correct formulation: $g(i, S) = $ minimum cost path that starts at vertex 1, visits all vertices in $S$ (each exactly once), and ends at $i$. Then the final answer requires returning to 1.

For $g(2, \{2,3,4\}) = 25$ via $j=4$: $g(4, \{2,4\}) = 17 = c_{1,2} + c_{2,4}$. So the path is $1 \to 2 \to 4$, with cost $17$. Then we add $c_{4,2} = 8$ to get $25$. So actually the path is $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 2$. That visits $2$ twice!

This is because in ATSP, the "subtour elimination" is handled by the DP structure itself, but only if the recurrence is correctly defined. The issue is that $g(4, \{2,4\})$ should mean "start at 1, visit ALL of $\{2,4\}$ exactly once, end at 4". So $g(4, \{2,4\}) = g(2, \{2\}) + c_{2,4} = 8 + 9 = 17$ — path is $1 \to 2 \to 4$. ✓

Then for $g(2, \{2,3,4\}) = 25$ via $j=4$: $g(4, \{2,3,4\} \setminus \{2\}) + c_{4,2} = g(4, \{3,4\}) + c_{4,2} = 19 + 8 = 27$. Wait, $g(4, \{3,4\}) = 19$ from above. So $27$, not $25$. Let me recompute $g(4, \{2,4\})$.

Oh I see my error: when computing $g(2, \{2,3,4\})$ with $j=4$, the set becomes $S \setminus \{2\} = \{3, 4\}$, and we use $g(4, \{3,4\})$, NOT $g(4, \{2,4\})$.

Let me redo:
- $g(2, \{2,3,4\})$:
  - $j=3$: $g(3, \{3,4\}) + c_{3,2} = 25 + 11 = 36$.
  - $j=4$: $g(4, \{3,4\}) + c_{4,2} = 19 + 8 = 27$.
  - $\min = 27$ via $j=4$. Path: $1 \to 3 \to 4 \to 2$ with cost $5 + 14 + 8 = 27$. ✓
- $g(3, \{2,3,4\})$:
  - $j=2$: $g(2, \{2,4\}) + c_{2,3} = 20 + 6 = 26$. Path: $1 \to 4 \to 2 \to 3$ with cost $12 + 8 + 6 = 26$. ✓
  - $j=4$: $g(4, \{2,4\}) + c_{4,3} = 17 + 13 = 30$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 4 \to 3$ with cost $8 + 9 + 13 = 30$.
  - $\min = 26$ via $j=2$. Path: $1 \to 4 \to 2 \to 3$.
- $g(4, \{2,3,4\})$:
  - $j=2$: $g(2, \{2,3\}) + c_{2,4} = 16 + 9 = 25$. Path: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4$ with cost $5 + 11 + 9 = 25$. ✓
  - $j=3$: $g(3, \{2,3\}) + c_{3,4} = 14 + 14 = 28$. Path: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 4$ with cost $8 + 6 + 14 = 28$.
  - $\min = 25$ via $j=2$. Path: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4$.

Final answer:
$$
\text{OPT} = \min\bigl[g(2,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{2,1},\; g(3,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{3,1},\; g(4,\{2,3,4\}) + c_{4,1}\bigr]
$$
$$
= \min[27 + 10, \; 26 + 7, \; 25 + 6] = \min[37, 33, 31] = \mathbf{31}
$$

**Optimal tour:** $31$ via $g(4, \{2,3,4\}) + c_{4,1} = 25 + 6$. The path through is $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1$ with cost $5 + 11 + 9 + 6 = 31$. ✓

**Optimal Tour: $1 \to 3 \to 2 \to 4 \to 1$ with cost 31.** ✓

**Verify by enumeration (best of all 6 ATSP tours):**
- $1\to 2\to 3\to 4\to 1$: $8+6+14+6=34$
- $1\to 2\to 4\to 3\to 1$: $8+9+13+7=37$
- $1\to 3\to 2\to 4\to 1$: $5+11+9+6=\mathbf{31}$ ✓
- $1\to 3\to 4\to 2\to 1$: $5+14+8+10=37$
- $1\to 4\to 2\to 3\to 1$: $12+8+6+7=33$
- $1\to 4\to 3\to 2\to 1$: $12+13+11+10=46$

Held–Karp correctly gives 31. ✓

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

### Core Concepts
- [x] TSP seeks the **shortest Hamiltonian cycle** visiting every city exactly once.
- [x] TSP is **NP-Hard** (optimization) / **NP-Complete** (decision form).
- [x] Brute force: $\mathcal{O}(n!)$ — only feasible for $n \le 10$.
- [x] Held–Karp DP: $\mathcal{O}(n^{2} \cdot 2^{n})$ — exact, feasible for $n \le 25$.

### Branch & Bound Essentials
- [x] **Two reductions** (row, then column) — order matters but result is same.
- [x] Root LB = sum of all row minimums + sum of all column minimums.
- [x] For edge $(i,j)$: set row $i = \infty$, col $j = \infty$, **and** $c_{j,i} = \infty$.
- [x] Re-reduce the modified matrix; new LB = parent LB + (cost in reduced matrix) + (new reductions).
- [x] **Always branch on the most promising child** (lowest LB) first.
- [x] Prune any node whose LB $\ge$ best-known tour cost so far.

### Held–Karp Essentials
- [x] Subproblem $g(i, S)$ = min cost of path from 1, through $S$, ending at $i$.
- [x] Recurrence: $g(i, S) = \min_{j \in S \setminus \{i\}}[g(j, S \setminus \{i\}) + c_{j,i}]$.
- [x] Base case: $g(i, \{i\}) = c_{1, i}$.
- [x] Final: $\text{OPT} = \min_{j \neq 1}[g(j, V \setminus \{1\}) + c_{j, 1}]$.

### Approximation Essentials
- [x] **MST-based**: 2-approximation for metric TSP.
- [x] **Christofides**: 1.5-approximation for metric TSP (uses MST + perfect matching).
- [x] **Nearest Neighbour**: $\mathcal{O}(n^{2})$ greedy, no guaranteed bound for general TSP, but $\le \lceil \log_2 n \rceil \cdot \text{OPT}$ for metric.

### Watch-Out Items
- [x] **Symmetric vs. Asymmetric**: $(n-1)!/2$ vs. $(n-1)!$ tours.
- [x] **Triangle inequality** must hold for approximation algorithms to be valid.
- [x] For ATSP, do NOT use symmetric shortcuts in B&B.
- [x] The reduced-matrix cost (not original cost) is added to the lower bound when extending a path.

> **Final Mantra for KTU Exam:** *Reduce → Bound → Branch → Prune → Repeat.* ⭐
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: 0/1 Knapsack Problem
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** 0/1 Knapsack Problem

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **0/1 Knapsack Problem** is a fundamental combinatorial optimization problem in the field of **Dynamic Programming** and **Algorithm Design**. Given a set of `n` items, where each item `i` has an associated weight `w[i]` and a value `v[i]`, and a knapsack with a maximum weight capacity `W`, the objective is to select a subset of items such that:

1. The **total weight** of selected items does not exceed the knapsack capacity `W`.
2. The **total value** of selected items is **maximized**.

The prefix **"0/1"** signifies that each item must be taken in its entirety (`1`) or rejected entirely (`0`) — fractional inclusion is **not permitted**. This is what distinguishes it from the **Fractional Knapsack Problem**, where items can be broken into smaller pieces.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PECAT415 — Module 4):** The 0/1 Knapsack Problem is a mandatory topic under the **Dynamic Programming** unit. It is a high-yield topic for KTU examinations, frequently appearing in Part A (definitions) and Part B (algorithm design + trace + complexity) questions.

**Mathematical Formulation:**

$$
\text{Maximize} \quad Z = \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot x_i
$$

$$
\text{Subject to} \quad \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \leq W, \quad x_i \in \{0, 1\}
$$

where `x[i] = 1` denotes the inclusion of item `i` and `x[i] = 0` denotes its exclusion.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a **treasure hunter** who has discovered a cave filled with valuable artifacts (gold, gems, ancient relics). However, your backpack has a **strict weight limit** — say it can carry only **10 kg**. Each artifact has a **weight** (how heavy it is) and a **value** (how much money it is worth). Your goal is to **pick the most valuable combination of artifacts** that fits within your backpack.

The catch? You **cannot cut an artifact in half** — you either take it whole or leave it behind. This is precisely the 0/1 Knapsack Problem in disguise.

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain English Summary:** *"Given a backpack with limited capacity, choose the best items (each with a weight and value) so that the total value is maximum and total weight does not exceed the limit. You cannot take fractions of any item."*

### 1.3 Why Dynamic Programming?

The 0/1 Knapsack Problem exhibits two critical properties that make it an ideal candidate for Dynamic Programming:

| Property | Description |
|----------|-------------|
| **Optimal Substructure** | The optimal solution for `i` items and capacity `W` can be constructed from optimal solutions of smaller subproblems (`i-1` items with reduced capacities). |
| **Overlapping Subproblems** | The same subproblem `(i, w)` is solved multiple times in a naive recursive approach, allowing for memoization. |

> [!TIP]
> **Naive Recursive Time Complexity:** $O(2^n)$ — Exponential and infeasible for large `n`.
> **Dynamic Programming Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W)$ — Pseudo-polynomial and dramatically faster in practice.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Recurrence Relation Derivation

Let `V[i][w]` denote the **maximum value** obtainable using the first `i` items and a knapsack of capacity `w`. We derive the recurrence by considering item `i`:

**Case 1: Item `i` is NOT included.**
The best value is simply the best we could achieve using the first `i-1` items with capacity `w`:

$$
V[i][w] = V[i-1][w]
$$

**Case 2: Item `i` IS included (only possible if `w[i] <= w`).**
We gain `v[i]` value but consume `w[i]` capacity, leaving `w - w[i]` for the remaining `i-1` items:

$$
V[i][w] = v[i] + V[i-1][w - w[i]]
$$

**Unified Recurrence Relation:**

$$
V[i][w] =
\begin{cases}
V[i-1][w] & \text{if } w_i > w \quad (\text{cannot include}) \\[4pt]
\max\!\bigl(V[i-1][w],\; v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i]\bigr) & \text{if } w_i \leq w
\end{cases}
$$

**Base Conditions:**

$$
V[0][w] = 0 \quad \forall w \in [0, W] \quad \text{(no items means no value)}
$$

$$
V[i][0] = 0 \quad \forall i \in [0, n] \quad \text{(zero capacity means no value)}
$$

---

### 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

The following table consolidates every formula, condition, and metric you need for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem in KTU examinations.

| Aspect | Formula / Definition | Units / Notes |
|--------|----------------------|---------------|
| **Objective Function** | $\max \sum_{i=1}^{n} v_i \cdot x_i$ | Maximize total value |
| **Constraint** | $\sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot x_i \leq W$ | Total weight within capacity |
| **Decision Variable** | $x_i \in \{0, 1\}$ | Binary (whole-item only) |
| **DP Recurrence** | $V[i][w] = V[i-1][w]$ if $w_i > w$ else $\max(V[i-1][w],\, v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i])$ | Core recurrence |
| **Base Case** | $V[0][w] = 0,\quad V[i][0] = 0$ | Initialization |
| **Optimal Value** | $V[n][W]$ | Bottom-right cell of DP table |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot W)$ | Pseudo-polynomial |
| **Space Complexity** | $O(n \cdot W)$ | Two-dimensional table |
| **Space-Optimized** | $O(W)$ | Single-row rolling array |
| **Naive Recursive** | $O(2^n)$ | Brute force exponential |
| **Table Dimensions** | $(n+1) \times (W+1)$ | Indices from 0 to n and 0 to W |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Never confuse `0/1 Knapsack` with `Fractional Knapsack`.** In 0/1, items cannot be broken; in Fractional, they can. The greedy strategy (sort by $v_i / w_i$) works for the **fractional** version but **fails** for the 0/1 version.

---

### 2.3 Algorithmic Steps (Bottom-Up Dynamic Programming)

1. **Initialize** a 2D table `V[0..n][0..W]` with all zeros.
2. **Iterate** `i` from `1` to `n` (each item).
3. **Iterate** `w` from `0` to `W` (each capacity).
4. **Apply** the recurrence relation based on whether `w[i] <= w`.
5. **Store** the result in `V[i][w]`.
6. **Return** `V[n][W]` as the maximum achievable value.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Applications

The 0/1 Knapsack Problem is not just an academic exercise. It has tangible applications across industries:

- **Cloud Computing Resource Allocation:** Allocating virtual machines with limited CPU/RAM budget.
- **Cargo Loading:** Determining which containers to load onto a ship with weight restrictions.
- **Investment Portfolio Selection:** Choosing projects with budget constraints and profit potential.
- **Cutting Stock Problems:** Optimizing material cuts in manufacturing.
- **Network Packet Scheduling:** Selecting high-priority packets within bandwidth limits.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Worked Example (KTU Board Standard Format)

**Problem Statement:**
Given `n = 4` items with the following weights and values, and a knapsack capacity `W = 5`, find the maximum value using Dynamic Programming.

| Item `i` | Weight `w[i]` | Value `v[i]` |
|----------|---------------|---------------|
| 1        | 2             | 3            |
| 2        | 3             | 4            |
| 3        | 4             | 5            |
| 4        | 5             | 6            |

---

### 3.2 Step-by-Step Construction of the DP Table

We construct a table `V[i][w]` of dimensions `(n+1) × (W+1) = 5 × 6`.

**Step 1: Initialize row 0 and column 0 with zeros.**

$$
V[0][w] = 0 \quad \text{and} \quad V[i][0] = 0 \quad \forall i, w
$$

**Step 2: Process Item 1 (w = 2, v = 3).**

For `i = 1`:

$$
V[1][0] = V[0][0] = 0
$$

$$
V[1][1] : w_1 = 2 > 1 \implies V[1][1] = V[0][1] = 0
$$

$$
V[1][2] : w_1 = 2 \leq 2 \implies V[1][2] = \max(V[0][2],\, 3 + V[0][0]) = \max(0, 3) = 3
$$

$$
V[1][3] : w_1 = 2 \leq 3 \implies V[1][3] = \max(V[0][3],\, 3 + V[0][1]) = \max(0, 3) = 3
$$

$$
V[1][4] : w_1 = 2 \leq 4 \implies V[1][4] = \max(V[0][4],\, 3 + V[0][2]) = \max(0, 3) = 3
$$

$$
V[1][5] : w_1 = 2 \leq 5 \implies V[1][5] = \max(V[0][5],\, 3 + V[0][3]) = \max(0, 3) = 3
$$

**Step 3: Process Item 2 (w = 3, v = 4).**

For `i = 2`:

$$
V[2][0] = V[1][0] = 0
$$

$$
V[2][1] : w_2 = 3 > 1 \implies V[2][1] = V[1][1] = 0
$$

$$
V[2][2] : w_2 = 3 > 2 \implies V[2][2] = V[1][2] = 3
$$

$$
V[2][3] : w_2 = 3 \leq 3 \implies V[2][3] = \max(V[1][3],\, 4 + V[1][0]) = \max(3, 4) = 4
$$

$$
V[2][4] : w_2 = 3 \leq 4 \implies V[2][4] = \max(V[1][4],\, 4 + V[1][1]) = \max(3, 4) = 4
$$

$$
V[2][5] : w_2 = 3 \leq 5 \implies V[2][5] = \max(V[1][5],\, 4 + V[1][2]) = \max(3, 7) = 7
$$

**Step 4: Process Item 3 (w = 4, v = 5).**

For `i = 3`:

$$
V[3][0] = V[2][0] = 0
$$

$$
V[3][1] : w_3 = 4 > 1 \implies V[3][1] = V[2][1] = 0
$$

$$
V[3][2] : w_3 = 4 > 2 \implies V[3][2] = V[2][2] = 3
$$

$$
V[3][3] : w_3 = 4 > 3 \implies V[3][3] = V[2][3] = 4
$$

$$
V[3][4] : w_3 = 4 \leq 4 \implies V[3][4] = \max(V[2][4],\, 5 + V[2][0]) = \max(4, 5) = 5
$$

$$
V[3][5] : w_3 = 4 \leq 5 \implies V[3][5] = \max(V[2][5],\, 5 + V[2][1]) = \max(7, 5) = 7
$$

**Step 5: Process Item 4 (w = 5, v = 6).**

For `i = 4`:

$$
V[4][0] = V[3][0] = 0
$$

$$
V[4][1] : w_4 = 5 > 1 \implies V[4][1] = V[3][1] = 0
$$

$$
V[4][2] : w_4 = 5 > 2 \implies V[4][2] = V[3][2] = 3
$$

$$
V[4][3] : w_4 = 5 > 3 \implies V[4][3] = V[3][3] = 4
$$

$$
V[4][4] : w_4 = 5 > 4 \implies V[4][4] = V[3][4] = 5
$$

$$
V[4][5] : w_4 = 5 \leq 5 \implies V[4][5] = \max(V[3][5],\, 6 + V[3][0]) = \max(7, 6) = 7
$$

---

### 3.3 Final DP Table

$$
\begin{array}{|c|c|c|c|c|c|c|}
\hline
\textbf{V[i][w]} & \textbf{w=0} & \textbf{w=1} & \textbf{w=2} & \textbf{w=3} & \textbf{w=4} & \textbf{w=5} \\
\hline
\textbf{i=0} & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 & 0 \\
\hline
\textbf{i=1} & 0 & 0 & 3 & 3 & 3 & 3 \\
\hline
\textbf{i=2} & 0 & 0 & 3 & 4 & 4 & \mathbf{7} \\
\hline
\textbf{i=3} & 0 & 0 & 3 & 4 & 5 & 7 \\
\hline
\textbf{i=4} & 0 & 0 & 3 & 4 & 5 & \mathbf{7} \\
\hline
\end{array}
$$

**Maximum Value:** $V[4][5] = 7$

---

### 3.4 Backtracking to Identify Selected Items

Starting from `V[4][5] = 7`, we trace backwards:

- **Item 4:** $V[4][5] = 7 = V[3][5]$ → Item 4 **NOT selected** (value did not increase).
- **Item 3:** $V[3][5] = 7 = V[2][5]$ → Item 3 **NOT selected**.
- **Item 2:** $V[2][5] = 7 \neq V[1][5] = 3$ → Item 2 **SELECTED**. New capacity: $5 - 3 = 2$. Move to $V[1][2]$.
- **Item 1:** $V[1][2] = 3 \neq V[0][2] = 0$ → Item 1 **SELECTED**. New capacity: $2 - 2 = 0$. Move to $V[0][0]$.
- **Base case reached.**

**Final Selection:** Items 1 and 2.
- Total Weight: $2 + 3 = 5 \leq W$ ✓
- Total Value: $3 + 4 = 7$ ✓

---

### 3.5 Complexity Analysis Summary

| Resource | Bottom-Up DP | Memoized Recursion | Brute Force |
|----------|-------------|--------------------|-------------|
| **Time** | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(2^n)$ |
| **Space** | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(n \cdot W)$ + recursion stack | $O(n)$ |
| **Optimality** | Guaranteed | Guaranteed | Guaranteed |

> [!NOTE]
> **Pseudo-Polynomial Nature:** The time complexity $O(n \cdot W)$ depends on the *magnitude* of `W`, not just `n`. For very large `W` (e.g., $10^9$), DP becomes impractical, and approximation algorithms are preferred.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Dynamic Programming Flowchart for 0/1 Knapsack

The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the algorithmic flow of the bottom-up DP approach, mapping decision points and iterative loops as a **Block-Level Functional Architecture**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[START Knapsack DP] --> B[Initialize V 0 to n by 0 to W to 0]
    B --> C[Set i = 1]
    C --> D[Set w = 0]
    D --> E{Is w_i greater than w?}
    E -->|Yes| F[V i w = V i-1 w]
    E -->|No| G[Compute A = V i-1 w]
    G --> H[Compute B = v_i + V i-1 w minus w_i]
    H --> I[V i w = max A B]
    F --> J[Increment w by 1]
    I --> J[Increment w by 1]
    J --> K{Is w less than or equal to W?}
    K -->|Yes| D
    K -->|No| L[Increment i by 1]
    L --> M{Is i less than or equal to n?}
    M -->|Yes| C
    M -->|No| N[Print Optimal Value V n W]
    N --> O[Backtrack to find selected items]
    O --> P[END]
```

**How to read this diagram:**
- **Rectangular blocks** represent processing actions (initialization, computations, assignments).
- **Diamond blocks** represent decision points (branching conditions).
- The two nested loops (over `i` and `w`) are represented by the iterative flow from `C` → `D` → `K` → `L` → `M`.
- The final backtracking step reconstructs the actual subset of items chosen.

---

### 4.2 Recursive Decision Tree (Conceptual Subproblem Decomposition)

The following diagram shows how the subproblem `V[i][w]` decomposes into smaller subproblems — illustrating **overlapping subproblems** that motivate memoization.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[V i w] --> S[V i-1 w]
    R --> T[v_i + V i-1 w minus w_i]
    S --> S1[V i-2 w]
    S --> S2[v i-1 + V i-2 w minus w i-1]
    T --> T1[V i-2 w minus w_i]
    T --> T2[v i-1 + V i-2 w minus w_i minus w i-1]
    S1 --> X1[V i-3 w]
    S1 --> X2[v i-2 + V i-3 w minus w i-2]
    T1 --> Y1[V i-2 w minus w_i]
    T1 --> Y2[v i-1 + V i-2 w minus w i-1 minus w_i]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Observing Overlap:** Notice that `V[i-2][w]` (from node `S1`) and `V[i-2][w - w_i]` (from node `T1`) are subproblems that could be recomputed multiple times. **Memoization** stores these values to avoid redundant computation — the foundation of Dynamic Programming.

---

### 4.3 Space-Optimized Version (1D Rolling Array)

For competitive programming and memory-constrained environments, the 2D table can be compressed to a 1D array of size `W+1`:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Initialize DP 0 to W to 0] --> B[For i = 1 to n]
    B --> C[For w = W down to w_i]
    C --> D[DP w = max DP w, v_i + DP w minus w_i]
    D --> E{w equals w_i?}
    E -->|No| C
    E -->|Yes| F[i equals n?}
    F -->|No| B
    F -->|Yes| G[Return DP W]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why iterate `w` from `W` down to `w_i`?**
> Because each item can only be used **once** (0/1 property), iterating downwards prevents the same item from being reused within the same iteration. For unbounded knapsack, you would iterate upwards.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`  
**Define the 0/1 Knapsack Problem. Why is it called "0/1"?**

**Model Answer:**

The 0/1 Knapsack Problem is a combinatorial optimization problem where, given `n` items each with a weight `w[i]` and value `v[i]`, we must select a subset of items to maximize total value while ensuring total weight does not exceed the knapsack capacity `W`. It is called **"0/1"** because each item must be taken in its entirety (`x[i] = 1`) or not taken at all (`x[i] = 0`) — fractional selection is not allowed. This binary decision constraint distinguishes it from the fractional knapsack variant.

---

**Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`  
**Differentiate between 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack problems.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | 0/1 Knapsack | Fractional Knapsack |
|--------|-------------|---------------------|
| **Item Selection** | Whole item only (`x[i]` ∈ {0,1}) | Fractions allowed (`x[i]` ∈ [0,1]) |
| **Strategy** | Dynamic Programming | Greedy (sort by $v_i/w_i$) |
| **Time Complexity** | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(n \log n)$ |
| **Solution Type** | Optimal (exact) | Optimal (exact) |
| **Difficulty** | NP-Hard (pseudo-poly) | Polynomial |

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Alert:**
> 1. Students frequently forget to **initialize the base cases** (`V[0][w] = 0` and `V[i][0] = 0`) — this costs **1–2 marks**.
> 2. Confusion between **upward** and **downward** iteration in the space-optimized version is a common error.
> 3. Many students write the **recurrence relation** without explaining the two cases — KTU expects both `w[i] > w` and `w[i] ≤ w` branches explicitly.
> 4. **Backtracking** to identify the actual subset is often skipped — losing **2 marks** even when the DP table is correct.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the 0/1 Knapsack Problem in detail. Derive the recurrence relation used in the Dynamic Programming approach. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Solve the following instance of the 0/1 Knapsack Problem using Dynamic Programming and identify the optimal subset of items: `n = 4`, items: `(w=2,v=3), (w=3,v=4), (w=4,v=5), (w=5,v=6)`, capacity `W = 5`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Explanation and Recurrence Derivation:**

The 0/1 Knapsack Problem seeks to maximize total value $\sum v_i x_i$ subject to $\sum w_i x_i \leq W$ with binary constraints $x_i \in \{0,1\}$.

Let `V[i][w]` = maximum value using first `i` items with capacity `w`.

- If item `i` is **not included**: $V[i][w] = V[i-1][w]$
- If item `i` is **included** (requires $w_i \leq w$): $V[i][w] = v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i]$

Combined recurrence:

$$
V[i][w] = \begin{cases} V[i-1][w] & \text{if } w_i > w \\ \max(V[i-1][w],\; v_i + V[i-1][w - w_i]) & \text{if } w_i \leq w \end{cases}
$$

Base cases: $V[0][w] = 0$ and $V[i][0] = 0$.

**(b) DP Table Solution:** (Refer to Section 3.3 for the complete table.)

Final answer: **Maximum Value = 7**, achieved by selecting **Items 1 and 2** (total weight = 5, total value = 7).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** Compare the time complexities of the brute-force, recursive, and Dynamic Programming approaches for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem. Justify why DP is preferred. (7 Marks)

**(b)** Write the space-optimized algorithm for the 0/1 Knapsack Problem using a 1D array and explain why we iterate `w` from `W` down to `w[i]`. Trace it for `n = 3`, items: `(w=2,v=3), (w=3,v=4), (w=4,v=5)`, capacity `W = 5`. (7 Marks)

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Complexity Comparison:**

| Approach | Time | Space | Remarks |
|----------|------|-------|---------|
| Brute Force (all subsets) | $O(2^n)$ | $O(n)$ | Exponential — infeasible |
| Naive Recursion | $O(2^n)$ | $O(n)$ stack | No memoization — redundant calls |
| Memoized Recursion (Top-Down) | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(n \cdot W)$ | Eliminates overlapping recomputation |
| Bottom-Up DP | $O(n \cdot W)$ | $O(n \cdot W)$ | Iterative, no recursion overhead |

**Justification:** DP reduces exponential time to pseudo-polynomial $O(n \cdot W)$ by exploiting **optimal substructure** and **overlapping subproblems**. Each subproblem `V[i][w]` is solved exactly once and stored for reuse.

**(b) Space-Optimized Algorithm:**

```
DP[0..W] = 0
For i = 1 to n:
    For w = W down to w[i]:
        DP[w] = max(DP[w], v[i] + DP[w - w[i]])
Return DP[W]
```

**Why iterate downward?** Because each item can be used only once. Iterating downward ensures that `DP[w - w[i]]` refers to the **previous iteration's state** (before item `i` is processed), preventing the same item from being counted multiple times.

**Trace for given problem:**

Initialize: `DP = [0, 0, 0, 0, 0, 0]`

**Item 1 (w=2, v=3):** Iterate `w` from 5 to 2:

$$
DP[2] = \max(0, 3 + 0) = 3
$$

$$
DP[3] = \max(0, 3 + 0) = 3,\quad DP[4] = 3,\quad DP[5] = 3
$$

`DP = [0, 0, 3, 3, 3, 3]`

**Item 2 (w=3, v=4):** Iterate `w` from 5 to 3:

$$
DP[5] = \max(3, 4 + 3) = 7
$$

$$
DP[4] = \max(3, 4 + 0) = 4,\quad DP[3] = \max(3, 4) = 4
$$

`DP = [0, 0, 3, 4, 4, 7]`

**Item 3 (w=4, v=5):** Iterate `w` from 5 to 4:

$$
DP[5] = \max(7, 5 + 0) = 7
$$

$$
DP[4] = \max(4, 5 + 0) = 5
$$

`DP = [0, 0, 3, 4, 5, 7]`

**Maximum Value:** `DP[5] = 7` ✓

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- The 0/1 Knapsack uses **binary decisions**: each item is either fully included or excluded.
- The core **recurrence relation** has two cases: $w_i > w$ (exclude) and $w_i \leq w$ (max of include/exclude).
- **Base cases:** Always initialize `V[0][w] = 0` and `V[i][0] = 0` — losing 1 mark if missed.
- **DP Table Dimensions:** `(n+1) × (W+1)` — indices go from 0 to n and 0 to W inclusive.
- **Time Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W)$ — pseudo-polynomial in `W`.
- **Space Complexity:** $O(n \cdot W)$ standard; can be reduced to $O(W)$ using rolling array.
- **Optimal value** is at `V[n][W]` (bottom-right cell).
- **Backtracking** is required to identify the *actual subset* of items selected.
- **Greedy fails** for 0/1 knapsack (it works only for fractional knapsack).
- **Space-optimized version** iterates `w` from `W` down to `w[i]` to prevent item reuse.
- The problem is **NP-Hard** in general — DP gives pseudo-polynomial, not true polynomial, solution.
- For very large `W`, consider **branch-and-bound** or **approximation algorithms**.
- **Memoization** (top-down) and **tabulation** (bottom-up) both achieve $O(n \cdot W)$ time.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: LC Branch and Bound Solution
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECAT415 - ALGORITHM ANALYSIS AND DESIGN
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** LC Branch and Bound Solution

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **LC (Least Cost) Branch and Bound** is a **state-space search technique** for solving optimization problems, principally the **0/1 Knapsack Problem**. It generates a *state-space tree* in which each node represents a subset of items, computes a **lower bound (cost-to-go estimate)** for the best feasible solution reachable from that node, and always **expands (E-node) the live node with the smallest lower bound first** using a **min-priority queue (LC-search)**.

In strict KTU 2024 syllabus wording, LC Branch and Bound is the **Bounding-Function-Driven BFS** flavour of branch-and-bound — as opposed to LIFO (FIFO) Branch and Bound which uses a stack (queue) without regard to cost.

### Bounding Function (Formal)

For a node at level $i$ (deciding items $1, 2, \dots, n$):

$$
LB = -\left(p_1 w_1 + p_2 w_2 + \cdots + p_i w_i\right) + \left(\text{best contribution from remaining items}\right)
$$

Where the contribution from the undecided items is computed as the **fractional knapsack value** starting from item $i+1$ (this gives a valid, *optimistic* lower bound for maximization of profit under weight constraint $W$).

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "Smartest Real-Estate Investor"**

Imagine you are a real-estate investor with a fixed budget $W$ and a list of $n$ properties. Each property $i$ has:
- A **price** $w_i$ (weight)
- A **profit/rental yield** $p_i$ (value)

You must pick a subset (no fractional ownership — either buy a whole property or skip it) to **maximise total profit** without exceeding budget.

- The **Branch** step = deciding "buy / skip" for each property → forms a decision tree.
- The **Bound** step = for every partial decision list, you ask: *"Even if I were allowed to break properties into pieces (fractional knapsack), what is the best I can do from here?"* That number is a **lower bound** — the *most you can possibly earn* if you continue down this branch.
- The **Least-Cost** part = you maintain a **leaderboard of partial plans sorted by their best-case profit**. You **always invest more time into the most promising plan first**.

Why "least cost"? Because the algorithm is named after its original minimization formulation (LC-search = Least-Cost search). When applied to **maximization** (knapsack), we minimise the *negative profit*, hence "least cost" expands the most negative node first.

### Standard Metrics You Must Know

| Metric | Symbol | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Weight of item $i$ | $w_i$ | Resource consumed (kg, RAM, budget units) |
| Profit of item $i$ | $p_i$ | Value gained (rupees, throughput) |
| Capacity | $W$ | Total resource available |
| Profit-to-weight ratio | $p_i/w_i$ | Used for fractional sort |
| Lower Bound at node | $LB$ | Optimistic best achievable |
| Upper Bound | $UB$ | Best *known* feasible solution so far |
| **Live Node** | – | Generated but not yet expanded |
| **E-Node** | – | Live node currently being expanded |
| **Dead Node** | – | Pruned — cannot lead to optimum |

---

## 1.3 Why LC Branch and Bound?

> [!TIP]
> - **FIFO / LIFO Branch and Bound** explores nodes in a fixed order — may waste effort on bad subtrees.
> - **LC Branch and Bound** uses a *cost-driven priority queue* — equivalent to **Dijkstra's shortest-path algorithm** on the state-space tree.
> - It **prunes more aggressively** because the lower bound is tighter (fractional knapsack gives the best optimistic bound for 0/1 knapsack).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Algorithm Skeleton (the *How*)

**Input:** $n$ items with weights $w_i$ and profits $p_i$, capacity $W$.

**Step 0 — Sort:** Order items by **decreasing** profit-to-weight ratio $p_i / w_i$ (this is a prerequisite for the bounding function to be valid).

**Step 1 — Initialise:** Compute the *fractional knapsack* value of the **entire sorted item set** as the initial lower bound. Insert it into a min-priority queue keyed by $-\text{LB}$.

**Step 2 — Loop:** While the priority queue is not empty:
1. Remove the node with the **minimum cost** (largest LB) → make it the **E-node**.
2. **Prune** if $LB(\text{E-node}) \le UB$ (current best feasible solution). If so, discard.
3. Otherwise, branch into two children:
   - **Left child** = include next item $i$ (if weight permits).
   - **Right child** = exclude next item $i$.
4. For each child, compute its lower bound. Insert it into the priority queue.
5. After expanding, **update $UB$** if either child yields a better *integer* feasible solution.

**Step 3 — Terminate:** When the queue is empty, $UB$ holds the optimal 0/1 knapsack profit.

---

## 2.2 The Lower Bound Formula (Critical for KTU)

For a node at level $k$ (already decided items $1, 2, \dots, k$):

$$
LB_k = \underbrace{\sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i x_i}_{\text{fixed profit (decided items)}} \;+\; \underbrace{\sum_{i=k+1}^{j-1} p_i}_{\text{fully-taken items}} \;+\; \underbrace{p_j \cdot \frac{W - W_k}{w_j}}_{\text{fractional part of item } j}
$$

Where:
- $x_i = 1$ if item $i$ is included, else $0$.
- Items $k+1, \dots, j-1$ are the next items that **fit completely** into the remaining capacity.
- $W_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} w_i x_i$ is the current accumulated weight.
- Item $j$ is the **first item that does not fully fit** — only a fraction $(W - W_k)/w_j$ is taken.
- Items $j+1, \dots, n$ are **rejected**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This is identical to the **fractional knapsack solution starting from the current partial assignment**. The fractional part is the reason the bound is *optimistic* (≥ true 0/1 optimum) — making safe pruning possible.

---

## 2.3 Pruning Rule

A node with lower bound $LB$ can be **killed** if:

$$
LB \le \text{Best feasible solution so far (Upper Bound)}
$$

*Because the best 0/1 knapsack achievable from this subtree cannot exceed $LB$, which is no better than what we already have.*

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol | Usage in Exam |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $p_i / w_i$ | Profit density | Sort items descending |
| 2 | $LB_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i x_i + \sum_{i=k+1}^{j-1} p_i + p_j \cdot \frac{W - W_k}{w_j}$ | Lower bound (maximisation) | Compute node cost |
| 3 | Cost $= -LB$ | Min-cost (LC-search) | Priority queue key |
| 4 | Prune when $-LB \ge -\text{BestSoFar}$ | Pruning rule | Discard node |
| 5 | $UB = \max(\text{feasible profits found})$ | Best integer solution | Update during search |
| 6 | E-node rule: smallest cost first | Search discipline | Always pop min-cost |
| 7 | $W_k + w_{k+1} \le W$ | Feasibility check | Left-child validity |
| 8 | $\lfloor x \rfloor$, $\lceil x \rceil$ | Round-off | For travel-salesman style LC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** LC Branch and Bound is used in **VLSI floorplanning**, **CPU job-scheduling** (where tasks = items, time-slice = capacity), **cargo loading**, **project selection with budgets**, and **resource-constrained portfolio optimisation** in finance.

---

## 2.5 Worked Outline (mini-example to anchor intuition)

> Suppose 3 items, sorted by $p_i/w_i$: weights = $(2, 3, 4)$, profits = $(40, 50, 60)$, $W = 5$.

| Step | Action | LB | Queue |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | Root (no decision) | $40 + 50 \cdot (3/5) = 70$ | $\{-70\}$ |
| 1 | Pop root, branch | L: 40, R: 0 | $\{-40, -50\}$ |
| 2 | Pop $L$ (cost $-40$, biggest) | L: $40+50\cdot(3/5)=70$ | $\{-70, -50\}$ |
| 3 | Pop $-70$ → feasible, set $UB=90$ (items 1+2) | – | $\{-50\}$ |
| 4 | Pop $-50$ (item 1 excluded) → subtree pruned | – | $\emptyset$ |

Optimum = **90** (take items 1 and 2). Node representing "exclude item 1" is pruned because its LB $\le 90$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Canonical KTU Worked Example (Full Derivation)

> **Problem (typical KTU board question):**
> Consider the 0/1 knapsack instance with $n = 4$ items, capacity $W = 15$.

| Item $i$ | Profit $p_i$ | Weight $w_i$ | $p_i / w_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | 10 | 2 | **5.00** |
| 2 | 10 | 4 | 2.50 |
| 3 | 12 | 6 | 2.00 |
| 4 | 18 | 9 | 2.00 |

(Already sorted by $p_i/w_i$ — items 3 and 4 are tied; we keep 3 before 4 for determinism.)

**Find the optimal profit using LC Branch and Bound.**

---

### Step 1 — Root Node (Level 0, no decision)

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_0 &= 0, \quad P_0 = 0 \\
\text{Take item 1 fully:} &\quad 10,\; W = 2 \\
\text{Take item 2 fully:} &\quad 10,\; W = 6 \\
\text{Take item 3 fully:} &\quad 12,\; W = 12 \\
\text{Remaining capacity} &= 15 - 12 = 3 \\
\text{Item 4 fraction} &= (3/9) \times 18 = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
LB_0 = 10 + 10 + 12 + 6 = 38
$$

> Cost in priority queue = $-38$.

---

### Step 2 — Expand Root (E-node = root)

Create **Left child** ($x_1 = 1$) and **Right child** ($x_1 = 0$).

#### Child A — Include Item 1 ($x_1 = 1$)

$W_1 = 2$, $P_1 = 10$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Take item 2 fully:} &\quad +10,\; W = 6 \\
\text{Take item 3 fully:} &\quad +12,\; W = 12 \\
\text{Remaining} &= 3, \text{ fraction of item 4} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
LB_A = 10 + 10 + 12 + 6 = 38
$$

> Queue: $\{-38_A, \dots\}$.

#### Child B — Exclude Item 1 ($x_1 = 0$)

$W_1 = 0$, $P_1 = 0$.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Take item 2 fully:} &\quad +10,\; W = 4 \\
\text{Take item 3 fully:} &\quad +12,\; W = 10 \\
\text{Take item 4 fully?} &\quad 9 > 5 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{fraction } (5/9)\times 18 = 10
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
LB_B = 0 + 10 + 12 + 10 = 32
$$

> Queue: $\{-38_A,\; -32_B\}$.

---

### Step 3 — Pop Smallest Cost (most negative → highest LB)

Pop **A** (cost $-38$). Expand it.

#### A-L (Include Item 2): $x_2 = 1$, $W_2 = 6$, $P_2 = 20$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Take item 3 fully:} &\quad +12,\; W = 12 \\
\text{Remaining} &= 3, \text{ fraction of item 4} = 6
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
LB_{A\text{-}L} = 20 + 12 + 6 = 38
$$

*Feasible* ($W_2 = 6 \le 15$). Update **UB = max(0, 20, 10, 12, …) → 20** (so far).

#### A-R (Exclude Item 2): $x_2 = 0$, $W_2 = 2$, $P_2 = 10$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Take item 3 fully:} &\quad +12,\; W = 8 \\
\text{Take item 4 fully?} &\quad 9 > 7 \;\Rightarrow\; \text{fraction } (7/9)\times 18 = 14
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
LB_{A\text{-}R} = 10 + 12 + 14 = 36
$$

> Queue: $\{-38_{A\text{-}L},\; -36_{A\text{-}R},\; -32_B\}$.

---

### Step 4 — Pop $A\text{-}L$ (cost $-38$, best)

$W = 6$, $P = 20$. Branch on item 3.

#### A-L-L (Include 3): $W = 12$, $P = 32$

Remaining capacity $= 3$, fraction of item 4 = 6.

$$
LB = 32 + 6 = 38
$$

Feasible — integer profit **32** is better than UB=20, so **UB ← 32**.

#### A-L-R (Exclude 3): $W = 6$, $P = 20$

Take item 4 fully? $9 > 9$? No — exactly fits. $W = 15$, $P = 20 + 18 = 38$.

$$
LB = 38
$$

Feasible — profit **38** > UB=32, so **UB ← 38**.

> Queue: $\{-38_{A\text{-}L\text{-}L},\; -38_{A\text{-}L\text{-}R},\; -36_{A\text{-}R},\; -32_B\}$.

---

### Step 5 — Pop $A\text{-}L\text{-}L$ (cost $-38$)

$W = 12$, $P = 32$. Last item to decide (item 4).

#### Include item 4: $W = 21 > 15$ → **Infeasible** — kill.

#### Exclude item 4: $W = 12$, $P = 32$ (final leaf)

$LB = 32$. Since $32 \le UB = 38$, **pruned**.

---

### Step 6 — Pop $A\text{-}L\text{-}R$ (cost $-38$)

$W = 15$, $P = 38$. Already at capacity, both children infeasible (no room for item 4). Final leaf — value **38** matches UB. **Optimal profit = 38**.

---

### Step 7 — Pop $A\text{-}R$ (cost $-36$)

$LB = 36 \le UB = 38$ → **PRUNED** (cannot beat current best).

### Step 8 — Pop $B$ (cost $-32$)

$LB = 32 \le UB = 38$ → **PRUNED**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Optimal Solution:** Take items **1, 2, 4** (i.e. $x_1=1, x_2=1, x_3=0, x_4=1$).
> Total weight = $2 + 4 + 9 = 15 = W$.
> **Total Profit = $10 + 10 + 18 = 38$**.

---

## 3.2 Complete Bound Calculation Table (For Revisions)

| Node | Path $(x_1 x_2 x_3 x_4)$ | $W_k$ | $P_k$ (int) | Remaining cap | Fraction of next | $LB$ | Cost $-LB$ | Status |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Root | $-$ | 0 | 0 | 15 | item 4 → 6 | **38** | $-38$ | E-node |
| A | $1$ | 2 | 10 | 13 | item 4 → 6 | **38** | $-38$ | Live |
| B | $0$ | 0 | 0 | 15 | item 4 → 10 | **32** | $-32$ | Live |
| AL | $11$ | 6 | 20 | 9 | item 4 → 6 | **38** | $-38$ | E-node |
| AR | $10$ | 2 | 10 | 13 | item 4 → 14 | **36** | $-36$ | Live |
| ALL | $111$ | 12 | 32 | 3 | item 4 → 6 | **38** | $-38$ | E-node |
| ALR | $110$ | 6 | 20 | 9 | item 4 → 18 | **38** | $-38$ | Live |
| ALLL | $1111$ | 21 | 50 | – | – | – | – | Infeasible |
| ALLR | $1110$ | 12 | 32 | 3 | – | **32** | $-32$ | Leaf, ≤ UB, pruned |
| ALRL | $1101$ | 15 | 38 | 0 | – | **38** | – | Leaf, **OPTIMAL** |
| ALRR | $1100$ | 6 | 20 | 9 | – | 20 | – | Leaf |
| AR… | $10xx$ | 2 | 10 | 13 | – | **36** | – | Pruned ($36 \le 38$) |
| B… | $0xxx$ | 0 | 0 | 15 | – | **32** | – | Pruned ($32 \le 38$) |

### Node Color Legend
- 🟦 **Blue** — Live / explored nodes.
- 🟩 **Green** — Optimal leaf (final answer).
- 🟥 **Red** — Pruned / infeasible leaves.
- 🟧 **Orange** — Subtree pruned at parent via bounding.

---

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture: LC Search Engine

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph IN["Input Layer"]
        ITEMS["Items n, wi, pi, W"]
        SORT["Sort by pi/wi DESCENDING"]
    end

    subgraph CORE["LC Search Engine"]
        ROOT["Compute Root LB via Fractional KP"]
        PQ["Min-Priority Queue keyed by -LB"]
        ENODE["Pop Smallest Cost → E-node"]
        BRANCH["Generate Left/Right Children"]
        CHK{"Feasible?\nwk+1 le W"}
        BND["Compute LB per child\n(Fractional KP from child state)"]
        PRUNE{"LB le UB?\n(Upper Bound)?"}
        UPD["Update UB = max UB, child integer profit"]
    end

    subgraph OUT["Output Layer"]
        RESULT["Final UB = Optimal Profit\nBest Item Subset"]
    end

    ITEMS --> SORT --> ROOT --> PQ
    PQ --> ENODE --> BRANCH --> CHK
    CHK -- Yes --> BND --> PRUNE
    CHK -- No --> KILL["Discard Infeasible"]
    PRUNE -- No --> PQ
    PRUNE -- Yes --> KILL
    BND --> UPD --> PQ
    PQ -.->|Queue empty| RESULT
    UPD -.-> RESULT
```

---

## 4.3 Bounding-Function Visualisation

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Decision: Include item i?"] --> B["W + wi le W ?"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Compute LB_left\n(current profit + pi + fractional tail)"]
    B -- No --> D["Skip item i (right child)"]
    D --> E["Compute LB_right\n(fractional tail from item i+1)"]
    C --> F["Insert -LB_left into PQ"]
    E --> G["Insert -LB_right into PQ"]
    F --> H["Pop min -LB → next E-node"]
    G --> H
    H --> I{"-LB_pop le -UB ?"}
    I -- Yes --> J["PRUNE Subtree"]
    I -- No --> K["Continue Branching"]
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**
**Define the LC-search in branch-and-bound. How does it differ from FIFO branch-and-bound?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **LC-search (Least-Cost search)** is a branch-and-bound strategy that expands live nodes from a **min-priority queue** keyed by the **cost (or lower bound)** of each node, ensuring the node with the **smallest cost (or highest bound)** is chosen as the next E-node.
>
> **Difference from FIFO B&B:**
>
> | Aspect | FIFO B&B | LC B&B |
> |---|---|---|
> | Data structure | Queue / Stack | Min-priority queue |
> | Selection rule | First-in-first-out | Smallest cost first |
> | Optimality | Yes (BFS) | Yes (Dijkstra-like) |
> | Pruning efficiency | Lower | Higher (cost-aware) |

*(1 mark definition, 1 mark data structure, 1 mark comparison.)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is a bounding function? State the lower-bound formula for the 0/1 knapsack problem used in LC branch-and-bound.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **bounding function** estimates the best possible value (upper or lower bound) achievable from a given node's subtree, used to decide whether to prune.

For **0/1 Knapsack** (after sorting by $p_i/w_i$), the lower bound at a node where items $1..k$ are decided:

$$
LB_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} p_i x_i \;+\; \sum_{i=k+1}^{j-1} p_i \;+\; p_j \cdot \frac{W - W_k}{w_j}
$$

where items $k+1, \dots, j-1$ fit completely and item $j$ is the first item that is taken fractionally.

*(1 mark bounding function definition, 2 marks formula with explanation.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to **sort by $p_i/w_i$ in descending order** *before* computing bounds. The fractional bound is **only valid** when items are sorted by profit density. **[-2 marks]**
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using the **upper bound** of fractional knapsack (which is the same as the LB!) instead of recognising it as the *optimistic* bound for **0/1** problem. **[-1 mark]**
> - **Common Mistake 3:** **Pruning rule** — students often confuse "kill when $LB \le$ BestSoFar" with "kill when $LB \ge$". Remember: for *maximisation*, prune if **no better than current best**, i.e. $LB \le UB$. **[-2 marks]**
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Not showing the **priority queue order at each step** — KTU examiners want to see the queue evolution. **[-1 mark]**
> - **Common Mistake 5:** Reporting only the **optimal value** but not the **optimal subset** (item set). **[-1 mark]**

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Consider the 0/1 knapsack with $n = 4$, $W = 15$, items:

| Item $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $w_i$ | 2 | 4 | 6 | 9 |
| $p_i$ | 10 | 10 | 12 | 18 |

Solve using **LC Branch and Bound**. Show the state-space tree, all lower-bound computations, and the order of node expansions.

**(b) [7 marks]** Explain with a diagram how a **min-priority queue** governs the LC search. Compare its time complexity with FIFO branch-and-bound for the knapsack problem.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Step-by-step solution (matches Section 3 worked example):**

1. **Sort** by $p_i/w_i$ descending → already sorted (5.0, 2.5, 2.0, 2.0).
2. **Root** $LB = 38$ (cost $-38$).
3. **Expand root** → child A ($x_1=1$, $LB=38$) and child B ($x_1=0$, $LB=32$).
4. **Pop A** → AL ($LB=38$, cost $-38$) and AR ($LB=36$).
5. **Pop AL** → ALL ($LB=38$), ALR ($LB=38$). Update $UB = 38$ at ALR.
6. Pop ALL → ALLL infeasible; ALLR pruned ($LB=32 \le 38$).
7. Pop ALR → ALRL gives $P=38$ at $W=15$ (capacity full) — **OPTIMAL**.
8. AR pruned ($36 \le 38$); B pruned ($32 \le 38$).
9. **Final Answer:** Take items $\{1, 2, 4\}$, total weight $15$, **profit $38$**.

*(Full mark breakdown: 1 mark sorting, 2 marks root + first level, 2 marks second level, 1 mark third level, 1 mark optimal subset.)*

**(b) Model Answer:**

> The **min-priority queue (PQ)** stores live nodes keyed by their cost (= $-LB$). At each step, the queue operation `extract-min` returns the node with the *smallest cost*, which becomes the **E-node**. After computing children, `insert` adds their costs to the PQ. The queue automatically maintains the invariant that the most promising node is at the front.
>
> **Time complexity:**
>
> | Algorithm | Best case | Worst case | Average |
> |---|---|---|---|
> | FIFO B&B | $O(2^n)$ | $O(2^n)$ | $O(2^n)$ |
> | LC B&B | $O(2^n)$ with heavy pruning | $O(2^n)$ | Often $O(\text{poly})$ on easy instances |
>
> PQ operations (insert/extract-min) cost $O(\log N)$ where $N$ = number of live nodes ≤ $2^n$. With aggressive pruning, LC B&B can be **exponentially faster in practice** than FIFO B&B on structured instances.

*(3 marks PQ mechanics, 2 marks diagram description, 2 marks complexity table.)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**(a) [7 marks]** For the following 0/1 knapsack instance, apply LC Branch and Bound to obtain the optimal solution. Items sorted by $p_i/w_i$:

| Item $i$ | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $w_i$ | 3 | 4 | 5 | 8 |
| $p_i$ | 15 | 12 | 12 | 16 |
| $W$ | 10 |

**(b) [7 marks]** What are **live nodes, E-nodes, and dead nodes** in branch-and-bound? Why is the LC variant often more efficient than the FIFO variant? Give two real-world applications.

#### Model Solution

**(a) Worked-out solution:**

1. **Sort:** $p_i/w_i$ = 5.0, 3.0, 2.4, 2.0 → already sorted.
2. **Root LB:** Take item 1 (W=3, P=15), take item 2 (W=7, P=27), take item 3 fully? $7+5=12>10$ → fractional $(3/5)\times12 = 7.2$. $LB_0 = 27 + 7.2 = \mathbf{34.2}$. Cost $-34.2$.
3. **Expand root:**
   - **Left** ($x_1=1$): $W=3, P=15$. Add item 2 fully ($W=7,P=27$). Item 3 fractional $(3/5)\times 12 = 7.2$. $LB_A = 34.2$. Cost $-34.2$.
   - **Right** ($x_1=0$): $W=0, P=0$. Item 2 fully ($W=4,P=12$), item 3 fully ($W=9,P=24$). Item 4 fractional $(1/8)\times 16 = 2$. $LB_B = 26$. Cost $-26$.
4. **PQ:** $\{-34.2_A,\; -26_B\}$. Pop A.
5. **Expand A:**
   - **AL** ($x_1x_2=11$): $W=7, P=27$. Item 3 fully ($W=12>10$!) → fractional $(3/5)\times12 = 7.2$. $LB_{AL} = 34.2$. Cost $-34.2$. **UB ← 27** (so far).
   - **AR** ($x_1x_2=10$): $W=3, P=15$. Item 3 fully ($W=8, P=27$). Item 4 fractional $(2/8)\times 16 = 4$. $LB_{AR} = 31$. Cost $-31$.
6. **PQ:** $\{-34.2_{AL},\; -31_{AR},\; -26_B\}$. Pop AL.
7. **Expand AL** ($x_1x_2x_3 = 111$ branch attempt): $W=7, P=27$. Item 3 fractional only.
   - **ALL** (include 3 fraction? — NO! 0/1 means we either fully include or skip). $W=7+5=12>10$ → **Infeasible**.
   - **ALR** (exclude 3): $W=7, P=27$. Item 4 fully? $7+8=15>10$ → fractional $(3/8)\times 16 = 6$. $LB = 33$. Cost $-33$.
8. **PQ:** $\{-33_{ALR},\; -31_{AR},\; -26_B\}$. **UB = 27** (current best feasible).
9. **Pop ALR:** $LB = 33 > UB=27$ → continue.
   - **ALRL** (include 4): $W = 7+8=15>10$ → **Infeasible**.
   - **ALRR** (exclude 4): $W=7, P=27$. **Leaf.** $27 \le UB$ → no improvement.
10. **Pop AR** (cost $-31$): $LB = 31 > 27$ → continue. $W=3, P=15$, deciding item 4.
    - **ARL** (include 4): $W=3+8=11>10$ → **Infeasible**.
    - **ARR** (exclude 4): $W=3, P=15$, add item 3 → $W=8, P=27$. Item 4 already decided (excluded). $W=8\le 10$, $P=27$. **Leaf, profit 27.** Equal to UB — no improvement.
11. **Pop B** (cost $-26$): $LB=26 \le UB=27$ → **PRUNED**.

> **Optimal Solution: items 1 and 2 (skip 3 and 4)**. Weight = $3+4 = 7 \le 10$, **Profit = 27**.

*(Distribution: 1 mark sort, 2 marks root/level 1, 2 marks level 2, 1 mark level 3, 1 mark final answer.)*

**(b) Model Answer:**

> - **Live Node:** A node that has been generated but not yet expanded.
> - **E-Node (Expanded Node):** The live node currently being expanded (its children are generated).
> - **Dead Node:** A node that has been generated, expanded (or determined to be a leaf/pruned), and is no longer in the priority queue — it will not be expanded further. A node becomes dead if (i) it is a leaf, (ii) its bound ≤ current best, (iii) its branch is infeasible.
>
> **Why LC is more efficient:** LC chooses the **most promising** node first (lowest cost / highest LB). This produces a **good feasible solution early**, tightening the upper bound $UB$, which then prunes more nodes later. FIFO has no such preference.
>
> **Real-world applications:**
> 1. **VLSI chip design** — assigning logic blocks to physical slots under area/power constraints.
> 2. **Project portfolio selection** — choosing R&D projects within a budget to maximise expected return.
> *(Bonus: cargo loading, CPU process scheduling, bioinformatics sequence alignment.)*

*(2 marks node definitions, 3 marks efficiency reasoning, 2 marks applications.)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — LC Branch and Bound (0/1 Knapsack)**

- [x] **Sort items by $p_i/w_i$ descending** *before* any computation. *(Essential — bound is invalid otherwise.)*
- [x] **Lower bound** = profit of decided items + profit of items that fit completely + fractional profit of next item.
- [x] **Cost in PQ** = $-LB$ (negative, since we use min-PQ for max problem).
- [x] **Pruning rule:** Kill a node if $LB \le$ current best feasible solution ($UB$).
- [x] **Update $UB$** whenever an integer-feasible node with profit $>UB$ is found.
- [x] **Left child** = include next item (only if weight permits).
- [x] **Right child** = exclude next item.
- [x] **Optimal subset must be reported** along with optimal value.
- [x] **Stop** when PQ is empty → $UB$ is the answer.
- [x] **LC vs FIFO:** LC = priority queue, FIFO = simple queue. LC prunes more.
- [x] **State-space tree** must be drawn with $x_i = 1$ (left) / $x_i = 0$ (right) branches.
- [x] For **minimisation** problems (e.g., assignment, TSP), use $LB$ directly without negation; PQ is still min-cost.
- [x] Complexity: $O(2^n)$ worst case, but often near-polynomial in practice due to pruning.
- [x] Examiners love asking: *"Why sort by $p_i/w_i$?"* → Answer: "It guarantees the fractional solution is the *maximum* possible; otherwise the bound is no longer optimistic."

> **Key Insight to Carry to Exam Hall:** LC Branch and Bound is essentially **Dijkstra's algorithm on the state-space tree** of an optimisation problem. The "distance" to a node is the *cost* (= $-$profit-so-far + estimated tail), and we always expand the most-promising (cheapest) frontier.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**FIFO (First-In-First-Out) Branch and Bound** is a general algorithmic technique for solving combinatorial optimization problems by systematically enumerating candidate solutions through implicit state space tree search. In this method, a **queue** data structure (FIFO discipline) is used to maintain the list of *live nodes* that have been generated but not yet expanded. The node at the front of the queue is removed next, expanded (becoming the **E-node** or *expansion node*), and its children are appended to the **rear** of the queue.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *FIFO Branch and Bound is a method of solving optimization problems in which the E-node remains E-node until it is proved to be non-promising. When the E-node is found to be non-promising, it becomes a D-node (dead node) and the next node in the queue (FIFO order) becomes the new E-node. The children of a promising E-node are added to the rear of the queue.*

A node is **promising** if its lower bound (or cost bound) is less than or equal to the current best upper bound solution found so far. Otherwise, it is **non-promising** and is discarded (becomes a D-node).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuitive Real-World Understanding)

**Analogy: A Government Job Application Queue at an Interview Hall**

Imagine you walk into a government recruitment office where 5,000 candidates are queued up for a limited number of positions. The officer follows a strict rule:
- Candidates are seated in chairs arranged in a row (the **queue**).
- The officer calls the candidate who has been **waiting the longest** (the one at the front).
- This candidate (now the **E-node**) goes through the interview desk.
- The officer checks whether the candidate's credentials are good enough (**bound check / promising**).
  - If the candidate is **promising** (passes the cutoff), they are kept; their children (documents to verify) are added to the **rear** of the queue.
  - If **not promising**, the candidate is rejected (**D-node**) and the next in line is called.
- This continues until the queue is empty or the best candidate is found.

In this analogy:
- **Queue** = FIFO data structure holding live nodes.
- **E-node** = Candidate currently being interviewed (being expanded).
- **D-node** = Rejected candidate (dead node, pruned branch).
- **Promising** = Above the cutoff score (passes the bound test).
- **Children** = Sub-options generated from the E-node.

## 1.3 Key Terminology (KTU Board Definition Format)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **State Space Tree (SST)** | A rooted tree representing all possible configurations/solutions of the problem. |
| **Live Node** | A node that has been generated but whose children are not yet explored. |
| **E-Node (Expansion Node)** | A live node currently being expanded to generate its children. |
| **D-Node (Dead Node)** | A node that cannot lead to an optimal solution; eliminated from further consideration. |
| **Bounding Function** | A function $\hat{c}(x)$ that computes a lower bound on the cost of any solution reachable from node $x$. |
| **Cost Matrix** | In TSP/Assignment, a 2D array storing travel/assignment costs between entities. |
| **Reduced Cost Matrix** | A cost matrix where row and column reductions yield a lower bound on path cost. |
| **Best-First** (comparison) | Uses a priority queue; FIFO uses simple queue. |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** FIFO Branch and Bound is a **simplest form** of branch and bound. It does *not* use a priority queue — all live nodes are treated with equal priority based on their arrival time. This is the key difference from LC-Search and LC-Branch-and-Bound (which use a min-heap / priority queue ordered by lower bound cost).

## 1.4 Applications in Engineering

FIFO Branch and Bound is widely used in:
- **Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP)** — finding minimum tour cost.
- **Assignment Problem** — optimal task-to-worker assignment.
- **0/1 Knapsack Problem** — maximum value with weight constraint.
- **Job Sequencing with Deadlines**.
- **Network Design** (route planning, VLSI circuit layout).
- **AI Search** (where uniform-cost exploration is acceptable).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Concept — Structured Logic Steps

The FIFO Branch and Bound algorithm follows a strict operational sequence. Below is the breakdown:

### Step 1: Initialize the Queue and Best Cost
- Create an empty queue $Q$.
- Set `best_cost = ∞`.
- Create the root node representing the empty state.

### Step 2: Insert Root into Queue
- Push root node $R$ into $Q$.
- The root represents the starting configuration (e.g., in TSP, vertex 1 is the starting city).

### Step 3: While Queue is Not Empty
1. **Remove** the front element of $Q$ → call it $u$ (E-node).
2. **Generate** all children of $u$.
3. For each child $v$ of $u$:
   - **Check feasibility:** Is $v$ a valid partial solution? (e.g., does it violate any constraint?)
   - If **infeasible** → discard $v$ (becomes D-node).
   - If **feasible**:
     - Compute the lower bound $LB(v)$.
     - If $LB(v) \geq$ `best_cost` → discard (D-node, **bounding/pruning**).
     - If $LB(v) <$ `best_cost`:
       - If $v$ is a **complete solution** → update `best_cost = LB(v)`.
       - Else (partial solution) → add $v$ to the **rear** of $Q$ (live node).

### Step 4: Termination
- Stop when $Q$ is empty.
- Return `best_cost` and the corresponding solution.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" Behind Each Step

- **Why FIFO Queue?** It guarantees that all live nodes are eventually expanded. A node cannot remain live forever — eventually it becomes the E-node, gets its chance, and is either expanded or pruned.
- **Why bounding function?** Without it, the state space tree explodes exponentially. Bounding prunes entire subtrees that provably cannot contain the optimum.
- **Why rear insertion?** Children of the *current* E-node must be processed *after* all live nodes already in the queue (preserves FIFO ordering).
- **How does pruning happen?** Two ways: (1) Infeasibility (constraint violation), (2) Lower bound ≥ current best upper bound.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Formulas for TSP using FIFO Branch and Bound**

| # | Concept | Formula / Rule |
|---|---------|----------------|
| 1 | Row Reduction (cost of row $i$) | $r_i = \min_j C[i][j]$ |
| 2 | Column Reduction (cost of column $j$) | $c_j = \min_i C[i][j]$ |
| 3 | Lower Bound of Root | $LB_{root} = \sum_i r_i + \sum_j c_j$ |
| 4 | Lower Bound after selecting edge $(i,j)$ | $LB_{v} = LB_{parent} + C[i][j] + \text{(additional reductions in new matrix)}$ |
| 5 | Additional Reduction Term | $A = \min_{k \neq j} C[i][k] + \min_{k \neq i} C[k][j]$ |
| 6 | Promising Node Condition | $LB_v < \text{best\_cost}$ |
| 7 | D-Node Condition | $LB_v \geq \text{best\_cost}$ |
| 8 | Updated Bound for Child (full) | $LB_{child} = LB_{parent} + C[i][j] + r + c$ where $r, c$ are extra reductions |
| 9 | Infeasibility Condition (TSP) | Cannot revisit city; cannot close to visited city |
| 10 | Best Upper Bound (initially) | $\text{best\_cost} = \infty$ |

> **Note on Absolute Value Notation:** All values inside matrices are non-negative integers. We never use absolute value bars inside the table above to avoid markdown parsing errors.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of FIFO Branch and Bound

| Engineering Field | Application |
|-------------------|-------------|
| **Operations Research** | Vehicle routing, supply chain optimization |
| **VLSI Design** | Component placement on chip to minimize wire length |
| **Logistics & Transportation** | Multi-stop delivery route minimization |
| **Manufacturing** | Job-shop scheduling with deadlines |
| **Telecommunications** | Network design and shortest path with constraints |
| **Bioinformatics** | DNA sequence alignment (constrained version) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why FIFO over LC Search?** FIFO Branch and Bound is **simpler to implement** (only needs a basic queue, not a heap). The trade-off is that it may explore more nodes before finding the optimum. However, it is guaranteed to find the *exact* optimal solution (unlike greedy or approximation methods).
